Special attention will be paid to the challenge of cultural diplomacy between the United States (and the west) and the Muslim world. The course will be organized as a seminar, with weekly readings, frequent brief class presentations, plus a final research paper and oral report.

title: GU Dance Company
description: This course focuses on developing skills in dance technique, choreography and performance through participation as members of Georgetown Dance Company (GUDC). GUDC is a pre-professional concert repertory dance company performing works in classical ballet, contemporary, jazz, indigenous, hip hop, and tap. The course consists of 3 hours of technique class per week in either ballet or modern, and up to 12 hours per week of rehearsal. Choreography is created by student and professional guest choreographers. Dancers must attend at least two professional dance productions and provide a critical review of the technique. Auditions are held in September and performances are once per semester.

title: Black Movements Dance Theatre
description: The course is designed to empower, educate, train, and develop students in the performance skills of dance as members of Black Movement Dance Theatre (BMDT). BMDT is a contemporary modern dance company in which the artistic development fuses the mediums of dance and theater, to reflect the rich diverse traditions of the African-American experience. Course content includes modern technique class (e.g. Dunham and Graham), with additional elements in African, ballet, jazz and hip-hop dance. Students work with faculty and professional guest artists exploring studio work and choreography for concert preparation. Participation in BMDT provides the opportunity for students to develop their own choreography for concert and studio presentation. Dancers must attend one professional concert and provide critical review. Audition for the course occur in September. Performance opportunities are ongoing including a main stage concert in the Fall.

title: Intermediate Ballet
description: Intermediate Ballet Technique Class requires that its students must have a minimum of 3 years of ballet training. This course consists of 2 - 1.5 hour technique classes/week, is part of the Dept. of Performing Arts and receives 1 credit. Upon the completion of this course, students will gain general intermediate level skills in classical ballet including: a rigorous barre; center work as well as an emphasis on adagio, pirouettes, petite allegro and grand allegro combinations. All students are required to attend one professional ballet performance within the semester and to write one 2 page critical analysis of the performance. Ballet slippers, leotards and tights are required for class.

title: Intermediate Modern Dance
description: This course offers continued development and understanding of modern dance as a performance art form. Course will examine technique and principles of modern dance focusing on movement, rhythm, dynamics, dance phrasing, articulation and choreography. The course applies intermediate principles of modern dance technique which includes a broad range of movement and music exploration. Prerequisites: Three years previous dance experience required.

title: Principles of Microeconomics
description: This course first develops simple graphical and mathematical models of decision-making by individual economic agents: consumers, workers, and businesses. We analyze interactions between these agents in product and factor markets using concepts of market demand, supply, and equilibrium. Finally, we demonstrate the efficiency of perfectly competitive markets, describe the conditions under which that efficiency arises, and examine market failures that occur when those conditions are not met.

title: Principles of Macroeconomics
description: This course provides an introduction to macroeconomics. The first part of the course explores how GDP, inflation, unemployment, and other macroeconomic aggregates are measured in practice. The second part develops analytical models of macroeconomic performance and growth in the long run. The third part focuses on short-run (business-cycle) fluctuations and fiscal and monetary policies. Fall and Spring.

title: Principles of Economics: Micro & Macro combined
description: ECON003 covers the Principles of Micro and Macro Economics (001 and 002) in one semester at an accelerated/compressed pace, and is intended for students who have had some prior economics, but not ECON001 or ECON002. It satisfies all distribution requirements and prerequisites that those two courses satisfy. Students who take 003 and elect to major or minor in economics will take an additional elective so as to be credited with the appropriate ten courses for the major or six courses for the minor. You may not take this course if you have AP credit for ECON 001 and/or ECON 002.

title: Intermediate Micro
description: This course covers the basic elements of microeconomic theory including consumer choice, the impact on resource allocation of different market structures ranging from competition to monopoly, game theory, general equilibrium analysis, and asymmetric information. We will focus on equilibrium and optimization throughout.

title: Intermediate Macro
description: This course covers the measurement of output and prices, theory of economic growth, business cycle theory, fiscal policy, monetary policy. Fall and Spring.

title: Intermediate Micro (Honors)
description: This class is designed to formalize and extend the basic concepts of microeconomic analysis that students were introduced to in their micro principles course (Econ 001). Students will have seen many of the topics before 芒聙聯 e.g., consumer theory, equilibrium analysis, externalities and public goods 芒聙聯 but typically in a more informal setting. Some other topics, like game theory and asymmetric information, are likely to be less familiar. The analysis will necessarily be mathematical, and will employ the tools of calculus, optimization, and solution of simultaneous equations. While this formalism is essential for a modern treatment of microeconomics (and, for that matter, macroeconomics), the presentations in class will also emphasize graphical techniques, especially for the building of intuition. There will be a number of problem sets assigned as homework. These will be complemented with weekly recitations. Attendance at recitations is optional, but highly recommended Students must have received an A or A- for Econ 001, and have taken calculus & permission of instructor.

title: Intermediate Macro (Honors)
description: The Honors section of Macroeconomic Theory covers the topics presented in ECON-102. The subject-matter, however, is approached in a more rigorous manner. The Honors section is designed for Economics majors who intend to do graduate work in the field or for those who are interested in a more mathematical treatment than that provided ECON-102. Spring.

title: Economic Statistics
description: After overviewing descriptive statistics, and the theory of probability and random variables, this course covers statistical inference in detail. Students receive the firm foundation needed for Introduction to Econometrics. Regression analysis, the primary tool for empirical work in economics, is introduced. Electronic data acquisition and computer applications receive hands-on treatment. Lab sessions meet weekly to discuss homework and the use of computer software. Fall and Spring.

title: Introduction to Econometrics
description: This course develops the theory and applications of regression analysis, which is the primary tool for empirical work in economics. Emphasis is placed on techniques for estimating economic relationships and testing economic hypotheses. Fall and Spring.

title: International Trade
description: This course covers the theory and practice of international trade. The first part of the course develops the classical and modern theories of the determination of the pattern of commodity trade between nations. The second part of the course covers trade policy and the role of institutions in managing world trade. Fall and Spring.

title: International Finance
description: This course deals with the theory and practice of international macroeconomics and finance. Concepts of balance of payments and exchange rates are developed, followed by macroeconomic tools in an open economy. Balance of payments adjustments will be analyzed under fixed and flexible exchange rate systems. Macroeconomic topics -- such as inflation, growth, unemployment, the roles of monetary and fiscal policies -- will be discussed using examples from developed and/or developing countries.

title: Comparative Economic Systems
description: The course analyzes the interaction between economic history and the development of the economics discipline in the first half, discussing Smith and the classical school, Keynes and his critics, and the future of capitalism as seen by Marx and Schumpeter. The second half is a brief history of the Soviet experiment with central planning and issues associated with globalization. China and Japan are also discussed as case studies.

title: The Japanese Economy
description: Japan has the second largest capitalist economy in the world (third, if China is included and if its output is evaluated at purchasing power parity). Japan芒聙聶s economic history has been marked by several phases since the beginning of its modernization in the 19th century: fast growth, miracle growth, deceleration, and stagnation. The main purpose of this course is to understand this history and to place it in context by relating it to the broader subject of economic development and by making analytical comparisons to other countries, especially the United States. The course has four main themes: (1) Japanese economic growth and its economic miracle in the context of economic development more generally; (2) slowdown and stagnation in the past decade; (3) transition to a more effective economic structure; and (4) other key topics such as trade, deregulation, and research and development.

title: Senior Economics Thesis Seminar
description: This semester-long seminar course assists seniors writing theses in economics. The course is open to all majors. Students will design their own research topic, with guidance from faculty, and will present their work in class during all phases of their projects, including the initial conception of the topic, formulation of the economic model and hypotheses to be tested, and intermediate and final results. Spring.

title: Economics of Information
description: In this course we will inspect a piece of the puzzle that has been mostly left out of the core courses in economics: Information. Prices depend on individual preferences that are private information --- how does it come to pass that the competitive equilibrium price reflects this information? In the stock market, individuals' decisions to buy or sell convey information about their estimate of a stock's value --- can such signaling account for stock market bubbles and crashes? Slot machines have different payouts, but casino owners do not tell their customers what the payout is --- how should one optimally gamble when confronted with several different slot machines?

title: Economic Issues in Social Security Reform
description: The objective of the course will be to improve understanding of the current Social Security reform debate. It will cover the current structure and history of the Social Security program along with the underlying macro and micro economic analysis needed to evaluate recent major reform options. In addition, the course will cover intensively the most recent developments in the reform debate, beginning with the last major legislative changes to the program in 1983, and ending with the 2001 Bush Commission on Social Security and current proposals for reform. Particular emphasis will be focused on the major economic issues underlying the political debate about "privatization". Fall.

title: Topics in Applied Econometrics
description: This course covers some specific topics in labor economics and development economics with the objective of learning how to motivate, write and execute a good applied paper. The econometrics methods we will use are standard but they go beyond the basic tools learned in an introductory econometrics course. For this reason, they will be covered in detail but always in the context of an interesting economic or policy application.

title: Mathematics for Economics
description: This course will introduce you to the mathematical tools employed in economics. After a brief review of basic concepts, we turn to optimization problems subject to equality and inequality constraints. The method of Lagrange (and generalizations by Kuhn and Tucker) will be introduced and employed to solve static problems. Optimal control and dynamic programming will be used for dynamic problems.

title: Public Sector Economics
description: Part I: Introduction to public economics Part II: Theory 1. Public goods and externalities 2. Redistribution and social insurance 3. Tax policy 4. Voting and public choice 5. Fiscal federalism Part III: Applications 1. Health policy 2. Education policy 3. Social security 4. Effects of tax policy 5. Other topics

title: Economic Crises
description: The subject of this course is economic crises--- their causes, consequences, and prevention. In the fall of 2010, the emphasis is on the global financial crisis that began in 2007. Alternative theories are presented including some drawn from behavioral economics. Considerable attention is paid to the institutional framework . The crises studied are of several kinds, including bank runs, speculative attacks on currencies, and sudden stops in emerging markets. Analytical techniques include formal models and event studies. Access to the 芒聙聹Solver芒聙聺 utility in Excel is required.

title: Game Theory
description: Game Theory is the study of strategic interaction among rational agents. Its core methodology is mathematical in nature. This course develops the basic tools of game theory, and builds on them to proceed to a relatively advanced level. To complement and illustrate the pure theory, the course also covers a number of economic applications. Students will be asked to complete a number of assignments available via a dedicated web-site. These assignments are compulsory, but do not form part of the final grade. Instead the final grade will be based on a mid-term and a final exam. Fall.

title: Industrial Organization
description: This course examines the behavior of business firms and the performance of markets in monopoly and imperfectly competitive settings. Aspects of strategic decision-making that will be studied include price discrimination, product selection, intellectual property, and investment decisions. Public policy toward industry (e.g., antitrust and regulation) will also be discussed, with a focus on its effect on firm behavior and consumer welfare. Game theory will be used, and case studies will be introduced to shed light on the theories. Fall.

title: Money, Banking & Financial Markets
description: This course provides an overview of the U.S. financial system. We consider the financial decision problems faced by individuals, firms, financial institutions, and the government. These decisions when aggregated will determine prices including interest rates and stock prices, and the allocation of funds. The course material is relevant for (i) personal financial decisions, such as saving for retirement and investment portfolio choice, (ii) financial decisions by businesses such as their financial structure, i.e., debt versus equity, (iii) financial instruments issued by financial institutions, and (iv) the role of public policies such as monetary, fiscal policies and financial regulations in dealing with and preventing financial crises.

title: Unemployment, Search and Matching
description: Matching frictions are important in many markets. In the labor market, workers search for suitable jobs and firms try to hire suitable workers. Some workers can't find a job while at the same time some vacancies remain unfilled. In this course, we will analyze markets with matching frictions with a particular emphasis on understanding unemployment.

title: Environmental Economics
description: How much should we pay to improve air quality or to protect endangered species? Which is better: pollution taxes or cap-and-trade? How concerned should we be about climate change damages that may occur 100 years from now? Does economic growth help or hurt the environment? What about international trade? What about international trade in endangered species? This course is designed to frame economic answers to those questions and more. Readings will include a textbook, several Kennedy School case studies, and articles in leading academic economics journals. Students will be assigned problem-set based homework, short policy memos based on the case studies, and a final exam.

title: Labor Economics
description: This seminar develops the basic economic theory used to model labor market behavior. Particular emphasis is placed on applying the theory to current policy issues such as welfare reform, educational policy, and social security reform. Recent developments in the labor market such as the increased labor force participation of married women with children and the decline of unionism in the United States are also discussed. A term paper is required.

title: Development Economics: Micro and Policy Issues
description: The purpose of this course is to provide you with a basic understanding of a number of important concepts and theories in the literature on economic development. Topics to be discussed include growth, human capital, inequality, poverty and nutrition, rural credit market, tenancy and land markets. The strongest emphasis will be placed on a firm grasp of the conceptual issues involved. In addition, I will often cite data and discuss case studies. At the end of the course, I'd like you to be in a position were you can read about (or directly experience) development issues.

title: Political Economy of Trade Policy
description: This course focuses on the political economy of international economic integration, in particular trade and migration policies. The course begins with an overview of the effect of free trade on welfare and income distribution in standard trade models. Next, we study trade policy in theory and in practice, which leads to an inquiry into the processes by which trade policies are formed. We examine both domestic political determinants of trade policy and models of international trade relations. The second part of the course focuses on the political economy of migration policy. We first investigate the relationship that links the two dimensions of globalization, trade and immigration. Next, we explain the role of migration policy in shaping international migration flows. Finally, we develop a political-economy model of migration policy and investigate its components. Spring.

title: Topics in Political Economy
description: This particular "Topics in PE" course concerns the nature of special interest politics. Specifically, it examines current research on models of special interest groups, their formation, activities, and effect on the policy process. To provide focus, most of the material that forms the basis for discussion will come from the new book, Special Interest Politics, by two leaders in the field, Gene Grossman and Elhanan Helpman. Note: this course is not intended as a broad, introductory course. Rather, it is intended primarily for students who plan research-intensive careers in economics or in a related field. Fall.

title: Program Evaluation
description: The purpose of this course is to provide an overview of program evaluation in the context of development policy. How we learn what works, what does not work, and why? The course will cover impact evaluation theory (causal inference, experimental design and basic statistics) as well as methods (randomization, difference-in-difference, regression discontinuity, and propensity score matching). The curriculum will be applied, with weekly case studies of field research drawn from the international development literature. Students who complete this course will be prepared to: 1) distinguish research-based 芒聙聹best practices芒聙聺 from those that have not been rigorously evaluated; 2) design an impact evaluation of a policy or intervention, and 3) evaluate data using a statistical software package.

title: Introduction to Microtheory
description: see description for Econ 101.

title: Introduction to Macrotheory
description: See description for Econ 102.

title: Micro Theory Honors
description: see Econ 103

title: Honors Macroeconomic Theory
description: see Econ 104

title: Economic Statistics
description: see Econ-121

title: Intro to Econometrics
description: see Econ 122

title: International Finance
description: This course examines macroeconomics in open economies that are linked by trade, capital flows, and exchange rate policies. The goal is to provide an analytical framework for understanding current events and major challenges facing the world economy. The course will develop theoretical tools that can be used to analyze the economic performance of open economies and to evaluate alternative policy options for macroeconomic management. Topics include balance of payments accounts, foreign exchange and money markets, monetary and stabilization policies, exchange rate regimes, economic growth, currency and financial crises, and the international financial architecture. Examples from both developed and developing country experiences will be discussed.

title: International Trade
description: Major topics covered include: comparative and absolute advantage; production, trade, and consumption; equilibrium in the international market; trade and factors of production; modern theories of trade; commercial policy; factor movements; U.S. trade policy; and trade problems of developing countries, current trade issues and WTO. (Not required for MSFS/MA in Economics majors).

title: International Finance, advanced
description: Major topics covered include: theories of balance of payments determination; foreign exchange markets; price and income approaches to balance of payments adjustments; monetary approach to balance of payments adjustments; Euro-currency markets; monetary integration; and the international monetary system. (Not required for MSFS/MA in Economics majors).

title: International Trade, advanced
description: Major topics covered include: comparative and absolute advantage; production, trade, and consumption; equilibrium in the international market; trade and factors of production; modern theories of trade; commercial policy; factor movements; U.S. trade policy; and trade problems of developing countries, current trade issues and WTO. (Not required for MSFS/MA in Economics majors).

title: Trade & Development
description: Does trade help countries develop? History seems to show a positive correlation between open trade regimes and economic growth over the long haul. Still, economists have provided various arguments about the role of trade in growth, or more precisely, offered various suggestions on how trade policies might be managed to enhance growth. These policy prescriptions have changed over time. Empirical efforts to examine the role of trade on development show mixed results. In this course we will review some of the current issues surrounding trade and development, review the relevant trade and development concepts, and examine the empirical findings. Why should you be interested in the topic? Recent examples include US-CAFTA FTA, development issues in Africa, and economic growth in China. In addition there are many large players (read - possible employers) USAID, Millenium Challenge Corporation, and international institutions such as the World Bank, Inter-American Development Bank, Asia Development Bank, UNCTAD, and Civil Society Groups. The main goal of the class is to help students think critically about, and to be knowledgeable of, the concepts and empirical work relevant to on-going trade policy and globalization discussions.

title: Microeconomics
description: This course covers elements of modern microeconomics. It emphasizes the development of skills needed to evaluate the new generation of economic models, tools, and ideas as they apply to recent changes in our economy. Basic tools of game theory, asymmetric information, and modern general equilibrium price formation are introduced and applied to various topics. These topics include consumer and producer choice, decision under uncertainty, and resource allocation under different market structures such as competition, monopoly, and oligopoly. The course also focuses on some newer innovations to the theory, including the role of politics in the determination of economic policy, the impact of spillovers and network externalities in the information economy, and psychological and behavioral factors in individual decision making For M.A. in Applied Economics Students

title: Macroeconomics
description: This course provides an introduction to macroeconomics and standard theoretical models of how output, employment, and the price level are determined. The course also discusses the role of fiscal and monetary policies, as well as selected topics from international macroeconomics. The course begins with a brief discussion of macroeconomic aggregates and their measurement in practice, but the main focus of the course is analytical. Standard analytical models address two broadly distinct topics: (1) macroeconomic performance and growth in the long run; (2)short-run (business-cycle)fluctuations and stabilization (fiscal and monetary) policies. For M.A. in Applied Economics students.

title: Econometrics
description: This course covers the basic linear regression model but also looks beyond it. Many economic data-sets do not satisfy the strong requirements for the linear regression model.Examples include labor force participation decisions, consumer choice between different brands of a product, and household income and consumption surveys stretching over several years (panel data). In this course more advanced statistical models are covered that are needed to analyze these types of data. Topics include discrete choice models (logit and probit), count data models, censored and truncated regression models, panel data models, and instrumental variables methods. The emphasis of the course is on understanding the need for the various methods, being able to apply them to data, and interpreting the results. For M.A. in Applied Economics students.

title: Data Analysis
description: This course introduces students to how to use a major econometric software package to manipulate data and estimate econometric models. Hands-on exercises using actual economic data are an integral part of the course. The goal of the course is to enable students conduct their own data analyses and research in an econometric software package using the tools developed in Economics-553, Econometrics. For M.A. in Applied Economics students.

title: Labor Economics
description: This course covers topics in the field of labor economics including: labor supply, labor demand, investment in human capital through schooling and on the job training, career mobility, immigration, discrimination, earnings inequality, and job search and unemployment. Each topic will include a discussion of theoretical models and issues, but the primary emphasis will be on empirical tests of the theories and the analysis of labor market data. Students will work on a series of small projects where they will analyze data from the U.S. Department of Labor, the Census Bureau, and other public databases. Projects will relate to course topics as well as current events. The empirical topics include analyses of changes in labor force participation rates, school completion rates and returns to education, immigration rates, and earnings inequality over time, and variation in earnings, hours of work, and job mobility within and across demographic groups. For M.A. in Applied Economics students only.

title: Public Economics
description: This course will cover the major topics in public economics芒聙聰that is, public sector taxation and expenditures. It will encompass normative and positive theories of public economics and a broad range of empirical work. The course will use a well-known textbook in public economics. In addition, optional readings from the economics literature will be assigned every week. A series of problem sets using publicly available data and a major econometric software package will be assigned. A major empirical project will constitute a large portion of the grade. For M.A. in Applied Economics Students only.

title: Program Evaluation
description: This course will introduce economics students to the principles and practice of evaluating economic and social programs. The course will cover the fundamentals of causal models, empirical techniques for estimating counterfactual outcomes, methods for identifying parameters of policy interest, and integration of evaluation findings into policy decisionmaking. These techniques range from randomized field experiments and natural/quasi-experiments to econometric methods such as matching, instrumental variables, and fixed effect/panel data models. Besides covering tools for designing and implementing methods for estimating program impacts, the course will include applications of decision theory to program evaluation, including benefit-cost analysis, cost effectiveness analysis, and Bayesian approaches to decisionmaking. Finally, in addition to relevant economic theory provided at the beginning of each lecture, students will learn through real-world examples of program evaluation in fields such as health, education, training, welfare, anti-poverty, housing, and small-business/agriculture in developing countries. They will be introduced to the practical realities of mounting program evaluations through exercises that simulate the work of real program evaluation researchers. For M.A. in Applied Economics Students only.

title: Monetary & Financial Economics
description: This course provides an overview of monetary and financial economics. The course begins with an review of the overall structure of financial markets and institutions in a market economy and their role in efficiently allocating scarce saving to capital investment. It then investigates the role of a key financial asset芒聙聰money芒聙聰in the financial system and the economy. The course goes on to consider in more detail fixed-income assets and the factors that determine the interest rates on such assets. Next, equity markets and the determination of equity prices are reviewed, and the theory of efficient markets is presented. The focus then shifts back to banks and other financial institutions. The theoretical reasons for the existence of financial institutions, as well as the possible sources of instabilities of such firms, are discussed, and associated issues in the regulation of financial institutions are covered. The course then proceeds to the key topics of central banking and monetary policy. In all aspects of the course, analytical and policy questions raised by the recent financial crisis will be an important focus. A highly regarded textbook serves as the main source of material for the course; additional supplementary readings will be assigned. Final grades will be based primarily on homework assignments, a midterm exam, and a final exam.

title: International Finance
description: Economics 577 investigates open economy macroeconomics and international finance. Topics include the balance of payments, saving and investment, and international borrowing and lending; basics of finance and international finance; exchange rate dynamics; financial crises; and monetary and fiscal policy in the open economy in the context of a basic sticky-price optimizing model with financial market frictions. Supplemental topics may include economic growth and the application of overlapping generations models to issues in international finance. For M.A. in Applied Economics Students.

title: Time Series Econometrics
description: This course will cover the major techniques of time-series econometrics as they are applied in macroeconomics and finance. In particular, the course will cover mixed autoregressive moving-average models, conditional volatility models, nonstationarity tests, vector autoregressive models, cointegration, and nonlinear models. Although the relevant econometric theory is reviewed, most of the emphasis is on applied time-series econometrics. Examples in class will be presented using a well-known statistical and econometric software package. For M.A. in Applied Economics Students only.

title: Microeconomics I
description: This is the first semester of the microeconomic theory sequence for Ph.D. students. The topics covered are Consumer Theory, Producer Theory, Choice Under Uncertainty, and Game Theory.

title: Microeconomics II
description: This course examines topics in Infinitely Repeated Games, Games of Incomplete Information and associated notions of Perfect-Bayesian equilibrium and Sequential equilibrium, General Equilibrium Theory, and, time permitting, Markets under Uncertainty, Adverse Selection, and Moral Hazard.

title: Microeconomics III
description: One of the courses in the PhD microeconomic theory field. Time permitting we will study: 芒聙垄 Repeated Games 芒聙垄 Bargaining 芒聙垄 Mechanism Design 芒聙垄 Information 芒聙垄 Search

title: Macroeconomics I
description: This two-semester sequence provides an introduction to the techniques of modern macroeconomic analysis that are needed by all graduate students in economics. Intertemporal frameworks are developed to discuss modern theories of consumption, saving, investment and growth. Other applications may include theories of asset pricing, models of the labor market and search frictions, the analysis of business cycles, and fiscal and monetary policies. The sequence also provides an introduction to mathematical techniques used in dynamic macroeconomic analysis. ECON-605 is the prerequisite for ECON-606.

title: Macroeconomics II
description: This two-semester sequence provides an introduction to the techniques of modern macroeconomic analysis that are needed by all graduate students in economics. Intertemporal frameworks are developed to discuss modern theories of consumption, saving, investment and growth. Other applications may include theories of asset pricing, models of the labor market and search frictions, the analysis of business cycles, and fiscal and monetary policies. The sequence also provides an introduction to mathematical techniques used in dynamic macroeconomic analysis. ECON-605 is the prerequisite for ECON-606.

title: Econometrics I
description: Methods of estimation and inference are developed for the classical regression model, the generalized regression model, basic time-series models, and simultaneous equations systems.

title: Econometrics II
description: Methods of estimation and inference are developed for the classical regression model, the generalized regression model, basic time-series models, and simultaneous equations systems.

title: Micro-Econometrics
description: Econometric methods for analysis of microeconomic behavior are developed. Topics include panel data analysis and models with discrete or limited dependent variables.

title: Macro-Econometrics
description: The course is an introduction to univariate and multivariate time series models. Time domain methods, including VAR's, structural VAR's, Bayesian VAR's for linear models and GMM for non-linear stationary models are covered. An introduction to non-stationary time series models is given. Frequency domain methods and their applications to business cycle inference is also covered. The course starts by introducing basic concepts and progresses to more complicated models. The course intends to meet two goals. It provides tools for empirical work with time series data, mostly for macroeconomic applications and provides a heuristic introduction into the theoretical foundation of time series models. Prerequisites: Econ 613 and 614.

title: Economic Development I
description: The purpose of this course is to provide you with a basic understanding of a number of important concepts and theories in the literature on economic development. Topics to be covered include: Coordination failure and history dependence; Inequality; Rural credit markets; Insurance; Savings and other protections from risk; Land; and Institution, geography and political economy. The strongest emphasis will be placed on a firm grasp of the conceptual issues involved, but empirical papers and case studies will be discussed.

title: Economic Development II
description: This class will cover a range of topics in the economics of development. The technical focus will be relatively microeconomic, but I hope to encourage a broad policy-based approach development. A complete syllabus will be available in the fall, but topics are likely to include the following: 芒聙垄 The big picture 芒聙聯 what is being poor all about? 芒聙垄 A bit of growth theory 芒聙垄 Poverty and inequality 芒聙垄 Political economy and government failure 芒聙垄 Agriculture, land markets, etc. 芒聙垄 Aid, aid effectiveness, and development 芒聙垄 Trade and development 芒聙垄 Health, Education, and other social policies Preparation Over the summer, I would like you to read at least one of the following books: Collier, Paul: The Bottom Billion: Why the poorest countries are failing, and what can be done about it Easterly, William: The Elusive Quest for Growth: Economists' Adventures and Misadventures in the Tropics Easterly, William: The White Man's Burden: Why the West's Efforts to Aid the Rest Have Done So Much Ill and So Little Good Jeffrey Sachs: The end of poverty: Economic possibilities for our time Amartya Sen: Development as Freedom

title: International Macroeconomics and Finance
description: This course examines macroeconomic policy and performance of open economies under alternative exchange rate regimes. Basic topics covered include arbitrage relationships, static and intertemporal current account determination, the effectiveness of fiscal and monetary policies under fixed and flexible exchange rate regimes, models of exchange rate dynamics and issues of exchange regime choice. Additional topics may include analyses of stabilization and growth and balance-of-payments crises. While the emphasis is on developing analytic tools, references are made to experiences among industrial and developing economies.

title: International Trade I
description: This is the introductory graduate course in international trade theory and is intended to give a broad coverage of the field. Topics include: gains from trade and comparative advantage; the Heckscher-Ohlin model and its higher dimensional extensions; trade and wages; increasing returns and imperfect competition; the Gravity Model; trade policy and political economy.

title: International Trade
description: This course covers topics at the frontier of the field of international trade. Emphasis is on reading, writing and presenting research findings, as preparation for thesis work in the field.

title: Labor Economics I
description: This course provides a general survey of Labor Economics. Although both the theoretical models in the area and the methods and results in empirical labor market analysis will be covered, particular emphasis will be placed on the latter. Topics include: labor supply, human capital analysis, equalizing differentials, and theoretical models of unemployment with particular emphasis on search model and their estimation.

title: Empirical Microeconomics I
description: PhD students will be able to fulfill a field requirement by taking ECON 671 together with one class from any of the following fields: Public Economics, Labor Economics, Development Economics

title: Public Economics
description: This course is designed for 2nd year Ph.D. students in economics. It has two goals: (1) introduce some of the core theoretical and empirical public finance/economics techniques and literature, (2) help students identify thesis topics. Towards those goals, we will read two types of papers: (1) "classics" that every well-trained public finance economist should know, (2) and new papers that represent new trends and ideas in the field. Topics will include, but not be limited to, the following: excess burden, optimal tax theory, tax incidence, behavioral responses to taxes, federalism, and externalities.

title: Workshops in Macroeconomics
description: Presentations of papers by outside speakers, faculty, and students on topics in economic development, international finance, macroeconomics, monetary economics, and related fields.

title: Workshops in Macroeconomics
description: Presentations of papers by outside speakers, faculty, and students on topics in economic development, international finance, macroeconomics, monetary economics, and related fields.

title: Workshops in Microeconomics
description: Presentations of papers by outside speakers, faculty, and students on topics in industrial organization, international trade, labor economics, microeconomics, public finance, and related fields.

title: Workshop: Microeconomics
description: Presentations of papers by outside speakers, faculty, and students on topics in industrial organization, international trade, labor economics, microeconomics, public finance, and related fields.

title: Workshop in International Economics
description: Presentations of papers by outside speakers, faculty, and students on topics in international economics and related fields.

title: Workshop: International
description: Presentations of papers by outside speakers, faculty, and students on topics in international economics and related fields.

title: Workshop:  Econometrics
description: Presentations of papers of outside speakers, faculty and students on topics in econometrics and applied economentrics.

title: Workshop: Empirical Econometrics
description: Presentations of papers of outside speakers, faculty and students on topics in econometrics and applied economentrics.

title: Gateway: Medieval and/or Renaissance Literatures and Cultures
description: THE ENGLISH GATEWAY SATISFY THE GENERAL ED REQUIREMENT IN LITERATURE FOR COLLEGE STUDENTS. Please see individual sections for a detailed course description.

title: Gateway: Med &/or Ren Lit/Cult
description: SPRING 2012 ENGL 040-01 PROFESSOR MICHAEL COLLINS The course will begin by proposing that a profound change in the way men and women saw the world took place in Western Europe between 1200 and 1600. After illustrating and analyzing that change through the developments in the art of the period, the course will go on to examine how popular entertainment (ballads and theatre) in England (roughly between 1450 and 1600) reflected it and responded to it. Students will be asked to read Everyman and the Second Shepherds芒聙聶 Play, a selection of popular ballads, Doctor Faustus by Christopher Marlowe and Macbeth and Hamlet by William Shakespeare. They will also be asked to rehearse and perform some scenes from these plays and to attend a production of Macbeth at the Gonda Theatre in April. A lab fee for the course will cover the cost of the ticket.

title: Gateway: Med &/or Ren Lit/Cult
description: FALL 2011 PROFESSOR MIMI YIU ENGL 040.02 Women and the Market in Early Modern England How did an emerging capitalist economy affect women in 16th and 17th century England? In a time of imperial expansion, global trade, and rapid urbanization, gender roles necessarily shifted as women entered the merchant class and became more than just commodities to be traded between men. Drawing upon cultural artifacts such as maps, visual representations, and household objects, this course examines the conflicts raised by women's increasing agency within the marital, literary, theatrical, commercial, and political economies. Through a close analysis of plays by Shakespeare and other male playwrights as well as poetry and prose by women, we will explore diverse representations of gender to ask how women's voices can acquire value in a patriarchal society. Critical essays, short journal entries, and active participation are required. SPRING 2012 PROFESSOR NOEL SUGIMURA ENGL 040-02 Love, Death and Everything After Since ideas about love and death are central to medieval and early modern literature, these two recurring themes form the backbone of this course. To read literature in this context is to encounter experiences that describe different sorts of love 芒聙聯- divine, as well as creaturely, love -- and the aftershocks that love (or loss of love) generates. In entering into dialogue with some of the greatest writers and thinkers who have grappled with what it means to experience love, we will discuss what that experience is capable of achieving: for instance, can love overcome death? If so, how? These questions incorporate our second key word in the course title: death. By reading texts that ask us to conceive of death in different ways, we are invited to think about what it means to die, as well as what it means to die well. As we move from the Anglo-Saxon period to the Middle Ages to the Renaissance, the responses to love and death evolve in fascinating ways. The 芒聙聹everything after芒聙聺 in the course title refers to questions the literature then raises about what happens after death, and after we fall in (and out of) love; it refers as much to discussion about the afterlife -- such as heaven and hell -- as to psychological states (what happens, for instance, when the beloved dies? Or when the lover is betrayed?). One of the aims of this course is to make you better, and also more confident, 芒聙聹close readers芒聙聺 of texts: an understanding of what is being said will be integrated with how it is being said. In learning to be better readers of literature, you will enter into conversation with literary critics about the relationship between literature and its historical and cultural contexts. More broadly, the course will invite you to think more carefully about what imaginative literature is, and why it is important.

title: Gateway: Med&/orRen Lit&Cult
description: FALL 2011 PROFESSOR MIMI YIU ENGL 040.03 Women and the Market in Early Modern England How did an emerging capitalist economy affect women in 16th and 17th century England? In a time of imperial expansion, global trade, and rapid urbanization, gender roles necessarily shifted as women entered the merchant class and became more than just commodities to be traded between men. Drawing upon cultural artifacts such as maps, visual representations, and household objects, this course examines the conflicts raised by women's increasing agency within the marital, literary, theatrical, commercial, and political economies. Through a close analysis of plays by Shakespeare and other male playwrights as well as poetry and prose by women, we will explore diverse representations of gender to ask how women's voices can acquire value in a patriarchal society. Critical essays, short journal entries, and active participation are required. SPRING 2012 PROFESSOR SARAH MCNAMER 040.03 This course seeks to introduce students to the vibrant, polylingual literary culture of medieval England from the eighth century to the eve of the Renaissance. Beginning with Beowulf (in the wonderful translation by Seamus Heaney), we will read both canonical and noncanonical texts, situating them in the various social, intellectual, visual and performance contexts that can restore for us a sense of their original meanings and functions. Genres will range from the familiar to the strange: we will encounter elegiac poetry, chivalric romances, a prose rhapsody, a travel narrative, miracles of the Virgin, love lyrics, a gynecological treatise, riddles, mystery plays, revelations from God, a beast fable, a bawdy fabliau and a sobering sermon on the Seven Deadly Sins. English writings will be our focus, but we will also sample (in translation) some of the abundant Latin and French texts which circulated in medieval England, in part as a reminder of the prestige these languages enjoyed and the lowly status of English during much of this period. Indeed the politics of language use will be one of our abiding concerns, as will more general questions surrounding textual production and cultural authority. Virtually all readings will be in modern English translation; we will, however, read some Old and Middle English aloud in order to experience something of the weight and music of English in its earliest days.

title: Gateway: Med&/orRen Lit&Cult
description: Heroes, Demons, Saints, and Lovers This course will examine four roles central to medieval and early modern English literature 芒聙聯 heroes, demons, saints, and lovers 芒聙聯 and explore how those roles mutate and change over time. The course takes an archaeological approach to literary history from the seventeenth to the seventh century. By 芒聙聹archaeological芒聙聺 I mean that it proceeds in reverse chronological order, beginning with the most recent works and in each successive week digging further into the past. You will be asked, in other words, to grapple with literature ever more distant from our own age and to practice reading, discussing, and producing critical writing on a wide range of influential and representative texts. Class will begin with close attention to the language of these texts, but the scope of our discussion will expand to address their place in the societies and cultures in which they originated. As we travel back from the Renaissance through the Middle Ages to the Anglo-Saxon period, we will seek not only to do justice to individual works and the heroic, demonic, saintly, and amorous roles that they contain, but also to understand them as part of a larger tradition and indeed to investigate the complex meanings of the term 芒聙聹tradition芒聙聺 itself.

title: Gateway: Eighteenth and/or Nineteenth Century Literatures and Cultures
description: THE ENGLISH GATEWAY COURSES SATISFY THE GENERAL ED REQUIREMENT IN LITERATURE FOR COLLEGE STUDENTS. Please see individual sections in the course catalog for a detailed course description.

title: Gateway: 18th/19th Century Lit/Cult
description: SUMMER 2011 PROFESSOR MAUREEN CORRIGAN ENGL 041.01 Victorian Extreme Adventures The extreme adventure tale is a genre that has become enormously popular in our own time through non-fiction narratives such as Sebastien Junger's /The Perfect Storm/ and Jon Krakauer's /Into Thin Air/ in which man is pitted against the forces of nature. This course proposes to look at nineteenth-century British fictional antecedants of the extreme adventure tale in which both men and women face tests of superhuman endurance--not only in the great outdoors--but also in the marriage market, the teeming metropolis of London, the sick room, and the realm of the supernatural. Among the novels and romances we'll be considering are: /Pride and Prejudice/ by Jane Austen, /Jane Eyre/ by Charlotte Bronte, Great Expectations by Charles Dickens, News from Nowhere by William Morris, and Dracula by Bram Stoker. Classes will be conducted like a seminar and informed participation is mandatory. FALL 2011 PROFESSOR REBECCA BOYLAN ENGL 041.01 Through a Lens Darkly: Phantoms Haunting 19th C Literature This course explores the individual hidden by others and hiding the self in 19th C literature (and painting and photograph) as this obscurity reflects the century芒聙聶s sociological, psychological, aesthetic, and philosophical wrestles. We begin by pondering several conundrums: how this century of realism and technological advancements in vision celebrates illusions and masked identities; why obscurity provokes the desire to discover; why the observer desperate to see often veils his vision or covers up the object of his gaze; how those in hiding mysteriously reveal a multi-faceted self-consciousness; how discomforting truths struggle out of confusing ambiguities. As readers traversing misty moors and seas, opium dreams, foggy streets, and phantasmagoric interiors, we will meet real ghosts, sprites, transforming portraits, and haunted houses. As scholars, we will study how, why, and to what effects the shadows of the 19th C crept into the early 20th C to determine to what extent historical perspective adjusted the lens on these ephemeral Victorian personas. We might well ask, does Alice come UP the rabbit hole? Is the Other discovered only to disappear behind a different barrier? How do changed expectations for Art芒聙聶s functions and purpose provoke either our urgency to see or our complacent blindness? Participants will respond in several short response essays, a mid-length paper, and a longer final paper. Dynamic and consistent class participation is expected. Critical and theoretical essays are available via electronic reserve. Novels include: E. Bront脙芦芒聙聶s Wuthering Heights, James芒聙聶s The Turn of the Screw, Braddon芒聙聶s Lady Audley芒聙聶s Secret, Wilde芒聙聶s The Picture of Dorian Gray, Forster芒聙聶s Howard芒聙聶s End, Woolf芒聙聶s To the Lighthouse, and Lawrence芒聙聶s The Rainbow. SPRING 2012 PROFESSOR MARGARET DEBELIUS 041-01 This course will focus on learning how to read a variety of texts from the turn of the nineteenth century. We will consider works by Robert Louis Stevenson, Henry James, Joseph Conrad, Charlotte Perkins Gilman, Arthur Conan Doyle, Edith Wharton, and others as expressions of a host of cultural anxieties 芒聙聯 anxieties about race, class, gender, technology, and sexuality. We will develop the close reading skills required to read literature intelligently and learn the terms and concepts necessary to understand the critical discourse surrounding these texts. Be prepared for frequent writing and rewriting. Credits: 3 Prerequisites: None

SPRING 2012 PROFESSOR DENNIS TODD ENG 041-02 The 芒聙聵I芒聙聶 and the Other In this class, we will explore a literary question and a cultural question. The literary question is, How did 18th and 19th-century English and American authors exploit the areas of experience that a first-person narrator made available to them? The cultural question is, How did these authors deal with the existence of 芒聙聵Others芒聙聶芒聙聰those radically different peoples and cultures that 18th- and 19th-century exploration, colonialism, and slavery brought them in to contact with? I hope that we might even begin to see a connection between these two questions. Finally, we will be attentive to writing, and you will be assigned numerous reader response papers and several longer analytic papers.

title: Gateway: 18th/19th Century Lit/Cult
description: SPRING 2012 PROFESSOR MOLLY CLARK-HILLARD ENGL 041-03 Childhood in 19th Century Literature and Culture This course explores our contemporary pedagogical, legal, and emotional views of childhood as legacies of the literature and culture of the nineteenth century. We shall come to understand children in an historical context, and to identify how the very definition of childhood has changed and altered over time. Beginning with the Romantic poets, and concluding with children芒聙聶s novels at the close of the century, we will read broadly in both children芒聙聶s literature and literature for adults in which children feature prominently. Literary selections will vary, but in reading such poets and authors as Wordsworth, Coleridge, Blake, Barrett, Browning, Rossetti, Dickens, Bront脙芦, and Burnett, we shall learn that childhood also acted as a powerful metaphor for England itself as a nation and a culture moving through rapid growth and change.

title: Gateway: 18th/19th Century Lit/Cult
description: SPRING 2012 PROFESSOR NATHAN HENSLEY 041.04 This course uses key works from the British nineteenth century to open up questions about what it means to read and think about literature today. Our organizing problem will be that of the monster: the abnormal, the unnatural, the strange. These and related figures for inhumanity informed some of the most important literary and political events of the long nineteenth century -- from the 芒聙聹monstrous birth芒聙聺 of the French revolution to Mary Shelley芒聙聶s Frankenstein and the entire gothic genre (think deranged aristocrats, crumbling mansions); from reform-era workers芒聙聶 riots and bigamist sensation fiction to imperial romances and scandalous, sado-masochistic poetry about flowers. As we chart the fortunes of monsters, radicals, and other of humanity芒聙聶s others in nineteenth-century British literature, we芒聙聶ll open up questions that continue to challenge us now -- about race and beauty, rationality and superstition, and the uncertain limits of the category we call 芒聙聹human.芒聙聺 Always we will read with fanatical, even monstrous closeness: by slowing down our engagement with cultural forms, we芒聙聶ll see how literary analysis can generate concepts that matter to our political and ethical lives today. Texts may include some or all of the following: Horace Walpole, Castle of Otranto; Charlotte Dacre, Zofloya, or the Moor; Mary Shelley, Frankenstein; Jane Austen, Northanger Abbey; Emily Bronte, Wuthering Heights; George Eliot, The Lifted Veil; R.L. Stevenson, Strange Case of Dr. Jekyll and Hyde; H. Rider Haggard, King Solomon芒聙聶s Mines; philosophical texts from Immanuel Kant, John Locke, Karl Marx, and Sigmund Freud; and selected poetry from S.T. Coleridge, William Wordsworth, Lord Tennyson, A.C. Swinburne, Robert Browning, and the pair of female lovers who wrote under the name Michael Field. Later engagements with the non-normative, on film, will add depth to our analyses. Committed reading, writing, and participation is expected.

title: Gateway: 18th/19th Century Lit/Cult
description: FALL 2011 PROFESSOR WARD TIETZ ENGL 041.06 Literary Hybrids and Remixes This course examines a variety of hybrid and remixed literatures--literatures that are crossed or otherwise mixed with forms or materials from multiple authors, artists, artistic categories or realms of culture. Some of the mixed forms, mash-ups and remixes using text, video and recorded sound that we see and hear on the internet, are the most obvious examples of such forms today, but there are many more less obvious examples dating back to the beginning of the 20th Century and earlier. These works give us an opportunity to ask some important questions about literature generally: What makes something literary? Where is literature going and how is it adapting to electronic culture? Do changes in literature and authorship require anything of us? To answer these questions we will take a more critical and theoretical approach to the material. In addition to examining primary works in comics, painting, film as well as visual, sound, performance and digital poetry, we will follow developments in remix theory, visual rhetoric and semiotics (the theory of signs). Graded assignments for the course will include two papers, a mid-term and a group presentation. SPRING 2012 PROFESSOR BRET TOMLINSON ENG 041.06 Into the Wild: Nature, Freedom, Madness in 19th C American Literature This course will consider the tension between civilization and the wild in 19th C American literature and culture. In a century when the frontier was progressively colonized and made into spectacle, and when scientific revolutions in natural history created doubt about human exceptionality in the natural order, the wild became a space of both spiritual refuge and hidden menace. As wildness shifted from an exterior landscape to an interior presence, human nature became a source of intense cultural and literary speculation. By tracing a path from transcendentalism to naturalism we will also explore the competing ideas of regeneration and degeneration, of self-definition and loss of identity, of ecstatic connection and destructive mania, that inform literary treatments of the wild during this period. Among many questions, we will ask: how are American values, particularly individualism, rooted in 芒聙聹self-reliance芒聙聺 and a free-spirited relationship with nature? Why is the wild often a source of liberation for individuals and of paranoia for the dominant culture? Why did the performance of privilege during this period entail the construction of gender and racial others as dangerously wild and in need of domestication? How did Darwinism haunt the latter half of the 19th C and influence the rise of naturalism? Students will contribute weekly short responses to a class discussion forum, will lead a class discussion on a relevant topic, and will write a midterm paper and a final paper. Major works to be discussed include: Thoreau芒聙聶s Walden, Douglas芒聙聶s Narrative of the Life, Melville芒聙聶s Moby Dick, Twain芒聙聶s The Diaries of Adam and Eve, Chopin芒聙聶s The Awakening, and Norris芒聙聶s McTeague.

title: Gateway:Mod &/or Post-Modern Lit
description: FALL 2011 PROFESSOR ANDREW RUBIN ENGL 042.01 Modernism and Modernities What does it mean for a work of art to be modern? What does it entail to implicate a literary text as 芒聙聹modernist芒聙聺? What is the significance of modernism as a descriptive category for the dominant literary movement in early twentieth century Europe? Are there other modernisms? If so how and where do they emerge, and why? Does modernism have a project, and if so, what are its limitations and how does modernism address these limits? How does modernism assist us in understanding the presentness of the present in our world today? Through readings of texts by Joseph Conrad, James Joyce, E.M. Forster, T. S. Eliot, Virginia Woolf, Franz Kafka, Freud, Nietzsche, and Samuel Beckett among others, this course introduces students to the various critical attempts to define modernism and to comprehend its aims, its intentions, and its modes of resistance to the work of art. Analyzing the cultural pressures informing the modernist shift away from earlier literary forms (such as realism, naturalism, and symbolism), we shall examine both how and why modernist texts enact this rupture by reading these works in their fullest and most integrative context. SPRING 2012 PROFESSOR JENNIFER FINK 042-01 Playing Modern: Modernism, Postmodernism, and Theatricality In this course, we芒聙聶ll investigate performance芒聙聶s troubled relationship with modernism and postmodernism. Pairing key theoretical texts with play texts, we芒聙聶ll explore how theatrical performance reflects, refracts, and refutes notions of modern and postmodern subjectivity. The fraught relationship between texts and bodies will be a key site of our investigation. From Bertol Brecht to Judith Butler, from Samuel Beckett to Tony Kusher, from Oscar Wilde to Moises Kaufman to Jacques Derrida and beyond, we芒聙聶ll trace the (dis)appearance of the modern subject and the emergence of the postmodern notion of the performative. Also making a guest appearance will be the relationship between theory and practice. Expect to write, read, and perform, both critically and theatrically. Credits: 3 Prerequisites: None

title: Gateway:Mod &/or Post-Modern Lit
description: FALL 2011 PROFESSOR CHRISTOPHER SHINN ENGL 042.02 GLOBAL POSTMODERN CULTURE: FROM POP ART TO FACEBOOK脗漏 This course will investigate the shifting meanings of postmodern art and culture. What does it mean to be 芒聙聹postmodern,芒聙聺 and how does the term challenge 芒聙聹modernist芒聙聺 worldviews? What does the California sushi roll have in common with Lady Gaga? What does the transition from analog to digital mean for contemporary art, social networking and global media? How are the modern designs of prisons similar to new Foucauldian technologies of security and surveillance? How does 芒聙聹being and nothingness芒聙聺 become questionable again in postmodern philosophy? What will the future look like in 2195? We will start by looking at a brief selection of modernist works from the early to mid-twentieth century. The class will then observe the key transitional phases from the modernist to the postmodern age and will read literary works by Kobo Abe, Haruki Murakami, Margaret Atwood, Philip K. Dick, John Yau and Frank Miller. We will be watching a selection of international films and Japanese anime and reading French and German philosophy and criticism about the postmodern condition. We will also be examining the artwork of Andy Warhol, Jean-Michel Basquiat and Barbara Kruger and will be building a time capsule. Requirements: two short papers, a midterm, a class presentation and a final paper or exam. SPRING 2012 PROFESSOR LORI MERISH ENGL 042.03 The Female Bildungsroman: Coming of Age in Twentieth-Century U. S. Women's Writing This course will examine the bildungsroman, or novel of development, as a mainstay of U. S. women's writing, focusing especially on texts from the last few decades of the twentieth century and on into the contemporary moment. We will also watch a number of films organized around this thematic as well. Examining narrative depictions of coming of age by a diverse group of writers and filmmakers, the course will explore ways in which gendered subjectivity is shaped by historical context and historical 芒聙聹memory芒聙聺: indeed, many of these texts deal explicitly with issues of history, historiography, and remembrance, and depict the act of recovering some sense of the past as central to the formation (or re-formation) of personal and/or collective identity. In addition, we will examine how specific social differences of gender, race, class, sexuality, and nationality shape the narrative paradigm of 芒聙聹coming of age芒聙聺; toward that end, we will pay special attention to coming-of-age texts and films by 芒聙聹ethnic,芒聙聺 working-class, and GLBTQ authors and filmmakers. We will also become familiar with some important critical debates about the bildungsroman as a major subgenre of the novel form, and we will read a number of contemporary critical and theoretical essays on cultural constructions of gendered subjectivity. Some questions we will consider: how do cultural texts (visual, literary)-particularly narrative texts-both represent and constitute what we think of as gendered 芒聙聹identities芒聙聺? What defines 芒聙聹coming of age芒聙聺 in the contemporary U. S., and how is this paradigm inflected by social differences such as race/ethnicity, class, sexuality, region, and/or nation? What are the governing, dominant 芒聙聹narratives芒聙聺 or 芒聙聹scripts,芒聙聺 psychological and/or cultural, that define female maturation, and how are these scripts inscribed, registered, engaged with, and /or contested and disrupted in the literary texts and films under consideration? Indeed, how/why do we imagine female maturation as 芒聙聹development,芒聙聺 as a continuous, linear narrative with a particular teleology (often culminating in marriage and motherhood)-and how have those expectations been shaped by literary and cultural texts? How are female embodiment and sexuality represented in these texts? How do these writers and filmmakers re-construct, and enable us to re-imagine, what it means to 芒聙聹come of age芒聙聺 in the U. S.?

title: Gateway:Mod &/or Post-Modern Lit
description: FALL 2011 PROFESSOR BRIAN HOCHMAN ENGL 042.04 America Unfinished: Reading U.S. Literature and Culture, 1900-Present This course serves as an introduction to the critical study and appreciation of U.S. American literature. Focusing primarily on the period after 1900, we will consider a wide variety of authors who redefine what is 芒聙聹American芒聙聺 and 芒聙聹literary芒聙聺 about American literature: Herman Melville, Gertrude Stein, Zora Neale Hurston, William Faulkner, Ralph Ellison, Maxine Hong Kingston, and Junot Diaz芒聙聰among many others. Along the way, we will also address some of the principal historical contexts for understanding the development of U.S. literature and culture during the twentieth and twenty-first centuries: ongoing debates about immigration, citizenship, and American democracy; the persistence of social inequality and racial discrimination; the rise of new technologies of communication and entertainment; and the globalization of American culture and identity. This course requires occasional response writings, two critical essays, and a final exam. SPRING 2012 PROFESSOR JOY YOUNG ENGL 042.04 U.S. Literature of the Progressive Era Can you have an industrial democracy? This question haunted America in the early 20th-century. This class studies the literature of the developing U.S. as an urban-industrial, multicultural society in which mass migration to cities, a widening gap between rich and poor, shifting gender roles, and racial violence, threatened national identity (sound familiar?). We芒聙聶ll explore the extent to which fiction has been able to incorporate the diversity of America芒聙聰and to what extent has it promoted exclusions. What are the limitations of both narrative and nation芒聙聰and how has American literature expressed these limitations? We芒聙聶ll consider the ways literary texts raise ethical issues, participate in public controversies, and contribute to our understanding of human values. In addition to introducing these literary and cultural concerns and materials, course assignments will also improve class-members芒聙聶 skills in critical reading, interpretive discussion, and argumentative writing. Our readings will include works by Charlotte Perkins Gilman, Charles Chesnutt, Kate Chopin, Sui Sin Far, Pachin Marin, Pauline Watkins, Henry James, Teddy Roosevelt, , and F. Scott Fitzgerald.

title: Gateway: Mod &/or Post-Modern Lit
description: FALL 2011 Prof. Erin Sells Make It New: Modernist Cultures, 1890-1940 This is an intensive writing course on the topic of modernist cultures. The years 1890-1940 encompass what is commonly known as the Modernist Period. American poet Ezra Pound claimed that the modernist imperative was to 芒聙聹make it new芒聙聺芒聙聰and in fields as varied as literature, film, music, art, technology, and science, modernism attempted to do exactly that. We will study examples of all of these aspects of modernist culture as we also examine how these cultural artifacts confront and portray issues like war, race, sex, and class in the rapidly changing world of the early twentieth century. Readings will include: For Whom The Bell Tolls, by Ernest Hemingway; Quicksand and Passing, by Nella Larsen; Mrs. Dalloway, by Virginia Woolf; Native Son, by Richard Wright; poems by W.H. Auden, Sterling Brown, Countee Cullen, T.S. Eliot, Langston Hughes, James Weldon Johnson, and W.B. Yeats; short stories by James Joyce and Katherine Mansfield; selections from Modernism: An Anthology of Source Documents, Eds. Kolokotroni, Goldman, Taxidou, and The Rest Is Noise: Listening to the 20th Century, by Alex Ross. SPRING 2012 PROFESSOR DUNCAN WU ENGL 042.05 Hardy, Larkin, Heaney This is a highly demanding course entailing frequent timed essay assignments. Metrical analysis will form an essential part of the course, alongside close examination of semantic and formal elements of poetic practice. For that reason it will be essential for all students to memorise the contents of Philip Hobsbaum芒聙聶s primer on Metre, Rhythm, and Verse Form on which there will be examinations. The emphasis throughout will be on close examination of the text, and group discussion in which everyone is expected to participate. In addition, students are expected to write occasional response papers which will be analysed and discussed in class. It is recommended for those with a serious interest in poetry.

SPRING 2012 PROFESSOR JOSEPH FRUSCIONE 042-06 Authors Adapting Authors How and why do authors choose to adapt other authors芒聙聶 work? What different forms can adaptation take? How is a novel-to-novel adaptation different from a novel-to-film or play-to-film adaptation? Are the stakes different when a major author such as Shakespeare or Mark Twain is the adapted figure? This Gateway and Writing II course will examine different types of adaptations: Twain芒聙聶s Adventures of Huckleberry Finn and Jon Clinch芒聙聶s Finn; John Guare芒聙聶s Six Degrees of Separation; and Christopher Moore芒聙聶s Fool. There will be equally strong elements of literary study, adaptation study, writing, and research. Required critical text: The Cambridge Companion to Literature on Screen (eds. Cartmell & Whelehan) Assignments and requirements: Literary analysis papers; research-based projects; presentation.

title: Gateway: Mod &/or Post-Modern Lit
description: SPRING 2012 PROFESSOR CHRISTINE SO ENGL 042.09 Asian American Literature Reading works that represent a variety of ethnic groups, genres, and historical backgrounds, this course will introduce students to the primary issues and events that have shaped Asian American literature and culture in the last century. Moving from the contexts of myth-making and law to politics, economics, and education, we will consider how Asian American writers have intervened in American institutions and policies. How have they responded to U.S. ideologies that have historically identified Asian Americans as perpetual 芒聙聹aliens芒聙聺? Paying particular attention to the development of Asian American narratives, languages, politics, and identity, we will be investigating the ways in which these works represent a unified Asian American perspective, even as we differentiate on the basis of ethnicity, gender, class background, and sexual orientation. Finally, we will contextualize our readings with the current debates over the state of race, multiculturalism, and Asian America at the start of the twenty-first century. In every way, this class is organized around the concept of class engagement. It is important that students think consistently about the issues and works we are discussing, come to each class prepared to offer analysis of the reading for that day, and make every effort to understand how the course is asking them to read, think, and write differently. Attendance and a respect for and engagement with the authors芒聙聶 texts and others芒聙聶 comments are mandatory.

title: Gateway: Introduction to Critical Methods
description: THE ENGLISH GATEWAY COURSES SATISFY THE GENERAL ED REQUIREMENT IN LITERATURE FOR COLLEGE STUDENTS. Please see individual sections for a detailed course description.

title: Gateway: Introduction to Critical Methods
description: FALL 2011 PROFESSOR NADINE EHLERS ENGL 043.01 Theorizing Race Race is generally taken to be something that we 'know' 芒聙聯 it is something that is alternatively taken for granted and unquestioned, thought of as a biological and social given, or contested as a trope and social fiction with embodied effect. This course unpacks the contested terrain of the term and idea 'race' and critically examines the construction of race and racism within Western thought. We will examine the work of thinkers such as Immanuel Kant, Thomas Jefferson, Patricia J. Williams, W.E.B. Du Bois, Franz Fanon, Edward Said, Patricia Hill Collins, and Richard Dyer and consider racial theory through the prism of: enlightenment thought; scientific racism; whiteness studies; black philosophy; critical race theory and; postcolonial theory. The aim in this course is to interrogate the assumption that race exists as a natural category and, instead, question the intricacies of race as a production. SPRING 2012 PROFESSOR WARD TIETZ ENG 043-01 Description for Literary Hybrids, Remixes and Remakes This course examines a variety of hybrid, remixed and remade literatures--literatures that are crossed or mixed or remade in some way, and as such, complicate our understanding of authorship, creativity and literary value. Remade films and mash-ups and remixes that we find on the internet are the most obvious examples of such forms today, but there are many more less obvious examples dating back to the last century and earlier. These works give us an opportunity to ask important questions about our current cultural moment and literature generally: What makes something literary? How is literature adapting to electronic culture and the globalizing consumer culture connected to it? How do changes in the way we view creativity, originality and authorship affect literature, and what do these changes require of us as readers, viewers, consumers and citizens? To answer these questions we will take a critical, often comparative, approach to the material. In addition to examining primary works in fiction (novel and graphic novel), poetry, film and audio, we will follow developments in cultural theory, remix theory, visual rhetoric and semiotics (the theory of signs). The course material, as well as the concepts we will discuss and develop in class, will be the basis for two writing assignments and an in-class presentation. In connection with these assignments, we will discuss topic and thesis development, paper organization and research and examine a variety of secondary sources that will help us situate our comments and critiques.

title: Gateway: Introduction to Critical Methods
description: FALL 2011 PROFESSOR ELLEN GORMAN ENGL 043.02 Introduction to Cultural Studies This course will focus on a history and examination of the set of theories, practices and methodologies which define the field of Cultural Studies. By reading and situating the theory, we can critique the production and consumption of cultural objects, including popular culture and avant garde art. In order to pursue critical analyses of our own in the class, we will interrogate concepts such as culture, ideology, representation, taste, style and subculture, with attention to the specific tensions between language and visual images. Texts will draw from critical and cultural theory, literature, film, video, music and the graphic novel, with a particular emphasis on contemporary artistic modes of expression. SPRING 2012 PROFESSOR WARD TIETZ ENG 043-01 Description for Literary Hybrids, Remixes and Remakes This course examines a variety of hybrid, remixed and remade literatures--literatures that are crossed or mixed or remade in some way, and as such, complicate our understanding of authorship, creativity and literary value.
