title: Gateway: Introduction to Critical Methods
description: SPRING 2012 ENG 043-03 PROFESSOR GAY CIMA Empathy & Activism This course introduces students to two particular critical methodologies, performance studies and critical race studies, to investigate how nineteenth-century abolitionists used literature to fight against slavery. We芒聙聶ll study theories of how sympathy and empathy function, so that we can understand abolitionist literature, from poems and plays to essays and speeches. Is empathy necessary to芒聙聰or a danger to芒聙聰those who use literature to fight for human rights? We芒聙聶ll trace the genealogy of abolitionist literature from the 1820s to present-day anti-human-trafficking literature, in an effort to address this question. The diverse reading list will include philosophical treatises on sympathy and political theories about citizenship as well as anti-slavery literature.

title: Gateway: Introduction to Critical Methods
description: SPRING 2012 PROFESSOR PAMELA FOX 043-04 Women Writers, Feminist Readers This Gateway methods course focuses on the field of feminist literary theory and criticism, teasing out its history, aims, contributions, and conflicts. In order to explore questions and theories surrounding the vexed category of 芒聙聹women芒聙聶s writing,芒聙聺 we will read a series of literary texts that now occupy a central芒聙聰some might say 芒聙聵canonical芒聙聶芒聙聰place in the feminist critical archive by authors such as Phillis Wheatley, Charlotte Bronte, Nella Larsen, Virginia Woolf, Sylvia Plath, Toni Morrison, and Julia Alvarez. At the same time, we will examine a diversity of feminist critical and theoretical approaches to each text (a mix of early and more recent work) as a way of charting its development as a field. Student work includes 3-4 papers and bi-weekly blogs. Credits: 3 Prerequisites: None

title: Gateway: Introduction to Critical Methods
description: FALL 2011 PROFESSOR WARD TIETZ ENGL 043.05 Literary Hybrids and Remixes This course examines a variety of hybrid and remixed literatures--literatures that are crossed or otherwise mixed with forms or materials from multiple authors, artists, artistic categories or realms of culture. Some of the mixed forms, mash-ups and remixes using text, video and recorded sound that we see and hear on the internet, are the most obvious examples of such forms today, but there are many more less obvious examples dating back to the beginning of the 20th Century and earlier. These works give us an opportunity to ask some important questions about literature generally: What makes something literary? Where is literature going and how is it adapting to electronic culture? Do changes in literature and authorship require anything of us? To answer these questions we will take a more critical and theoretical approach to the material. In addition to examining primary works in comics, painting, film as well as visual, sound, performance and digital poetry, we will follow developments in remix theory, visual rhetoric and semiotics (the theory of signs). Graded assignments for the course will include two papers, a mid-term and a group presentation. SPRING 2012 PROFESSOR PATRICIA O'CONNOR ENGL 043.05 Narratology. In this course we will note structuralist and deconstructivist theories of narrative as we read closely, critique, and craft three major genres which employ narrative: autobiography, historical fiction, and the short story. Early in the course, students will learn how to do discourse analysis to locate and analyze spoken narratives in everyday speech following methods of linguists William Labov and Deborah Tannen. Students will tape, transcribe, and analyze sample conversations for their narrative thrust. Students will also read the brief autobiography of Eudora Welty --One Writer芒聙聶s Beginnings; the historical fiction novel Out of the Dust (written as a series of poems) by Karen Hesse -- and the Short Stories of Breece D芒聙聶J Pancake. Students will explore these texts as well as the oral and written narratives collected from everyday life using structural approaches as found in On Narratology by Mieke Bal and Michael Toolan芒聙聰Narrative: A Critical Linguistic Introduction. Using the work of Wolfgang Iser in Prospecting we next delve into reader-response theory wherein we react to how writers set up for readerly engagement as they and we compose texts. Thus, in the course we will move from a structuralist understanding into a variety of deconstructive positionings as we examine the narrative choices made by the authors, paying particular attention to matters of external and /or internal narration, focalization, and manipulation of time. As a major project, we will put all this exploration together as students use historical information, print media, and digital archives as well as family stories, to produce a piece of historical fiction. Diana Hacker芒聙聶s A Pocket Style Manual will be used for matters of clarity and style in writing and for producing MLA citation forms where appropriate.

description: FALL 2011 PROFESSOR WARD TIETZ ENGL 043.06 Literary Hybrids and Remixes This course examines a variety of hybrid and remixed literatures--literatures that are crossed or otherwise mixed with forms or materials from multiple authors, artists, artistic categories or realms of culture. SPRING 2012 PROFESSOR HENRY SCHWARZ ENGL 043.06 This course is an introduction to some of the prevailing theories and methods of cultural criticism practiced in Europe and America in the 20th century. We will discuss why critical cultural analysis is important and why and how to think theoretically. Methods covered will include Marxism, Feminism, Postmodernism, Postcolonialism, Cultural Studies and Media Literacy, among others.

title: Gateway: Introduction to Critical Methods
description: FALL 2011 PROFESSOR JOY YOUNG ENGL 043.07 Introduction to Literary Ethics & Theory Can literature really do anyone any good? This class will introduce the ethical dimension of literary studies: the ethics of writing, reading, and interpretation and the scholarly discussions that comprise the so-called 芒聙聹ethical turn芒聙聺 in literary theory. We will explore questions about what it means to read in an ethically-informed way in our pluralistic society and how the reading of literature, can affect our understanding and our world through a theoretically-informed engagement with it. We will study works by Mark Twain, Henry James, Toni Morrison, Sarah Kane, Steven Spielberg, and Errol Morris. Theoretical readings will draw from work by Trilling, Booth, Levinas, Badiou, Derrida, Spivak, Butler, J.H. Miller, Harpham, Appiah, and Nussbaum. We will try and understand the ways in which what we write, what we do, and what we say, are grounded in theoretical premises which, if we aren芒聙聶t mindful of, we might naively reproduce without being wholly aware of how we're using them (let alone how they are using us). Among questions we will ask: what is the connection between theorizing, politics, and ethical engagement? And, is literary criticism merely a form of xeroxing, a 芒聙聹tell me your theory and I芒聙聶ll tell you in advance what you will say about any work of literature芒聙聺? SPRING 2012 PROFESSOR ELLEN GORMAN 043-07 Introduction to Cultural Studies This course will focus on a history and examination of the set of theories, practices and methodologies which define the field of Cultural Studies. By reading and situating the theory, we can critique the production and consumption of cultural objects, including popular culture and avant garde art. In order to pursue critical analyses of our own in the class, we will interrogate concepts such as culture, ideology, representation, taste, style and subculture, with attention to the specific tensions between language and visual images. Texts will draw from critical and cultural theory, literature, film, video, music and the graphic novel, with a particular emphasis on contemporary artistic modes of expression. Pre-requisites: None. Credits: 3

title: Medieval British Literature
description: This course will involve readings in medieval English literature, both in translation and in the original Middle English. Its readings will range from the Old English epic poem Beowulf, through such Middle English works as Sir Gawain and the Green Knight, numerous lyric poems, and selections from Chaucer's Canterbury Tales, and including as well works reflecting late medieval religious practices, Julian of Norwich's Revelations, the Cloud of Unknowing, and the Book of Margery Kempe. It will include three papers and a final exam, and there will be a creative option offered for two of the papers.

title: Sem: Med. Euro. Lit.
description: In this seminar, we will read classics of the medieval European literary tradition: troubadour lyrics, Chr脙漏tien de Troyes芒聙聶s Lancelot, the Lais of Marie de France, Gottfried von Strassburg芒聙聶s Tristan, legends of St. Francis of Assisi, the Cantigas de Santa Maria, and selections from Dante芒聙聶s Divine Comedy, Boccaccio芒聙聶s Decameron, and Chaucer芒聙聶s Troilus and Criseyde. Our focus will be on close readings of the literary works, but we will also bring cultural contexts to bear in our efforts to better understand and enjoy them. All readings will be in modern English translation.

title: Chaucer
description: In this course we will undertake a critical study of that brilliant masterpiece, The Canterbury Tales. By turns bawdy and lofty, racy and religious, hilarious and deeply moving, this collection of stories will challenge us to develop ways of reading adequate to its double status as relic of the distant past and as a text that anticipates many contemporary understandings of identity, meaning, and truth. We will read the Tales in the original Middle English, which is really quite easy; no prior exposure to the language is necessary. Requirements: 3 short papers (3 pages each), 1 longer paper (10 pages), frequent quizzes, and active participation in class discussion.

title: Chaucer
description: A reading and discussion of Chaucer's great (if unfinished) master-work, the Canterbury Tales, a work of first importance to anyone who reads (or writes) narrative fiction. Students will read the work in original Middle English in which Chaucer wrote it, and discuss it in small and in large groups, focusing upon the work's narrative strategies, intellectual constructions, and artistic accomplishment. They will also write, in a variety of ways, about those aspects which particularly take their interest.

title: Med & Early Mod Women's Writing
description: The Latin word auctoritas can signify a number of different things, including authority (political, literary, cultural, etc) and authorship, both of which were generally denied to women during the medieval and early modern periods. This class will focus on how female writers of those periods could and did claim auctoritas in textual form, whether literary, political, or religious--and frequently more than one at once. Writers to be studied will include Sei Sh?nagon (c. 966-1017), Marie de France (late 12th-century), Saint Julian of Norwich (1342-c.1416) and other contemporary mystical writers, Christine de Pizan (1363-c.1430), Anne Askew (c.1520-1546), Queen Elizabeth I (1536-1603), Veronica Franco (1546-1591), Aemilia Lanyer (1569-1645), and Elizabeth Cary (c.1585-1639). Assignments will include four short papers, one major research paper, and two presentations.

title: Shakespeare
description: Spring 2012 Shakespeare and the Other Shakespeare and the Other asks how it is that some of Shakespeare芒聙聶s most memorable 芒聙聹others芒聙聺芒聙聰his stigmatized and unruly women, a money-lending Jew, a noble African, and a New World cannibal芒聙聰speak themselves so powerfully from the stage of an essentially conservative society. We will also discuss whether these voices are silenced or otherwise contained when the texts conclude. And through a combination of close reading and performance review, students will observe to what extent and in what way the choices made by actors and directors change our understanding of these characters, their motives, and their social identity. We will view several performances on the screen and at least one, possibly two, on the stage in an attempt to answer these questions and fully engage with the plays as performance. The main menu? Taming of the Shrew, Merchant of Venice, Othello, the Tempest and finally, Macbeth, a play somewhat outside the paradigm of the course but one scheduled to be performed in April in Georgetown芒聙聶s own Gonda Theatre.

title: Women in Shakespeare
description: This course will focus on Shakespeare芒聙聶s treatment of female characters in seven different plays across his playwriting career: Titus Andronicus, Richard III, The Merchant of Venice, Much Ado About Nothing, Twelfth Night, Othello, and Antony and Cleopatra. All plays will be read in the context of their source material and some modern criticism. What function do these characters serve in each play? How do they conform to or overturn assumptions about women in the early modern period? What does it mean in this period to be voicing women onstage, especially in light of the fact that all these parts were played by men? We will also be reading John Webster芒聙聶s The Duchess of Malfi at the end of the semester to situate Shakespeare芒聙聶s women in the context of other early modern playwrights. Assignments will include short response papers for each play, one research paper, and scene performances.

title: Shakespeare's Exotic Romances
description: From puckish sprites to island savages, from man-eating bears to speaking statues, Shakespeare芒聙聶s plays are populated with otherworldly or extraordinary elements that we might term exotic. Interestingly, most of these examples of exoticism occur within the context of romance, understood in two primary senses: as a genre encompassing Shakespeare芒聙聶s last four plays, and as a gendered narrative trajectory of the knight-wins-damsel variety. Rather than simply survey his last plays, this course will try to deconstruct that generic category by drawing connections to two earlier plays and asking how Shakespeare makes exotic the conventions of romance. There will be a strong theoretical/critical component, as well as some archival research. Primary texts may include A Midsummer Night芒聙聶s Dream, Othello, A Winter芒聙聶s Tale, Pericles, and The Tempest.

title: 17th Century Poetry
description: The main focus of this course is seventeenth-century lyric poetry. In particular, it will examine five poets who have been traditionally classified as 芒聙聹metaphysical poets.芒聙聺 In placing these poets芒聙聰Donne, Herbert, Vaughan, Crashaw, and Marvell芒聙聰alongside some of their contemporaries (i.e. Jonson, Carew, Lady Mary Wroth, Traherne, Milton, Cowley, etc), we will explore the purchase the term 芒聙聹metaphysical芒聙聺 has (if any), and also discuss the literary history behind it. We will examine how these poets incorporate and transmute the poetic traditions on which they draw, and how this practice enlivens the way the past is presented, while simultaneously inviting us to see that past differently. One question we will address is: In what does the ingenuity芒聙聰and, even, outrageousness芒聙聰of this poetry consist? And how do we describe it? Through close readings of the poems, we will also reflect on the way the analytic and the affective emerge in these poems, and whether the one need come at the exclusion of the other. Last but not least, we will discuss how this poetry challenges us to reassess the role of the imagination, not only in our encounters with seventeenth-century poetry, but also in our readings of later lyric poets (some of whose poetry may be read at the end of the course). Requirements: active participation in classes, reading responses (which will be graded), quizzes, and a final paper.

title: Milton
description: How does a poet make a poem? How does God make a world? How do we make a choice? John Milton芒聙聶s great epic, Paradise Lost, is centrally concerned with these three kinds of making 芒聙聯 poiesis, creation, and decision 芒聙聯 and the relationships between them. To understand what kind of a maker Milton is, we will read his epic alongside the shorter poetry and prose and compare his accounts of making to those of Christian and classical predecessors as well as early modern contemporaries. Students should come ready to try their own hand at making arguments, poems, fateful choices, and imagined worlds.

title: Literature of the Atlantic Empire: From Colonies to Republic
description: In this course, we will examine some British, Native American, Anglo-American, African-British, and American literature that grew out of the collision of cultures in the Americas in the late seventeenth and eighteenth centuries, particularly, though not exclusively, in the Chesapeake and Caribbean regions. In these fictional and non-fictional narratives and poems, we will be interested in several interrelated concerns: the construction of ideas of civilization and savagery; the working out of the dynamics of slavery and freedom (understood historically and economically, but also as figures for psychological and spiritual processes); and the articulation of selfhood and subjectivity in a colonial context. In addition, we will pay attention to the question of whether there are significant and growing differences between Old World and New World identities. Among the works we will read are Oroonoko; Robinson Crusoe; Byrd芒聙聶s Secret History; Equiano芒聙聶s Interesting Narrative of the Life; Franklin芒聙聶s Autogiography; Foster芒聙聶s The Coquette; Brown芒聙聶s Wieland; and James Fenimore Cooper芒聙聶s The Prairie. There will be several shorter papers, two longer papers, and a final exam.

title: 18C Literature: Highways, Sewers, Ports: Building Modern Britain 1650 - 1850
description: This course asks what some of the most fascinating writers of 17th-, 18th-, and 19th-century Britain had to say about the urgent social and environmental problems of their day, from the contamination of drinking water to the flammability of cities, to the navigability of waterways. By investigating the works of Defoe, Swift, Pope, Austen, Wordsworth, Eliot, Dickens, and others for themes of urban planning, transportation, sanitation, and land-use policy, this course considers the role of public works (in the sense of literary texts and construction projects) in the development of modern Britain. To illuminate our readings of literature, we will also study contemporary works of social and economic theory, such as Locke芒聙聶s Two Treatises of Government, Adam Smith芒聙聶s Wealth of Nations, and John Ruskin芒聙聶s Stones of Venice. Analyzing the literal and literary construction of Britain during the Long Eighteenth Century will also provide students with a critical framework for understanding the impact of public works programs in the present moment, like the 2009 American Recovery and Reinvestment Act.

title: Eighteenth-Century British Novel
description: This course will explore a number of British novels of the eighteenth century within a literary and historical context that includes questions of gender, class, and nation as well as those of narrative structure, generic traditions, and aesthetic forms. Our engagement with these novels will include close and careful reading and analysis, active class discussion, and focused critical writing. Authors may include Defoe, Swift, Richardson, Fielding, Sterne, Radcliffe, and Austen.

title: 19C Am. Ghost Stories
description: Haunting the Margins: Gender, Race and Ghosts in 19th Century America This course will explore how nineteenth-century American writers used the ghost story to negotiate problems in American culture. Reading both canonical and popular literature, we will analyze the popularity of the ghost in the American imagination, examining ghost stories that address histories of racial injustice and land appropriation, anxieties over changing gender roles and forbidden or non-normative sexualities, and crises of faith and belief. Authors may include Harriet Beecher Stowe, Nathaniel Hawthorne, Elizabeth Stuart Phelps, Harriet Prescott Spofford, Edgar Allan Poe, Charles W. Chesnutt, Henry James, Jr., Pauline E. Hopkins, Mary E. Wilkins Freeman, Emily Dickinson, Edith Wharton, and others, as well as writings and images from the Modern Spiritualist movement. Students will write several short papers and participate in class presentations and a group research project.

title: Progressive Era Literature
description: Contending Forces: American Novel at the Turn of the Century By the first decades of the 20th-century,certain crises preoccupy American novels: mass migration to cities, a widening gap between rich and poor, changing gender roles, racial violence, and a threatened national identity (sound familiar?). In this class we芒聙聶ll read compelling works by Mark Twain, Charles Chesnutt, Pauline Hopkins, Henry James, Kate Chopin, and F. Scott Fitzgerald, to explore how these areas of concern intersect. We芒聙聶ll focus on the cultural, political, philosophical, and ethical dimensions of fiction and how thoughts and emotions are embodied in the novel form and in the readers who experience them.

title: Staging Anti-Slavery
description: Students in American Studies, Performance Studies, African American Studies, Women芒聙聶s Studies, Literary History, and Justice & Peace Studies may be especially interested in the material of this course. We芒聙聶ll examine late eighteenth and early nineteenth-century women芒聙聶s anti-slavery texts, in hopes of understanding how concepts of religion, race, and gender became entangled during the formative years of the United States. What propels black and white women to enter the risky world of abolitionism, and how do they protect themselves as they enter fiercely contested public debates about race? What are the limits of their imaginings? What is the relationship between self-love and sympathy within women's abolitionist performances? We芒聙聶ll track the changes in how abolitionists gained access to the public sphere or redefined the 芒聙聹private芒聙聺 sphere, and search out archival materials as we analyze anti-slavery poems, essays, plays, speeches, short stories, letters, and autobiographical novels. Our readings may include Phillis Wheatley芒聙聶s poems, Susanna Rowson芒聙聶s plays, Maria W. Stewart芒聙聶s speeches, and Ellen Craft芒聙聶s narrative, along with a host of lesser-known anti-slavery texts.

title: Oscar Wilde & Late Victorian Culture
description: In this class, we will read Wilde芒聙聶s work in several genres--poetry, narrative, non-fiction prose, drama, essays, and letters--analyzing his notions of gender, sexuality, nation, religion, and literary style. We will consider how the texts construct a literary persona and how that persona interacts with and challenges Wilde芒聙聶s contemporary culture as well as our own. How does Wilde produce--and undermine--our notion of 芒聙聹Wilde芒聙聺? In addition to reading Wilde芒聙聶s own literature, the class will also read extensive contextual works, including other nineteenth-century literary texts, the transcripts of the trials, as well as recent critical, theoretical, and literary accounts of Wilde芒聙聶s work and life. There will be both papers and in-class writing assignments in the course of the semester.

title: Wordsworth in 1799
description: This is a highly demanding course centered on one of the most difficult poems of the British Romantic period. Metrical analysis will form an essential part of the course, alongside close examination of semantic and formal elements of poetic practice. For that reason it will be essential for all students to memorise the contents of Philip Hobsbaum芒聙聶s primer on Metre, Rhythm, and Verse Form on which there will be examinations. The emphasis throughout will be on close examination of the text, and group discussion in which everyone is expected to participate. In addition, students are expected to write occasional response papers which will be analysed and discussed in class. It is recommended for those with a serious interest in poetry. My approach prioritises analysis not merely of formal elements but of the language and structure of the poem, in order to understand what Wordsworth was attempting in this great work. For that reason, the course will focus less on critical exegesis than on the poem itself, as well as on the biography of its author. There will be a great deal of preparatory reading. In addition, students are expected to write frequent response papers which will be analysed and discussed in class. These writing assignments will be graded, and each will contribute to the overall assessment of each student芒聙聶s performance.

title: Keats and Byron
description: This is a highly demanding course centered on two of the greatest poets of the British Romantic period. Metrical analysis will form an essential part of the course, alongside close examination of semantic and formal elements of poetic practice. For that reason it will be essential for all students to memorise the contents of Philip Hobsbaum芒聙聶s primer on Metre, Rhythm, and Verse Form on which there will be examinations. The emphasis throughout will be on close examination of the text, and group discussion in which everyone is expected to participate. In addition, students are expected to write occasional response papers which will be analysed and discussed in class. It is recommended for those with a serious interest in poetry.

title: 19C Novel
description: SPRING 2012 PROFESSOR MOLLY CLARK-HILLARD ENG 194-01 The 19th Century Novel This course will explore the rise of the novel as a popular and vital genre in the British nineteenth century. We will trace the novel through the century, examining both its evolution, and the ways in which themes and plots repeat and speak to each other across novels. Students will be introduced to and learn to identify several kinds of novels, such as realist, gothic, domestic, fictional autobiography, and sensation. We will also address formal effects common to nineteenth-century novels, like modes of narration and narrative voice, serial publication, epistolarity, and multiplot structure. Students will learn how the novel was used to explore various issues important to the time period, such as individualism and the problem of self-making, details of science and technology, the ethics of industrialism and capitalism, money, class, and status, emergent notions of time and history, modes of spectacle and surveillance, fallen women and prostitution, the 芒聙聹marriage market,芒聙聺 and emerging discourses about sex and gender. Novel selections may vary, but students might expect to read novels by Jane Austen, Charles Dickens, the Bront脙芦s, George Eliot, and Wilkie Collins. Finally, while we will focus on the novel, students can also expect to read and reflect on selected historical documents, visual artistry, and popular journalism.

title: 19th Century African American Literature
description: The purpose of this course is to study African American literary and cultural production from its beginnings in the 18th century until the publication of W.E.B. Du Bois芒聙聶 seminal text, The Souls of Black Folk, in 1903. As we examine how African American writers transformed the different literary modes in which they wrote, we will also consider how the literary texts articulate the cultural, political, and national concerns that shaped 18th and 19th century America. Throughout the semester, we will return repeatedly to issues of race, class, gender, sexuality, and religion to consider why these authors are concerned with these particular matters. We will think energetically about the historicity of the texts, and the textuality of history; in other words, we will consider how history enriches our understanding of the texts, and how the texts enrich our understanding of history. In addition to the primary texts and literary and cultural criticism that we will read, we also will engage historical documents. Slavery, emancipation, Reconstruction, and the suffragist movement are just a few examples of the historical moments we must interrogate to make our study most productive. While previous familiarity with African American literature is not a requirement for this course, regular attendance, active reading, and a firm commitment to succeed are. Students enrolling in this course should expect to read the primary works by the following authors: Lucy Terry, Phillis Wheatley, David Walker, Maria Stewart, Frederick Douglass, Harriet Jacobs, Harriet Wilson, Sojourner Truth, Booker T. Washington, Anna Julia Cooper, and W.E.B. Du Bois. Potential Assignments/Grade determination: class participation (15%), two in-class examinations (40%), one paper (6 pages) (15%), 3-4 quizzes (15%), and 1 final exercise (15%).

title: Wicked: 19th C Morality and Madness
description: Morality芒聙聶s Complex 19th C Literary Portraits This course examines, via literature, film and philosophical excerpts, the 19th century芒聙聶s 芒聙聹requirements芒聙聺 to be good in order to probe the complexity of the wicked. Is wicked the opposite of good? Is wicked synonymous with evil? Who determines/identifies another as wicked? How do different writers explain how someone 芒聙聹becomes芒聙聺 wicked? Does the 19th century believe that one might be born wicked? How might they distinguish natural wickedness from learned ill behavior? With what other factions of human nature and society does this century align the wicked: gender, age, class, educational background, genetic background, etc? What causes the reader to sympathize with the wicked rather than spurning them? How do the wicked prompt our fear and anger? What happens to the wicked? Also to be considered is how discovering and pursuing the wicked shapes the narrative 芒聙聯 are their discernable common characterizations, patterns in plot, or implied purposes in these novels? Or do the wicked provoke as many distinct styles, moods, and voices as stories focused on individuals who are weak or struggling unjustly? How did the 19th C view the relationship between evil and madness? Between madness and the uncanny? These quests and others will direct our reading of Charles Dickens芒聙聶 Bleak House, Henry James芒聙聶s The Princess Casamassima, Thomas Hardy芒聙聶s Tess of the D芒聙聶Urbervilles, and Fyodor Dostoevsky芒聙聶s Crime and Punishment. Mill, Marx, Nietzsche, and Aristotle offer us some keen insights into the 19th C reader discerning how to interpret the portraits of human nature in these novels. Films of the 21st Century will provide some understanding of the good and evil conundrums representing the world more familiar to us, serving as critical thinking lenses through which to explore the Victorian underworld. Students will produce several short response papers, a major class presentation, and a final research paper.

title: Victorian Literature
description: This survey course focuses on the literature produced during Queen Victoria芒聙聶s reign (1837-1901), that long two-thirds-of-a-century in which England asserted itself as history芒聙聶s first global democratic empire and spread networks of trade, communication, and war to every corner of the earth. The social challenges that attended this phase of globalization triggered revolutionary developments in literary practice, renovating the novel, poetry, nonfiction prose, journalism, and drama in turn. As we sample the full range of Victorian literary production, we芒聙聶ll read both recognized monuments of the age (Wuthering Heights, On Liberty) and those ephemeral or anticanonical texts (King Solomon芒聙聶s Mines, Swinburne芒聙聶s poetry) that testify with surprising eloquence to the creative energy of the era modernism would later belittle. As we work against stereotypes of a prim and proper Victorian age, we芒聙聶ll trace a theme of vital concern to Victorians themselves: social disorder. We芒聙聶ll use close readings to see how the specter of unrest structured debates across a variety of social and literary contexts: the rise of industry, the widening of democratic representation, the expansion of empire, the birth of realism, 芒聙聹the woman question.芒聙聺 What we芒聙聶ll discover, I expect, is that Victorian writing opened new possibilities for human identity while foreclosing others; modeled alternative social forms while enforcing the status quo; institutionalized human liberty while expanding an empire; and invented key aspects of our administered world while subjecting others, in advance, to dynamic and trenchant critique. Are we Victorians, after all? It芒聙聶s more possible than we think. Our texts come from each of the major Victorian genres: fiction from Emily Bronte, Wuthering Heights; H. Rider Haggard, King Solomon芒聙聶s Mines; R.L. Stevenson, Strange Case of Dr. Jekyll and Mr. Hyde; Nonfiction prose from JS Mill, On Liberty; Matthew Arnold, Culture and Anarchy; Charles Darwin, Herbert Spencer, and others; poetry from Lord Tennyson, G.M. Hopkins, A.C. Swinburne, C. Rossetti, Robert Browning, Michael Field, and anonymous soldiers abroad; and drama from Charles Dickens and C.H. Hazlewood, Lady Audley芒聙聶s Secret. Committed reading, writing, and participation is expected.

title: American Modernisms
description: Using a variety of literary texts芒聙聰Toomer芒聙聶s Cane, Faulkner芒聙聶s As I Lay Dying, Stein芒聙聶s The Autobiography of Alice B. Toklas, Wright芒聙聶s Uncle Tom's Children, and Morrison芒聙聶s Jazz芒聙聰this course will examine the various forms of American modernism took and continues to take. We will study the era as a lived experience, an aesthetic statement, a site of gender-racial conflict, and a memory. Required critical text: The Cambridge Companion to American Modernism (ed. Kalaidjian) Assignments and requirements: Literary analysis papers; research-based projects; presentation.

title: New York Stories
description: SPRING 2012 PROFESSOR MAUREEN CORRIGAN ENG 209-01 New York Stories New York City is, arguably, the cultural capital of twentieth- (and, so far), twenty-first century America芒聙聰the center of literature, music, art, architecture, and fashion as well as the focal point for political debates on the viability of cities, immigration, and the meaning of America itself. This course proposes to study autobiographical, fictional, non-fictional, poetic, and cinematic accounts of twentieth-century life in New York City in order to understand the dreams and nightmares embodied by the city in the popular imagination. Beginning with the first decades of the twentieth century, we will investigate the decline of 芒聙聹old guard芒聙聺 aristocratic society in the early 1900s and the nature of the immigrant experience in New York. The course will then focus on the Jazz Age when New York artists, writers, and architects shaped a new ethos of modernity that would define 芒聙聹The American Century.芒聙聺 Attention will also be devoted to the various 芒聙聹counter-cultural芒聙聺 movements (e.g. the rise of the American Communist Party, the Beat Movement) that erupted in New York City during the early and middle decades of the last century. The course will conclude with contemporary dystopian and stubbornly utopian predictions about the future of New York City and urban life in America. 芒聙聹There芒聙聶s only one city that belongs to the whole country, and that芒聙聶s New York.芒聙聺 William Dean Howells 芒聙聹New York is more full of reflections than of itself.芒聙聺 Zelda Fitzgerald 芒聙聹It is an ugly city, a dirty city. Its climate is a scandal. Its politics are used to frighten children. Its traffic is madness. Its competition is murderous. But there is one thing about it芒聙聰once you have lived in New York and it has become your home, no other place is good enough.芒聙聺 John Steinbeck Texts: Edith Wharton, The House of Mirth Alfred Kazin, A Walker in the City F. Scott Fitzgerald, The Great Gatsby Pete Hamill, A Drinking Life Jan Morris, Manhattan, 1945 Ann Petry, The Street E.B. White, Here is New York Anatole Broyard, Kafka Was the Rage Selections from: Ann Douglas, Terrible Honesty; Joseph Mitchell, McSorley芒聙聶s Wonderful Saloon, The Poetry of the Harlem Renaissance, Vivian Gornick, The Romance of American Communism Films: A Tree Grows In Brooklyn The Clock The Out-of-Towners Annie Hall Do the Right Thing

title: The Female Bildungsroman: Coming of Age in US Women's Literature
description: This course will examine the bildungsroman, or novel of development, as a mainstay of U. S. women芒聙聶s writing, focusing especially on texts from the last few decades of the twentieth century and on into the contemporary moment. We will also watch a number of films organized around this thematic as well. Examining narrative depictions of coming of age by a diverse group of writers and filmmakers, the course will explore ways in which gendered subjectivity is shaped by historical context and historical 芒聙聹memory芒聙聺: indeed, many of these texts deal explicitly with issues of history, historiography, and remembrance, and depict the act of recovering some sense of the past as central to the formation (or re-formation) of personal and/or collective identity. In addition, we will examine how specific social differences of gender, race, class, sexuality, and nationality shape the narrative paradigm of 芒聙聹coming of age芒聙聺; toward that end, we will pay special attention to coming-of-age texts and films by 芒聙聹ethnic,芒聙聺 working-class, and GLBTQ authors and filmmakers. We will also become familiar with some important critical debates about the bildungsroman as a major subgenre of the novel form, and we will read a number of contemporary critical and theoretical essays on cultural constructions of gendered subjectivity. Some questions we will consider: how do cultural texts (visual, literary)芒聙聰particularly narrative texts芒聙聰both represent and constitute what we think of as gendered 芒聙聹identities芒聙聺? What defines 芒聙聹coming of age芒聙聺 in the contemporary U. S., and how is this paradigm inflected by social differences such as race/ethnicity, class, sexuality, region, and/or nation? What are the governing, dominant 芒聙聹narratives芒聙聺 or 芒聙聹scripts,芒聙聺 psychological and/or cultural, that define female maturation, and how are these scripts inscribed, registered, engaged with, and /or contested and disrupted in the literary texts and films under consideration? Indeed, how/why do we imagine female maturation as 芒聙聹development,芒聙聺 as a continuous, linear narrative with a particular teleology (often culminating in marriage and motherhood)芒聙聰and how have those expectations been shaped by literary and cultural texts? How are female embodiment and sexuality represented in these texts? How do these writers and filmmakers re-construct, and enable us to re-imagine, what it means to 芒聙聹come of age芒聙聺 in the U. S.?

title: 20th C American Poetry
description: From the start, 20th century poetry has been a collective practice. In the first part of the century there were Ezra Pound芒聙聶s Imagists and a range of Modernist movements, John Crowe Ransom and the southern Fugitives, and the flowerings of African American poetry both in Harlem and here in Washington, among many others. The second half of the century gave birth to the San Francisco and Beat scenes, the Black Arts Movement, the New York Schools of poets and painters, and the slam scene, to name a few more. In this class we芒聙聶ll try to read poems in the context of these social networks, seeking to recreate poetry scenes through primary materials such as manifestoes, little magazines, correspondence, recordings of poetry readings, web publications, etc.

title: Transnational Modernism
description: Examining the genealogies of modernism as it traverses imperial, colonial, and postcolonial cultures, we shall analyze how postcolonial literature has unsettled the conventional distinction between modernism and postmodernism. In our readings of texts by Samuel Beckett, V. S. Naipaul, Chris Abani, Kazuo Ishiguro, Zadie Smith, and J. M. Coetzee, among others, we shall consider the ways in which modernism is a condition made possible by imperialism. Topics to be addressed include: decolonization芒聙聶s contribution to modernism; the relationship between aesthetics and politics; and the relationship between diaspora, exile, cosmopolitanism and poetics. This seminar will include one lengthy research essay, short weekly writing assignments, and one class presentation.

title: Asian Americans and the Public Sphere
description: This class will address issues concerning power and visibility in public life for Asian Americans in the 21st century. We will make an informed historical and cultural evaluation of the critical factors that have led to being perennially understood as an 芒聙聹invisible minority芒聙聺 and propose real-world solutions to reverse this trend. The class will center on politics, culture, media and representation with some important critical discussions about government and the public sphere. Using Asian American literature, performance art, multimedia projects, manga, television and film, we will observe the limits and opportunities that accompany entrance into the U.S. cultural mainstream. Readings may include works by Jessica Hagedorn, Adrian Tomine, Catherine Liu, Sesshu Foster, David Henry Hwang, Karen Yamashita, Garrett Hongo and Kaiji Kawaguchi. We will be viewing recently acclaimed films by Asian Americans such as Tze Chun芒聙聶s Children of Invention, Frances Hsueh and Steven Hahn芒聙聶s Party, and Greg Pak芒聙聶s Robot Stories, among others. Requirements will consist of two short essays, a midterm exam, a class presentation and a final research paper or final exam.

title: Seminar: African Drama
description: Staging Africa: African Drama and Performance Across the Continent This seminar will focus on the history and development of various theatrical traditions in African drama. It will emphasize both specific national contexts and broader theoretical concerns in African performance studies. Authors will include Ngugi Wa Thiong芒聙聶o, Ama Ata Aidoo, Wole Soyinka, and Tawfik al Hakim, as well a range of lesser-known writers/texts and histories of dance and musical performances from across the continent. Paying particular attention to issues of gender and sexuality, this class can include those interested in Theatre/Performance Studies, African Studies, Women芒聙聶s and Gender Studies, and Literary Studies, and no prior knowledge of the performance theory or African literature is required. Assignments will include group presentations, short response papers, a long critical paper, and a final assignment that involves analytical research but can take creative or critical form.

title: African American Poetry
description: In this class, we芒聙聶ll bear witness to the 芒聙聹furious flowering芒聙聺 of African American poetry and poetic cultures at critical junctures over the course of the last century. We芒聙聶ll delve into the theory and practice of the 芒聙聹New Negro芒聙聺 Movement (also known as the Harlem Renaissance) of the 1920s and 30s, the Black Arts movement of the 1960s, and the contemporary spoken word scene, devoting special attention to the journals, salons, small presses, clubs, and record labels that have and continue to nurture them. We芒聙聶ll encounter a wide range of poets, from Paul Laurence Dunbar and Gwendolyn Bennett to Elizabeth Alexander, to Saul Williams, to DJ Spooky, in addition to extended study of the long and varied careers of Langston Hughes, Gwendolyn Brooks, and Amiri Baraka.

title: Modern British Class Novel
description: This course examines literary representations of the British working class during a charged, transformative era in modern British labor history from the 1890s through the 1940s: the rise of the Labour Party, along with other Socialist movements; trade union activism, culminating in the 1926 General Strike; implementation of the welfare state; and the flourishing of 芒聙聹Independent Working-Class Education芒聙聺 through the Labour College movement. This same period proved to be an exciting moment of literary 芒聙聹revolution芒聙聺 as well, producing new modernist styles of narrative alongside more traditional forms of realism. Drawing on readings in social/cultural/literary history and theory about this period as well as current theories of class as a gendered and racialized construct, we will explore a range of novels芒聙聰and in a few cases, short fiction芒聙聰 that address class conflict and an emerging working-class identity written by more celebrated as well as lesser-known authors who themselves span the working and middle/educated classes: the 1890s 芒聙聹slum novel芒聙聺 of East End life, such as George Gissing芒聙聶s Thyrza and Arthur Morrison芒聙聶s A Child of the Jago; E.M. Forster芒聙聶s Howard芒聙聶s End; D.H. Lawrence芒聙聶s stories, such as 芒聙聹Odour of Chrysanthemums芒聙聺 and 芒聙聹Tickets, Please,芒聙聺 and his novel Sons and Lovers; Katherine Mansfield芒聙聶s modernist short stories; Ellen Wilkinson芒聙聶s Clash; Ethel Carnie Holdsworth芒聙聶s This Slavery; excerpts from Robert Tressell芒聙聶s The Ragged Trousered Philanthropists and Lewis Grassic Gibbon芒聙聶s Scottish trilogy A Scots Quair. We芒聙聶ll complement these texts with selections from literary and educational magazines produced either by or for working-class writers/readers, such as the 芒聙聵Plebs芒聙聶 Magazine and Left Review. We芒聙聶ll pay particular attention to thinking through notions of class shame, resistance, and guilt, studying how all three shape narrative form as well as content.

title: Twentieth Century British Novel
description: This course is an intensive survey of some of the most influential British novels of the twentieth century. Moving chronologically from the fin-de-siecle, through the modernist period, postwar, and finally postmodern periods, we will attempt to mark the important formal, generic, thematic, theoretical, cultural, and political shifts represented in this extraordinarily novel age. Readings will include novels by: Joseph Conrad, E.M. Forster, James Joyce, Virginia Woolf, Evelyn Waugh, Jean Rhys, Doris Lessing, Graham Greene, Iris Murdoch, V.S. Naipaul, Jeanette Winterson, A.S. Byatt, Salman Rushdie

title: Orwell Between Culture and System
description: Orwell Between Culture and System The course examines the texts of George Orwell, among the most widely read and visible figures of twentieth-century literature. In our reading of works such as The Road to Wigan Pier, Homage to Catalonia, Keep the Aspidistra Flying, Burmese Days, Coming Up for Air, Animal Farm, Nineteen Eighty-Four, as well as his essays, we shall consider the interrelated subjects of Orwell芒聙聶s representation of Englishness, his accounts of the role of the writer, and the confluence of forces under which he was writing芒聙聰the Spanish Civil War, the rise of Fascism, Stalinism, the growing resistance to British colonial rule, and the lateness of empire. A strong emphasis will be placed on the problems raised by Orwell芒聙聶s literary techniques, particularly as they relate to questions of ideology, knowledge, and subjectivity.

title: Tolkien and Medieval Roots
description: Also listed as MVST 247-01 J.R.R. Tolkien was a medievalist and a philologist who, by writing fiction and poetry, created a world in which his languages could live. We will read widely in medieval works from Irish, Finnish, Old English, Middle English, Icelandic and French contexts, and examine Tolkien芒聙聶s biography, letters, critical and scholarly publications, and lesser known fictional works, all to get a better sense of what Tolkien knew and worked with as he wrote. We will read some of his scholarship, poetry, translations, draft work, and unfinished pieces as well as the major works /The Silmarillion/ and /The Lord of the Rings/. Our interests focus on Tolkien as both writer and scholar. The final one third of the course will be spent on careful re-reading of /The Lord of the Rings/ in light of our extensive contextual study. Students can expect to keep a regular critical journal, research and report on supplementary topics on Blackboard, write a paper on medieval texts, and finish with a substantial take-home exam. Everyone taking the course must have read /The Lord of the Rings/ prior to the first day of class, as it is simply too large and complex to come to for the first time in this class. The Jackson movies are definitely not a substitute! Note: All students must have read The Lord of the Rings prior to the beginning of class.

title: Faulkner
description: This course will investigate one of the greatest American literary achievements of the modern era. We will begin with Go Down, Moses, a collection of interrelated short stories as an entree into the world of Faulkner. Subsequently, we will read and discuss, in chronological order, the major works of Faulkner芒聙聶s most prolific years: The Sound and the Fury; Sanctuary; As I Lay Dying; Light in August; and Absalom, Absalom. We will discuss these works in terms of predominant themes, psychological and sociological, and Faulkner芒聙聶s corresponding experimentation in a rich variety of literary techniques. We will also view and discuss The Reivers in movie form. Requirements: a mid-term examination, two 5 page papers and one 10-12 page final paper (or a journal).

title: Eliot's Wasteland
description: We will begin the semester by reading several of Eliot's earlier poems to investigate both his experimentation with poetic technique as well as his thematic concerns. The course will concentrate, however, on The Waste Land. Properly to understand Eliot's opus, however, requires reading its principal allusions: Scripture (Old and New), Dante, Virgil, St. Augustine, Tarot cards, fertility gods and rituals, Grail Knight romances, Shakespeare, Thomas Kyd, Baudelaire, and the Baghavad Gita--virtually a liberal arts curriculum in itself. The final re-reading of The Waste Land, however, with allusions understood and experienced, should represent an extraordinary experience of poetic technique and literature, both East and West.

title: Beckett, Borges, and Nabokov
description: In his seminal 1967 article 芒聙聹The Literature of Exhaustion,芒聙聺 John Barth identifies Samuel Beckett, Jorge Luis Borges, and Vladimir Nabokov as the standard bearers of the new literary tradition. For Barth, Beckett, Borges, and Nabokov are artists who are technically up to date and yet able to speak to the human condition as few others. This course will examine the literary works of these three authors and their respective roles in ushering in the literary tradition we now call postmodernism. We will explore the literary and cultural contexts of their writing, a selection of their major works, and the narrative and dramatic techniques of each. Readings will include Three Novels: Malloy, Malone Dies, and The Unnamable, Waiting for Godot, The Garden of Forking Paths, The Aleph, Lolita, Pale Fire, and Ada, or Ardor.

title: Reading Invisible Man
description: Lower Frequencies: Reading Invisible Man This course considers Ralph Ellison芒聙聶s classic 1952 novel _Invisible Man_ within and against the literary, philosophical, and vernacular traditions that influenced its composition. Not only will we read Ellison芒聙聶s writings (both fiction and non-fiction) in detail, we will also examine many of the cultural sources on which _Invisible Man_ draws: music by Louis Armstrong, Jimmy Rushing, and Charlie Parker; fiction by Fyodor Dostoevsky, Richard Wright, Mark Twain, James Joyce, Ernest Hemingway, and William Faulkner; and essays by Frederick Douglass, W.E.B. Du Bois, Ralph Waldo Emerson, and T.S. Eliot--among many others. We will have two main goals throughout the course of the semester: first, to understand Ellison芒聙聶s complex vision of the African American experience; second, to understand the centrality of that experience to modern American life and art. This is a reading-intensive course that requires occasional response essays and a longer final paper.

title: Brit. Theatre Since 1950
description: SPRING 2012 PROFESSOR MICHAEL COLLINS ENG 266-01 The productions in London of Samuel Beckett芒聙聶s Waiting for Godot (1954) and of John Osborne's Look Back in Anger (1956) at the Royal Court Theatre are said to mark a turning point in the history of British theatre in the twentieth century. The plays that followed on the British stage were different in theme and usually in form from those that had been written and produced in the first half of the twentieth century, and they reflected not simply a new kind of theatre in Britain, but a new way of seeing the world outside it. The course will consider some memorable plays by important British playwrights of the last fifty to sixty years, giving attention to theme, form, and possibilities for performance. It will define British loosely, including not only such English playwrights as John Osborne, Harold Pinter, Tom Stoppard, and Caryl Churchill, but also such Irish playwrights Samuel Beckett, Brian Friel, Martin McDonough, and Conor McPherson, and the South African playwright Athol Fugard. The course hopes to come to some understanding of the distinct focus and idiom of the British theatre over the last 50 to 60 years and to discover how it might reflect the values and beliefs of the world in which the plays were written and performed. Students will be asked to take part in a number of acting exercises throughout the semester to enhance their appreciation of the theatrical power of these plays. (The course carries four credits in order to provide them an opportunity to do so). They will also be asked to attend at least one performance of a relevant play if one is in production in the area.

title: Television & American Society
description: This course examines the history of television and its complex relationship to American culture and society. As a source of information, a purveyor of entertainment, and a tool of socialization, television had a profound impact on almost every aspect of 20th century American life. Over the past decade, new technologies and media convergence have done little to erode its significance. Our investigation of the medium will begin with television芒聙聶s origins in radio and trace its aesthetic and economic development over the past half-century. It will cover a range of topics from broadcast regulation to identity politics. Through a variety of theoretical and methodological approaches, we will consider how television shapes and is shaped by our national preoccupations.

title: Women's Autobiography
description: This course investigates the notoriously devious genre of autobiography. Among the many questions we芒聙聶ll be considering this semester are the following: --What distinguishes autobiography from fiction? --Does it matter if a subject tells the truth in her autobiography? --Do men and women tell their stories differently and is it liberating or damaging to argue for gender difference in writing? --Can women ever really write their lives truthfully through autobiography if it is, as many scholars argue, an inherently male genre? Are there really such things as 芒聙聹women芒聙聶s autobiographies芒聙聺? --Why is autobiography such an enormously popular genre at this moment? --What, if anything, about the language and worldview of these autobiographies makes them distinctively American? --Is the self perceived or created through writing? In an attempt to arrive at some answers to these questions, we芒聙聶ll be reading a wide selection of American women芒聙聶s autobiographies throughout the twentieth century. Students will also write their own five page autobiographical essay and a five-page critique of that essay-writing experience. In addition, each student will participate in three different groups: an autobiographical essay reading group, a group that will lead class discussion of one of the assigned texts, and a final project group that will read, discuss, and present insights about autobiographies not assigned in the course.

title: Prison Literature
description: Using the fact that the U.S. has over 7 million people on probation, parole, in jail or prison in America, this course will investigate the uses of imprisonment diachronically by interrogating the theoretical and philosophical grounding provided by Michel Foucault芒聙聶s Discipline and Punish and synchronically using the recent exploration of agency and moral responsibility in life stories of prisoners in Speaking of Crime by Patricia E. O芒聙聶Connor and current research into connections with drugs and crime. We will also read (and sometimes perform) a selection of literature by and about the imprisoned from longer works by Jack Abbott, Etheridge Knight, Patricia McConnell; Patrice Gaines, Nathan McCall, Kody Scott, Wilbert Rideau and Ron Wikberg and short selections from Bruce Franklin芒聙聶s Prison Writing in the 20th Century. Students involved in Georgetown芒聙聶s outreach into prisons and work with adjudicated youth may be interested in taking this course for community-based learning credit. Students will be asked to write reflectively and analytically on topics in the texts, take on mid-term, lead some discussions and do oral presentations. All students will engage in a research project on a topic of their interest reflecting current issues and conflicts in imprisonment and their relation to historical, social, political, and economic practices that affect or that are impacted by the criminal justice system芒聙聶s uses of incarceration.

title: Cult Politics of Migration
description: Narratives of Shifting Identities (to be taught spring 2012 in SFS-Qatar & in Washington, DC from simulcast classroom GW03 in New North, Washington Campus) This course taught simultaneously in Washington and in Qatar (via the RPX video presence classroom) will acquaint students with a variety of migration stories both within the USA and to the USA and examine the ways that migration yields a doubled/troubled identity. Through examination of narratives of migration, we will also interrogate the idea of the terminology 芒聙聹migration story芒聙聺 as a means for negatively stereotyping or essentializing the recent migrant vs a 芒聙聹legitimate芒聙聺 member of a community. Though we will look briefly at early migrations to the North American continent, its colonial conquests and forced migrations of native peoples and slaves to set the US context, we will focus on the USA from 1900芒聙聶s to current day (2010芒聙聶s) in the literature. Students will identify cultural and political factors that affect acceptance/rejection of immigrants. We will examine factors that contribute to holding onto old identities and to forging new identities that are shaped and re-shaped by the migration experiences. As an English department offering this course will provide tools for narrative analysis in the collecting of narrative and in reading of cultural and political shifts as revealed in the texts. We will broaden the theoretical bases by giving some attention to various migration theories: neoclassical economic theory on global supply and demand for labor; segmented labor-market theory that considers using immigrants for less desired labor and world-systems theory that sees global capitalism drawing workers from the periphery to the core. We will examine whether/how these theories play out in the literary texts. Literature will include: Chinese migration to USA in Woman Warrior by Maxine Hong Kingston. Migration within the nation such as shown in movement of workers from the American south to the industrial north in WWII in The Dollmaker by Harriet Arnow and in Jacob Lawrence芒聙聶s paintings; lives of recent immigrants to America from Cuba and Mexico in works by Cecilia R Milanes and Rigoberto Gonz脙隆lez. 20th c narratives of Middle East and African migration will be read in works by Ameen Rihani芒聙聶s The Book of Khalid (1911) a novel about a Lebanese immigrant to NY, and late 20th c migration from Ethiopia in Dinaw Mengestu芒聙聶s novel The Beautiful Things that Heaven Bears. As well as writing 2 short analytical papers on the course texts, students will collect, write and digitally produce the migration story of one person, paying particular attention to the 芒聙聹push芒聙聺 as well as the 芒聙聹pull芒聙聺 that contributes to migration in order to work in another country or part of a country.

title: Testimonial Fictions: Atrocity, Memory and Sexuality in Post-Dictatorship US Latino Literature
description: The course surveys mostly literary representations of US Latino diasporic communities negotiating the historical, political and cultural legacies of extreme state-sponsored violence on the part of dictatorial regimes in their homelands. Case studies include Dominican America after Trujillo, Haitian America after the Duvaliers, Cuban America "after" Castro, and Guatemalan/Salvadoran America after the 1980's-era juntas. Writers and texts include Julia Alvarez (In the Name of Salom脙漏), Junot D脙颅az (Oscar Wao), Edwidge Danticat (The Dew Breaker and Brother, I'm Dying), Reinaldo Arenas (Before Night Falls), Cristina Garc脙颅a (A Handbook to Luck) and Hector Tobar (The Tattooed Soldier). Course readings will also include significant scholarly work in Latin American and US Latino Studies, as well as in critical and political theory.

title: Testimonial Fictions: Atrocity, Memory and Sexuality in Post-Dictatorship US Latino Literature
description: The course surveys mostly literary representations of US Latino diasporic communities negotiating the historical, political and cultural legacies of extreme state-sponsored violence on the part of dictatorial regimes in their homelands. Case studies include Dominican America after Trujillo, Haitian America after the Duvaliers, Cuban America "after" Castro, and Guatemalan/Salvadoran America after the 1980's-era juntas. Writers and texts include Julia Alvarez (In the Name of Salom脙漏), Junot D脙颅az (Oscar Wao), Edwidge Danticat (The Dew Breaker and Brother, I'm Dying), Reinaldo Arenas (Before Night Falls), Cristina Garc脙颅a (A Handbook to Luck) and Hector Tobar (The Tattooed Soldier). Course readings will also include significant scholarly work in Latin American and US Latino Studies, as well as in critical and political theory.

title: Race in American Literature
description: In this course, we will examine Anglo-American and African American narratives from 1845 to 1940 in order to understand how race along the color line has been constructed, narrated, employed, and deployed in America. We will be particularly interested in how the binary of black and white signifies and symbolizes social and cultural conflicts and interests as depicted in American literature. What is literary whiteness? What is literary blackness? What strategies are used to construct race in American literature? Additionally, what role does race play in the interpretation of American literature? Readings may include, among others, works by Douglass, Hawthorne, Larsen, Fitzgerald, Wright and Faulkner. Students will also read literary and critical theory. This course is both reading and writing intensive. Students should expect to participate in close and engaged readings, active class discussions and presentations, and multiple critical writings opportunities.

title: Marxism and Culture
description: This course provides neither a comprehensive survey nor historical overview of the 芒聙聹theoretical practice芒聙聺 of Marxist cultural production. Instead, it will offer a highly selective and idiosyncratic probe into some of the key theoretical issues and actions associated with the Marxist cultural tradition around the world. We will explore the realm of culture in its varied relationships with the political and revolutionary demands of socialism in several contexts, including both those of actually-existing socialist states and the capitalist West. We will read representative statements from Marx and Engels from the mid-nineteenth century including the 芒聙聹Communist Manifesto,芒聙聺 Capital Vol. I and the Grundrisse, but will concentrate on more recent culture and criticism as it relates to postcolonial liberation movements, democracy, civil rights, gender and racial equality post World War II. Texts will include literature, theory, philosophy, political science, economics, film, critical media studies. One short paper, midterm exam, one long collaborative research paper.

title: Young Adult Literature
description: Arguably one of the liveliest genres to appear in the last thirty years, young adult (YA) or adolescent fiction is innovative, far-ranging, and controversial. Both texts written for adolescents (such as the later Harry Potters and Stephenie Meyer's Twilight), who are usually defined as 12-15 years of age, and those appropriated by them (such The Curious Incident of the Dog in the Nighttime and The Book Thief) will be examined, as will films that have been made from those texts (such as Laurie Halse Anderson's novel Speak). We will also read the extremely popular trilogy The Hunger Games and attend the movie due out in late March. Students will attend required film viewings; do in-class, spontaneous writing; write several short essays; and write an original research paper or creative short story with commentary as a final project.

title: Contexts for the Study of Sexuality
description: This course provides an overview of the theoretical, historical and political contexts within which the fields of queer theory and sexuality and gender studies operate. It highlights topics such as the emergence of modern sexual identity, LGBT rights, queer community-building and world-making, and transgender politics. Specific attention will be paid to the relationship between feminist and queer thought, and to the gendered, raced and classed dimensions of sexual politics. This class is reading-intensive; students will write regular short papers, engage in both in-class and online discussions, and complete an independent research project. [This course will fulfill the Feminist Theory requirement for the Women芒聙聶s and Gender Studies major, minor and certificate.]

title: Intro to Creative Writing
description: This course is an introduction to the art and discipline of creative writing. Our exploration will take a number of forms: close study of the elements of craft (imagery, voice, character, etc.); vigorous discussion; analysis of the fiction, poetry, drama and creative non-fiction of published authors and of your peers, with special emphasis on the workshop process; and, above all, intensive writing of your own, both inside and outside of class. The course will culminate in a portfolio of your selected and revised work.

title: Intro to Creative Writing
description: This course will introduce you to genres of creative writing by studying and practicing four modes: fiction, dialogue, poetry, and personal prose. We will explore and discuss great models of each of these forms; we'll hear craft lectures from the great writers in our Department and elsewhere who produce them; we will write critical prose on selected works; and then we'll try our hand at composing poems, stories, personal essays, and dialogue (for radio). Among the authors studied: D.H. Lawrence, Michael Ondaatje, and Anna Deavere Smith. You should already have experience in studying and writing in one of these genres, and be prepared for intensive research in how each form of writing happens, how it renders human experience, voice, plot, and vision. Be prepared both to experiment with literary forms and to share your work with others.

title: Short Fiction Workshop
description: What makes a story sing? In this workshop, each student will develop his or her own original short stories while examining the elements that make any work of fiction truly brilliant. Questions of form, genre, style, and voice will be central to our investigation as we construct our own pieces and critique our classmates'. While the emphasis will be on the act of writing and the craft of fiction, we will also read selected short masterpieces of the genre as we move from draft to piece to masterpiece. Prerequisites: By permission of instructor ONLY. To apply, send a one page letter explaining who you are and why you're interested in this class, as well as a one page sample of your creative writing. Send to jnf5@georgetown.edu by April 7, 2011; you will hear back by April 11. Students who have taken ENGL 325 may not enroll in ENGL 326.

title: Fiction Writing Workshop
description: In this class, you芒聙聶ll immerse yourselves in the art and discipline of fiction writing, with particular focus on the short story. Partly this means acquiring a writer芒聙聶s critical eye for fiction, and you芒聙聶ll study the basic elements of successful fiction (character, plot, description, etc.) and use these tools to read and analyze contemporary short stories. Of course, the writer above all learns through doing; you芒聙聶ll therefore write a great deal of your own fiction芒聙聰regular exercises as well as more fully developed and revised work. Much of this development will happen as a result of the workshop process, where you芒聙聶ll give one another extensive feedback on work submitted to the class as a whole. Admission by permission of the instructor ONLY. Interested students should e-mail sample writing (5-10 pp.) and personal statement about what they hope to get out of the class, by November 7th, to dhe8@georgetown.edu.

title: Craft Studies: Lit/Comp/Creative (seminar)
description: The field of Craft Studies is succinctly described by one of its leading practitioners as 芒聙聹engaged theorizing芒聙聺 about creative writing. But what does it mean to attach the word 芒聙聹studies芒聙聺 to the craft of writing? to describe the course as a 芒聙聹seminar芒聙聺 as well as a creative writing 芒聙聹workshop芒聙聺? If such questions arise, it is because, within the institutions where we write and study 芒聙聹English,芒聙聺 we long ago accepted a simplistic and artificial distinction between the mastery of writing skills and the imaginative work of making meaning. Craft Studies challenges that distinction; in so doing, it makes possible a theoretical engagement with creative writing. At once a seminar and a writing workshop, this course will explore the intersection of three strands of English studies--literature, composition, and imaginative writing芒聙聯as they converge in the study of writing as craft. What does it mean to read like a writer? What do writers have to say about their craft? We will examine theories of writing and the teaching of writing as formulated by practicing writers in many genres: poets, essayists, fiction writers, memoirists, and literary/cultural critics. Over the course of the semester, students will participate in an ongoing creative writing workshop, write and revise a critical essay, and develop a substantial first-person piece of creative nonfiction. [Readings include Dillard, The Writing Life; Agee and Evans, Let Us Now Praise Famous Men; Orwell, Why I Write; O芒聙聶Brien, The Things They Carried; and writing produced by participants in the workshop.]

title: Lannan Poetry Seminar
description: Instructor Permission Only Lannan Poetry Seminar encourages dynamic critical thinking and creativity with a view to examining literary forms and practices; the work of individual authors; the relationship between writing and other arts; and the place of writing in contemporary culture. Fellows study and discuss the work of the year芒聙聶s scheduled guests in the Lannan 芒聙聹Readings and Talks芒聙聺 series, and then produce an independent final project (creative or critical) under the guidance of the instructor. There are also opportunities for students to share their own writing and to participate in a service-learning component芒聙聰organizing and facilitating educational outreach efforts in the D.C. Community. Enrollment in this seminar is limited to the previously selected Lannan Fellows.

title: Film Making for Writers
description: This course introduces novice screenwriters to the fundamental dynamic of cinematic and dramatic narrative. It is the first in a three-course ladder designed for the student who wishes to graduate from Georgetown with a full-length, original feature script. In this introductory course classes combine exercises in imagination with scrutiny of iconic films from the classic cinema canon to help students generate their own screen-oriented stories. Although film is studied in the course, and studied closely, English 335 is not primarily a film study class. It is designed and taught as a writing workshop aimed at assisting the student to develop and enrich his or her own creative instincts and imagination. PERMISSION ONLY: FOR PERMISSION TO REGISTER STUDENTS SHOULD MAKE AN APPOINTMENT TO TALK WITH PROFESSOR GLAVIN THROUGH CARROLLPROGRAMS@GEORGETOWN.EDU.

title: Tutorial: Intermediate Screenwriting
description: An individual tutorial offered to students who have successfully completed English 335. The student will complete an original, full-length feature script in a genre and with a story line of the student's choice.

title: Intermediate Scriptwriting
description: Students who demonstrate aptitude in Film-Making for Writers (English 335) may apply for permission to take Intermediate Scriptwriting. Working in tutorials, accepted students will write an original short script which they will then shoot during the second half of the term. Because time within the semester is limited, students are encouraged to discuss script ideas with the instructor before the beginning of the term.

title: Advanced Scriptwriting
description: An individual tutorial offered to students who have successfully completed English 335. The student will complete an original, full-length feature script in a genre and with a story line of the student's choice.

title: Adv Poetry Writing
description: [None available].

title: Seminar: Writing Poetry
description: This seminar is an introduction to poetry writing, critique and revision for students in all levels of experience. We will primarily focus on the writing of original poems, responding to prompts and exercises in traditional, contemporary and experimental forms. We will also be reading contemporary and traditional poems and studying the basics of prosody and craft. Our final project will be a creative manuscript.

title: Revolution/Respect: Writing for a Cause
description: Using Black protest literature of the Civil Rights era as a model, this course will show students how to craft fiction for a purpose. Do you want to reform schools, expose genocide, stop abortion, end a war, promote or limit immigration? Whatever your political agenda, you can advance it芒聙聰as writers always have芒聙聰through thoughtful and intentional storytelling. Readings will include authors such as James Baldwin, Lorraine Hansberry, and John A. Williams, and students will be asked to reflect, in writing, on how these artists identify and attack injustice in their work. The goal of the course, however, will be for each student to produce, through various exercises and extensive drafts, one solid piece of short fiction that advocates a position on a public issue.

title: Writing: Children's Rights
description: In this class we will focus on the conversion of 芒聙聹true life芒聙聺 into fiction through actual practice. We will put our attention on early childhood and children's stories and then on young adult fiction, both classic and new. There will be two weekend workshops in March with kids from 826DC, when we work with them to convert their own stories into fiction. Half of each class will involve the discussion of assigned readings and the other half will be the sharing of creative work. Previous experience with a writing workshop would be very helpful.

title: Empathy and Activism
description: SPRING 2012 ENG 356-01 PROFESSOR GAY CIMA What is the relationship between the practice of empathy and political activism in the arts? How do performance, poetry, and politics link people together? Is empathy necessary to芒聙聰or a danger to芒聙聰those building a civic space? In the eighteenth century, philosophers thought that sympathetic feeling was central to civic activity. We芒聙聶ll test that notion by investigating the relationship between empathy and activism in the transatlantic world of nineteenth-century abolitionist literature, both in nonfiction and fiction. We芒聙聶ll trace the genealogy of abolitionism into contemporary performances involving memory and forgetting. The diverse reading list will include philosophical treatises on sympathy, political theories about citizenship, and anti-slavery and human rights poems, plays, short stories, speeches, letters, and essays.

title: Creative Writing: Exchanging Narratives
description: Lannan Chair Coll: Exchanging Narratives This is a writing workshop with an interactive component. Students will be asked to write their own personal history in any way that most suits them: through fiction, poetry, or descriptive prose. They can switch between these genres week to week, but they all will be writing about childhood, early youth and leaving home in that order. In collaboration with the Guestbook Project at Boston College, students will record conversations they have with young people outside the Georgetown community, who might have some ideas on why the city is so divided and what can be done about it. This collaborative report will be posted online and shared with students as far off as Derry in Northern Ireland and the workshop will 芒聙聹tune in芒聙聺 to a classroom without walls, exchanging narratives. Graduate students interested in this course should register through a paper add/drop form.

title: The Art of Editing
description: The title of this course may be confusing: most consider editing not an art, but a skill, and not a very glamorous one at that. But in fact the point of this class will be to illuminate the subtle, challenging, and mysterious art of editing芒聙聰or rather, the many arts of editing, from coming up with ideas to building a suspenseful narrative to whittling down sentences to dealing with tricky authors. Weekly assignments will include close readings and article critiques focused on tracing the editor's hand in familiar publications and learning to assess others' editorial choices; students will also assign, write, and edit columns, reported stories, and reviews. Grades will be in part based on how much students can elevate their peers芒聙聶 work. By the end, students should have strengthened their critical thinking as readers and writers of nonfiction and will be well prepared for any magazine or online internship. (Britt Peterson is the deputy managing editor at Foreign Policy, where she is responsible for editing the culture section, In Other Words, as well as online narrative work, including FP's first-ever ebook, Waiting for the Taliban, based on a series of dispatches from northern Afghanistan. Before that, she was an editor at The New Republic for three years. Her writing has appeared in TNR, the Washington Post, Bookforum, the Nation, the Chronicle of Higher Education, n+1, and a number of other publications.)

title: Introduction to Journalism
description: SPRING 2012 PROFESSOR BARBARA FEINMAN TODD 470-01 This course will teach students the basics of journalism, beginning with how to assess the news value of an event or situation, how to develop story ideas and how to put together a story. Much attention will be paid to writing clearly and accurately and developing interviewing skills. The ethical issues facing journalists will be also be a focus. Guest speakers will include reporters, critics and editors from Washington and Baltimore. There will be several short writing assignments and a final project. (Barbara Feinman Todd is the Journalism Director at Georgetown University. She is also the co-director of the Pearl Project.
