Her work has appeared in various media outlets including The Washington Post, NPR, the Daily Beast, Glamour, and Newsweek International.)

title: Introduction to Journalism
description: SPRING 2012 PROFESSOR ALTHEIA KNIGHT 470-02 This course will teach students the basics of newspaper, magazine and online news writing, beginning with how to assess the news value of an event or situation, how to develop story ideas and how to put together a story. Much attention will be paid to improving students芒聙聶 writing styles and developing interviewing techniques, as well as consideration of ethical issues facing journalists today. Several guest speakers from the Washington journalism community will visit. Writing assignments will include several short news assignments and one longer feature story. (Athelia Knight, a 33-year veteran of The Washington Post, has worked as a reporter and a coordinator of a program that helped train and develop young journalists.)

title: Introduction to Journalism
description: SPRING 2012 PROFESSOR ALTHEIA KNIGHT 470-02 This course will teach students the basics of newspaper, magazine and online news writing, beginning with how to assess the news value of an event or situation, how to develop story ideas and how to put together a story. Much attention will be paid to improving students芒聙聶 writing styles and developing interviewing techniques, as well as consideration of ethical issues facing journalists today. Several guest speakers from the Washington journalism community will visit. Writing assignments will include several short news assignments and one longer feature story. (Athelia Knight, a 33-year veteran of The Washington Post, has worked as a reporter and a coordinator of a program that helped train and develop young journalists.)

title: Online Production
description: SPRING 2012 SCOTT ANDERSON ENGL 471 ONLINE NEWS PRODUCTION This course gives students a firm grasp of the skills necessary to produce online news content as well as an understanding of the forces that are driving the rapidly changing and diversifying online news industry. Students will receive a mixture of practical instruction in online news writing, multimedia news production, search engine optimization techniques, blog posting and social media management. Students will also be expected to engage in critical analysis of the journalism profession, contemporary online journalism and the economic, social and technological drivers shaping online news consumption. Assignments will include individual news stories and multimedia elements. Students will also be asked to contribute to a class news blog and other group projects. Scott Anderson is Georgetown University's Director of Web Communications and has worked as a producer at CNN.com, washingtonpost.com, and PBS NewsHour.

title: Media Techniques
description: This elective is for students who will be participating in an internship at a local media outlet (print, broadcast or Internet) during the summer or fall. An alternative to the traditional one-on-one tutorial, this workshop-style course will give students an opportunity to share their experiences with other students engaged in similar journalistic pursuits. Written assignments will include a semester-long journal and ongoing occasional short pieces and a final story that students are required to submit to a professional media outlet. Students will have the opportunity, if they would like, to post their stories on a GU journalism web site. There will also be required readings about issues that are common to any journalistic endeavor such as ethics and fairness. Students are strongly encouraged to have completed 芒聙聹Introduction to Journalism芒聙聺 before taking this class. Permission of instructor is mandatory. Students need the journalism director's permission to enroll by by December 15. The journalism director will not provide letters verifying credit until the student is officially registered for the course: NO EXCEPTIONS. Please contact the instructor at feinmanb@georgetown.edu (Barbara Feinman Todd, the English Department芒聙聶s Journalism Director, has worked as an investigative researcher, a freelance reporter, a ghostwriter and a newsroom writing coach.)

title: Living Out Loud:Writ for Radio
description: Radio is an old-fashioned medium experiencing a renaissance in creativity and audience growth. It芒聙聶s an ideal home for journalism and narrative storytelling. This course will guide students through the basics of newsgathering and news presentation as it芒聙聶s done at NPR. We will address breaking news, sourcing and bias; discover strong interviewing techniques; sharpen our feature writing; consider tone and ethics, and consider other ways to create strong journalism out loud. The course will provide an overview of audio editing and production. Students should expect a heavy writing workload. This is an advanced course for students with a strong command of grammar and the written word. Kitty Eisele is the Supervising Senior Editor of Morning Edition at NPR News, where she has worked for the past fifteen years. She got her start in filmmaking with Ken Burns, and received an Emmy Award for her production work on The Civil War series.

title: Reading, Teaching, Social Reflection
description: Reading, Teaching and Social reflection is intended for students interested in teaching, and besides the usual classes, involves working for two hours one morning a week (the morning is chosen by the student) as a teacher's aide in one of three inner-city Catholic schools. These schools include Assumption School in Anacostia, Immaculate Conception School in Shaw, and Sacred Heart Bi-lingual School in Mount Pleasant, all of which have been welcoming Georgetown students for the past 13 years. The focus of the course is on teaching reading in the primary grades, and students from this class have gone on to Teach for America, the Inner-City-Teaching-Corps and to degree programs in Education at Berkeley, Columbia Teachers College and Harvard, among other places.

title: Issues in Education
description: This course is intended primarily for students in the Georgetown University Catholic Schools Project who wish to continue in their placement during the second semester, though other students who have worked actively in education can add in with the Instructor's permission. It assumes, however, a good knowledge of teaching strategies and a reasonable amount of practice in executing them, and considers such topics as the role of standardized tests, the place of whole language and of phonics, and the impact and meanings of inner-city Catholic primary education, among other topics. It requires, among other things, an extended research paper on an educational topic agreed to in consultation with the instructor, and researched in Washington libraries other than our own.

title: Comm. Tutoring: Sursum Corda
description: FALL 2010 PROFESSOR JOHN HIRSH ENGL 484 Community Tutoring: Sursum Corda This course is designed for students wishing to tutor, on Tuesdays and Thursdays evenings, K-6 children at the Sursum Corda community. Founded in 1970 by Rev. Horace McKenna, S.J. and attached to the Sursum Corda Tenants Association, the Sursum Corda-Georgetown University Tutoring Program is the oldest such program in the country established between an American university and a specific urban community. Students learn in a weekly seminar what is involved in teaching a young child to read, and work, twice a week, in the Sursum Corda Community Center, teaching young learners the language arts, and learning themselves about the community of which they are a part.

title: Sursum Corda
description: This course is designed for students wishing to tutor, on Tuesdays and Thursdays evenings, K-6 children at the Sursum Corda community. Founded in 1970 by Rev. Horace McKenna, S.J. and attached to the Sursum Corda Tenants Association, the Sursum Corda-Georgetown University Tutoring Program is the oldest such program in the country established between an American university and a specific urban community. Students learn in a weekly seminar what is involved in teaching a young child to read, and work, twice a week, in the Sursum Corda Community Center, teaching young learners the language arts, and learning themselves about the community of which they are a part.

title: Proseminar
description: This course is for students accepted into the Honors Program. Students will be expected to read and comment on critical and theoretical texts related to the honors projects, to read and comment on each other's work, and to present their own work for critique. Attendance and engagement as well as periodic written responses are required. Student should expect to complete about 25 pages of the honors thesis by the end of the course. Permission from department.

title: Chaucer
description: In this course, we will assess various critical approaches to Chaucer (especially those informed by feminism, the new historicism, the old historicism, marxism, material philology, cultural studies, post-colonial theory, and psychoanalysis) even as we employ them to read selections from his masterpiece, The Canterbury Tales, and his finest single poem -- the complex, comic, and gorgeous love story, Troilus and Criseyde. We will read Chaucer in the original Middle English, which is really quite easy; plenty of coaching and language exercises will be provided early in the semester. Our largest goal is to assess the value and the limitations of contemporary theories and methods as tools for reading literature from a distinctly distant past. The course may thus prove useful for the study of other pre-twentieth-century literatures and cultures. No previous exposure to Middle English or to critical theory is required. Written work will consist of short (5-7 p) papers during the term rather than one long paper at the end. Oral work will consist of giving a class presentation and learning to pronounce Middle English beautifully. Please address any questions about the course to Professor McNamer: mcnamers@georgetown.edu.

title: Milton and the Philosophers
description: In this course, we will read and reflect on the intersection of philosophy and literature in Milton芒聙聶s poetry, particularly in his magnificent epic poem, Paradise Lost. Although the course title is 芒聙聹Milton and Philosophy,芒聙聺 the term 芒聙聹philosophy芒聙聺 is used heuristically: we will explore what it means for a poem to be 芒聙聹philosophical,芒聙聺 and how different modes of philosophic discourse are present in, or emergent from, Milton芒聙聶s poetry. In this context, the term 芒聙聹philosophy芒聙聺 will be opened up to include a range of 芒聙聹philosophies,芒聙聺 or philosophical commitments (ontological, epistemological, etc), many of which may seem at odds with one another. Over the course of the semester, we will discuss the implications of philosophic thought in Milton芒聙聶s poetry with increasing complexity. No previous knowledge of Milton or philosophy is necessary. One substantial aim of this course is to integrate close readings of the poetry with an understanding of Milton芒聙聶s own historical, political, and theological engagements. Aside from Milton芒聙聶s poetry, primary readings will include excerpts from philosophical works; secondary literature will draw on important studies by historians, philosophers, and literary critics. In addition, you will be asked to read a variety of critical approaches to Milton, and to assess what purchase each of these theories has on Milton芒聙聶s poetry, along with its limitations (if any). Written work will include a number of short reading responses (4-6 pages) throughout the term and a final paper. Participation in class discussion is mandatory.

title: Shakespeare's Exotic Romances
description: From puckish sprites to island savages, from man-eating bears to speaking statues, Shakespeare芒聙聶s plays are populated with otherworldly or extraordinary elements that we might term exotic. Interestingly, most of these examples of exoticism occur within the context of romance, understood in two primary senses: as a genre encompassing Shakespeare芒聙聶s last four plays, and as a gendered narrative trajectory of the knight-wins-damsel variety. Rather than simply survey his last plays, this course will try to deconstruct that generic category by drawing connections to two earlier plays and asking how Shakespeare makes exotic the conventions of romance. There will be a strong theoretical/critical component, as well as some archival research. Primary texts may include A Midsummer Night芒聙聶s Dream, Othello, A Winter芒聙聶s Tale, Pericles, and The Tempest.

title: Literature of the Atlantic Empire
description: In this course, we will examine some British, Native American, Anglo-American, African-British, and American literature that grew out of the collision of cultures in the Americas in the late seventeenth and eighteenth centuries, particularly, though not exclusively, in the Chesapeake and the Caribbean. In these fictional and non-fictional narratives and poems, we will pay attention to several related matters: the ideas of civilization and savagery; the dynamics of slavery and freedom (historically, economically, and politically, and also as figures for psychological and spiritual processes); and the articulation of selfhood in a colonial context. Also, we will explore whether there are significant and growing differences between Old and New World identities, and we will chart some of the anxieties of dealing with a new American identity in the literature of the early Republic. Among the works we will likely study: Behn, Oroonoko; Defoe, Robinson Crusoe; the captivity narrative of Mary Rowlandson; narratives of indentured servitude by Moraley and Ashbridge; Sarah Knight芒聙聶s Journal; Cooke芒聙聶s Sot-Weed Factor; Byrd芒聙聶s Secret History of the Dividing Line; Olaudah Equiano芒聙聶s Interesting Narrative; Franklin芒聙聶s Autobiography; Foster芒聙聶s The Coquette; Irving芒聙聶s 芒聙聹Legend of Sleepy Hollow芒聙聺; Brown芒聙聶s Edgar Huntly; and Cooper芒聙聶s The Pioneers.

title: Romanticism and the Problem of History
description: This seminar on British literature of the romantic period (c. 1780-1837) focuses on a set of conceptual questions crucially important to writers in the age of the French Revolution: what is 芒聙聹history芒聙聺; how do things change; and how might imaginative activity affect the processes we think of as real? For many romantic-era writers, these core concerns about historical experience spun off to trigger further investigations into memory, trauma, and the nature of literary writing itself: what does poetry do, anyway? In addition to fleshing out the dynamic relationship between literary form and historical change in the period, we will also see how romantic-era works framed what have become the enduring methodological dilemmas of literary and historical scholarship: duration, succession, periodization, evidence, and mediation. The course will introduce key debates in current criticism and consider the long afterlives of romantic-era thought, going so far as to question the boundaries of 芒聙聹the romantic period芒聙聺 in the first place. But it is also designed as an introduction to the major works and authors writing during those years: Jane Austen, Joanna Baillie, William Blake, Edmund Burke, Lord Byron, Samuel Coleridge, Olaudah Equiano, William Godwin, Felicia Hemans, John Keats, Anne Radcliffe, Walter Scott, Percy and Mary Shelley, Mary Wollstonecraft, and William Wordsworth. Critical work will come from sources including James Chandler, Alan Liu, Mary Favret, Kevis Goodman, and Clifford Siskin; short theoretical interventions from Walter Benjamin, Paul de Man, G.W.F. Hegel, Georg Lukacs, and Fredric Jameson. Over the course of the term, students will produce a substantial piece of academic writing, developing an idea from conference abstract and brief position paper to an article-length seminar essay. No prior knowledge of romanticism is required.

title: Thinking Through Ghosts
description: 芒聙聹There has never been a scholar who really芒聙娄 deals with ghosts,芒聙聺 Jacques Derrida asserts in Specters of Marx. This course will examine both the preoccupation with haunting and spectrality that marks literary and cultural criticism, especially in the past two decades, and the numerous permutations of the ghostly in nineteenth-century American literature and culture. We will read theory and criticism by Derrida, Sigmund Freud, Michel Foucault, Nicolas Abraham and Maria Torok, Toni Morrison, Avery Gordon, Lynne Huffer, Terry Castle, Simon During, Molly McGarry and others. Literary texts will include works by Elizabeth Stuart Phelps, Harriet Prescott Spofford, Edgar Allan Poe, Charles W. Chesnutt, Henry James, Jr., Pauline E. Hopkins, Mary E. Wilkins Freeman, Emily Dickinson, and others, as well as writings and images from the Modern Spiritualist movement and some examples of the early ghost film. Students will write several short essays and complete a longer seminar project including an independent research component. Note: Students should have read Toni Morrison芒聙聶s Beloved before the semester begins.

title: Thinking Through Ghosts
description: 芒聙聹There has never been a scholar who really芒聙娄 deals with ghosts,芒聙聺 Jacques Derrida asserts in Specters of Marx. This course will examine both the preoccupation with haunting and spectrality that marks literary and cultural criticism, especially in the past two decades, and the numerous permutations of the ghostly in nineteenth-century American literature and culture. We will read theory and criticism by Derrida, Sigmund Freud, Michel Foucault, Nicolas Abraham and Maria Torok, Toni Morrison, Avery Gordon, Lynne Huffer, Terry Castle, Simon During, Molly McGarry and others. Literary texts will include works by Elizabeth Stuart Phelps, Harriet Prescott Spofford, Edgar Allan Poe, Charles W. Chesnutt, Henry James, Jr., Pauline E. Hopkins, Mary E. Wilkins Freeman, Emily Dickinson, and others, as well as writings and images from the Modern Spiritualist movement and some examples of the early ghost film. Students will write several short essays and complete a longer seminar project including an independent research component. Note: Students should have read Toni Morrison芒聙聶s Beloved before the semester begins.

title: Staging Anti-Slavery
description: What happens when we approach abolitionist literature芒聙聰fiction and non-fiction芒聙聰as performance? Late eighteenth-century and nineteenth-century black and white women developed sophisticated strategies for performing their abolitionist activism in poems, short stories, essays, and longer narratives, as well as in plays and speeches. We芒聙聶ll examine these strategies for defining the relationship between the self and the other, the citizen and sufferer, in eighteenth- and nineteenth-century women芒聙聶s reform literature. We芒聙聶ll begin by studying the 芒聙聹performative芒聙聺 categories through which we traditionally understand the self芒聙聰categories such as race, gender, and American-ness芒聙聰analyzing the way in which these categories depend upon repeated behaviors that, paradoxically, are never duplicated. We芒聙聶ll focus on the work of black and white female abolitionists from 1770 to 1865, analyzing their disparate avenues toward publication as well as their actual anti-slavery essays, speeches, letters, poems, short stories, plays, slave narratives, and autobiographical novels. How did these women perform their abolitionist activism, in print and onstage, and what can we learn from their practices? Our theoretical readings and archival adventures will help us illuminate the women芒聙聶s performances and their legacies in a world that still sustains the global trafficking of persons and fosters what many call the 芒聙聹new anti-slavery.芒聙聺 Our readings will include works by Phillis Wheatley, Suzanna Rowson, Elizabeth Chandler, Sarah Forten, Maria W. Stewart, Frances Ellen Watkins Harper, and a host of lesser-known anti-slavery lecturers and essayists.

title: Dickens
description: A close examination of three novels in which Dickens experiments with the genre of fictional autobiography: David Copperfield [May 1849 芒聙聯 Nov. 1850], Bleak House [March 1852 芒聙聯 Sept. 1853], Great Expectations [1861]. Seminar participants will take turns presenting the conclusions of their papers which we will all then discuss. Our approach will be eclectic, welcoming a wide range of theoretical approaches, most of them proposed by the seminar participants. Along with a fair amount of reading from the novels each week we will take a look at a couple of helpful essays in criticism from JSTOR and other online resources.

title: Aestheticism and Decadence
description: This class will focus on the development in late Victorian Britain of the literary Aesthetic and Decadent movements, from their antecedents in Pre-Raphaelitism to their late-century expression in the works of such writers as Walter Pater, Oscar Wilde, Aubrey Beardsley, Charles Swinburne, and others. While this will be primarily a literature course, we will also consider the ways that the visual and architectural arts contributed to the movements. Throughout, we will read the primary texts in relationship to the critical and theoretical approaches, including gender, sexuality, religion, class, and nation, that have been brought to bear on this topic.

title: Transnational Modernisms
description: In what ways does the return of modernity in contemporary discussions of globalization reshape our understanding of literary modernism? How has the renewed interest in modernity unsettled the opposition between postmodernism and 芒聙聹classical芒聙聺 modernism, and how has this revived an interest in the modernist project? What is this new modernist project, and how did it emerge? To what extent is it part of analogous process through which the historic avant-garde grew out of metropolitan encounters between immigrants and mainstream culture? How these encounters produce breaks with traditional culture in the form of dissident groups, or were they absorbed into the dominant culture of a succeeding metropolitan generation? Finally, how does the idea of 芒聙聵alternative modernisms, 芒聙聵delayed芒聙聶 modernisms, or 芒聙聵liquid芒聙聶 modernisms, transform our understanding of modernism芒聙聶s current revival? Examining the genealogies of modernism as it traverses imperial, colonial, and postcolonial cultures, we shall analyze how postcolonial literature has unsettled the conventional distinction between modernism and postmodernism in theoretical accounts by Appadurai, Gaonkar, Huyssen, Jameson, Latour, Said, and Spivak. Our primary readings will likely include texts by Zafer ?enocak, Yoko Tawada, Tayeb Salih, Mahmoud Darwish, J. M. Coetzee, Michael Ondaatje, Laszlo Kraszahorkai, Roberto Bola脙卤o, and Chris Abani. We shall explore contemporary problems of movement, migration, displacement, violence, war, genocide, and trauma. This seminar will involve extensive primary and secondary reading. It will include one long research essay, and one class presentation.

title: The Postmodern American Novel
description: This course will explore the postmodern novel in the mid to late 20th Century (and early in the 21st Century) in the North and South America. Through an exploration of primary texts and theoretical contexts, our goal will be to come to a clear, articulate understanding of postmodernism (in all its various forms) and its influence on our contemporary literary tradition. The course will involve a significant amount of reading in both literature and critical theory, particularly poststructuralist traditions that often paralleled postmodern thinking. The tentative reading list (subject to change) includes novels by Vladimir Nabokov, Gabriel Garc脙颅a M脙隆rquez, Thomas Pynchon, Kathy Acker, Margaret Atwood, Manuel Puig, Ana Castillo, Anne Michaels, Mark Z. Danielewski, and David Foster Wallace. Theoretical texts will be culled from the works of Jean-Fran脙搂ois Lyotard, Fredric Jameson, Jacques Derrida, Roland Barthes, Ihab Hassan, Brian McHale, and Linda Hutcheon, amongst others. Students will be expected to lead seminar discussion, produce weekly writing, and complete a final research project.

title: Multimedia Spectatorship
description: Spring 2012 The term 芒聙聹spectator芒聙聺 refers to the ideal viewer a movie insinuates and encourages us to identify with through formal and narrative cues. Thus spectatorship theory aims to account for how we experience media and how media try to guide or shape that experience. While spectatorship theory developed disciplinary coherence in the 1970s around a set of French, British, and later American approaches to the study of film, today we need to address how viewers understand motion pictures on the variety of platforms they currently occupy芒聙聰from celluloid and home video to fixed and mobile computer screens. This course will commandeer the historical insights of film studies芒聙聶 theories of spectatorship to guide future research on the many motion picture experiences waiting for us in the twenty-first century. It offers an introduction to both film theory and new media studies, as well as the opportunity to contribute to lively debates happening in the field right now. Together we will ask a focused set of questions about how viewers engage and are engaged by the media they watch, but I encourage you to extend that inquiry to include other time-based media, art, and literature. Course materials will focus on feature-length and short films as well as digital media productions and readings from film and new media theory. These will include essays by Karl Marx, Sigmund Freud, Jacques Lacan, Laura Mulvey, Christian Metz, Jean-Louis Baudry, bell hooks, Teresa de Lauretis, and selections from books by Anne Friedberg, Lev Manovich, Mark Hansen, Dudley Andrew, and D. N. Rodowick, among others. This course will require weekly film screenings as well as one oral presentation (with attendant writing) and a longer research paper for which students will be asked to submit an advance abstract and annotated bibliography.

title: Race in American Literature
description: In this course, we will examine Anglo-American and African American narratives from 1845 to 1940 in order to understand how race along the color line has been constructed, narrated, employed, and deployed in America. We will be particularly interested in how the binary of black and white signifies and symbolizes social and cultural conflicts and interests as depicted in American literature. What is literary whiteness? What is literary blackness? What strategies are used to construct race in American literature? Additionally, what role does race play in the interpretation of American literature? Readings may include, among others, works by Douglass, Hawthorne, Larsen, Fitzgerald, Wright, Faulkner and Morrison. Students will read and apply literary and critical theory. Students should expect to participate in close and engaged readings, active class discussions and presentations, and multiple critical writings opportunities. Students are expected to produce a publishable r! esearched essay.

title: Intro to Critical Theory
description: While the phrase 芒聙聹critical theory芒聙聺 was initially coined in the mid-20th century to name the work done by mostly Marxist intellectuals to make more practical sense of how both high- and mass-cultural production participated in either the legitimization or subversion of the operations of industrialized capital, it has since that moment come to mean more generally the history (initially Western, more recently post-colonial, and even global) of intellectual attempts to make sense of, and even to intervene into, the operations of culture in all its most salient contexts (political, economic, social, historical, technological). This course will survey the most influential figures and statements in that latter construction of 芒聙聹critical theory,芒聙聺 but with a keen interest in how, and why, that latter construction came to subsume the former. While no one problem, question, or theme will govern the selection of topics or readings, or even guide most discussions, the course will devote significant attention to the fate of critical theory, and of intellectual labor more generally, in what many have been calling our post-historical, post-cultural and certainly post-theoretical moment. Course readings will consist primarily of essay-length selections collected in at least one anthology (most likely Rivkin and Ryan芒聙聶s Literary Theory: an Anthology) and across a short list of representative monographs of fairly recent vintage (candidates include Fredric Jameson芒聙聶s The Cultural Turn, Judith Butler芒聙聶s Excitable Speech, and George Y脙潞dice芒聙聶s The Expediency of Culture). Course assignments will include one oral presentation, a short paper related to the presentation, and a longer end-of-term paper/project.

title: Marxism and Culture
description: This course provides neither a comprehensive survey nor historical overview of the 芒聙聹theoretical practice芒聙聺 of Marxist cultural production. Instead, it will offer a selective probe into some of the key theoretical issues associated with the Marxist cultural tradition around the world. We will explore the realm of culture in its varied relationships to social change in diverse contexts. We will read representative statements from Marx and Engels from the mid-nineteenth century including the Communist Manifesto, selections from Capital Vol. I and the Grundrisse, but will concentrate on more recent culture and criticism as it relates to struggles for democracy and human rights post World War II. Our goal will be to situate contemporary marxist literary criticism within broader contexts of literary study. Texts will include literature, theory and cultural criticism. Texts: The Marx-Engels Reader, ed. Robert Tucker Literary Theory: An Introduction, Terry Eagleton One short paper, one research-based prospectus, and one longer collaborative research paper.

title: Approaches to Teaching Writing
description: This course offers a practical introduction to the history, theories, and issues that shape the field of Writing Studies. The systematic study of the production of texts 芒聙聯 rhetoric and composition --began as a 芒聙聹school芒聙聺 discipline, and these origins are preserved in the title of our course. Written assignments and class discussion will focus on teaching and course design, with particular attention to the integration of reading and writing. Weekly readings will assist you to design writing assignments and to theorize your own practice in composing assignments for your courses. But the teachable parts of writing need not be reduced to techniques, whether of close reading or mechanisms of process, skill, and craft. In the words of theorist Walter Ong, 芒聙聹Writing is a technology that restructures thought.芒聙聺 We will explore issues of cultural and critical literacy as these concepts inform the teaching of writing. By definition, the graduate seminar offers the opportunity to enter an ongoing conversation among scholars in a particular field as well as a forum for intellectual exchange among peers. Students will be expected to take an active role in the course as evidenced by close, critical attention to the readings; contributions to discussion; organized seminar presentations of their research; and engaged, informed writing. Bulletin: A practical introduction to the history, theories, and issues that shape the field of Writing Studies and inform the teaching of writing.

title: Feminist Lit/Cult Theory
description: At this juncture, the field called 芒聙聹feminist literary/cultural theory芒聙聺 has a paradoxical status in the academy, at once moribund and thriving. In their representative late 1990s debate over 芒聙聹What Ails Feminist Criticism?,芒聙聺 Susan Gubar and Robyn Wiegman芒聙聰two 芒聙聵stars芒聙聶 representing different generations within feminist studies芒聙聰laid bare decades-long conflicts that had been brewing over the history and future of the field, including: various modes of identity politics, particularly gatekeeping by white heterosexual U.S. women scholars; the role of critical theory within feminist critical practices; the relationship of gender study to the burgeoning field of queer studies; and the increasing interdisciplinarity of feminist methodologies as well as objects of study. That debate, and others like it, prompted further dialogue about the vitality and relevance of feminist literary and cultural theory: anachronistic, if limited to outmoded notions of 芒聙聹the literary芒聙聺? Or, as Weigman argues, finding 芒聙聹a range of political and intellectual possibilities for feminism in 芒聙娄 postcolonial, U.S. ethnic, and poststructuralist practices芒聙聺? We will explore this conflict by focusing on the thorny category of 芒聙聹women芒聙聶s writing,芒聙聺 which certainly includes conventional literary genres but which we will also broaden to encompass hybrid genres like the graphic novel as well as various media texts created by women. Feminist literary criticism and theory brought the study of women writers into the mainstream of English as a discipline, and despite raising myriad controversies , research and courses on women芒聙聶s literature remain highly popular. Focusing on this aspect of the field芒聙聰theories of women芒聙聶s writing and how to read it--serves as one revealing in-road to its history, aims, contributions, and problems. After exploring key concepts, issues, and debates, the course will take a two-pronged approach: we芒聙聶ll read a series of cultural texts that now occupy a central芒聙聰some might say 芒聙聹canonical芒聙聺芒聙聰place in the evolving feminist critical archive while simultaneously examining diverse critical/theoretical approaches to each text (a mix of early and more recent work) as a way of charting the field芒聙聶s development over time and assessing its current 芒聙聹health.芒聙聺 (See course texts for a starting list of authors and critic-theorists.) Additionally, students will write weekly blog postings, one short paper, one seminar paper, and make one substantial class presentation.

title: Experimental Fiction
description: In this advanced writing laboratory, we will read key texts of 20th and 21st century experimental fiction. The first half of the course examines modern and postmodern experiments; the second half examines how these same experiments get reworked, extended, and contested in contemporary literature. Like scientists in a laboratory, we will conduct experiments, using the work of previous writers as points of departure (and, occasionally, return). We will explore existing ideas, practices, and experiments in fiction through fiction: through the act of creating our own experimental texts. Equal emphasis will be placed on theory, practice, and praxis. A concern of our praxis will be the complex, contested relationship between theory and practice in experimental literature. We will also consider how, why, and where experimental fiction intersects with and departs from other praxes, such as poetry, visual art, and performance. This class is largely student-led, and demands enormous commitment from its members. This class also presumes a basic knowledge of the craft of fiction writing and requires an adventurous, inquisitive spirit. Play (and rigor) will be essential to this process. Be prepared to break with convention as we excavate, innovate, and create. Necessarily, our class will itself be experimental, improvisational, and unprecedented. Prepare for saints.

title: After the Rhetorical Turn: Composition Pedagogy and Writing In Situs
description: Over the last fifty years or so, scholars of many disciplines have begun to pay attention to language in a way that has constituted a fundamental epistemological shift. This shift, often referred to as the 芒聙聹rhetorical turn,芒聙聺 has wrought changes in many fields of study, none more perhaps than composition pedagogy. Whereas before the rhetorical turn, composition pedagogy implicitly relied on the notion that 芒聙聹good writing芒聙聺 was a universal and universally recognizable phenomenon, the field now boasts many approaches to the teaching of writing that proceed from one shared principle: that writing is never universal芒聙聰indeed, it is always situated. In this course, we will explore different approaches to teaching writing as a situated (or contextualized) activity, including genre theory, public writing, service-learning programs, and Writing Across the Curriculum (WAC) and Writing in the Disciplines (WID) programs. On the other hand, we will also consider recent texts that resist this shift, such as Stanley Fish芒聙聶s How to Write a Sentence and Gerald Graff芒聙聶s Clueless in Academe and They Say/I Say. Course assignments will include short weekly writing assignments, one oral presentation, and a final project of significant length.

title: Literacy and the Law
description: Literature and the Law This seminar explores the relationships between literacy and law. The course will explore learning theories and practices regarding emergent literacy and examine their legal and social implications. Readings will be drawn from three areas: educational theory and practice, law, and children芒聙聶s literature. The course will focus upon whole language and other approaches which now inform literacy instruction in America. Legal implications to be considered may include restrictions on literacy due to slavery and educational disadvantage, statutory interpretation, equal protection and diversity, freedom of speech, school finance, discipline, and English as a second language. In order to provide practical experience in emergent literacy, students are required to participate in a practicum of at least one hour per week in which they work with a student in emergent literacy. The practicum may be a tutorial or literacy program sponsored by the Law Center (normally, the beginning reading program at Sursum Corda community near the Law Center) or some other arrangement approved by the instructors. Writing requirements include both a journal and short paper. The seminar is also open to graduate students in the Department of English, with permission of the instructor and Director of Graduate Studies. Cross-listed with LAWJ-315-05

title: Fundamentals of Finance
description: This course is a survey course, designed to explore the major concepts in corporate finance. Topics include Time Value of Money; Net Present Value/Internal Rate of Return; Valuation of Stocks and Bonds; Financial Statements/Ratios; Managing Growth; Discounted Cash Flow; Risk, Return and the Cost of Capital; Capital Structure; Dividends; Raising Capital/Going Public; Mergers and Acquisitions; and Corporate Restructuring. The course focuses on applications of finance to assist students outside the Business School to understand financial statements, apply financial analytical techniques, and learn the basics of the capital markets. Note: students will not be expected to have prior knowledge of course concepts.

title: Business Financial Management
description: This basic course in finance covers the functions of financial managers, financial markets, and financial analysis tools, and corporate financial decision making. It provides an introduction to cost of capital, investment analysis, capital budgeting and the valuation of securities.

title: Advanced Financial Management
description: This course provides an extensive coverage of corporate finance theory and the applicability of this theory for the financial manager. Topics include: capital budgeting under uncertainty; the relevance of capital structure decisions on security valuation and riskiness; the theory and practice of dividend policy; implications of financial market efficiency for management; risks and rewards of international financial markets; security valuation including the use of option pricing models; the market for corporate control; risk management through the use of options and future contracts; financial engineering; corporate restructuring and bankruptcy. Cases are used. Prerequisite: Business Financial Management.

title: Real Estate Finance
description: The course is structured to give the student an introduction to the principles of real estate with an emphasis in finance. Topics include real estate and the economy, financing residential properties, sales contracts, leases, appraisal, land use controls, and the real estate development process.

title: Investment Banking
description: The primary goal of the course is to provide a broad overview of the investment banking business and mergers and acquisitions. The first half of the class will deal with general investment banking topics, including the structure and direction of the securities industry and its participants, the securities offering process (focusing on initial public offerings), convertible and high yield debt securities, spin offs and carveouts. The second half of the course will examine mergers and acquisitions. Topics to be covered include deal design and structure, valuation of synergies, deal tactics, hostile takeovers, and private equity. The course will have a case orientation.

title: Principles of Investment
description: Course Objective: The objective of this course is to teach finance students about investment theory and practice. The course will utilize a combination of theoretical studies, case applications and a portfolio project to teach students about: 1) the structure of the capital markets; 2) theories and practice in portfolio management; 3) asset pricing theories used to analyze securities; 4) equity and debt securities; and 5) derivative instruments. The course will focus primarily on the U.S. markets, although we will touch briefly on international investments. This course will provide students with information and skills needed to succeed in careers in asset management, corporate finance, research, sales or trading.

title: Fixed Income Securities
description: The objective of this course is to provide an on overview of fixed income markets and securities. The course will be useful to students who are interested in pursuing careers in corporate finance, banking or investment management. The course will provide highly practical information as well as theoretical information regarding US Government and foreign government securities, corporate bonds, municipal bonds, asset backed securities, hybrid securities, bond funds, and strategies related to investing in and issuing fixed income securities. During each class, in addition to discussing the theory critical to understanding the fixed income markets, we will discuss the current market environment and real-life decisions that finance professionals must make in this environment.

title: Global Financial Markets and Institutions
description: A study of the flow of funds through the domestic and international markets and institutions. Emphasis will be on the roles of money and capital markets and financial institutions. Topics will include interest rate determination, financial intermediation, regulation of financial services, and the role of the Federal Reserve and other central banks.

title: International Finance
description: The course addresses key issues of international financial management. It studies the international financial environment as well as corporations' strategic alternatives and operational practices. The following topics are among those covered: foreign exchange markets and the management of foreign exchange exposure; capital budgeting in multinational corporations; cross-border lending by banks; trade finance; and foreign direct investment. The reading materials and class discussions include both theoretical analyses and cases.

title: Corporate Governance & Valuation
description: Recent corporate scandals have put the spotlight on the importance of effective corporate governance. This course will examine the financial aspects of corporate governance and its effect on value creation for shareholders. Various readings and case studies will be used to provide students with real-life examples of the key elements of corporate governance. The course is geared toward undergraduate business students who are majoring in Finance. This course is ideal for students who are considering careers in corporate finance, investment banking, auditing, or law. To be successful in this course a student should possess strong analytical and communication skills. The emphasis of the course will be on the impact of corporate governance decisions on the value of the firm and value creation for shareholders of publicly traded firms. The course will apply valuation techniques to case studies involving corporate governance decisions within firms.

title: Derivatives and Financial Markets
description: This course program is designed to expand participants' understanding of derivative-related financial instruments (forwards, futures and options) and their use in investment and corporate financial management.

title: Entreprenuerial Finance
description: The course would seek to examine entrepreneurial firms and challenges in financing such firms. The course would be heavily based on cases, many of which would be based on young firms although some would be about more established firms. An entrepreneur needs to understand how to identify a business opportunity as well as how to marshal resources (especially capital) to and exploit the business opportunity. The course would reinforce skills needed to analyze a business (with a strong emphasis on valuation techniques). The course would then focus on issues relevant for financing of entrepreneurial ventures. This would include understanding financial institutions (Angel investors, Venture Capitalists, and Banks) that provide risk capital for such firms. The course would conclude by examining the harvesting of entrepreneurial ventures where by the entrepreneur and the financiers realize the returns for their effort.

title: Fundamentals of Finance
description: This course is a survey course, designed to explore the major concepts in corporate finance. Topics include Time Value of Money; Net Present Value/Internal Rate of Return; Valuation of Stocks and Bonds; Financial Statements/Ratios; Managing Growth; Discounted Cash Flow; Risk, Return and the Cost of Capital; Capital Structure; Dividends; Raising Capital/Going Public; Mergers and Acquisitions; and Corporate Restructuring. The course focuses on applications of finance to assist students outside the Business School to understand financial statements, apply financial analytical techniques, and learn the basics of the capital markets. Note: students will not be expected to have prior knowledge of course concepts.

title: International Finance
description: This course extends the principles of financial management and investments to the international context. The course will be concerned with understanding the international finance environment and the implication of exchange rates on firm value and investments. This includes the understanding of the determinants of exchange rates and the international currency markets, including institutional structures and underlying economics. This section includes hedging practices of international firms. This course is designed from the viewpoint of someone that participates in the international financial environment, whether this person is a CFO, General Manager or Treasurer of an international firm, an investment banker, an investor, or a consultant.

title: Financial Markets
description: Additional content will be added soon to reflect the changes to the new curriculum. MBA Core In FINC 551, and FINC 557 we shall take the perspective of a high-level executive (CEO, CFO) and we shall focus extensively on a specific scarce resource which economists call CAPITAL and you and I call cash or money. We know cash is scarce - after all we would all like to have more cash/capital. Since there is not enough capital to go around - how is it (and how should it) be allocated is the central issue underlying this course. This capital allocation takes place at two levels - First, capital is allocated by capital markets across the wider economy (e.g. corporations such as Starbucks, IBM, Gap etc., as well as Federal, State, and Local governments). At the second level, capital is allocated to different projects within a corporation (e.g. Ford's decision to invest in a Chinese plant). Thus as a business manager you would be faced with two types of decisions where knowledge of Finance is critical. 芒聙垄How to gain access to capital? (This decision is the focus of FINANCE I) 芒聙垄How to invest available capital? (This decision is the focus of FINANCE II)

title: Investment Analysis
description: This course focuses on the application of investment theory and contemporary research to the practice of equity securities analysis and portfolio management. Topics include valuation of equity securities, investment management for private clients, institutional equity portfolio management, hedge funds and alternative investments, and portfolio performance evaluation. The primary focus of this course is to demonstrate the relationship between investment theory and the practice of equity portfolios management.

title: Investment Banking
description: The primary goal of the course is to provide a broad overview of the investment banking business. Topics will include current industry issues and trends, the basics of valuation, the securities offering process, a review of various types of debt securities, and an overview of leveraged buyouts and private equity.

title: Derivatives and Financial Markets
description: This course develops understanding of the basic derivative-related financial instruments (forwards, swaps, futures and at-the-money options), and their use in transforming and managing risky investments and projects. Objectives are (1) to provide a basic understanding of derivatives practice and use in financial markets, (2) to provide practical and simple investment and corporate financial management strategies using derivatives and (3) to allow students to apply these concepts and skills to meet investment and corporate finance objectives, using a series of examples.

title: Corporate Finance
description: This course focuses on aspects of corporate financing decisions including cost of capital, capital structure, dividend policy, and an introduction to the use of derivatives for risk management. Emphasis is placed on the application of finance principles to business cases. Prerequisite: FINC 551. MBA 1st years students only.

title: Real Estate Finance
description: This course covers the financing of residential and commercial real estate from the standpoint of both equity and debt investors. Major topics include valuation of income-producing real estate, analysis of markets & risk factors unique to real estate & development, mortgage financing, secondary mortgage markets, land development and construction loans, legal and regulatory issues.

title: Fixed Income Securities
description: This course provides highly practical information as well as theoretical information regarding US Treasury securities, corporate bonds, municipal bonds, asset backed securities, hybrid securities, bond funds, and strategies related to investing in and issuing fixed income securities. We will also discuss the current market environment and real-life decisions that finance professionals must make in this environment.

title: Applied Project Finance
description: Project Finance, that is, non-recourse or limited recourse financing, is one of the most exciting growth areas in finance today. New financing structures, access to private equity and innovative credit enhancements make project finance the preferred alternative in large-scale natural resource and infrastructure projects. The class will define project financing, differentiate it from traditional corporate lending, and explore the types of instruments and transactions that can be used in such financings. The legal, political and environmental considerations involved in project financings will also be evaluated. The course will be taught from a practitioner's view and will emphasize international aspects of project finance. The course will be taught by Rusty Brewer and Laura Hegedus. Rusty and Laura are attorneys at the law firm of Cooley LLP. Biographical information of Rusty and Laura can be found at //www.cooley.com.

title: Corporate Risk Management
description: Corporate Risk Management is the sequel to Advanced Corporate Finance. Topics include: extensions of capital budgeting, mergers and acquisition, and valuation into an international context; foundations of option pricing and application of option pricing to capital budgeting and security valuation including real options; measuring and managing a firm芒聙聶s risk exposures. Topics in international financial markets are studied in depth from a managerial perspective with an emphasis on hedging exchange rate risk.

title: Real Estate Financial Markets
description: This course will cover highly specialized topics in real estate finance, underwriting and market analysis, including: Advanced financial modeling/underwriting techniques for real estate investments & Excel spreadsheet analysis; advanced issues in real estate capital markets, risk management & insurance; international real estate investments; corporate real estate applications; advanced issues in real estate taxation and entity structuring; regulatory & legal issues in real estate investments; regulatory response to 2008 credit markets crisis & role of real estate finance. 芒聙垄 Capital markets for real estate are similar to other asset classes, in the sense that equity and debt interests are valued using similar methods of cash flow discounting and multiples. The underlying risk factors of owning real estate (or taking real estate as loan collateral) involve some similar factors such as market, interest rate, term and credit risk, but also require nuanced understanding of risks somewhat unique real estate - particularly cyclical supply/demand dynamics which may vary widely in terms of capital markets (cyclicality of financing and liquidity) as well as geography (cyclicality of supply/demand for real estate assets). 芒聙垄 The course will examine the process of financing commercial real estate from the standpoint of both debt and equity investors. Consideration will be given to asset valuation methodologies, debt and equity structures, examination of special risks and risk management techniques. Public market instruments, such as Real Estate Investment Trusts (REITs) and commercial mortgage backed securities (CMBS), will be examined as well as private equity instruments such as limited partnership interests. 芒聙垄 Each week will mix class lecture with analysis of case studies, with students leading class discussion on group case study assignments. Note: you are responsible for reading ALL case studies, not just those assigned to your group. I reserve the right to deduct points from your grade if you are unprepared for the case discussion in any given week.

title: Alternative Investments
description: Alternative Investments have grown tremendously as an asset class in recent years and yet are not well-understood. This course will focus mainly on two classes of Alternative Investments: Hedge Funds and Private Equity. It will cover hedge fund operations and strategies, investment and valuation, risk management, performance measurement, and regulatory issues. The course will provide an understanding of the private equity market, market participants, financial strategies, value creation, deal structuring, and exit strategies. We will also discuss the current state of the industry and its role in global capital markets including the entrance of new participants, such as, sovereign funds. Lectures will be supplemented by case studies and a number of guest speakers from industry and regulatory organizations.

title: Venture Capital
description: Venture Capital" is unique form of capital and the impact of this source of capital is considerably larger than its size. It is frequently cited as one of the main reasons why entrepreneurial activity is much higher in the United States compared to the other parts of the world. On the other hand popular press has demonized venture capitalist as 芒聙聹Vulture Capitalist芒聙聺 seeking to hoodwink hard working entrepreneurs in giving away their companies for cheap. This course aims to increase students芒聙聶 understanding of how these organizations are structured and operate, why they take the form they do, and what special challenges and opportunities this industry face.

title: Measuring & Creating Value
description: This course focuses on core principles of value creation for businesses with the bulk of the course involving the detailed mechanics of how to measure firm value. Students will learn different valuation methods and why value is a relevant metric for managers to examine. The course will conclude with a broad overview of the types of decisions which managers can implement to create and sustain value for their shareholders.

title: Corporate Restructuring
description: This Seminar in Corporate Restructuring is led by Senior Managing Directors of FTI Consulting and supplemented with the experience of various guest lecturers from the business community. The class explores the strategic, financial and operational skills critical to successfully improving performance during periods of significant transition including business turnarounds, distressed company sales and bankruptcy.

title: Financial Markets:  Institutions, Innovations and Regulations
description: This course aims to develop a deep understanding about how real-life financial markets function and how banks, hedge funds and financial institutions profit from a changing technological, competitive and regulatory environment. The emphasis of the course is on how actual financial markets function. The course will integrate and strengthen your knowledge about financial markets in three ways: (1) interacting with lots of experienced practitioners that will be coming to class as guest speakers, (2) discussing how real financial markets operate, though the use of case studies (rather than solely by abstract theoretical models) and (3) delivering hands-on expertise and analyzing the latest developments in the market.

title: Financial Services, Management and Regulations
description: This course focuses on financial analysis and regulation of insured American depository institutions, the Federal Reserve, the European Central Bank, the Bank of Japan, the World Bank and the International Monetary Fund. Key balance sheet and income ratios will be employed to analyze bank assets, liabilities, capital, and risk management. Financial institutions芒聙聶 cost structures, revenue models, and competitive markets are employed to examine the implications of regulation and optimal decision making.

title: Private Client Wealth Management
description: A 2010 CFA Research Foundation monograph defines private wealth management as 芒聙聹the investment management specialization focused on high-net-worth individuals and families.芒聙聺 (Jennings, Horan, and Reichenstein, 2010) Private wealth management requires comprehensive and customized solutions to a client芒聙聶s complex investment and personal financial planning goals. Private wealth managers must evaluate a client芒聙聶s complete financial picture in a well-integrated fashion that incorporates each client芒聙聶s total financial picture, including such concerns as asset and liability management, tax and estate planning, retirement planning, philanthropic objectives, and behavioral issues. This course focuses on the application of investment theory and contemporary research to the private wealth arena. Topics include asset allocation for private clients, after-tax investment management for individual investors, investing in mutual funds, retirement planning, portfolio performance evaluation, and investing in a variety of alternative investments such as hedge funds, insurance annuities, structured notes, master limited partnerships, real estate, and private equity.

title: Financial Engineering in a Time of Crisis
description: In this course students will learn how to apply the tools of corporate finance to analyze a series of market interventions that were made by the Government during the recent financial crisis. We will review the impact of the crisis on specific markets; the various alternatives considered by policy makers in response and then assess the actual programs. There will be an emphasis on developing an understanding of the objectives, the detailed financial design, and the outcome of each program. Key to understanding whether a policy approach results in 芒聙聹success芒聙聺 will be developing an ability to recognize how to balance the often competing interests of the marketplace, politics, and public policy. While this class has been designed to complement Phil Swagel芒聙聶s class on the macro-economic causes of the crises, Professor Swagel芒聙聶s class is not a pre-requisite. We will be digging in at the micro level to understand specific market dynamics, financial engineering choices faced by policy makers and the pro and cons of the numerous programs they designed.

title: Introductory French I
description: This sequence is intended for students with little or no previous background in French. Through an audiovisual introduction to French culture, emphasis is placed on the active use of the spoken language with diversified oral and written exercises, dictation, and conversational practice. Required laboratory.

title: Introductory French I
description: This sequence is intended for students with little or no previous background in French. Through an audiovisual introduction to French culture, emphasis is placed on the active use of the spoken language with diversified oral and written exercises, dictation, and conversational practice. Required laboratory. This course is open to SFS-Qatar students only.

title: Introductory French II
description: This sequence is intended for students with little or no previous background in French. Through an audiovisual introduction to French culture, emphasis is placed on the active use of the spoken language with diversified oral and written exercises, dictation, and conversational practice. Required laboratory.

title: Introductory French II
description: This sequence is intended for students with little or no previous background in French. Through an audiovisual introduction to French culture, emphasis is placed on the active use of the spoken language with diversified oral and written exercises, dictation, and conversational practice. Required laboratory. This course is open to SFS-Qatar students only.

title: Intensive Basic French
description: This sequence is designed for beginners who desire intensive instruction in oral communication as well as in basic grammar and writing skills. Pronunciation drills, grammatical patterns, aural comprehension exercises, acquisition of common vocabulary, and conversational practice are integrated within an audio-visual introduction to French culture.

title: Intermediate French I
description: Intermediate French I (FREN 021) is the continuation of Introductory French II (FREN 002). Students who enroll in this course have typically taken Introductory French II (FREN 002) or have been placed in this course by means of the French Department Placement Test. This course is based on the Intermediate French method 芒聙聹Ensemble芒聙聺 (John Wiley & Sons, Inc.), which consists of a grammar text (with an optional workbook) and a reader. Additional audio-visual material will be provided by the instructor. This course will help you develop and strengthen all four language skills (speaking, listening, reading, and writing) and expand on your every-day vocabulary.

title: Intermediate French I
description: In developing their speaking, listening, reading, and writing skills, students have the option of either continuing and diversifying work with the audiovisual program introduced in the Basic French program, or embarking on a more traditionally text-based program of intermediate study. This course is open to SFS-Qatar students only.

title: Intermediate French II
description: Intermediate French II (FREN 022) is the continuation of Intermediate French I (FREN 021). Students who enroll in this course have typically taken Intermediate French I (FREN 021) or have been placed in this course by means of the French Department Placement Test. This course is based on the Intermediate French method 芒聙聹Ensemble芒聙聺 (John Wiley & Sons, Inc.), which consists of a grammar text (with an optional workbook) and a reader. Additional audio-visual material will be provided by the instructor. This course will help you develop and strengthen all four language skills (speaking, listening, reading, and writing) and expand on your every-day vocabulary.

title: Intermediate French II
description: In developing their speaking, listening, reading, and writing skills, students have the option of either continuing and diversifying work with the audiovisual program introduced in the Basic French program, or embarking on a more traditionally text-based program of intermediate study. This course is open to SFS-Qatar students only.

title: Intensive Intermediate French
description: This sequence is designed for intermediate language learners who desire intensive instruction in phonology and oral communication as well as in basic grammar and writing skills. Pronunciation drills, grammatical patterns, aural comprehension exercises, acquisition of common vocabulary, and conversational practice are integrated within an audio-visual introduction to French culture. Prerequisite: Basic French (FREN-011 or FREN- 002) or placement.

title: Knights of Old & Harry Potter
description: The objective of this course is to explore the medievalism of J.K. Rowling芒聙聶s Harry Potter novels. To do this we need to go back to their medieval antecedents in the 12th-15th centuries, which will allow us to contrast and compare the old and the new. We will read masterpieces of imaginative storytelling from French, German, and English medieval literature in addition to selected Harry Potter volumes, but we will also consult Plato and Joseph Campbell. The old and the new are linked thematically in that they are all narratives about growing up and finding one芒聙聶s identity: a complex, mysterious, and sometimes arduous process that the hero/heroine experiences as a magical world where the natural laws governing human existence are suspended, the unexpected is bound to occur, and marvels are reserved for the chosen few. The readings and discussion are in English. Counts as English Department Gateway equivalent.

title: Knights of Old & Harry Potter
description: The objective of this course is to explore the medievalism of J.K. Rowling芒聙聶s Harry Potter novels. To do this we need to go back to their medieval antecedents in the 12th-15th centuries, which will allow us to contrast and compare the old and the new. We will read masterpieces of imaginative storytelling from French, German, and English medieval literature in addition to selected Harry Potter volumes, but we will also consult Plato and Joseph Campbell. The old and the new are linked thematically in that they are all narratives about growing up and finding one芒聙聶s identity: a complex, mysterious, and sometimes arduous process that the hero/heroine experiences as a magical world where the natural laws governing human existence are suspended, the unexpected is bound to occur, and marvels are reserved for the chosen few. The readings and discussion are in English.

title: Advanced French I
description: A general survey of French civilization is integrated with continued work on mastery of all requisite language skills. Readings and compositions focus on material from the French media. The three hours of class instruction are supplemented by a required one-hour conversation section each week.

title: Advanced French II
description: A general survey of French civilization is integrated with continued work on mastery of all requisite language skills. Readings and compositions focus on material from the French media. The three hours of class instruction are supplemented by a required one-hour conversation section each week.

title: Advanced French II: Contemporary Civilization
description: This course is a continuation of Advanced I but qualified students who have not taken Advanced I at SFS/Q may be enrolled with permission of the instructor. This course prepares students for gateway and upper-division courses in French. Students will work on improving their oral and writing skills in French through a review of grammar, intensive writing, a study of vocabulary and idioms through magazines and TV news program, as well as the close reading of an integralnovel. They will also gain insights into contemporary issues concerning the French speaking world through a regular reading of the Francophone weekly L芒聙聶Express, Le Point, Le Nouvel Observateur and Jeune Afrique, watching the boroadcated news on France 2, and reading Amin Maalouf芒聙聶s novel L脙漏on l芒聙聶Africain in its integrality. Classes will emphasize oral communication by means of oral presentations, debates and discussions. Written grammatical exercises and essays will also contribute to develop a wider and a more precise ability to write in French. This course is for SFS-Qatar students only.

title: Intensive Advanced French I
description: Designed for French majors and other students seeking intensive advanced training in the language, this course provides rigorous and comprehensive work on French speaking, writing, reading, and listening skills as preparation for upper-division offerings. Required laboratory. Prerequisite: Intermediate French (FREN-032 or FREN-022) or placement.

title: Intensive Advanced French II
description: For French majors and other students seeking intensive advanced training in the language, this course provides rigorous and comprehensive work on French speaking, writing, reading, and listening skills as preparation for upper-division offerings or direct matriculation studies in francophone programs. Prerequisite: Advanced I French (FREN-111 or FREN-101) or placement.

title: Advanced Grammar and Writing
description: Through the grammatical, structural, and stylistic study of a variety of literary and non-literary models, this course explores techniques of expression that are practiced in a number of writing forms: descriptions, summaries, critical essays, narrations, and textual analysis.

title: Topics for Oral Proficiency
description: Guided conversations, discussions, and student presentations based on contemporary material from the French media.

title: Readings in French Literature
description: Targeted for non-majors, this course provides an introductory study to selected works of French literature from the seventeenth to the twentieth century,conducted in discussion-style format with a view toward preparing students for more advanced literature courses at the 300-400 levels.

title: Social History of the French People
description: Targeted for non-majors, this course presents a chronological survey of French civilization in its historical and social context, and prepares students for upper-division culture electives.

title: Rdg Txts/Fr-Speak World: Cultures
description: General Description of the Sequence: French 250 and 251, required of all French majors and open to other qualified undergraduates, is a post-advanced level sequence that can be taken in any order and is designed to meet two goals. First, the sequence prepares students for upper-division French courses and for direct matriculation overseas study programs by focusing on the development of critical thinking and advanced writing abilities. Second, it enhances students芒聙聶 capacities to perform close reading and analysis by introducing them to a variety of texts and documents selected to increase their content knowledge of the culture, geography, history, literature, and artistic media of the French-speaking world. Individual sections of these courses will emphasize specific approaches to French and Francophone cultures and literatures, such as the anthropological, the historical, the thematic, the formal (including notions of genre) and/or the rhetorical, depending on instructor expertise. Study and analysis will be carried out in class discussion, as well as through specific writing formats (such as explication de texte, dissertation, research paper ) for which students will receive structured training, including systematic procedures for organization, drafting, and rewriting, as well as an introduction to research materials and methodology in the discipline. The final paper for each course will be designed to mobilize the reading, analytical and research competencies developed over the course of the semester. Prerequisites for these courses are Intensive Advanced French II (112) or Advanced Grammar & Writing (151) or direct placement through AP, SAT II and/or formal placement test results. FREN 250 Reading Texts in the French-Speaking World: Cultures French 250 offers a broadly-defined introduction to the complex, multi-ethnic cultures and social practices of France and the French-speaking world. This course focuses on texts, from all historical periods but especially since the French Revolution, that might be categorized as 芒聙聹documents芒聙聺: among them, essays, ethnographies, political and historical tracts, life writing and testimonials, journalism, studies, literature, documentary images, and films. In what context were these texts created, how were they crafted, and what impact have they had? What issues and problems do they illuminate within and beyond their communities of origin, and how do we appreciate their 芒聙聹documentary芒聙聺 value or 芒聙聹authority芒聙聺? In order to address such questions, students will be given methodological tools and techniques that will allow them to approach texts in a systematic, cogent, and critical manner, both for their content and their form. Key notions such as genre, ideology, autobiography and autofiction, (self-)censorship, and frame will be explored and applied to multiple readings of works selected.

title: Rdg Txts/Fr-Speak World: Lits
description: General Description of the Sequence: French 250 and 251, required of all French majors and open to other qualified undergraduates, is a post-advanced level sequence that can be taken in any order and is designed to meet two goals. First, the sequence prepares students for upper-division French courses and for direct matriculation overseas study programs by focusing on the development of critical thinking and advanced writing abilities. Second, it enhances students芒聙聶 capacities to perform close reading and analysis by introducing them to a variety of texts and documents selected to increase their content knowledge of the culture, geography, history, literature, and artistic media of the French-speaking world. Individual sections of these courses will emphasize specific approaches to French and Francophone cultures and literatures, such as the anthropological, the historical, the thematic, the formal (including notions of genre) and/or the rhetorical, depending on instructor expertise. Study and analysis will be carried out in class discussion, as well as through specific writing formats (such as explication de texte, dissertation, research paper) for which students will receive structured training, including systematic procedures for organization, drafting, and rewriting, as well as an introduction to research materials and methodology in the discipline. The final paper for each course will be designed to mobilize the reading, analytical and research competencies developed over the course of the semester. Prerequisites for these courses are Intensive Advanced French II (112) or Advanced Grammar & Writing (151) or direct placement through AP, SAT II and/or formal placement test results. FREN 251 Reading Texts in the French-Speaking World: Literatures French 251 offers a broadly-defined introduction to the rich, varied literatures and other art forms of France and the French-speaking world. This course focuses on texts, from all historical periods and French-speaking regions, that might be categorized as 芒聙聹fiction芒聙聺: among them, works of literature (novels, short stories, poems, plays, essays), 芒聙聹popular芒聙聺 narrative forms (comics, detective stories), films, music, and art. In what context were these texts created, how were they crafted, and what impact have they had? What issues and problems do they illuminate within and beyond their communities of origin, and how do we appreciate their aesthetic value? Where do the boundaries of 芒聙聹fiction芒聙聺 lie and what formal characteristics and vocabulary help us describe different genres? In order to address such questions, students will be given methodological tools and techniques that will allow them to approach texts in a systematic, cogent, and critical manner, both for their content and their form. Key notions such as genre, narrative structures and strategies, rhetorical techniques, reader response, and 芒聙聹horizon of expectations芒聙聺 will be explored and applied to multiple readings of works selected.

title: French Theater Workshop: Ionesco and Beckett
Making Sense of Language
description: How dependent upon language is our experience and understanding of the universe around us? How do we "make sense" of language? This course offers an exploration of the human linguistic condition through the analysis of authentic documents in French (brochures, articles, print and TV commercials, speeches, clips from movies, objects, etc.), which bring to life some of the most important concepts in language-related studies. This course fulfills the linguistics requirement and the post-advanced language requirement for the French major and satisfies one semester of the College's social science general education requirement.

title: Business French
description: This course is aimed to students who already have a good command of the French language, are interested in improving their language skills in the field of business, and in learning about how business is conducted in France and other francophone countries. Students will have the opportunity to improve not only their oral proficiency in French (comprehension and expression) through presentations, discussions and play roles, but they will also enhance their written skills (reading and writing) through summaries of specialized press articles, memos, minutes, emails and business letters. The course is entirely in French and is taught in a very interactive way, mostly based on the case study method - analysis of real life situations. Students will learn how to communicate orally and in writing with business partners, to become entrepreneur themselves and how to develop a business plan. There will be three tests during the semester and a final project based on the business and marketing techniques learned during the semester. At the end of the semester, upon request, students will have the opportunity to take the Certification of the Paris Chamber of Commerce and Industry (CCIP) This course fulfills the upper division post advanced language requirement for the French major.

title: Tutorial: French
description: In a tutorial, a student works independently, under the guidance of a faculty member, on an in-depth research project.
