It is to understand why, over half a millennium later, the Tudors continue to captivate our imaginations and live on, through their legacies, into the twenty-first century. 332-02: This course seeks to understand the dominant strains of political thought in Europe from the sixteenth to the eighteenth centuries. To do so, we will look at those events, people, cultures, and entities that influenced ideas of state formation, church-state relations, the origins of civil society, the relationship between the citizen and the state, and the role of those such as women and religious minorities whose inclusion often was tenuous at best. The course will be structured around class participation, including one short presentation, and two papers.

title: Religion and the State in Western Europe
description: The role of religion in the political sphere is a matter of great moment in today's world. This course examines the historical evolution of the relationship between Church and State beginning in the Medieval World and moving into the Modern World, up to and including modern-day Christian fundamentalism. We begin with the moment that the Roman Empire became an officially Christian empire and then look at the evolving relationship of the papacy toward political power. By the thirteenth century the papacy had gone far toward becoming a papal monarchy over Christian Europe. We will then examine a variety of radical responses to a politicized papacy as well as the impact that the Protestant Reformation had on religious politics. Of particular interest are emerging ideas of the separation of Church and State and the novel concept of religious toleration. The last few weeks of the course focus on political writings from seventeenth and eighteenth-century Europe (most notably John Locke), the role of religion in the founding of the American Republic, and the political significance of Christian fundamentalism today.

title: Christians and the Holocaust
description: This course addresses the reactions of European Christians to the Nazi persecution and killing of Jews. The course starts with an overview of the history of antisemitism throughout the centuries, and then moves to the core of the course: an exploration of the specific attitudes and actions of both Catholics and Protestants in the inter-war and World War II period. In the section on Catholics and the Catholic Church, two to three weeks is spent examining the issue of the papacy and the Jews, concentrating especially on the controversy over Pius XII. Students will read some of the most important literature by the detractors and by the supporters of Pius XII so as to understand the chief arguments and the evidence offered by the two sides. Following that, the course considers the role of the Catholic clergy, the religious orders, and the laity, with particular attention to the efforts of a distinguished Georgetown professor, the late Dr. Jan Karski, sometimes known as "the man who tried to stop the Holocaust." The section on Protestants will survey the actions of the various denominations, certain key pastors, and rescue operations on the part of brave Protestant families or individuals. The course will conclude with a short survey of post-WW II efforts to clarify the historical record and improve Jewish-Christian relations.

title: Europe in WW II: History fiction film
description: This course examines the European dimension of the most destructive and comprehensive war in the history of humankind. As one part of an integrated global conflict, the European theater can only be understood within a wider spacial context, including that theater芒聙聶s geographical extension into the Atlantic Ocean and North Africa and its relationship to the linked war in Pacific Asia. Likewise, the events of 1939-1945 in Europe must be situated within the broader chronological framework of the tumultuous twentieth century, including the First World War and the Great Depression, as provenance, and the Cold War and global decolonization, as legacy. Among other matters to be considered will be the bases and limits of German-Axis conquest, the construction and character of Hitler芒聙聶s terrifying New Order, and the emergence of collaboration with and resistence to it. Special attention will be accorded the jacked-up evolution of 芒聙聹total war,芒聙聺 from all-out social mobilization in the First World War to purposeful targeting of mass categories of non-combatants in the Second: the Allies芒聙聶 city-busting air campaign over Germany, the ethnic warfare of 芒聙聹annihilation芒聙聺 in the eastern borderlands and Russia, and the destruction of European Jewry in the Holocaust.

title: Medieval saints: cults, legends, and culture
description: This course is about how and why saints fascinated medieval people and still fascinate modern historians. We will examine the various kinds of medieval saints: male and female, high-born and lowly, popular and official, aspiring and failed. We will also look at the ways that medieval people reacted to saints and the role that devotion to the saints played in medieval culture. We will study how medieval people honored their saints (and sometimes defamed them) and what was expected from saints in return; what kinds of people became recognized saints, and how this recognition happened (or failed to happen); and what sort of stories were told about saints and how this hagiography took on enormous cultural importance. We will be examining popular (and unusual) practices regarding saints, such as the treatment of saints芒聙聶 corpses, as well as the development of the juridical process that led to canonization. Our approach to the phenomena of saints and their veneration in medieval society will be historical, anthropological, literary, and sociological.

title: Collaboration and Resistance in Europe under Hitler and Stalin
description: Using a mixture of film and written sources (memoirs, autobiographical accounts, collections of documents), we will examine the question of collaboration and resistance in the decades around World War II. The classes will be a mixture of films, lecture, and discussion.

title: European Fascism
description: This course will look at the origins, practice, and nature of Fascism as it developed in Europe between the world wars. To that end, readings by Fascist theorists such as Mussolini and Hitler, as well as by historians who analyze and describe the phenomenon will be used. Films from and about the era will also be part of the material for the course. In addition to discussing historical Fascism, the course will also take a look at what, if anything, connects the meaning of Fascism as it existed in the past and how that term is used today.

title: Topics in Latin American History (see description)
description: This number will be used for seminars devoted to specific subjects in the area of Latin American history. Students may take more than one of the courses offered under this number. Each course will be announced in the course schedule and receive its own sub-title. In Fall 2011 the following course is offered: History of the U.S.-Mexico border: This class with analyze the parallel cultural, political and economic developments in the histories of Mexico and the southern United States during the 芒聙聹long芒聙聺 twentieth century (late 19th century through 2010), focusing on the eastern border region. Due to limited access to sources, language barriers, or lack of interest on behalf of researchers, the range of historical analysis is often limited to the boundaries of one nation. This class takes a broad look at the region by considering how it was shaped by people and events on both sides of the Texas-Mexico border. History does not end at a border, just as the power of historical forces usually transcends boundaries of nation-states. That is not to say that the border or the influence of governments does not matter. Historical actors 芒聙聯 individuals, political factions, business interests 芒聙聯 in border areas are particularly aware of the power and the limit of states and must learn to navigate the challenges and opportunities offered by the border. By taking a transnational perspective, this class will allow a broader understanding of the historical forces that have shaped the U.S.-Mexico border.

title: Latinos in the U.S,
description: In 2003, the U.S. Bureau of the Census declared that Hispanics had surpassed African Americans in population, an announcement that occasioned a flourish of commentary. Most analysts concluded that a fundamental turning point had been reached. Still, the moment raised questions. Who were these people, and what did it mean that they had become 芒聙聹America芒聙聶s largest minority芒聙聺? A very small number of this group could trace their heritage back to 16th century Spanish settlements. More could claim ancestors from Mexican lands conquered by Americans in the 1840s. However, the greatest number had come since the beginning of the twentieth century. They came from various countries and regions at different times, lived in different places, did different work, and often saw themselves as quite different, not only from the mainstream society, but also from one another. Yet, out of their varied experiences, a new story was being written. This course examines that development, the making of a Latino United States. It begins among the Spanish settlers in the Southwest, and ends with late 20th century Mexican immigrants in New York City. Along the way, we will explore the various ways that Latinos have sought power and recognition in the United States. We will learn of their efforts to establish secure and prosperous communities, their fights for justice and equality on the job, in schools, and at the polls. We will assess their experience with 芒聙聹becoming American,芒聙聺 including their complicated relationship to Latino immigrants. Although the majority of readings concern the efforts of Mexican Americans and Puerto Ricans, we will also discuss the challenges they and others faced in developing shared institutions and a sense of themselves as a single ethnic group. All the while, we will situate our analysis within the broader arc of United States history. This is not a lecture course. Therefore, students must complete the week芒聙聶s readings, and come ready to contribute to a lively, informed discussion.

title: Pirates, Soldiers, and Diplomats: Islam and the West, 1500-1914
description: The course examines violence and diplomacy between the Islamic "gunpowder" empires (Ottoman, Safavid, and Mughal) and some of their adversaries (Spanish and Austrian Habsburgs, and Russian empires). It studies competing ideologies (jihad and crusade) and practices, raids (e.g. Tatar and Cossack), piracy (the Barbary corsairs and the Knights of St. John), imperial campaigns, frontier warfare, as well as wars of nationalism and imperialism. Studying the diplomatic relations between Islam and the West, special attention will be given to questions such as Islamic and Western intelligence, permanent embassies in Istanbul, and the conversion from unilateral to reciprocal diplomacy. Spring.

title: Islam in the West
description: The course will examine the formation and growth of the Muslim communities in North America and Europe. It will explore the history and dynamics of community development in a western context, the creation of an Islamic minority perspective, the challenges the community and the host countries face, and the variety of efforts to meet these challenges.

title: Revolutionary Thought in Islam
description: This course examines the history of revolutionary activity in Islam. It will focus on the interaction between the paradigm of the Prophet's example during the formative period of Islam, the Muslim debates on issues of peace, jihad, revolutionary change, government accountability and human responsibility for social, political and economic decay. It will study how these paradigms functioned in a variety of revolutionary movements in Islamic history including the Abbasids, Ikhwan al-Safa, the Fulani Jihad in Sub Saharan Africa, the Mahdi revolution in the Sudan, the Muslim Brotherhood of Egypt, Hamas, Jihad Islami and Tahrir in Palestine and Hisbullah in Lebanon.

title: Empires and the Greater Middle East, 1500-2012
description: The course seeks to understand how imperial grand strategies of the European Great Powers regarding the Greater Middle East 芒聙聯defined as the Arab Middle East, Asia Minor, Iran, the Caucasus and Central Asia芒聙聯 as well as the strategies and policies of regional agencies influenced the fate of the region. Of the regional Islamic empires special attention will be given to Ottoman Turkey and Safavid and Qajar Persia. With regard to the European powers the course focuses on the British, Russian, Soviet and American strategies and experiences in the Middle East.

title: The Eastern mediterranean since 1911
description: This class will be offered at Alanya Turkey.

title: Novels of empire
description: A close reading of Rudyard Kipling's Kim, Orhan Pamuk's White Castle, Mikhail Lermontov's A Hero of Our Time, and Gillo Pontecorvo's film, The Battle of Algiers, in the context of their respective empires - British, Ottoman, Russian, and French. Some attention to theories of empire, but emphasis on historical setting. History majors may apply this course to various world regions.

title: Soviet dissident movements
description: This seminar addresses the rise, activities, and achievements of anti-Soviet opposition movements during the last decades of the USSR (1960s芒聙聯1991) and their subsequent transformation in the post-Soviet space. It examines these movements芒聙聶 wide variety of ideological currents and political strategies, surveys their contribution to the emergence of an alternative human rights culture, and reexamines their impact on the fall of the Soviet Union. It addresses the human rights activists芒聙聶 debates over the defense of human rights, the right to national self-determination, the right to emigrate, religious and cultural freedom, and environmental issues. Special emphasis is put on how dissidents were perceived by Soviet authorities and in the international arena and how those perceptions have contributed to shape East-West diplomatic relations during the Cold War. This seminar also surveys the instrumental role dissidents played in the aftermath of the collapse of the Communist rule before being isolated from post-Soviet politics.

title: Military History of NATO
description: This colloquium will trace the development of the Atlantic Alliance of 1949 into a military organization under United States leadership. The impact of particular events from the Korean War in 1950 through the Berlin and Cuban crises of 1948-1962 to the INF agreement of 1987 and the subsequent implosion of the Soviet Union will be examined. Special attention will be paid to the differing views on nuclear weaponry between the United States and its European allies. The course will conclude with an evaluation of NATO's status in 2009 and of its potential roles in the immediate future.

title: Topics in US History: see description
description: This number will be used for seminars devoted to specific subjects in the area of American history. Students may take more than one of the courses offered under this number. Each course will be announced in the course schedule and receive its own sub-title. In Spring 2012 the following course is offered: History of U.S. social policy: At the height of affluence in the middle decades of the twentieth century, the United States devoted less attention and fewer resources to social welfare provision than any other industrialized power. Its welfare state was more constrained, in particular, than its western European counterparts芒聙聰less generous, more targeted, and almost uniquely reliant on the private sector to tend to the needs of the sick and the elderly, the ill-housed and ill-fed. Looking back from Europe in the early twenty first century, in light of recent rollbacks and austerity cuts, however, the American example seems not just unexceptional, but prophetic. This course examines the evolution of American social policy in the twentieth century, tracing it from its roots in the reformist impulse of the Progressive Era, through the heyday of liberal programs in the 1930s and 1960s, to the conservative turn and the end of 芒聙聹welfare as we know it芒聙聺 at century芒聙聶s end. Though the content of the policies are examined in some depth, this course places particular emphasis on the intellectual underpinnings and political implications of policy choices. We use the term 芒聙聹social policy芒聙聺 rather than 芒聙聹welfare芒聙聺 because it is more inclusive芒聙聰including health care, social insurance, and anti-poverty programs, the traditional staples of welfare state scholarship, as well as housing, education, and employment policies. More importantly, the expansive framework will force us to examine more carefully the roles played by non-state actors, particularly private sector business and issue-oriented social movements, in shaping political demands and constraining policy choices. Several themes and questions run through the course. How have reigning conceptions of 芒聙聹poverty芒聙聺 and 芒聙聹need芒聙聺 changed over the course of the century? What were the political ramifications of these shifts? Why and how has policy changed in the core areas of social welfare provision: health insurance, old-age pensions, and poverty relief? What role have social movements played in shaping the American welfare state? How should we understand the role of private sector business? Ultimately, this course is designed to inform students with a historical perspective on these key questions, so often discussed without historical context in contemporary political discourse. There are no prerequisites for this course, but a basic familiarity with twentieth century American history and American government will help.

title: Mark Twain's America
description: This course explores the broader socio-cultural conditions within which America's most important late nineteenth century writer pursued his craft. The Western Frontier, race relations, urban-rural conflict, technology and Victorian culture will be studied and linked to Mark Twain's novels, short stories and work as a journalist.

title: Working Lives: Theory and Practice of Oral History
description: Students in this class will participate in a hands-on historical research project that documents the recent history of service workers in Washington, DC. We will both read about the history of working people and produce historical materials and interpretations, focused on the history of janitors and service work. There will be three major assignments: 1) conduct at least one oral history interview with a working person or labor activist; 2) produce a 15-page research paper on an issue, event, or individual related to DC janitors and their organizations, and 3) participate fully in class discussions and other activities. We will also produce a digital history web exhibit to showcase oral histories and our research. This project is a group effort and the class will work closely together over the course of the semester. At the end, we will have helped write history, not just read it.

title: The Reagan era
description: This seminar will examine the period in American history that coincides with the political career Ronald Reagan, from his rise as a leader of the conservative movement through the administration of his successor in the presidency, George H.W. Bush. It will examine themes in American politics, economy, culture, and foreign policy, illuminated by Reagan芒聙聶s career between the 1960s and the 1990s. The subject will be covered in weekly readings and discussions. The major writing assignment will be a 20-page research paper.

title: Antebellum South and the Confederacy
description: Perhaps no region or era of the United States has been more romanticized than the 芒聙聹Old South,芒聙聺 but how does the image of white-pillared plantations full of ease and elegance compare to the reality of a region characterized by geographic diversity, class conflict, a strong market orientation, and a population that was 40% black and enslaved? From 1820 to 1860, the South dominated the nation politically, economically, and culturally, and it did so primarily thanks to the institution of slavery. When both the dominance and the institution of slavery began to look insecure, eleven states in the South broke away to form the Confederacy. As we examine the South from 1820 to 1865, we will be interested in the lives of white, black, and Native American Southerners. Our themes will include politics and ideology, capitalism and the market, class conflict, gender roles, slavery and violence, and the central place of race within the history of the South. Broad questions guiding our semester will include: What made the southern states in the nineteenth-century United States The South? What was southern about it, and what was American about it? In short, we will ask, was the South, with all its contradictions, conflict, and diversity, an exception to U.S. history, or at the heart of U.S. history from 1820 to 1865?

title: War and environment in the modern world
description: This course investigates the role of modern warfare in environmental change, as well as the natural environment芒聙聶s role in shaping military conflict. Readings and discussions focus on the following questions: How has demand for resources emerged as a cause and consequence of military conflict? What has been the role of the natural landscape in shaping military strategy? How have wartime considerations led to changes in environmental policy and management? How has war affected people芒聙聶s conceptions of the environment? How have ideas about nature factored into wartime propaganda and the representation of enemies as the 芒聙聹Other.芒聙聺 The class examines these issues from an international perspective, analyzing the environmental dimensions of military conflicts in Africa, the Americas, Asia, and the Middle East from the nineteenth century to the present.

title: Senior Honors Seminar in History
description: HIST 408-409 form a two-semester study of History as an intellectual discipline. Enrollment is by invitation of the Department. Fall: Readings and discussions with departmental faculty on the various methods, concepts, and philosophies of history, and the development of a research prospectus with a faculty mentor. Spring: Research seminar under the guidance of the mentor and Seminar Director. (Enrollment only by permission of the Director of Undergraduate Studies). Students must commit themselves for the full two semesters.

title: Senior Honors Seminar in History
description: HIST 408-409 form a two-semester study of History as an intellectual discipline. Enrollment is by invitation of the Department. Fall: Readings and discussions with departmental faculty on the various methods, concepts, and philosophies of history, and the development of a research prospectus with a faculty mentor. Spring: Research seminar under the guidance of the mentor and Seminar Director. (Enrollment only by permission of the Director of Undergraduate Studies). Students must commit themselves for the full two semesters.

title: The African Atlantic
description: Africans were crucial to the development of the Atlantic World from their early encounters with Europeans in the 1400s through the era of abolition and the first modern human rights movement in the 1800s. Team-taught by historians of different sides of the Atlantic ocean, this course focuses on the causes and effects of Africa芒聙聶s integration into Atlantic commerce, especially the slave trade, and the traumatic experience of millions of captive Africans forcibly transported to the Americas. Themes to be covered include African concepts of slavery; the linkage between war and captivity; the technological transformation of trade; the dynamics of the Middle Passage; African labor, knowledge, and culture in the Americas; Africans芒聙聶 participation in the fight against slavery; and the long-term impact of the slave trade and its abolition on Africa. We will study historians芒聙聶 diverse perspectives on these issues and explore the astonishing array of primary sources, from slave traders芒聙聶 letters to ex-slaves芒聙聶 folktales, that reveal the participation of Africans themselves in the making of Atlantic history.

title: African encounters with development
description: Has development been a blessing or a curse for Africa and Africans in the 20th century? In this course, we will examine how development programs have been conceived and carried out with respect to Africa in the colonial and post colonial periods, and ask how their impact on Africans' lives has been represented and understood by African people, African governments, and international actors. Combining intellectual history with history of practice, this course will explore the interaction of ideas and experience in the development era芒聙聰from macro theories of production, growth, and well-being to the micro practices of farmers, bureaucrats, activists, and social scientists. In the first half of the course we will consider the 芒聙聹colonial roots of development," covering themes such as agricultural improvement, conservation, famine relief, and urban sanitation. In the second half of the course we will examine diverse post-colonial experiences of development, from nationalism to neoliberalism and beyond.

title: Kipling's India: The making of a colonial society
description: Take up the White Man's burden Send forth the best ye breed Go, bind your sons to exile To serve your captives' need; To wait, in heavy harness, On fluttered folk and wild Your new-caught sullen peoples, Half devil and half child. - Kipling Rudyard Kipling (1865-1936), the English author was born in British India and is best known for his works The Jungle Book and Kim. Distinguished by their literary brilliance, Kipling芒聙聶s works were unequivocally a metaphor for the age he lived in. This course will use Kipling芒聙聶s writings as a doorway to enter the social and cultural world of nineteenth and twentieth century British India. It will explore the different ways in which the colonizer and the colonized engaged with the 芒聙聵Other芒聙聶 and, in doing so, reconstituted it. This cultural dialogue was evident, as the students will discover, in the way the colonialism sought to colonize the mind, body, territory, history, landscape and aesthetic sensibilities of the Indians, and also in the way it constituted the British sense of nationhood. The course will examine these themes in depth to bring to fore the 芒聙聵experience芒聙聶 of the Raj 芒聙聯 that is, what it meant to be a colonizer and colonizer; and what it was to live in a colonial world.

title: War and peace in East Asia
description: This course focuses on the historical contexts of conflicts and resolutions in East Asia in the 19th and 20th centuries. Its purpose is three-fold: first, to study the history of international relations in East Asia; second, to explore the political, economic, and social impact of war and peace at the local, national and regional levels; and third, to become familiar with recent scholarship on themes of nationalism, economic development, international systems, collective memory, and governance, among others.

title: Global health and disease: Asia
description: Utilizing a case study approach focused on societies in East and South Asia, this seminar provides a historical perspective on many contemporary global health issues. Global health challenges now clearly transcend national borders: in the age of the jumbo-jet, infectious and vector-borne diseases can spread easily from one country to another. Certain health problems such as smoking and obesity are of such magnitude that they have global impact. Food and drug safety are of increasing concern in our interconnected world of globalized trade. At the same time, previously distinct medical traditions and practices such as Ayurveda and Chinese acupuncture now circulate globally even as biomedicine prevails as the dominant form of health care around the world. These and many other issues of global health are deeply rooted in the past. Historical forces and events, from colonialism to the global spread of HIV/AIDS, have shaped the current state of global health. Understanding the history of global health in local Asian contexts can thus help us to respond to the many health challenges of today. Through focused case studies drawn primarily from the historical experiences of China and India since 1800, we will examine how social, economic, and political forces芒聙聰increasingly global in nature芒聙聰have influenced medical processes and disease outcomes in Asia. We will also investigate how developments in Asia have affected global health since the nineteenth century. Topics include public health campaigns in colonial and post-colonial contexts; changing ideas about hygiene, sanitation, and contagion; state and societal responses to specific disease outbreaks; the emergence of international health institutions and their impact on local Asian societies; disparate approaches to primary health care; the individual and collective experience of illness due to consumer culture and changing life-styles; and the persistent presence and global diffusion of Asian healing cultures. The course is open to all undergraduate students and may be of special interest to those studying Asian Studies; History; International History; Science, Technology, and International Affairs; International Development; International Health; or the Biology of Global Health. Interested graduate students may also enroll in this over/under seminar. This course is cross-listed in the History Department (HIST 423) and International Affairs (INAF 482).

title: Mao and China's cultural revolution
description: The 芒聙聹Great Proletarian Cultural Revolution芒聙聺 began when Mao Zedong called on the Chinese 芒聙聹masses芒聙聺 to renew the Chinese revolution by attacking the Communist Party and state apparatus. Remembered officially as the 芒聙聹ten years of chaos,芒聙聺 the events of this period (1966-76) were marked by social upheaval, personal vendettas, senseless violence, and extreme ideological pressure. Yet throughout these years many people continued on with fairly mundane lives: marrying, raising children, and enjoying the love and companionship of family and close friends. Still others remember the Cultural Revolution not as a gloomy and dark period but one in which youthful idealism ignited hopes for a brighter, more equitable future. Ironically, far from making the Chinese more revolutionary or Chinese society more just, the Cultural Revolution ushered in widespread cynicism and disengagement from mass politics. What were the causes of the Cultural Revolution? How was it experienced and how is it remembered? To what extent are its reverberations still felt in contemporary Chinese society, politics, and culture? How have subsequent events affected our understanding of the Cultural Revolution decade? This seminar will explore Mao芒聙聶s Cultural Revolution from a variety of perspectives, utilizing a range of representations of the period that have been constructed through history, memoir, and film.

title: European encounters with Asia 1450-1750
description: This upper-division seminar in global history will explore the creative encounters as well as turbulent clashes that took place between European explorers, merchants, and missionaries and the peoples and cultures of India, Japan, China, and the Philippines, from the late 15th to the late 18th centuries. The European maritime empires involved in this colonial, commercial, and religious enterprise included Portugal, Spain, England, and the Netherlands. From the moment Europeans first reached the shores of Southeast and East Asia, they struggled to come to terms with the cultural divide between East and West. Students will analyze a number of key primary texts written as a result of this encounter by famous European explorers, missionaries, and merchants, as well as by their Asian counterparts. Students will also explore how the invention of the printing press芒聙聰the first IT revolution芒聙聰helped to disseminate knowledge about Asia in Europe & the Americas.

title: The Americanization of Europe
description: This colloquium will be a historical inquiry into the following questions: Has Europe been Americanized? Have Europeans in the course of the 20th century been Coca-colonized or McDonaldized? Have Americans and Europeans grown increasingly alike with respect to adopting mass culture, consumer society, and market economies? If so why? What is the explanation for the seemingly irresistible power of Americanization? When did this process begin and what has America's role been in this process? Or, conversely, is Americanization an illusion? Has there been appropriation and resistance so that national identities and diversity have been sustained and even intensified? Has it provoked a search for identity in national or ethnic difference? Above all what does this transformation mean?

title: Women and gender in modern Europe
description: This course is a seminar on the history of women in modern Europe. It will begin with a two week focus on the emergence of women芒聙聶s and gender history in the second half of the 20th century, acquainting people with just the basic terminology, concepts and theories in the field. Thereafter, it will dig into the history, starting with the emergence of feminist ideas in 18th century Europe and ending with developments up to and immediately following the fall of Communism in Eastern Europe. The goal of the course is to give students a solid understanding of this important history, one that will serve them well into later adult life. Though the focus of the course will be on women, we will obviously need to address gender expectations for both men and women. Thus, we will follow the relational history of women and men in chronological order so as to gain a clear idea of this history. There will be a basic textbook for the course, Bonnie Smith芒聙聶s CHANGING LIVES: WOMEN IN EUROPEAN HISTORY SINCE 1700, along with parallel readings in primary and secondary sources. Parallel books for the course will include Aristophanes芒聙聶 LYSISTRATA, Mary Wollstonecraft芒聙聶s a VINDICATION OF THE RIGHTS OF WOMEN, John Stuart Mill芒聙聶s THE SUBJECTION OF WOMEN, George Sand芒聙聶s INDIANA, John Ruskin芒聙聶s SESAME AND LILIES, Virginia Woolf芒聙聶s A ROOM OF ONE芒聙聶S OWN, and Paula Bartley芒聙聶s EMMELINE PANKHURST, Irene Guenther芒聙聶s NAZI CHIC?: FASHIONING WOMEN IN THE THIRD REICH (DRESS, BODY, CULTURE), and Simone de Beauvoir芒聙聶s MEMOIRS OF A DUTIFUL DAUGHTER as well as a couple of similar works for the twentieth century. The remainder of the parallel readings will include fascinating articles or chapters in books. In building the reading list for the course, there has been a concerted effort to find good readings on women (and men) in a variety of different European countries so as to shed light on both the similarities and the differences across the face of Europe. This should give students a good grip on the trends over time, across Europe, for the past two and a half centuries. Written work for the course will consist of three 3 to 4 page comparative papers and five one-page response papers. There are no written examinations in this course.

title: The Old Regime and the French Revolution
description: The seminar will explore the social and political revolutions in the Atlantic World at the end of the 18th century. Our central focus will be France, but we will also look at the revolutions in Poland, Holland, US, and Haiti for comparative purposes. All students will write a seminar paper, roughly 30-35 pages, focused on primary sources. Sessions will be split equally between common readings and individualized assignments. Each student will give an oral presentation on her/his research topic.

title: Kings or People: Authority and democracy in modern Europe
description: Before the eighteenth century the institution of monarchy and all that was associated with it was largely unchallenged as the defining element in European state and society. With the advent of the Enlightenment, however, projects for the reform of monarchy started to appear and with the French Revolution its very right to exist was challenged. From that point forward the idea of popular sovereignty, the claims of political ideologies, and the rise of nationalism presented powerful challenges to the validity of monarchy in a modernizing Europe. Yet, until the European-wide catastrophe of World War I, monarchy not only survived, but continued to dominate much of European life. Why this should have been the case is what will be explored in this course. Through the use of documents and works of history and literature the question of "kings or people" will be examined from its origins in the 18th century to its apparent resolution in the events of the first world war.

title: Religion and politics in the West since 1400
description: This course presents a framework grounded in understanding the early and continuing efforts to structure the juncture where religious and political organizations and values meet. It examines the shifting relationships between religions and states in western Europe and the United States, with some comparative analysis with Islam and states in the Middle East. The course traces the historical evolution of the ideas of religious toleration and separation of church and state from the late medieval conflicts between the papacy and the newly emerging states of Europe through the early modern religious wars to religion and the state issues in colonial US and the twentieth century. The course will concentrate on readings in political thought, such as the writings of Machiavelli, Luther, Locke, Thomas Jefferson and others within the context of the struggle for religious freedom in the west.

title: The Enlightenment
description: This course examines some of the most important European thinkers of the late seventeenth and eighteenth centuries, a period in which literacy was growing rapidly and printing made new ideas accessible to a widening reading public. Its purpose is to get students acquainted with the historical origins of ideas that can seem to us self-evident芒聙聰for example, that authority rests on rationality rather than appeals to tradition, sacred texts, or some unmediated concept of nature; that toleration is a virtue; that freedom and equality are fundamental to both public and private life; that self-determining individuality is a higher value than obedience. Texts will vary from year to year but will usually include works by Diderot, Goethe, Hume, Kant, Montesquieu, Rousseau, and Voltaire.

title: Jesuit enterprises, 1540-1773
description: In the early modern period the Jesuits were involved in virtually every facet of culture almost around the globe. They were, besides their directly religious engagements, political theorists, astronomers, dramatists, architects, engineers of city fortifications, governors of Amerindian settlements, cartographers, musicians, painters, and, above all, missionaries and schoolmasters. They are thus an ideal subject for a seminar because they allow students to pursue their special interests while they at the same time provide a common center for class discussion. The focus will be 1540-1773, but students can do their projects on a later period. Although the required readings (primary and secondary sources) will be in English, any other language students know will be of great use. Besides the final research paper, students will be responsible for smaller projects and for leading discussions on them. As fitting for a seminar, classes be conducted so as to emphasize student participation and initiative, but they will also contain some lecture by the professor.

title: European intellectual history, 19th century
description: This course treats the fate of reason in the context of social and political change from the Enlightenment to the outbreak of WWI. Topics include: the public and private functions of reason, the social role of intellectuals, the theory of the novel, the emergence of the social sciences and the objectification of social life, and skepticism concerning the Enlightenment promise of emancipation through reason. Readings from Kant, Hegel, Marx, Mill, Tocqueville, Stendhal, Flaubert, Durkheim, Nietzsche, and Weber.

title: Food in European History
description: During the Renaissance, the foods and cuisines of Europe bore a strong family resemblance to their counterparts in late Roman antiquity and the Muslim cultures of the medieval world. Cereals dominated the diet and the prevailing culinary style favored complex, spicy sauces. Wine was the universal beverage of choice, distilled spirits were rare, and coffee, tea, and other caffeinated drinks were unknown. Medical advice about diet and health drew on the teachings of Hippocratic medicine (which originated in ancient Greece) as expanded and amended by Roman and Arab physicians. Shortly after 1500, however, this picture began to change, laying the foundations of the dietary and culinary regimes with which we are familiar today. This course will examine the transformation of the habits of cooking, eating, and drinking in Europe between 1500-1900 with an emphasis on France and England. Topics will include: the reception of new foods from the Americas; the growing availability and popularity of sugar and colonial beverages (coffee, tea, and chocolate); the uneven impact of economic growth on the diets of rich and poor Europeans; changing ideas about diet, health, and disease; changing manners and patterns of social behavior associated with food and drink; the rise of the restaurant, and the emergence of modern culinary styles, including national ones.

title: Native Americnas making North America
description: Native Americans shaped North America not only before the coming of Europeans, but through long colonial centuries and into the national era. This course explores the peoples from the Mexican central highlands to broad American West, seeking to understand their diverse indigenous ways, their divergent encounters with European colonialism, and their active roles in the emergence of Mexico and the United States.

title: Making Nations in Latin America
description: Different social groups have had a vital role in the making of the nation-state in Latin America after independence. This course will explore the growth of a sense of nationalism in the late colonial period and then examine the role of the elites, the urban lower classes, and the peasantry in the constitution of the nation-state from the nineteenth into the early twentieth century. In addition to intellectual history, the course will show the political and military contributions of subaltern people.

title: Islamic Modernism
description: This course examines the development of efforts to create an intellectual and religious synthesis of modern and Islamic thought. The works of major figures in the definition and development of this Islamic Modernism from the early presentations of thinkers like al-Afghani) and Abduh to twentieth century scholars like Iqbal and Fazlur Rahman will be studied, both in terms of their intellectual context and their historical contexts.

title: Intellectuals, Communisn, and Fascism
description: This course has two components. In the first, we will examine how intellectuals (broadly speaking, writers, artists, professionals, scientists, and other cultural and creative figures) acted and were treated in Nazi Germany and the Soviet Union. The course will begin with a general introduction to the two regimes, with an emphasis on cultural policy and other issues most relevant to the role of intellectuals. The two regimes are often linked together as totalitarian, as violent and repressive to an unprecedented degree, and as revolutionary in their goals to remake man and society. This first part of the course raises questions about the two systems芒聙聶 differences and similarities; the role of intellectuals in constructing the 芒聙聹new society芒聙聺; the dilemmas the two regimes faced in their approaches to the educated and professional classes; and their political and ideological underpinnings. This part of the course will also serve as a good introduction to the methods and goals of comparative history. The second part of the course examines Western intellectual admirers of these two regimes, and will ask questions such as: Why did some of the greatest minds of the twentieth century embrace the most repressive regimes of the far Left and the far Right? What did intellectual travelers see when they visited these regimes, and why? Placed in tandem, the two parts of the course raise questions about how intellectuals acted when they viewed these regimes from within and when they viewed them from afar. The course will end with a discussion of the visionary Russian dystopian novel by Zamiatin, We.

title: The Terrorists
description: Why have some people over at least the past 2000 years, spanning the entire globe, and outside of organized militaries or armed rebellions, induced themselves or others to employ violence 芒聙聯 not only targeted killings, but even mass murder of civilians 芒聙聯 and sometimes their own folk? This reading and writing course, starting with the extreme wing of the Judaean Zealots and their like-minded Ancient ilk, continuing with the mediaeval Assassins and Millenarians, picking up again with most radical nationalists, anarchists, and social revolutionaries of the 19th and 20th centuries, and concluding with the religious and non-religious theorists and practitioners terror tactics, will examine both the ideas behind such actions and the actions themselves. Students are at liberty to select a special individual or group for their specific research, analysis, writing, and oral presentation.

title: Inventing the Illegal Alien: The immigration crisis in the Progressive Era U.S.
description: This course offers a chance to do original research on the fascinating time period in immigration history from 1880 to 1929. It was during these years that the legal restrictions that ended mass immigration to the United States until the 1950s were put into place. Using both primary and secondary sources, the course will trace the rise of immigration and restrictions placed on it, changing notions of citizenship, and the differentiation by race芒聙聰or scientific racism芒聙聰among immigrants of Asian, Mexican, and eastern and southern European background. Topics explored will include the relationship between race and citizenship status; the history of the idea of the 芒聙聹illegal alien芒聙聺; and the gendered aspects of the immigration experience. Each student will have the opportunity to do original research using both published and archival primary sources. A field trip to the National Archives is part of the course. The first half of the course will focus on common readings to establish themes and background; the second half will be built around developing individual research interests.

title: America in Vietnam
description: This colloquium examines the war in southeast Asia as a problem of American foreign policy, exploring the reasons for US involvement, escalation, fighting of a limited engagement, intervention into Laos and Cambodia as well as the decision to enter into negotiations and sign the Paris Accords in 1973. To understand the pressures on policy makers the course also explores the domestic context within which decisions were made, the ramifications of alliances and the involvement of wider adversary relationships. The course also looks at the Vietnamese dynamic from 1945 to 1975.

title: The African-American Great Migration
description: This course examines the African-American Great Migration, the mass movement of African-Americans from the South to Northern cities. The course will begin with an examination of the conditions that compelled African-Americans to flee the South, particularly World War I, Northern industrialization and the codification of Jim Crow laws. Then, students will read texts, memoirs and original documents about Great Migration cities--New York, Chicago, Milwaukee, Pittsburgh and Detroit. The course will emphasize thew ways in which the Migration informed African-American religion, gender relations, business, educational opportunities and politics.

title: Women and immigration in the US
description: This seminar attempts to understand the role of women and gender in the great history of immigration in the United States. It begins by comparing women in slavery and indentured servitude in colonial America, considers the great 芒聙聹waves芒聙聺 of European immigration in the nineteenth and early twentieth century, then proceeds to the new diasporic immigrant communities of the late twentieth century and present-day. After considering the lives of Scandinavian, Irish, and German women in the mid-nineteenth-century, it turns to the family economies, labor struggles, and marriage strategies among Italian and Eastern European Jewish women, as well as such 芒聙聹lighter芒聙聺 but important topics such as changing foodways and child-rearing practices. The course continues to consider the impact of immigration exclusion laws in the 1920s, and then the relaxation of these in the 1950s and 1960s, ushering in an unprecedented number of immigrants from Asia, Africa, and Latin America. The course concludes with today芒聙聶s controversies over migrant workers and 芒聙聹illegal芒聙聺 immigration芒聙聶s impact on women in particular. The course has a demanding reading list of both primary and secondary sources, will include viewing several films, and will require two brief papers and an independent research paper with a topic devised in concert with the instructor.

title: History Core Colloquium
description: This course is required for all incoming doctoral students in the department. It will (1) provide an introduction to historical method and ways of thinking and (2) explore what is distinctive about the subspecialties of history and what is common to the discipline as a whole. (Fall)

title: Introduction to Global and International History
description: This course is a selective introduction to some of the key issues and main themes in global and international history. Our weekly discussions will focus on common reading assignments. Each of you is also to write a 25-page research or historiographical (lit-review) paper, to be due on Monday, May 9. In addition, there will be two short papers assigned. Term-paper topics are by no means confined to Europe or the modern era; feel free to pick a case study from any other part of the world or time period (as long as your choice of region conforms with the major or minor field towards which you are counting this course). I will be glad to review drafts of your papers in advance of the submission deadline (May 9). Specific goals: To be added This course is also designed to address the following elements of the departmental graduate-level learning goals: 1. Students will develop the ability to absorb vast quantities of materials relating to specific historiographical debates, and learn how to evaluate scholarly arguments by analyzing responses to previous contributions, investigating use of primary evidence, weighing logical construction and development, and assessing rhetorical style. 2. Students will hone their skills in conducting secondary research, familiarizing themselves with major global library holdings, digital databases, and tools for identifying and acquiring access to published material. Students will be able to assess the extent of available published materials on any specific historical topic in their field.

title: Comparative History
description: Explores key historical developments across the world since about 1500 through comparative perspectives. Emphases include the rise and fall of empires, revolutions, industrialization, state making, and nationalism.

title: Latin American Origins and Transformations
description: This course examines the origins of the nation-state in Latin America and how elites and subalterns participated in the shaping of modern states in the region. It provides a multi-disciplinary perspective on the problem, through readings by historians, political scientists, and anthropologists. Usually taught in conjunction with LASP 501, for Master's-level students of the Latin American Studies program.

title: Arab Intellectuals in the Modern World
description: Selected topics in Arabic intellectual history during the 19th and 20th centuries. The course will look at debates about Arab nationalism, socialism and Islamism as well as a variety of issues such as democracy, social justice, gender, heresy, pluralism and globalization.

title: Post-Colonial North Africa
description: The course offers advanced readings on the topic of French colonialism in North Africa (Maghrib) in order to acquaint students with the main theoretical frameworks of (post/)colonial studies. It will highlight recent trends in historical scholarship on the Maghrib by addressing such issues as the impact of colonialism on the formation of cultural/national identities; the various modes of interaction between "colonizers" and "colonized;" representation, resistance, and subjectivity; the concepts of "subalternity" and "overlapping histories;" the processes of decolonization; and the construction of (post/)colonial "memories."

title: Approaching Ottoman History
description: The aim of this course is to introduce graduate students to Ottoman studies and to equip them with the basic historiographical, theoretical and practical knowledge required to start research on Ottoman history. The course will be divided into two parts. The first one is devoted to the evolution of Ottomanist historiography, major scholarly debates (problems of periodization, the ghaza and decline theses etc.) as well as to some selected topics. While there are numerous possible topics one can discuss (e.g. state formation, the Ottomans芒聙聶 understanding of Islam, military slavery, Ottoman pragmatism and flexibility, imperial ideology etc.), the list of actual themes is compiled in consultation with the students and takes into consideration their specific interests and research projects. The second part of the course deals with archival and published sources. Here too, the actual list of archives, major manuscript collections, source publications and types of source material we consider reflects the students芒聙聶 interests and expertise and varies accordingly from course to course.

title: US Labor and Social History
description: This course will survey U.S. social and labor history from the mid-nineteenth century through the end of the twentieth century. It will provide a basic historiographical understanding of development of labor and working-class history. And it will explore the ways in which labor and working-class history has been shaped by developments in the historiography of race, gender, immigration, business, state-building, and law.

title: Occupation, Collaboration and Resistance in W W II
description: Collaboration with, and resistance to, the Axis powers had a significant impact on the dynamics of the Second World War and on the fate of those societies that fell under the Axis yoke during that conflict. This course takes a comparative and global perspective on the political and ideological dimensions of collaboration with Germany, Italy, and Japan in occupied and/or allied countries of Europe and Asia during World War II. Among the topics and issues to be addressed: What sources of legitimacy (e.g. national self-determination aspirations, racial internationalism, anti-Communism, anti-imperialism, pragmatic mitigation of occupation's harshness) did collaborationist regimes and groups draw on, and which resonated most deeply among broad cross-sections of society? How effective or self-defeating were the Axis powers in their responses to collaborationist opportunities? What was the relationship between the diverse juridical and administrative forms of occupation/domination and the various dynamics of collaboration and resistance? How did the notorious "grey zone" between collaboration and resistance expand, contract, and shift shapes in the course of the war? What were the practical and moral consequences of collaboration? How have memories of (or amnesias about) collaboration and resistance shaped post-war political development in countries formerly occupied by, or allied with, the Axis powers? Classes will concentrate on discussion of weekly assigned readings. Term papers can focus on any aspect of collaboration and/or resistance in any country or countries involved in the Second World War.

title: China's Challenge to the U.S.
description: This class examines Chinese-American relations from the late 19th to the early 21st century. It looks at issues of security, politics and economic exchange as well as cultural interaction, and contrasting values and institutions. Students will be asked to examine current developments in historical perspective, coming to appreciate the motives and myths that guide policies. The class will also explore the prognosis for the relationship given the complex legacies of the past that impede mutual understanding.

title: China in World History
description: Perhaps because of a growing awareness of China芒聙聶s global demographic and economic weight, China has been the focal point of controversies in the field of world history. By examining these debates, this seminar evaluates China's changing global significance as way to introduce students to the most important recent writings on world history. The course is geared towards graduate students interested in global, international, and comparative history, as well as China specialists who want to find ways to make their research more relevant to historians of other parts of the world.

title: Culture, Politics, and Identity in a Divided Germany, 1945-1989
description: This graduate course will provide a stimulating and wide-ranging examination of the parallel political, cultural, and social histories of the two German states 芒聙聯 the capitalist liberal-democratic West and the Socialist East 芒聙聯 that were built on the ruins of the genocidal and militaristic Nazi dictatorship after 1945. While the focus of the reading will initially be on the first two post-war decades (which have been at the centre of historical scholarship in recent years), the course will cover the whole period of German division until the collapse of communism in 1989. The culture, politics, and identity of these two separate states on opposing sides of the ideological divide of the Cold War will be regarded as 芒聙聵asymmetrically interwoven with each other芒聙聶. Students will thus gain a thorough understanding of West German history beyond the redemptive tale of a smooth transition to liberal-democracy after 芒聙聵zero hour芒聙聶, insights into how best to interpret the 芒聙聵second German dictatorship芒聙聶, and the skills necessary to interpret the complex linkages between two German states that were 芒聙聵divided, but not disconnected芒聙聶 during the Cold War. The topics covered include, but are not limited to, the multifaceted legacies of war; the quest to establish new national identities and founding myths; memories of the past (both public representations and private perspectives); territorial division, including the construction of the Berlin wall; Jewish life in post-war Germany; immigration and 芒聙聵guest worker芒聙聶 programs; popular culture and the impact of consumerism; women, gender, and the family; 芒聙聵Americanization芒聙聶 and 芒聙聵Sovietization芒聙聶; youth rebellion; political protest and violence; Eastern dissident movements; and the revolution of 1989. The course aims to combine empirical reconstructions about these topics with methodological and conceptual discussions that will aide students in their future study and research. As part of our weekly seminar we will also explore themes such as transnational and comparative history writing, gender, generation, class, and ethnicity as markers of social identity, and we will critique some of the broader concepts used to study twentieth century Europe, such as 芒聙聵Americanization芒聙聶 and 芒聙聵the postwar芒聙聶.

title: Collective Identities in 20th C Europe
description: This colloquium will focus on how collective identities like nation, class, gender, race, religion, and ethnicity have defined and motivated Europeans and how these identities have evolved over the century. For example we shall examine: how collective identities functioned as causes or explanations of events, e.g. nationalism and the First World War, or in creating political movements, e.g. social class and socialism and communism. Similarly we shall look at how collective identities competed with or complemented one another, e.g. nation vs. class, or gender vs. class. We conclude with questions about the alleged decline or transformation of traditional loyalties like nation, class, and religion and the rise of commitment to other sub national and transnational identities. 芒聙聹How different were the identities of Europeans in 1914 from today?芒聙聺 is the grand question we will address. Students will be asked to write one short paper (5 pages) due at mid semester and one longer (12 to 15 pages) paper.

title: Production, Power, and Culture in Mexico
description: Readings in core themes of Mexican history from the era of national independence through the transformations of the 20th-century revolution. Focus is on changing economic structures, state organizations, and social relations, along with related cultural constructions, conflicts, and transformations.

title: Political History of North Africa
description: The course introduces the students to readings on the political, social, and cultural profiles of modern North Africa (Maghrib) from the establishment of the Ottoman Regency of Algiers in the early sixteenth century until national independence and the post-colonial period. It also examines the current state of North African historiography, and presents an overview of the principal schools of thought on the history of the Maghrib. Topics of study include: the impact of Ottoman European colonialism on North African societies, states, and economies; the social, economic, cultural, and political dimensions of colonial rule and administration; the rise of Maghribi anti-colonial and nationalist voices; the different paths taken by Morocco, Algeria, Tunisia, and Libya towards independence; and the various issues confronting the post-independence state.

title: Colloquium: Muscovy
description: This course will touch upon the political and institutional development of Russia, 1300-1613, but will focus upon religion, culture, and ideas of this long period. We will work with original sources, which include architecture, the plastic arts, and liturgies, as well as standard written documents. Permission of the instructor required.

title: Major Approaches to Russian and Soviet History.
description: This course is a core colloquium providing a thorough, analytical examination of the major schools and approaches that have shaped and continue to influence modern Russian and Soviet history. The focus is on English-language historiography, although Russian/Soviet and European contributions and trends are considered as well. The course begins by examining the history of Russian Studies after World War II, and the proceeds to examine the split between those interpreting imperial Russia as European and those understanding its trajectory as a special path. It then imparts a critical, in-depth examination of such areas as the semiotics of Russian culture, the theory of dual polarization, totalitarianism theory, social history and revisionism, the history of everyday life, the cultural and linguistic turn, and, most recently, transnational history. But the course is about more than historiography. It also proceeds chronologically to treat those key historical developments, processes, and turning points from the imperial period to the Soviet period that have been focal points of the successive methodologies and interpretations in the field. Major topics examined include the state and political system; roots of revolution; total war and political violence; empire and borderlands; Soviet civilization; and the disputed question of Russian and Soviet modernity.

title: History 685. Slavery, Civil War, and Emancipation in the U.S.
description: Once regarded as something of an intellectual backwater, or at least the refuge of the amateur enthusiast, the field of Civil War history is currently in ferment, fostering new journals, new professional societies, and new questions, and influencing fields outside Civil War history in important new ways. Many reasons account for the resurgence, but one of the most important is wider recognition (both inside and outside the field) of Civil War history芒聙聶s inextricability from the world histories of slavery and emancipation. We will focus on identifying the present state of key historiographical conversations: what do the driving questions seem to be right now, how did those questions grow from prior work and how do the answers change what we thought we knew about slavery, the Civil War and emancipation? Students will work toward the goal of gaining command over the current state of the field, which is to say gaining fluency in the central questions and themes occupying the field right now, and identifying important questions and directions that cry out for further investigation.

title: Topics in the History of Late Imperial China (Ming and Qing Dynasties)
description: This is a graduate reading course on the history of late imperial China, primarily the Qing dynasty. There have been exciting developments in the historiography of this period over the past few years, which we will examine, along with major milestones of the development of the field. Each week we will read and survey a number of books and articles on one theme from the considerable literature in English on this period. Students will be expected to read at least one monograph and familiarize themselves with other works in preparation for class each week.

title: East Asia research seminar
description: Students will prepare a significant original research paper on a topic in East Asian history based on primary sources in Chinese, Japanese, and other relevant languages. Students will select research topics, explore historical materials in detail, and compile bibliographies of primary and secondary sources with the instructor芒聙聶s guidance before undertaking research and writing.

title: Theory/Practice in Early Modern European History
description: Many of the intellectuals whose theories undergird so much current historiography have developed those theories looking at early modern Europe. In France, Michel Foucault, Michel de Certeau, and Pierre Bourdieu, to cite obvious cases, have created far-reaching theories in thinking about early modern societies. In Germany, Reinhard Kosellech and Juergen Habermas, or, going back even further, Norbert Elias and even Karl Marx, have done so. Our class will look at the writings of some of these theorists in a given week, then follow up the next week with individualized readings that show historians putting the theories to work. In their papers, students will follow Foucault's "archeology of knowledge" approach, looking at the intellectual roots of given theorists, as well as at their subsequent impact.

title: Islamic Movements
description: This course examines the utility of social movement theory as an approach for the study of the history and development of Islamic movements in the modern and contemporary world. A general introduction to social movement theory is followed by a critical analysis of a series of major studies and movements. Each person undertakes a study of a particular movement.

title: US as a World Power - Research Seminar
description: This course explores critical events and controversial issues in the history of American foreign relations during the 20th century. In the autumn term it is organized as a reading colloquium emphasizing the complexity of the international environment and the literature that tries to capture it as well as questions of historical interpretation. Enrollment is open to students from diverse PhD and MA programs to enliven classroom debate. In the spring semester students who continue in the course shift gears and engage in original research in primary source materials and write either dissertation chapters or articles of publishable quality.

title: The United States as a World Power
description: This course is a continuation of History 790 that explored critical events and controversial issues in the history of American foreign relations during the 20th and 21st centuries. Students engage in original research in primary source materials and write articles of publishable quality or dissertation chapters. Senior scholars will talk with students about strategies for research and writing.

title: Transregional Research Seminar
description: A year-long research seminar in which students will produce a publishable article. In the fall semester, we will read some exemplary works and explore some of the methodological issues connected to writing transregional history. Students will also produce a research prospectus. The spring semester is devoted to research and writing. Students enrolled in this class will also be required to participate in one of the History Department芒聙聶s Institute for Global History seminars (either the Early Modern Global History seminar or the International History Seminar). Only students who have taken History 803 may enroll in History 804.

title: Transregional Research Seminar
description: A year-long research seminar in which students will produce a publishable article. In the fall semester, we will read some exemplary works and explore some of the methodological issues connected to writing transregional history. Students will also produce a research prospectus. The spring semester is devoted to research and writing. Students enrolled in this class will also be required to participate in one of the History Department芒聙聶s Institute for Global History seminars (either the Early Modern Global History seminar or the International History Seminar). Only students who have taken History 803 may enroll in History 804.

title: Middle East Research Seminar
description: A full-year research seminar on the social and cultural history of the Middle East, with particular emphasis on the Arab World in the early modern and modern periods. In the fall semester, we read exemplary recent work in the field, with attention to issues of theoretical approach, method and sources. In the spring semester, students undertake individual research projects. Participants may take the fall semester only, but only those who have taken the fall semester may enroll in the spring semester segment.

title: Middle East Research Seminar - Social Movements
description: A full-year research seminar on the social history of the Middle East, with particular emphasis on the Arab World in the early modern and modern periods. In the fall semester, we read exemplary recent work in the field, with attention to issues of theoretical approach, method and sources. In the spring semester, students undertake individual research projects. Participants may take the fall semester only, but only those who have taken the fall semester may enroll in the spring semester segment.

title: Language of Health and Disease
description: This course is designed for the student to become conversant in the terminology and technology associated with human health, disease, and health care in order to effectively participate and communicate in health care administration. Case studies and other applications will be analyzed and discussed that relate to today芒聙聶s preventive, diagnostic, therapeutic, and rehabilitative practices. Mastery of the concepts and terminology presented will provide a fundamental vocabulary of human health and disease employed in modern health care practice and its management.

title: Human Biology I
description: A series of lectures and laboratory experiences devoted to the anatomy of the human are presented. Basic biological, principles are introduced and the integrative systems of the body are also studied. Three lecture hours and one three-hour laboratory. Parallel Courses: CHEM-001 or HSCI-110. Fall.

title: Human Biology II
description: A series of lectures and laboratory experiences devoted to the physiology of the human are presented. The circulatory respiratory, digestive, excretory, and reproductive systems are studied in detail. Laboratory experiences include blood pressure determination, cardiovascular physiology as demonstrated on the dog, and urinalysis. Three lecture hours and one three-hour laboratory. Prerequisite: HSCI-101, CHEM-001 or HSCI-110. Parallel course: CHEM-002 or HSCI-111. Spring.

title: Biochemistry and Human Functioning
description: This is a one semester biochemistry lecture non-laboratory course primarily intended for undergraduate nursing and international health majors. It is also suitable for other students who desire a one semester biochemistry survey course with human clinical applications. HSCI-111 will not fulfill pre-medical, pre-dental or graduate school requirements for a laboratory biochemistry course.

title: Health Promotion and Disease Prevention
description: This course provides an introduction to the theory and application of health promotion principles. Current issues and controversies in health promotion will be discussed. Students will utilize needs assessment data to plan a health promotion program as an outcome of the course. Learning Objectives At the completion of this course the student will be able to 1. Discuss the domestic and global perspectives on health promotion. 2. Discuss the planning process to design a health promotion intervention: needs assessment, culture, program philosophy, mission, goals, objectives, and evaluation. 3. Describe and apply theories/models used in designing health promotion and disease prevention strategies. 4. Discuss marketing of health promotion programs. 5. Discuss the evaluation of health promotion programs. 6. Identify the qualities of effective assessment instruments used in needs assessment. 7. Discuss health literacy as a cross-cutting issue impacting quality of health care. 8. Discuss current topics in health promotion. HSCI Laboratory Learning Objectives: At the completion of this course students will be able to: 1. List and describe the human science fundamentals of a given public health problem, including the larger program and policy context of the problem. 2. Identify and explain the activities of major federal, state and local public health agencies that promote public health and perform needed regulatory functions that support public health and disease prevention 3. Describe the influence of foundations and associations on public health. 4. Discuss the uses and limitations of web-based public health information.

title: Research Theory and Communication in Science and Healthcare
description: This course is designed to introduce students to the theories of quantitative and qualitative research using healthcare as its focus. It is intended to lay a conceptual foundation about research and we will explore these concepts using examples of research in the basic sciences, clinical practices, and domestic and international healthcare systems. This course also will introduce fundamental practices in communicating science through written exercises and oral presentations.

title: Introduction to Genetics and Genomics
description: This course is designed to provide nursing students with a basic understanding of the form and function of DNA, genomic organization, cytogenetics, principles of simple and complex inheritance, whole genome association, and genomic diversity in disease. Translational and clinical applications will be emphasized throughout the course, utilizing case presentations, problem-based learning, and lectures.

title: Microbiology
description: A series of lectures and laboratory experiences devoted to the pathogenic bacteria and viruses of humans. The mechanism of pathogenicity, the clinical picture of the disease, prophylaxis, and therapeutic are discussed. The laboratory experiences include identification of pathogens in sputum, spinal fluid, blood, urine, and stool cultures. Aseptic technique is stressed in the laboratory. Three lecture hours and one three-hour laboratory. Prerequisites: CHEM-001 and -002 or HSCI-110 and -111, HSCI-101 and -102 or permission of the instructors. Fall.

title: Pathophysiology
description: This course provides foundational knowledge which a health care professional needs to understand illness, its manifestations in a person, the anatomical and physiological basis for prevention and treatment. Genetics, biological and other agents of disease are discussed. The body's response to disease is examined with an emphasis placed on the inflammatory response. The disorders discussed are representative of those commonly occurring in the population.

title: Group Dynamics in Health Promotion
description: This seminar course is offered for 3 credit hours and will examine the role of students芒聙聶 teamwork, leadership skills, developmental theory and community dynamics to improve the health and well-being of college students. The campus community as the setting in which health promotion takes place will be integrated throughout the course. The course will feature a high level of discussion and student engagement, and will include case analysis, theory presentations, student-led seminar discussions, and student group projects. Explore questions like: What is useful about exploring 芒聙聹the campus community芒聙聺 as a unit of measure in pursuing health promotion efforts? How is a campus community distinct from and similar to other kinds of local and virtual communities? What theories of leadership provide useful lenses for looking at how individuals can make a difference in health promotion? What theories are useful in understanding the developmental transitions and tasks faced by college students? What characterizes a high-performing team and distinguishes it from an average team? How are teams uniquely situated to address community health issues and engage in health promotion?

title: Immunology
description: The field of immunology studies how the body distinguishes between what is self and what is non-self. Although we are accustomed to thinking about immunology with respect to pathogens and disease, the function of the immune system in fighting microbes is really a manifestation of its fundamental role as a system whereby the body knows itself. Throughout our lives, the immune system gathers and interprets information about the body. When something is not right, whether it is due to a microbial pathogen, or traumatic tissue damage, or cells growing abnormally to become a tumor, or the presence of transplanted foreign tissue, our immune system is activated to try to restore homeostasis. When it malfunctions it contributes to disease. In this course, we will learn the basic principles governing the functioning of the human immune system.
