Students are expected to work on problems dealing with phonology, morphology, and syntax. The course ends with the discussion of semantics, historical linguistics, and language and culture.

title: Forensic Linguistics
description: This course provides students with an overview of the broad field of Forensic Linguistics 芒聙聯 namely, linguistic science applied in legal settings. Topics covered include the language of police and suspects (e.g. the language of 芒聙聵consensual芒聙聶 searches, interrogations, confessions, advising of/requesting right to counsel); language crimes (e.g. solicitation, conspiracy, bribery, threats, perjury, plagiarism); and linguistics as evidence (e.g. identifying anonymous authors of documents associated with criminal activity, identifying anonymous voices). We will also discuss the status of linguistic evidence in court and how best to disseminate relevant knowledge about linguistics to law enforcement practitioners, judges, lawyers, juries, etc. The course will incorporate lecture, class discussion, demonstrations and exercises pertaining to language crimes and linguistic evidence, and presentations by practitioners in the field. Students will conduct a term research project and present their results in (1) a final presentation to the class at the end of the term, and (2) a final paper suitable for presentation at a professional linguistics or forensic linguistics conference. Students will also be expected to participate actively in class and complete all in-class exercises.

title: Field Methods
description: Prof. S. Sara, S.J. I.C.C. 455 Spring 2012 Class: M-W 5:00-6:15 p.m. O.hs: (Poulton 250) M-W ICC 221A by appointment: anytime Tel:(202)687-6226/4004 e-mail:Saras@georgetown.edu Informant: Goals of the Course: This course will simulate the situation of a field linguist, as we attempt to understand the structure of an unfamiliar language. The class will elicit data orally from a native speaker, the students transcribe the utterances, submit them to analysis and thus develop skills of linguistic analysis as we cumulatively proceed through phonological, morphological and syntactic topics. The aim of the course will be to produce a mini grammar of the language with its phonology, morphology and syntax Procedures of Elicitation and Analysis: In general, every class will begin with an elicitation session. We will prepare lists of materials and work with the informant as a group, with each student preparing her/his own transcriptions. This will be followed by a period in which we discuss the analyses of the previous class芒聙聶 materials. At the end of each class we will again consult the informant on topics which have arisen during the discussion. There will be frequent oral and written progress reports on the class work, and a final report on the language. Adjustments along the way will be made as the need arises. Intended Audience for the Course: The course is open to undergraduate and graduate students. Prerequisites for this course are basic linguistic courses. Linguistics 404 will be useful to any student who foresees doing fieldwork in any area of linguistics; we all end up doing some field work. It may also be attractive to those students who simply wish to experience the unmediated contact with an unknown language and culture. Finally, those interested in any area(s) of theoretical linguistics will find the challenge of confronting an unknown language to provide excellent practice for their analytical skills. Language: Spoken Semitic language like Hebrew or Arabic. The choice of language is significant. Semitic structure is different from Indo-European, so this gives one a fresh experience of an unknown structure and its complexity. The preferred choice of a language will be the least studied dialect of the language which gives one more freedom not to be beholden to previous analyses.. Short description: The course will simulate the situation of a field linguist, as we attempt to understand the structure of an unfamiliar language. The class will elicit data orally from a native speaker, and students will develop skills of linguistic analysis as the class cumulatively proceeds through phonological, morphological and syntactic topics. The aim is to sharpen one芒聙聶s linguistic skills and in the process to produce an explanatory description of as much of the language as possible. Prerequisites for the class are: interest and any basic phonetics, phonology, morphology and syntax courses. The course is open to undergraduate and graduate students. The time frame is that each of the three language components will be given its equal share of time. Readings: Bowern, Clair. 2008. Linguistic Fieldwork: A practical Guide. New York: Palgrave. Bouquiax, Luc and J.M.C. Thomas. 1992. Studying and Describing languages. SIL Burling, Robins. 1984. Learning a Field Language. Ann Arbor: Univ. of Michigan Press. Comrie, Bernard and Norval Smith. 1977. Lingua Descriptive Studies: Questionnaire. Lingua 42. 1-72, not a textbook but a guide to describing data. Crowley, Terry. 2007. Field Linguistics: A Beginner芒聙聶s Guide. New York: Oxford. Kibrik, A. K. 1977. Methodology of Field Investigation in Linguistics. The Hague: Mouton. Ladefoged, P. 2003. Phonetic Data Analysis: An Introduction to Fieldwork and Instrumental Phonetics. New York: Blackwell Newman, Paul, and Martha Ratliff. 2001. Linguistic Fieldwork: New York: Cambridge. Pike, Kenneth. 1947. Phonemics: A Technique for Reducing Languages to Writing. Ann Arbor: The Univ. of Michigan Press. Samarin, William. 1967. Field Linguistics: A Guide to Linguistic Field Work. New York: Holt Rinehart and Winston. Vaux, Bert and Justin Cooper. 1999. Introduction to Linguistic Field Methods. Munchen: Lincom Europa.

title: Language and New Media
description: Ling. 405 Language and Social Media Fall 2011 (Tannen) When and where: Tuesday 2:40-5:10 Professor: Deborah Tannen ICC 471; 687-2637 (no voice mail) office hours: Tue 5:15-6:15 & by appointment, gladly email: tannend1@georgetown.edu This is a course in the discourse analysis of digital conversational interaction. We will examine the structure and use of language in electronic social media such as e-mail, Instant Messaging, SMS, texting, Facebook, Twitter, personal blogging, and so on. The class will survey prior research on this topic, and students will conduct original research by gathering and analyzing naturally-occurring discourse in one or more of these media. Students will present the findings of their research in small groups and to the entire class. The goals of the course are 1) to examine and analyze the structure and use of digital social media discourse and 2) to investigate the ways that communicating via these media shapes and changes interpersonal relationships. The first half of each class will be devoted to lecture and discussion of the readings. The second half will be devoted to analysis and discussion of student-gathered discourse. Required reading: Baron, Naomi S. 2008. Always on: Language in an online and mobile world. New York and Oxford: Oxford University Press. Crystal, David. 2009. Txtng: The gr8 db8. New York and Oxford: Oxford University Press. Gershon, Ilana. 2010. The breakup 2.0: Disconnecting over new media. Ithaca, NY: Cornell University Press. Articles (see syllabus and reading list) Other requirements: 1. Attendance in class 2. Assigned readings 3. 1-page summary/response for each week's readings 4. 10 field notes 5. 2 analytic papers: one 5 and one 10 pages long Written responses to required readings. In lieu of a final exam, students write brief summaries of and critical responses to each week's readings. These "reading responses" must be handed in at the time of class; they will be graded weekly. Do not email work unless special arrangements have been made in advance. 10 field notes describing and commenting on your own use or observation of new media discourse. When you experience, recall, or observe interactions that bear a relation to what we have read or discussed in class, write them down and discuss how the course readings or discussions shed light on the communication you experienced or observed, or how the interaction you describe sheds light on or relates to course readings or classroom discussion. In some cases, I will assign a specific observation and ask you to write a field note on that. If no specific instructions have been given for a particular week, field notes need not relate to that week's readings; they may be relevant to another week's readings or to the course material in general. Field notes, which are typically a single page single-spaced but may be shorter or longer (minimum is one paragraph each for description and comment), will be handed in and graded weekly. Here too: please, no emails. I may on occasion read a field note aloud to the class, but I will always ask the student芒聙聶s permission before I do so. Two analytic papers; one five-page, one ten-page. In each of two papers, students present examples of electronic discourse that they gathered, and analyze it in relation to course readings or discussion. Each paper must include reference to at least two outside sources that were not assigned in class. These papers will have the form of academic papers in the field of discourse analysis. Topics and guidelines will be distributed. Attendance is required. Since there are no exams, it is crucial that students attend all classes. If you are forced to miss a class due to illness or other unavoidable emergency, you must notify me in advance; provide documentation on your return; and make up the work in an appropriate writing assignment. Please note that lateness = partial absence, so don't miss class because you're printing something out that's due that day. Prerequisite: (1) At least one previous course in analyzing discourse, such as Cross-Cultural Communication; Women, Men and Language; Text and Talk; Discourse Analysis: Conversation; or Discourse Analysis: Narrative, or (2) permission of instructor. Email: I will communicate with the class by email. You are responsible for checking your email and reading these messages. Limitations: No late papers, no incompletes, no auditors, no laptops in class (and, again, attendance is required). If you take this class, you agree to these limitations. Grading: 25% reading responses; 25% field notes; 25% each paper. Class participation is expected.

title: Phonology and Phonetics I
description: This course is designed as a one-semester graduate-level introduction to the basics of phonetics and phonology, that is, to the linguistic analysis of the sound patterns of the languages of the world. It is divided into four sections: 1. Articulatory phonetics (how speech sounds are made) 2. Acoustic phonetics (how sound travels and how sound is measured) 3. Segmental phonology (how sounds contrast and alternate) 4. Suprasegmental phonology (how sounds are grouped into hierarchical constituents) At the end of the class, students will have learned the terms and concepts central to phonology and phonetics, and will have acquired basic data analysis skills (including IPA transcription, recording and basic acoustic analysis, and approaches to solving phonology problems), and will be prepared to move on to more advanced courses. In order to accomplish these goals, students will participate in daily production and transcription drills and complete daily homework assignments to reinforce concepts and skills. There will be four (non-cumulative) quizzes, one for each quarter of the course. Prerequisites and course sequencing: This course has no prerequisites, but is itself prerequisite for all other graduate-level courses in phonology and phonetics. Ling410, Phonology and Phonetics I, is the first in a three-course sequence.

title: Phonetics and Phonology II
description: Ling 411: Phonetics and Phonology II Course description This course is the second in a two-semester sequence: it continues the introduction to phonetics and phonology begun in either Ling 215 (for undergrads) or Ling 410 (for grad students). The four topic areas introduced in the first semester course are explored in greater depth in 411. These are: 1. Articulatory phonetics (how speech sounds are made). We will explore the IPA chart in greater depth, with more production and transcription practice with non-English sounds. We will also learn more about the anatomy and physiology of speech production. 2. Acoustic phonetics (how sound travels and how sound is measured). After a review of the source/filter model of speech production, we will explore algorithms for digital speech analysis (pitch tracks, spectrograms, etc.), including extensive practice with the Praat signal analysis program. We will also discuss the basics of hearing and speech perception. 3. Segmental phonology (how sounds contrast and alternate). We will further explore rule-based and constraint-based approaches to phonological theory, tackling more challenging data with more sophisticated analytical tools. We will also discuss some principles of historical sound change, sociolinguistic variation, and first and second language acquisition. 4. Suprasegmental phonology (prosody, tone, and stress). More data and analysis on cross-linguistic patterns of syllable structure, prosodic domains, stress patterns, tone, and intonation. Students will complete transcription assignments for every class, weekly homework assignments that reinforce concepts and skills, two reading reports that will introduce students to the primary literature in this area, and one final project that will allow students to demonstrate analytical skills in both phonology and phonetics. At the end of the class, students will have acquired the skills necessary to: 1. Understand the major points of current phonological and phonetic theories 2. Follow a talk on phonology or phonetics with critical understanding 3. Record, transcribe, and analyze speech for their own linguistic investigations 4. Be prepared to move on to more advanced courses that focus on current theoretical problems and research Prerequisite: Ling 215 (Sounds of Speech), or Ling 410 (Phonetics and Phonology I), or permission of the instructor Readings: This course has been organized to follow the format of my new textbook, which will be published next year by Wiley-Blackwell (The Sounds of Language: An Introduction to Phonetics and Phonology). However, the text has not yet appeared in print. Therefore, readings for each class will be posted to the Blackboard site. (Essentially, we will cover the chapters/topics that were skipped in semester 1.)

title: Language and Education
description: What role does dialect play in educational outcomes? How can the tools of sociolinguistic analysis provide insight into the public discourse on education? What can concerned sociolinguists do to advance the cause of educational equity? Through a highly participatory seminar, students explore these questions to develop a more informed perspective on addressing language variation in educational contexts, deepen their understanding of contemporary issues in education policy and practice, and gain skills in developing and implementing sociolinguistic educational interventions. The primary focus of course content is on first-language education in the US context, with particular emphasis on issues related to African American Vernacular English.

title: Sociophonetics
description: This course examines the phonetic aspects of sociolinguistic variation and the social significance of phonetic variation, from the perspectives of both theory and methodology. Our goals will be to (a) acquire the phonetic foundation necessary to conduct sociophonetic research, pursuing social lines of inquiry with proper techniques for phonetic analysis; (b) locate and explain phonetic variation in its full social context while drawing on current approaches to the relationship between language and society; and (c) survey new research that addresses the intersection of the social and the phonetic. We will address all domains of sound, including vowels, consonants, prosody, and voice quality. Class time will consist of lectures, discussions, and data workshops. Prerequisites: (a) Ling 213 or Ling 510 (or equivalent) and (b) at least one previous sociolinguistics course

title: Introduction to Statistical Natural Language Processing
description: This course will introduce students to the basics of statistical natural language processing (NLP), a field in which the tools of statistical analysis and learning are applied to traditional natural language processing tasks such as part-of-speech tagging, parsing and natural language understanding. We will review traditional concepts from probability theory and statistics, show how these are applied to language processing tasks. We will cover a range of topics, moving from simple representations of language, such as finite-state techniques and n-gram analysis, to more advanced representations, such as those found in probablistic context-free grammars. Students who take this course will gain a thorough understanding of the fundamental methods used in statistical NLP.

title: Ethnography of Poetry
description: The Text: A reading package of collection 100 poems from various cultures. Other materials: T. S. Eliot, Selected Essays (1932); S. Langer, Feeling and Form (1933); Paul Valery, Selected Writings (1964). 1. Introduction: Ethnography of poetry: Eliot on Dante. 2. Poetry as ethnography of life: (the life of emotions and desires; an instrument of knowledge, an art in the service of culture and civilization; a collection of words that bears in itself the substance of culture: Eliot on Shakespeare; , Langer, Chapters 1, 2, 3; Valery, Essays on various subjects. 3. Poetry and feeling: Langer, Poesis; Valery, On poetry 4. Readings, analysis, and discussion: From the collection of 100 poems from various cultures.

title: Diachronic Syntax
description: Description: When children develop their native language capacity, they acquire properties for which there is no evidence in the speech they encounter; these are so-called 芒聙聹emergent phenomena.芒聙聺 Furthermore, children sometimes acquire systems that are significantly different from those of their parents and the language undergoes rapid structural change, a 芒聙聹phase transition.芒聙聺 We can understand emergent phenomena and phase transitions through a principled approach to language acquisition by young children. We will consider the methods, successes and failures of 19th-century work on language change, which was the foundation for a new discipline of linguistics and of great interest to thinkers in evolutionary biology, beginning with Darwin, and early researchers in what we now call political science, including Marx. We will then move to language acquisition, arguments from the poverty of the stimulus and cue-based acquisition. We will investigate how historical change can be understood (within limits) through children芒聙聶s acquisition and how it enables us to understand the birth and death of languages more generally. This will involve a crucial distinction between external and internal languages and new methods for historical linguistics. Throughout the course we will see how linguistics is a lead science in understanding change and complex dynamics quite generally. Texts: Lightfoot 2006 How New Languages Emerge. Cambridge UP. Ian Roberts 2007 Diachronic syntax. Oxford UP. Requirements: students need at least one course in generative syntax and will make a class presentation and write a paper dealing with a specific syntactic change in some language. Syllabus: Week 1 The beginnings: 19th-century work on language change: methods, successes and failures. Week 2 Syntax as a complex system, poverty of stimulus reasoning. What is learned and how. Week 3 Models of acquisition: learning algorithms, cue-based acquisition. Week 4 Phenomena of syntactic change in English: Modal auxiliaries, verb movement, be+part, change in meaning of like, repent, ail, etc. Methodologies. Week 5 Explanations for historical changes through acquisition (thresholds and phase transitions). E-language vs. I-language. The birth and death of languages (creoles, language endangerment) Week 6 Other explanations for change: grammar evaluation, principles of change (grammaticalization, UG biases). Gradualism. Week 7 More case studies: lexical passives, quantifiers, group genitives, infinitives. Week 8 Other languages: verb movement, null subjects. Week 9 Reconstruction of proto-syntax. Week 10 Evolution of the language faculty: mutations and natural selection. Week 11 How diachronic syntax differs from synchronic work and how it illuminates some of the idealizations of synchronic work. Week 12 New methods for historical linguistics, working with corpora. Research topics: microvariation and abstract categories. Linguistics as a lead science in complex dynamics. Weeks 13-14 Student presentations.

title: Historical Linguistics
description: The Textbook: W. P. Lehmann, Historical Linguistics (3rd edition). Other books: Raimo Anttila, And Introduction to Historical and Comparative Linguistics (1972); L. Bloomfield, Language (1933); L. Campbell, Historical Linguistics (1998); R. A. Hall, Introductory Linguistics (1964); C. F. Hockett, A Course in Modern Linguistics (1958); E. Sapir, Language (1921), Selected Writings (1949, David Mandelbaum, ed.). 1. Introduction: synchronic vs. diachronic linguistics; history and nature of historical research; beginning of historical research in language. Anttila Chapter1; Bloomfield Chapter 1, Lehmann, Chapter 2. 2. Writing and language: written records. Anttila 1, 2, 3; Bloomfield 17; Hall 44, 45; Hockett 42, 43, 62; Lehmann 4. 3. The nature of linguistic change: kinds and causes of linguistic change; lineal and collateral relationships; the discovery of linguistic relationships and groupings. Anttila 4-9; Bloomfield 17; Hall 44, 45; Hockett 42, 43, 62; Lehmann 10-15. 4. Classification of languages: genealogical and typological; major language families of the world. Lehmann 2, 3; Bloomfield 4; Hall 50, 51; Hockett 5-60. 5. Methods in comparative linguistics: (a) The comparative method: Anttila 10, 11, 13; Lehmann 7; Bloomfield 4; Hall 50, 51; Hockett 5-60. (b) Internal reconstruction: Anttila 12; Lehmann 8; Bloomfield 4; Hall 50, 51; Hockett 5-60. (c) On the nature of linguistic grouping: Lehmann 14. 6. Linguistic change and change in culture: Sapir, Selected writings, pp. 432-460.

title: History of the English Language
description: Starting with a preliminary look at the evolution of English from its prehistoric beginning, the course examines the internal history of English芒聙聰its sounds, grammar, and vocabulary. The Old, Middle, and Modern English sounds and grammar are individually compared and studied. Students are expected to work on problems of various types. Some ask the students to demonstrate the knowledge of specific facts to be derived from the textbook (The Origins and Development of the English Language by Thomas Pyles and John Algeo) others provide supplementary data for them to analyze and draw conclusion from.

title: Linguistics and Reading
description: This theoretical and practical course provides an in-depth investigation to the application of linguistics and reading instruction. Among the topics addressed are: processing strategies and their interactions; the influences of L1 features (orthographic systems, phonology, morphology) on English (L2) literacy practices; an investigation of writing systems of English and other languages; the role of listening and reading; different approaches to phonics instruction; an investigation of an analytical reading inventory, including word lists, miscue analyses, fluency, and multi-leveled comprehension questions; and formal and informal reading assessments. The course is designed to allow the student to develop a draft of a case study by the end of the semester.

title: Supervised Teaching Practicum
description: This class provides an intensive supervised student teaching experience in language classrooms. Participants are required to be at their assigned site for a minimum of 120 hours for 6 credit hours or 60 hours for 3 credit hours. This practicum provides opportunities for extended practice under the guidance of experienced professionals. Permission of instructor required. Credits: 3 or 6

title: Machine Translation
description: Course: Machine Translation - Lingustics 466 Instructor: Dr. George V. Wilson Course Text: "Statistical Machine Translation", by Philipp Koehn Grading: Assignments 30% Midterm 30% Term Paper 40% Major Topics: 1. Introduction 2. Why is Machine Translation difficult? 3. Transfer Systems - Taxonomy of transfer systems 4. Grammars and Parsers - Class develops a grammar and rule-based MT system 5. Extended Study of Syntactic Transfer Systems 6. Statistical MT - Parallel Corpora - Language Modeling - Alignment Models 7. Evaluation of MT systems 8. Internationalization

title: Language and Mind
description: Fish swim, birds fly and humans talk. Just as fish are genetically equipped to move in the way they do, so human beings have the cognitive properties to talk and to understand speech in the way they do. LING-273/473 Language and Mind Professor David Lightfoot MW 11:00 am- 12:15 pm In this class, we will seek to characterize that ability (a "language organ"), which may vary depending on where a person is raised: people have different capacities depending on whether they were raised in Toronto, Tokyo or Tromso. We shall try to tease apart general properties of the human language capacity, common to the species, from specific capacities which are a function of initial experience. We shall examine how the interplay of nature and nurture results in people coming to know so much more than they experience in childhood. We shall attend to (a) language variation, looking at languages as variants of a kind of basic "periodic table" which is genetically prescribed, (b) the acquisition of language by children in the first few years of life, as the basic periodic table develops into the specific capacity of, say, a speaker of some form of French, and (c) the capacity to understand spoken language. We shall compare what is known about the human language organ with other aspects of our cognitive make-up, considering how human cognition and the language faculty have been viewed by thinkers like Plato, Descartes, Darwin and others. No Prerequisites.

title: Discourse Analysis: Narrative
description: Narratives have been studied by many different disciplines: literary theory, clinical, cognitive and developmental psychology, folklore, anthropology, sociology and linguistics. The primary focus of this course is the sociolinguistic study of narrative through discourse analysis. Our initial attention is spontaneous oral narratives told during conversations or conversation-like interactions. However, we will also compare and contrast stories that are written and electronically communicated. Likewise, although our discussions and analyses will dwell primarily on narrative told by one person, in one place, and at one time, in everyday life, we will also touch on narratives that develop across time, places and persons. We begin by discussing basic features of discourse analysis and sociolinguistic frameworks for the study of narrative, while collecting and transcribing narratives for our own analyses. We then turn to how different parts of language (e.g. tense, referring terms, reported speech) are used to create a story world, and how that story world both constructs, and reflects, different facets of context (including identity, relationships and social interaction). Included will be discussion of very short stories, long life stories, public stories, retold performances of stories, intertextual stories, and the infiltration of the notion of 芒聙聹narrative芒聙聺 into therapy, medicine, politics, history, law and business.

title: Discourse Analysis: Conversation
description: Ling. 484 Discourse Analysis: Conversation (Tannen) Spring 2012 Tuesday 2-4:30 ICC 106 D. Tannen Poulton 256 admin. assistant: Gwynne Mapes 687-5910 email: tannend@georgetown.edu Description: This is a companion course, not a sequel, to Discourse Analysis: Narrative. Students may take one or both in any order. A combination lecture/workshop, the course introduces, and provides practice trying out, a variety of types of microanalysis of conversational discourse. The theoretical focus is interactional sociolinguistics with an anthropological orientation. Topics include conversational involvement; conversational style; coherence; turn-taking; transcription theory and practice; framing/positioning/stance; listenership; the poetic dimension of conversation; intertextuality; and identity. Requirements. Students record a naturally-occurring conversation that they happen to take part in, and choose a 10-minute to half-hour segment to work with. Of this, a short segment (2-3 minutes) is transcribed (3 ways) and analyzed throughout the semester. Students apply the methods and theories of assigned readings to their own and classmates' conversational segments. On 3 occasions, they write up these applications as a 3-page assignment, two of which may be revised and resubmitted. Students present analyses of their own segments in small groups, and each student is responsible for one 10-minute class presentation showing how one of the readings applies to their conversation. In a 10-15 page final paper, each student analyzes a different conversation, or the larger conversation from which the small segment came, or discourse of another type. Final papers are not simply long assignments (i.e. applications of a single reading) but rather employ a variant, combination, or alternative analytic method and include reference to at least three sources not included in required course readings. The final exam will be an oral one. With doctoral students in mind, the course focuses on training to write publishable academic papers. Other graduate students and advanced undergraduates with appropriate backgrounds are welcome. Required Reading: Tannen, Conversational Style: Analyzing Talk Among Friends Second Edition (Oxford, 2005). Tannen, Talking Voices: Repetition, Dialogue, and Imagery in Conversational Discourse, Second Edition (Cambridge, 2007) Articles to be made available in a packet. I will donate the equivalent of royalties from the sale of my own books to GU's DC Schools Project. Limitations: Attendance is required; participation in class discussion is expected. No late papers, no incompletes, no auditors, no laptops in class. If you take this class, you agree to these limitations.

title: Cognitive and Functional Approaches to Language
description: LING 485 : INTRODUCTION TO COGNITIVE and FUNCTIONAL APPROACHES TO LANGUAGE Professor Andrea Tyler Time: MW 5:00-6:15 pm A range of models fall under the general category of functional approaches to linguistic structure. For instance, the work of cognitive linguists (e.g. Goldberg, Langacker) as well as that of functional typologists (e.g. Givon, Hopper) all represent a functional perspective. In this course, we will focus on the core ideas that are shared by these models. Through the detailed analysis of linguistic data, we will consider the particular questions they raise and the insights into language they provide. Core concepts that will be highlighted include: -Language structure cannot be adequately understood or described in isolation from its context of use. There is a central interaction between syntax, meaning, and discourse such that no strict boundaries between syntax, semantics, pragmatics, and discourse exist. -The communicative functions served by language crucially influence the structures that occur in language. -No two structures are completely synonymous. People choose between 芒聙聹competing芒聙聺 structures, for instance between active and passive, in order to accomplish particular communicative goals. -The kinds of brains and the kinds of bodies human have, i.e. basic human biology, have important effects on the nature of language. Because humans all share the same basic neurological and physical architecture, languages will share universal or near universal properties. At least part of the structure of language is constrained by, and thus explained by, aspects of human cognitive processing. This course will be useful to any student interested in learning to think about linguistic structure in ways that relate meaning and context to linguistic form. This course meets the syntax requirement for MAT students.

title: MLC Proseminar
description: This course is a professional development course for students in the MA in Linguistics with a concentration in Language and Communication (MLC) Program. The course is designed to help students select an area of professional interest which they will research in depth to aid in designing targeted materials in 芒聙聹Storybuilding:芒聙聺 designing individual professional portfolios. The class will be a mixture of lecture, discussion, and activity-based interaction. We will develop our understanding of professional applications of sociolinguistics through readings and lectures and presentations by guest lecturers who themselves engage with the question of combining sociolinguistic theory.

title: Task-based Language Learning and Teaching
description: This course explores task-based approaches to second/foreign language learning and teaching using four organizational principles: (a) the theoretical underpinnings for task-facilitated language learning; (b) research frameworks for the evaluation of tasks as learning tools; (c) the process of developing and empirically testing task-based materials; (d) practical classroom considerations for the use/implementation of tasks in an instructional context. Assessment will include a short literature-based essay and a final project on communicative tasks in either a classroom or experimental context. This course can be taken as a regular graduate class (LING-494) or as a graduate seminar (LING-558). The syllabus and meeting times will be the same for each class, but seminar participants will undertake a final project that is more substantial in scope. Recommended background Reading: Ellis, R. (2003). Task-Based Language Teaching and Learning. Oxford: Oxford University Press.

title: Ethnography of Communication
description: This course is designed to introduce students to the Ethnography of Communication (EoC) an approach to the analysis of language and communication in context. The EoC approach, drawn principally from Anthropology and Linguistics, suggests that 芒聙聹what language is cannot be separated from how and why it is used.芒聙聺 The course is a blend of theory, practice, and method, with particular focus on 'participant observation.' Along with some of the basic 'core' articles that present this perspective, students will read about ethnographic work which has been done (and which is currently being done) on specific topics and in particular geographic locations, while at the same time conducting ethnographic observations within a chosen community or site of interaction (e.g. a coffee shop, a supermarket, a gym, an office, a team working together on a long term project, Gallaudet University). Class time will be a combination of lecture, work shopping of data (that collected by students as well as data brought by the professor drawn from larger ethnographic projects), and discussion (of course readings and students芒聙聶 application of the theoretical concepts in ongoing research projects). Course Objectives: To cultivate the skills linguistic observation and analysis through extended participant observation with a group that is culturally, socially, or linguistically different from the researcher. Students will learn to balance insider / outsider perspectives to discover the interactional norms both of the studied group as well as their own culture, based on the premise that the best way to learn about one芒聙聶s own 芒聙聹ways of speaking芒聙聺 is to contrast them with those of other communities, a process which reveals that what is assumed to be 芒聙聹natural芒聙聺 is in fact culturally unique. Course Goals: The goal of the course is to uncover some of the ways that language is intertwined with social actions, values, beliefs, group memberships, identities and social institutions.

title: Intercultural Communication
description: This course explores how language use varies from culture to culture, and how knowledge of these differences (and similarities) can help us to understand the interactional dynamics involved in intercultural contact, both across different societies and within a single society. In order to provide students with the tools for conducting original research projects on how these differences and similarities might be usefully studied, we will review major theoretical and methodological concepts in the field of intercultural communication, as well as discuss specific case studies of intercultural (mis)/communication in a variety of contexts, from casual interpersonal conversation to interaction in specific institutional settings. In addition to carrying out an original research project, students will be required to write a midterm project, present weekly journals, as well as summaries of scholarly articles to be discussed in class. The course will begin by looking at the big picture, exploring a range of linguistic and anthropological understanding of language communication and culture. Thereafter we will focus on specific topics and approaches of analysis. Emphasis will always be placed on how people use language in concrete situations, and how 芒聙聹interdiscourse communication芒聙聺 emerges from such use.

title: Linguistics Teaching Practicum
description: This course is designed for Linguistics graduate students interested in complementing their research skills with good teaching practice. It is required for all doctoral fellows. Good teaching is not a mystery, but a learnable skill and a rewarding intellectual endeavor. The course will meet on the second, third and fourth Tuesdays of each month throughout the fall and spring semesters to explore topics in teaching and learning. In addition, students will participate in at least two workshops offered by the Center for New Designs in Learning and Scholarship in each semester. In addition to investigating best practices of well-known classroom strategies, such as lecturing, leading discussions, designing assignments and group projects, and assessment, we will discuss such questions as 芒聙聹What does learner-centered teaching in Linguistics look like?芒聙聺 and 芒聙聹How do learning styles affect the Linguistics classroom?芒聙聺 We will also discuss collaborative learning, different kinds and uses of assessment, and the use of technology in the classroom. In the fall, participants will observe two linguistics classes (a graduate class, in which they may be enrolled, and an undergraduate class), and report on and discuss what they observe. Participants will also have a frank discussion with current and former TAs and graduate student Instructors about teaching in the Linguistics Department at Georgetown. In the spring, each participant will develop a lesson plan and related homework assignment, a syllabus for a course of his/her own choosing, and a statement of personal teaching philosophy. Each participant will present a teaching demonstration based upon his/her lesson plan; where possible, this demo class will be presented as a guest lecture in a real course. Demonstration classes will be videotaped, and each participant will receive his/her own videofile. Many colleges and universities want evidence of teaching training when hiring new faculty. The work products from this course can be included in a teaching portfolio (which is valuable in the academic job search). Participants who complete the course and all assignments will also receive a certificate attesting to their training.

title: Linguistics Teaching Practicum
description: This is a certificate course designed for Linguistics PhD students who wish to complement their research skills with good teaching practice. Good teaching is not a mystery, but a learnable skill and even a rewarding intellectual endeavor. The course will meet on the second, third and fourth Tuesdays of each month, from 11:40-12:55 throughout the fall and spring semesters, to explore topics in teaching and learning. In addition to investigating best practices of well-known classroom strategies, such as lecturing, leading discussions, designing assignments and group projects, and assessment, we will discuss such questions as, 芒聙聹What does learner-centered teaching in Linguistics look like?芒聙聺 or 芒聙聹How do learning styles affect the Linguistics classroom?芒聙聺 We will also discuss collaborative learning, different kinds and uses of assessment, and the use of technology in the classroom. In the fall, participants will observe several classes, and discuss what they observe. Participants will also have a frank discussion with current and former TAs and graduate student instructors, about teaching in the Linguistics Department at Georgetown. In the spring, each participant will develop a lesson plan and related homework assignment, a syllabus for a course of his/her own choosing, and a statement of personal teaching philosophy. Each participant will present a teaching demonstration based upon his/her lesson plan; where possible this demo class will be presented as a guest lecture in a real course. Demonstration classes will be videotaped, and each participant will receive his/her own videofile. Many colleges and universities want evidence of teacher training when hiring new faculty. The work products from this course documents can be included in a teaching portfolio (which is valuable in the academic job search). Participants who complete the course and all assignments will also receive a certificate attesting to their training. Students wishing to enroll should register for LING 501 (or add it during the Add/Drop period) this fall. Students will also enroll in LING 502 in the spring semester. Tuition is not charged for this course. The workload of the Linguistics Teaching Practicum is intentionally targeted to be less than half that of a regular Linguistics, so that students can take this course in addition to a full courseload. NOTE: PhD students who have been awarded fellowships are required to take the Linguistics Teaching Practicum in their first year of study, in preparation for their TA/Instructor assignments. However, all PhD students are encouraged to enroll at some point in their course of study.

title: Generative Syntax I
description: After a brief introduction to the philosophy and methods of generative grammar, this course examines some of the basic aspects of modern syntactic theory. It starts with an in depth discussion of the need for postulating syntactic structures and then moves on to examine how best to account for phenomena such as word order variation, the distribution and referential properties of noun phrases, and the differences among clausal types. These phenomena will be analyzed mainly through the insights offered by the Principles and Parameters framework. The course consists of lecture presentations, class discussion and intensive problem solving and argumentation on the part of the students. In addition to the Tuesday-Thursday lectures, it comprises a weekly discussion session led by the Teaching Asssistant; this provides an opportunity for students to ask further clarification questions, go over issues that one might feel were not covered in sufficient detail in class, and discuss the weekly homework assignments. LING/SPAN 527 is the first segment of a two-semester sequence which aims to familiarize the students with the assumptions, goals and methodology of generative syntax, while also providing them the opportunity to explore a few topics in some depth through readings from the primary literature. The second semester course will discuss the relation between the Principles & Parameters and the Minimalist framework.

title: Generative Syntax II (Spanish 528)
description: This course is the second in a series of four courses and its main objective is to provide the student with a working knowledge of the philosophy and methods of generative grammar. This course seeks:(1) to provide a practical and thorough review and expansion of the theory of Principles and Parameters; (3) to introduce the basics of the Minimalist Program; (4) to guide the student in the writing of professional and publishable abstracts and squibs; (5) to give the student tools to evaluate squibs and articles. After this course the student is expected: - to have a complete knowledge of the principles of the theory of Government and Binding - to understand how the different principles of the Minimalist Program came about - to apply the principles to analyze data of English, Spanish, Greek, Albanian, Vietnamese, among many other languages. - to be able to translate analyses from the Government and Binding framework to the Minimalist Program. - to be able to read and criticize major articles that motivated the Minimalist Program - to be able to write a 5-8 page squib describing and analyzing a theoretical problem BASIC BIBLIOGRAPHY L. Haegeman (1994) Introduction to Government and Binding Theory. N. Chomsky (1995) The Minimalist Program N. Hornstein et al. (2005) Understanding Minimalism A. Radford (2009) An introduction to English sentence structure. D. Adger (2003) Core Syntax: A Minimalist Approach.

title: Generative Syntax III
description: The main goal of this course is to enable graduate students to reach the degree of familiarity with syntactic theory that allows them to read and critically evaluate the literature and to develop an independent research project in syntax. We will read selected foundational papers on Minimalism and use the ideas there as a springboard to explore several key empirical phenomena in depth. The readings will be discussed in class presentations given by both the instructor and the students. A research paper is required.

title: Semantics & Pragmatics
description: Semantics/Pragmatics I This course begins with a three-week introduction to formal tools used in semantics and pragmatics (set theory, basic logic, functions). Then, the bulk of the semester is spend on reading and discussion of original papers and overview articles covering a representative range of contemporary research in these areas. We may work with readings on the following topics, as well as others based on class preferences: argument structure, predicates, adjectives, definiteness, quantification, tense, aspect, modality, negation, conjunction, presupposition, implicature, topic and focus, information structure, discourse particles. The goal of the course is to give students a practical understanding of the nature of linguistic meaning, an ability to read in semantics/pragmatics, and a foundation for advanced courses in these areas.

title: Semantics and Pragmatics II
description: In Semantics and Pragmatics 2, we extend the knowledge and skills gained in Semantics and Pragmatics 1 with the goal of giving students the abilities to read semantics/pragmatics literature on a virtually any topic which interests them and to do focused individual research projects in these areas. The course combines a 芒聙聹taught芒聙聺 component, with readings, lectures, and exercises, with a seminar-like component. This year, the course will concentrate on topics in intensional semantics, in particular tense (e.g., past, present, future), mood (indicative and subjunctive), and modality. We may also touch on aspect (perfect and progressive) and sentence types (interrogatives and imperatives), if student interest warrants. Prerequisites: Semantics and Pragmatics 1, or permission of instructor. Requirements: Reading, participation, homework assignments (especially in the first half of the semester), term paper.

title: Language Acquisition
description: In this introductory graduate-credit course we survey some of the important issues and ideas surrounding the accomplishment of an extraordinary feat芒聙聯芒聙聯the near-effortless intellectual mastery by young children of systems so complex that they have so far defied precise description and modeling by computer scientists, linguists, philosophers and psychologists. We will consider at what point children are thought to know certain things about the language they芒聙聶re acquiring (and how we think we know this); how children learn to segment the speech stream (and why this may be much harder for adult language learners); the use of special kinds of input modification (such as 芒聙聵motherese芒聙聶) and whether/how this is helpful or necessary for language development; how children芒聙聶s phonological, grammatical, lexical-semantic, and pragmatic communicative competence systems develop; the critical period hypothesis, with implications for adult L2 acquisition and L1 acquisition by Deaf children; and possibly other topics (as time and interest allow) such as L1 attrition and specific language impairment. There will be some emphasis on close reading and critical analysis of primary-source literature (i.e. actual acquisition studies), along with some data analysis so that you will have an opportunity to become more familiar with some of the design and experimental methods employed in language acquisition research. This course satisfies the departmental PhD distributional requirement in language acquisition.

title: Intro to SLA/Bilingualism
description: This course provides a general introduction to bilingualism and second language acquisition (SLA) research. The first part of the course focuses on bilingualism. Key topics include dimensions and assessment of bilingualism; bilingual language acquisition; the cognitive and neural basis of bilingualism, as well as social, cultural, and educational perspectives on bilingualism. In the second part of the course, different approaches to SLA are discussed, including cognitive-interactionist, emergentist, input-processing and psycholinguistic perspectives. We then move to discussing how L2 learning proceses are impacted by individual variables, including age, L1, motivation, aptitude, and learning style. The course will conclude by discussing research and practice in instructed SLA, in other words, how second languages can best be taught. Ling-553 fulfils the department芒聙聶s Language Acquisition distributional requirement, and serves as a prerequisite for some of the more advanced classes in SLA and/or bilingualism.

title: Computational Grammar Formalisms
description: This course will focus both on nonderivational theories of syntax and semantics applications to computational linguistics, and on using software to analyze syntactic structure. On the formal side, we will examine Head-Driven Phrase Structure Grammar (HPSG), and related lexicalist approaches to syntax. On the practical side, we will use parsers based on HPSG, as well as others that incorporate probabilistic methods to cope with ambiguity. The course will provide opportunities to test these parsers and examine human and machine linguistic analyses based on them. Although no programming knowledge is required, some level of comfort and patience with software is. Prerequisite: Generative Syntax I, or Introduction to NLP, or permission of instructor

title: Sociolinguistic Field Methods
description: This course will provide you with hands-on experience with methods for collecting data on language in its social context, e.g. participant observation, surveys, sociolinguistic interviews, subjective reaction tests. We will learn about different methods in the context of a sociolinguistic study of two or three nearby communities that we will initiate at the start of the semester. In addition, we will analyze short problems that can be addressed through the data. General topics related to the methods will be addressed, e.g. objectivity/subjectivity; community involvement; recording, managing, and transcribing data; strengths, weaknesses, and purposes of various approaches. Course Goals: The overall goals of the course include the following: 芒聙垄 Develop competence in the range of methods used to collect and analyze data in different areas of sociolinguistics 芒聙垄 Develop skills in observing, recording, and transcribing language in its social setting 芒聙垄 Review some major sociolinguistic field projects; design and conduct a small-scale community-based project in the local area that will connect to our larger departmental project, Language and Communication in the Washington, DC, metropolitan area. 芒聙垄 Be able to evaluate others芒聙聶 research from a methodological standpoint 芒聙垄 Evaluate the appropriateness (and interdependence) of quantitative and qualitative methods 芒聙垄 Understand the interdependence of theory and method 芒聙垄 Examine the relationship between researchers and those who provide data 芒聙垄 Gain confidence in designing and conducting research projects

title: Sociolinguistic Variation
description: Ling 582: Sociolinguistic Variation Spring 2011 MW 3:30-4:45, ICC 106 Professor Natalie Schilling Poulton 225; 202-687-6211 ns3@georgetown.edu Course Description/Objectives: In this course, we will investigate language variation and change from the perspective of quantitative sociolinguistic, or variation analysis. We will examine how both linguistic and extralinguistic (social) factors affect the patterning of language variation, as well as how language works to shape our social world. The course will provide you with the knowledge and skill you need to design, conduct, and present a small-scale quantitative analysis. In addition, you will gain hands-on experience, as you work on a group or individual research project during the course of the semester. For this project, you may use your own data or data from our department芒聙聶s ongoing study of Language and Communication in the Washington, DC, Metropolitan Area (LCDC). Finally, the course will provide you with the theoretical grounding you need to achieve a firm understanding of quantitative sociolinguistics, including its foundations, development, and future directions; its strengths and weakness; and its placement within the broader field of sociolinguistics, and within linguistics as a whole. Required Readings: Texts: 芒聙垄 Milroy, Lesley, and Matthew Gordon. 2003. Sociolinguistics: Method and interpretation. Malden/Oxford: Blackwell. 芒聙垄 Tagliamonte, Sali A. 2006. Analysing sociolinguistic variation. Cambridge: Cambridge University Press. Articles: A series of articles will be required. These will be made available on the course Blackboard site. Format: Each week芒聙聶s classes will consist of a combination of lectures and student discussions/presentations. Each of you will make a presentation of an article or chapter we read in class, consisting of a brief summary of the article/chapter, followed by critique and class discussion, which you will lead. In addition, you will present on your final project near the end of the semester. You are also expected to actively participate in general in class discussion. Presentations and research projects may be group or individual. Group projects will be assigned group grades. Requirements: Grades will be based on the following: Class participation, including presentation of article: 15% Assignment 1: Coding data for variation analysis: 10% Assignment 1: Basic quantitative analysis: 10% Assignment 2: Basic statistical analysis: 10% Assignment 3: Multivariate regression analysis: 15% Assignment 4: Transcript of 30 minutes of a sociolinguistic interview: 10% Proposal for final research project (c. 5 pp.): 10% Final paper (write-up of final research project, c. 20 pp.; presentation of final project, c. 15 mins.): 20%

title: Statistics for Linguistics Research
description: Linguistics 584-F2011 Jeff Connor-Linton STATISTICS FOR LINGUISTIC RESEARCH ICC 474/687-6156 connorlj@georgetown.edu STATISTICS FOR LINGUISTIC RESEARCH Course Description This course is designed to introduce students to basic descriptive and inferential statistics芒聙聯to teach students how to interpret statistics presented in published linguistics research and to use statistics in their own research. Students will learn: (1) what statistics is, (2) how to choose appropriate statistical procedures for different kinds of linguistic research, (3) how to assess the validity of statistics芒聙聯and interpretations of results芒聙聯in a wide range of linguistic research, (4) how to perform the most frequently used statistical procedures and interpret their results. An additional goal of the course is an understanding of the assumptions that underlie statistical analysis. The central textbook for this course is Learning Statistics Using SPSS by Andy Field. Selections from The Research Manual: Design and Statistics for Applied Linguistics, by Evelyn Hatch and Anne Lazaraton (out of print) and other readings will be posted on Blackboard. Generally, on any topic, you should read H&L first, and then work through the reading in Field, using the website. The course grade is based on homework assignments, an article critique and a final project. Class participation is required. Homework Assignments 55% Article Critique 20% Final Project 25% Note: Work handed in late will receive no credit. title: Language and Identity
description: This course explores the nature of identity and how linguistic resources like accent, grammar, and discourse contribute to its expression and interpretation. Students will review the literature on language and identity, ranging from early foundational work in sociolinguistics to the most recent debates on identity as an object of sociolinguistic study. Examining the construction of a number of aspects of identity 芒聙聯 gender and sexuality, race and ethnicity, age and the life course, and nationhood, as well as how they intersect 芒聙聯 will bring to the surface several important themes, among them access, agency, authenticity, borders, community, ideology, indexicality, and style. Together these themes will provide students with a framework for conducting an original research project on the linguistic construction of identity. In addition to carrying out this project, students will be required to present an article of their choice and to write three brief papers bringing together and building off of material in the assigned reading. The intended product of this course will be a theory of language and identity broad enough to encompass all course topics, yet sufficiently refined to be useful when framing individual projects.

title: Institutional Discourse
description: LING 589/789: INSTITUTIONAL DISCOURSE H. Hamilton Fall 2011 Fridays 11:00-1:30 Over the past thirty years or so, scholars of language and social life have investigated discourse within a variety of institutional contexts, most notably within schools, courtrooms, corporations, clinics and hospitals. For some of these scholars, these research sites offer intriguing real-life contexts in which their favorite discourse phenomena can be examined in situ; others are lured by a concrete place in which they can investigate the role of language in the relationship between ideology and practice; still others are attracted by an opportunity to work towards the solution of important societal problems, such as those found in relationships (and discourse) between teachers and their students, judges and jury members, employers and their employees, and health care providers and their patients. In Institutional Discourse, you will have multiple opportunities to build on your knowledge and practice of discourse analysis by exploring some of the intriguing regions in which institutions and discourse intersect. During the first few weeks of the course, we will read and discuss both classic and current studies by interactional sociolinguists, conversation analysts, ethnographers, and sociologists (see list below for sample readings) in order to familiarize ourselves with the wide range of notions and phenomena addressed by scholars in the field of institutional discourse. Such topics may include: narratives in institutions; intertextuality; identity construction; 芒聙聵talking an institution into being芒聙聶; institutional gatekeeping encounters; 芒聙聵total芒聙聶 institutions; front- and backstage in institutions; frame-shifting; facework and linguistic politeness; epistemic stance; language and power/agency; discourse shaping effects of professional codes of ethics and principles; and the interrelationships between professional and institutional discourses. Against this backdrop, you will design and carry out an investigation of the discourse associated with a particular institutional context, typically within the areas of education, law, business, or medicine, although others may certainly be explored. Projects may examine discourse along a variety of dimensions, including: 1) inter-professional, intra-professional or professional-lay discourse; 2) face-to-face, computer-mediated/web-based, telephone or snail mail encounters; 3) spoken or written discourse; 4) public or private discourse; 5) monologic, dialogic or multi-party discourse; and 6) type of speech activity or event. You may work on interactions that you have recorded or have identified in an existing data resource, or you may choose to work on interactions that were recorded as part of my own research and/or consulting projects. Students who are interested in working as a linguistic researcher and/or consultant within institutional contexts will find LING 589/789 Institutional Discourse to be a solid introduction to the challenges of doing discourse analysis across disciplinary boundaries. Throughout the semester we will discuss the challenges and rewards of carrying out discourse analysis in the 芒聙聵real芒聙聶 world, drawing on experiences of guest lecturers, my own consulting experiences, and the emerging experiences of students in the course. In the process, we will consider the influence of different disciplinary paradigms in all phases of our research projects -- including decisions about what research questions are thought to be both answerable and important or useful; what participants and settings can be included in a study; what kinds of language data can be collected and how; what types of theoretical frameworks and analytical units can be brought to the research; what counts as research findings; and, finally, how those findings should be reported and to whom. Although specific areas of focus and course activities will be attuned to participants芒聙聶 interests, preparation, and experience levels, it is expected that they will include the following: 芒聙垄 Reading seminal and innovative studies of institutional discourse; 芒聙垄 Identifying how linguistic discourse analysis complements and extends analyses from other disciplines; 芒聙垄 Interacting with guest speakers; 芒聙垄 Learning how to position qualitative work and explain key concepts within discourse analysis and linguistics to scholars and professionals in other fields; 芒聙垄 Designing and implementing a study of institutional discourse Prerequisite At least one course in discourse analysis or permission of instructor Requirements Attendance is required; active participation in class discussions based on required readings and analysis of texts and interactions is expected Collection of appropriate data set for the analysis of institutional discourse Preliminary analysis of your data written up in a short paper Facilitation of one in-class workshop on your data, including leading a discussion of readings you selected; Reading journal (reflections on readings and guest speaker visits, including discussion of relevance to your own data set) Brief written responses to other student-facilitated workshops Final paper Questions? title: Research Design and Methods
description: COURSE AIMS: The course covers both quantitative and qualitative applied linguistics research methods and focuses on the following: (a) How to identify research problems and questions (and how to come up with testable hypotheses); (b) How to choose, design and/or adapt data elicitation measures; (c) Ethical issues related to data gathering (IRB proposals) (d) How to assess validity and reliability (credibility and dependability) in research; (e) Conducting research in second and foreign language classroom contexts; (f) The many different ways to describe, code, and analyze data; (g) Writing up research reports, from term papers to conference presentations to journal submissions. This class is suitable for students at a range of different stages in their graduate study, and the course (and assessment) accommodates differing goals. Advanced students who intend to use a particular methodology for their doctoral research can design and carry out their own pilot studies, whereas students new to the area have the option to work in a team as part of two guided class projects focusing on data collection and coding. COURSE COMPONENTS: Preparation and Participation: Since this is a practical methods class, participation is key. The most basic element of preparation for participation includes reading the assigned chapters and research articles and reviewing materials in advance of class in order to be able to participate in often lively discussions. Preparation for discussion of the methods used in published research studies varies from student to student, but generally includes preparing some sort of notebook/database which facilitates easy recall of the details of an empirical investigation, as well as the major theoretical motivations and arguments of the researchers. Participation in this class also involves discussion of problems and critiquing of designs as well as raising questions for general class discussion. Research design problems/case studies will be presented regularly for in-class small-group work with the goals of identifying, understanding and overcoming research challenges. COURSE ASSESSMENT: 芒聙垄 1. Class preparation and participation: 15% 芒聙垄 2. (a) Critique of methods in a published research study, or (b) Data collection or coding exercise, or (c) Review paper on one topic of the course: 30% 芒聙垄 3. Research methods proposal: Presentation of proposal with handout/slides: 20% 芒聙垄 4. Research methods report: Presentation of execution & challenges with handout/slides & write-up: 35% *This class usually fills up. If you are an interested consortium student, please email the instructor about your background and to request permission. Please note, no audits under any circumstances.

title: Intertextuality
description: "The word in language is half someone else's芒聙娄[I]t exists in other people's mouths, in other people's contexts, serving other people's intentions: it is from there that one must take the word, and make it one's own." Bakhtin 芒聙聹All languaging is..taking old language and pushing it into new contexts.芒聙聺 芒聙聹Social groups seem to be bound primarily by a shared repertoire of prior texts.芒聙聺 Becker Intertextuality is a notion that has become widely used as a theoretical framework within the field of literary criticism since it was first named as such by Kristeva (1974), drawing on Bakhtin (1965, 1973, see also 1981). Over the past fifteen years or so, linguists have joined scholars in anthropology, the visual arts and music in their fascination with intertextuality; prior texts are seen to be important to general linguistic theory as well as to an understanding of textual meaning and the discursive construction of identity, master narratives, and ideology. But the theoretical approaches and the types of texts used in these fields are often so different as to make one believe that any similarity begins and ends with the label 芒聙聵intertextuality芒聙聶. It is the aim of this course to attempt to bridge the chasm at the intersection of these disciplinary approaches as we attempt to move the notion of 芒聙聵intertextuality芒聙聶 from that of a multidisciplinary nature (i.e., of interest to different disciplines) to that of an interdisciplinary nature (i.e., drawing on and synthesizing the analyses of different disciplines). After becoming familiar with foundational works on intertextuality in a variety of fields, students will collect spoken and/or written texts and use these to ground their emerging understanding of intertextuality. As we begin to operationalize the notion of intertextuality, we will grapple with a variety of issues due, in part, to disciplinary differences across the fields of linguistics, anthropology, literary criticism, and the arts. Three of the most critical issues to be addressed early will be 1) the definition of 芒聙聵text芒聙聶 and, subsequently, of 芒聙聵intertextuality芒聙聶; 2) the problematization of the notion of 芒聙聵author/speaker芒聙聶; and 3) the role of 芒聙聵author/speaker芒聙聶 meaning and intentionality within intertextual studies. In previous semesters of this course, students have investigated the interconnections that exist across spoken, written, and visual texts (both public and private discourse) as well as the ways in which people use these interconnections to do things in the world. Their projects have examined the linguistic construction of identity in family discourse and in personal blogs, the construction of autism within personal and institutional webpages, the intertextual origins of public opinion in published media, the role of master narratives related to pregnancy within pregnancy magazines, how meaning is accomplished in Japanese tanka poetry, the construction of racism in everyday narratives, and the achievement of humor in a variety of contexts, including improvisational theater, children芒聙聶s animated films, television shows and comic strips. title: Classical Arabic Phoneticians
description: Classical Arab phoneticians: Ling-711 Fall 2011 Prof: Solomon Sara, S.J. Telephone : (202)-687-6226/4004 Tuesday/ Thursday O.hs: (ICC 455) by appointment: anytime saras@georgetown.edu Topic of the seminar: The topic will be the Arab linguists who explicitly dealt with phonetic/ phonological issues. Treatises and sections of treatises that deal with phonetic/ phonological issues will be the topics for discussion. The seminar will emphasize the study of the earliest Arab phoneticians . The discussion will focus on the primary sources themselves. The earliest primary texts come to us from the 8th century that are seminal for any discussion of the phonetics and phonology of Arabic. The two 8th century authors who were the architects of Arabic linguistics are Al-Khal?l and S?bawayh. Both have included in their linguistic writings detailed phonetic/ phonological treatises on classical Arabic that defined the parameters of these two sub-disciplines for subsequent centuries. Other authors who followed them, like Ibn Duraid, al-Mubarrad, al-Azhari, Ibn Manthuwr and Ibn S?n? will also be discussed in the seminar. Purpose: To explore the origins and the development of phonetics/ phonology among the Arabs using a new paradigm. When approaching a new tradition in linguistics, the challenge is to determine what makes that tradition original and unique in its contributions. What is of interest in this seminar, then, is to capture the creative genius of these early linguists in providing the technical terminology and the analytical tools for the study of Arabic that are unique to this tradition. The task of this seminar will be to study their texts, explore their language data, isolate their technical terms, examine their procedures, capture their theoretical orientation, and evaluate the results of their efforts in the context of the overall linguistic theory and the study of Arabic in particular. Procedures: Seminars of this type are successful when the participants, without prejudice, tackle the problems, analyze the text, disentangle the underlying theoretical assumptions, and frame their results within the context of the language that is being analyzed. The success of this seminar will depend on the participation and contributions of all the members. Pre-requisites: Interest in the history of linguistics and interest in the Arab linguistic tradition. Knowledge of Arabic is Not required. All relevant Arabic sources will be provided in both Arabic and their English translation. Members of the seminar will be assigned sections of the texts for presentation, analysis, and discussion. Even though all members of the seminar will be responsible for all the texts, the more effective method of participation is to have a leader of the discussion for each meeting who will summarize the appropriate texts, present the issues discussed in the texts, analyze them, and lead the subsequent discussion. Papers: Seminar evaluations do not depend on exams that require mastery of the basic materials. The evaluations depend on active participation in the seminar meetings, and original personal contributions to the topic of the seminar. Each member of the seminar will be required to write two/three seminar papers on a phonetic/phonological topic both assigned and of their choice. The topic can be drawn from the materials discussed in class or may be analyses of phonetic/ phonological topics of a linguist from the same period not discussed specifically in class. Decision on the topic, with consultation with the professor, will be due by the beginning of November.
