title: Acquisitions and Dispositions
description: The Office Acquisitions & Dispositions course, MPRE-630, presents a high-level survey of transactions engaged in by commercial real estate professionals with a particular focus on the office sector. The course primarily focuses on the acquisition and disposition process as well as discussions regarding the debt and equity markets for real estate investing today. The course begins with an in-depth look at the capital markets and a historical perspective on the commercial real estate industry. A thorough discussion of the acquisition and disposition process, along with the corresponding analytics involved will follow. A class project involves the underwriting and valuation of real properties from the buyer芒聙聶s and seller芒聙聶s perspective. The course then considers lending transactions in some depth. It devotes special attention to the basic mortgage loan transaction between the borrower and lender and the equity return requirements and structures for investors. A panel discussion featuring top real estate executives representing real estate investment vehicles including REITs, Pension Funds, Opportunity Funds and Private Investors gives the class real time feedback and perspectives on the transactional market place. Office Acquisitions and Dispositions builds upon Foundations of Real Estate Finance and Foundations of Real Estate Accounting. In addition to building upon these Foundational courses, aspects of the course are related to such other MPS/RE courses as Structured Finance and Real Estate Software. Proficiency with Argus software and access to CoStar a plus.

title: Foundations of Real Estate Markets
description: This course will focus on how real estate practitioners analyze information to make the informed market analysis decisions that drive what they do. The class will cover a broad range of real estate disciplines from single-family and multifamily residential, retail, office, hospitality, and industrial and introduce the perspectives of developers/builders, investors, lenders, landlords, tenants, planners, public officials, and end users/consumers. The class will offer a mix of theory, analytic tools, and practical experiences/real-life examples. The goal of the course structure is a highly interactive class environment in which the students play an essential role in the education process. Active participation in class discussions is expected of every student and a key component of participant performance. The class is built around a final case study project that will consist of a case study report and presentation. This final project and presentation will build upon the work completed throughout the semester, including assignments given throughout the semester. The final presentation and report will be due on the final exam date (not the last day of class). Working in a team environment is a critical aspect of future success in the real estate environment. The class will be broken into smaller groups of 4-6 participants, depending on class size, which will serve as final project teams. Project teams will be expected to work with their project team outside of class time.

Project teams will be expected to work with their project team outside of class time.

title: Foundations of Real Estate Accounting
description: Foundations of Real Estate Accounting provides a graduate level introduction to real estate accounting, taxation concepts and equity incentives utilizing various tax credit and incentive programs. Topics include GAAP financial statements for real estate companies, financial analysis, fair value and impairment analysis, consolidation, joint venture accounting, Low Income Housing Tax Credits, Historic Tax Credits, New Markets Tax Credits, Energy Tax Credits, internal controls for real estate companies, and understanding operating and partnership agreements. Taxation topics include partnership tax concepts and tax-free exchanges. This course is designed to give you maximum exposure to the leaders in the real estate accounting industry. As a result, some sessions of this course will be taught by various real estate professionals. The objective of this course is to provide you with an understanding of the current financial accounting and taxation issues likely to be encountered in the real estate sector. At the end of this course, you will be able to analyze GAAP prepared accounting statements and identify potential financial and tax issues. Regular attendance is vital. Be sure to keep pace with the assigned work and class discussions. This course covers many different areas of accounting for real estate transactions. If you are not familiar with the subject area or do not understand the assigned material, please ask for clarification or assistance. Do not wait until the end of the course to obtain help. At the end of the course, students should: 芒聙垄 Understand the principles and rules of real estate accounting 芒聙垄 Be familiar with the accounting standards and pronouncements related to real estate accounting 芒聙垄 Understand the principles for the capitalization of preacquisition costs 芒聙垄 Be familiar with the costs incurred to sell or rent a real estate project and the accounting rules associated with those costs 芒聙垄Be familiar with the valuation process of real estate and how to account for acquisitions of real estate 芒聙垄 Understand the principles of non-monetary exchanges of real estate; particularly the basic principles of section 1031: like-kind exchanges 芒聙垄 Understand the process underlying a sale of real estate and the different accounting methods associated with profit recognition 芒聙垄 Be familiar with other applicable methods for recording a sale of real estate 芒聙垄 Understand the principles of a lease and be able to identify the key characteristics of and differences between a capital lease and an operating lease 芒聙垄 Understand the accounting methods used by both the lessee and lessor 芒聙垄 Understand the differences between the four methods of accounting for interests in real estate ventures: consolidation, equity method of accounting, pro rata consolidation, and the cost method 芒聙垄 Understand what a variable and non-variable interest entity are and how to account for them 芒聙垄 Be familiar with time-sharing transactions and how to account for them 芒聙垄 Be familiar with the special accounting rules related to retail land sales

title: Writing and Business Presentations
description: MPRE651 Business Plans In order become a polished top leader, with the analytical skill and positive image to succeed in the real estate industry, the real estate professional must write well, and must be able to create plans for substantial real estate business enterprises. Through extensive writing exercises simulating common real world situations, this course will teach you to draft documents that persuade lenders to finance, towns to approve, architects to design, contractors to construct, and buyers to purchase your real estate projects. Frequent, detailed feedback on these exercises hones your writing ability to achieve these objectives to a fine edge in combination with getting practice in presenting compositions from a visual/verbal perspective to a group audience. The course culminates in the development of a detailed business plan for a significant entrepreneurial real estate project. Course Objectives: - Teach students to prepare a polished Business Plan. - Facilitate the self critique of each student芒聙聶s writing weaknesses for the purposes of making corrections and improvements in each student芒聙聶s individual writing so as to create a positive professional self-image to others in the real estate field. - Integrate the written word into the world of visual presentation so as to develop the confidence to make an integrated presentation effectively and convincingly to a larger group. - Provide real world tools to assist the real estate professional when the professional composes documents outside the Classroom.

title: Real Estate Development: Art and Science
description: A successful, real estate developer, running a sustainable development company, is neither a pure analytical number cruncher or a force of personality 芒聙聹cowboy芒聙聺 entrepreneur. Technical and analytical skills allow 芒聙聹informed intuition芒聙聺 in the value creation process. The course will analyze revenue generation and the impact of fixed and variable cost on profitability. This course, as an introduction to the fundamentals of real estate development, will explore the art and the science of the process of value creation in real estate. The course will utilize both case studies and guest panels. Building upon Foundations of Real Estate Markets and Foundations of Real Estate Finance, this course will examine the information needed to generate accurate proformas at the project level, and use that information to create cash flow analysis necessary at the enterprise level to operate a profitable development company. Proforma assumptions include pursuit cost, land acquisition strategies, feasibility, market analysis, and debt and equity alternatives including construction debt, permanent debt and joint venture structures. The due diligence process will be reviewed in depth including title, land use, permits, environmental issues and site engineering as they impact the use and viability of a project. Enterprise cost analysis will look at the cash flow impact of development revenue versus the burn rate associated with fixed and variable costs of operating a real estate development company. Case studies will look specifically at the process of building an effective development team and the collaboration of development, architectural and construction professionals including acquisition and retention of talent, and the leadership skills needed to operate a development organization efficiently. Specialist panels will discuss the skills needed to anticipate and overcome problems in the development process with municipalities, lenders, contractors, and tenants, as well as litigation, arbitration, and other means of resolving development-related disputes.

title: Washington By Design
description: Optimism and an entrepreneurial spirit bred by economic expansion and the exuberance of a country coming into its own as a world power marked Washington at the beginning of the 20th century. The remaking of Washington occurred in the context of a particular set of social and cultural preoccupations prevalent in the years leading up to and following the turn of the century. Unlike today, in the past architects were at the forefront of the city芒聙聶s urban form and development. The precise manner in which the 芒聙聹new United States芒聙聺 was to be realized in Washington over the 100 years of the 20th century is a story of the coalescence of private and public vision, of architecture and real estate, of ideologies and practices expressed through building designs (and rhetoric about them) coupled with means for achieving their construction through expert commissions, committees, and regulating bodies, through private and public will. With a view to understanding these forces, this course will highlight the role of design in the nation芒聙聶s most significant symbolic city.

title: Development & Construction Project Management
description: This course helps students master the art and science of managing projects for development and construction. Taking the project from inception to an on time, under-budget closeout, the tools and techniques for successfully leading an eclectic group of consultants and subcontractors, managing budgets, conflicts and schedules are studied in detail. Going far beyond the secrets of successful scheduling, this course adapts the best practices of managing large projects from the infrastructure, IT, and heavy industrial manufacturing industries, and applies them to real estate development and construction projects. Upon successful completion of this course, students will know the industry best practices for creation and management of the project team, document management and recordkeeping, scheduling, cost control and value engineering, procurement research and negotiations, subcontract management and administration. Students will also be able to apply effective techniques for managing a construction or development project within the framework of construction law, AIA contracts and subcontracts, and ethical business practices.

Taking the project from inception to an on time, under budget closeout, the tools and techniques for successfully leading an eclectic group of consultants and subcontractors, managing budgets, conflicts and schedules are studied in detail. title: Green Building and Asset Management
description: Green and sustainable development generally includes planning, design, site and material selection, and construction. One area, which has received comparatively less attention, is green building operations and asset management. The number of existing non-green buildings vastly exceeds new green building development. Given the significant negative impact buildings can have on the environment, there exists a tremendous opportunity to improve the environment by implementing green and sustainable operating practices. This course examines commercial real estate operations with a focus on improving the environmental impact and performance of existing non-green buildings. Topics will include property management, asset management, and commercial leasing. In addition to commercial properties, we will examine multi-family apartment management, operations, marketing, and leasing. Students will learn to prepare management plans and operating budgets, commercial lease negotiations, and project marketing using green operations as a differentiator. Additional areas of focus include the specific roles of leasing agents, brokers, property managers, asset managers, and tenant coordinators, along with risk management, insurance, green vendor selection, service contracts and green purchasing policies. After successful completion of this course, students will be able to fully discuss green and sustainable asset management. This includes how to write and use a management plan and prepare a property level operating budget. Students will be able to clearly describe the roles the following positions: property manager, leasing agent, real estate broker, tenant coordinator and asset manager. Students will be able to discuss contracts, risk management, insurance, and discuss how to effectively work with third party vendors. Students will also be able to negotiate a commercial lease letter of intent, broker commission agreement, vendor contract, and property management agreement. Students will also be able to discuss how to use green and sustainable practices as a differentiator in marketing property, in addition to green leasing techniques.

title: Hotels and Resorts
description: Hotels and Resorts provides a graduate level introduction to hotel and resort development, financing and operation. The class will focus on the overall development process from the initial planning stages, to design, construction, legal issues, financing and operational considerations as well as an introduction to some of the various players in the hospitality industry. This class will also be enhanced by a number of practical experiences and examples. We will also have a number of guest lecturers from the industry to provide differing views and perspectives. Required Material: Walter A. Rutes, FAIA; Richard A. Penner; Lawrence Adams, Hotel Design: Planning and Development, Norton, 2001, ISBN 13: 978-0-393-73055-7 and ISBN 10: 0-393-73055-7 Joseph Ransley & Hayden Ingram, Developing Hospitality Properties & Facilities, Second Edition, Elsevier Butterworth Heinemann, 2004, ISBN 978-0-7506-5982-6 James J. Eyster and Jan De Roos, The Negotiation and Administration of Hotel Management Contracts, 4th ed., Pearson, 2009, ISBN 10: 0-558-07968-7 and ISBN 13: 978-0-558-07968-0 Book found here //realestate.cornell.edu/index.php/news/news_detail/the_negotiation_and_administration_of_hotel_management_contracts/ In addition, you will also be asked to read a variety of articles on current hotel and resort industry issues. These will be provided in class.

title: Hotels: Valuations/Transactions
description: Class One - Review of overall Basics from Fall Semester and a preview of the spring curriculum including discussions of the semester project Class Two 芒聙聯 Introduction to Asset Management: Learning the fiduciary responsibilities of managing hospitality assets. Reviewing management contracts from the view of an asset manager, benchmarking performance hurdles, capital improvement decisions. Class Three 芒聙聯 Tour of Property through the eyes of an asset Manager. Possible hotels to tour would be St. Regis or W. Class Four - Detailed analysis of Hospitality financing. Blending together all of the hurdles needed to satisfy debt such as Debt Coverage, Loan to Value, Loan to Cost. For new construction (when the market returns) how to develop profomas that will coincide with appraisals of future value. Class Five 芒聙聯 Hospitality finance continued. Detailed analysis of the capital stack. Particular emphasis on the brave new world of new IRR芒聙聶s for equity and higher spreads on different stacks of debt. Possible guest speaker showing an actual case study from concept through construction. Class Six 芒聙聯 Acquiring assets through debt. CMBS, Special Servicers, Receivers, FDIC etc. Class Seven-Valuations past and future trends. Guest Speaker Dan Lesser, head of Valuation for CBRE Hotels. Class Eight 芒聙聯 Flags and Brands comparing and contrasting and how do you decide which is best for your property. Possible guest speaker Todd Repport of Morgans Hotels Class Nine 芒聙聯 Resort and Golf course valuation and feasibility analysis. Class Ten 芒聙聯 Negotiations in the Hospitality Industry, Part I Class Eleven芒聙聯 Negotiations in the Hospitality Industry, Part II, possibly including role playing.

title: Leasing
description: This course will give students a broad overview of leases and their importance to commercial real estate. We will discuss the various roles that leases play in commercial real estate, and the differences and similarities between different types of leases. Students will learn to parse out the true and complete economic terms of a lease transaction in a manner that permits meaningful comparisons. We will review the various elements of commercial leases, placing particular emphasis on construction, pass-through of expenses, flexibility and exit strategies, options, and the effect of the current economic situation. Throughout, we will discuss the roles and motivations of the various players, and how competing interests reach agreement. Finally, through one or more guest speakers, we will hear how leases are interpreted in the real world on a day-to-day basis. Course Objectives: 芒聙垄 Provide an understanding of leases as the economic underpinning of commercial real estate 芒聙垄 Impart a working knowledge of a broad array of lease types 芒聙垄 Teach students to analyze the full economic impact of a lease in a manner that facilitates comparisons across leases 芒聙垄 Give students a full working knowledge of the key components of commercial leases and how they fit together 芒聙垄 Explore the motivations, similar and dissimilar, of the parties (landlord, tenant, lender) and the usual compromise positions 芒聙垄 Students will leave the course with the ability to read a commercial lease and understand and extract its key terms, and with an understanding of how those terms are put into practice

title: Real Estate Investment Trusts
description: From the standpoint of a REIT economist and topmost trade association leader, the Real Estate Investment Trusts course presents a comprehensive survey of this crucial area of real estate investment. The course describes the origins of REITs in the 1960s, their behavior over the business cycles since that time, and their performance in comparison with common stocks, bonds, and other real estate investments. It emphasizes that REITs are the easiest way for individuals to own commercial real estate, that they offer the liquidity of being publicly traded, and that their relatively low correlation with other assets makes them advantageous for diversification purposes. It addresses distinctive aspects of REITs that invest in specific sectors of the real estate industry: office REITs like Boston Properties, industrial REIT芒聙聶s like Pro-Logis, retail REITs like Kimco and Simon, apartment REITs like Equity Residential, diversified REITs like Vornado, hospitality REITs like Host, medical REITs like Health Care Property Investors, and mortgage REITS like Friedman, Billings, Ramsey. It explains the differences between equity REITs, mortgage REITS, and hybrid REITs, between public and private REITs, between UPREITs and DownREITs, and between lending REITs and ownership REITs. The course discusses and analyzes Maryland芒聙聶s favorable REIT law, which leads many REITs to be incorporated in Maryland and often located there, and which makes REITs disproportionately important in Greater Washington. In addition, it discusses the 1999 REIT Modernization Act. It also considers the difference between corporate profits and REIT funds from operation and the tax treatment of REITs, including the favorable impact of the 1986 Tax Reform Act. The course invites frequent guest speakers from such major area REITs as Avalon Bay, Host Hotels & Resorts, Federal Realty Investment Trust, Washington Realty Investment Trust, Saul Centers, LaSalle Hotel Properties, Diamond Rock Hospitality Company, First Potomac Realty Trust, American Capital Agency Corp., JER Investors Trust Inc., and Gladstone Commercial Corp.

title: Due Diligence
description: Due Diligence: Commercial Real Estate Transactions course will provide an overview of what REITS, developers, investors and lenders require in terms of due diligence, permitting and underwriting for land and improvements for commercial transactions. From pre-contract through the due diligence and closing phases of the transaction the full array of checklist items will be examined through a technician芒聙聶s perspective. Whether its acquisition of the fee, ground lease or securing debt, each step requires pre-closing due diligence: valuation, tax & flood certification, title search, title insurance, survey, zoning certification, property condition assessment, environmental phase analysis, comprehensive property and casualty insurance and more. You芒聙聶ll learn how this array of pre-closing issues culminates in the escrow and closing phase of the commercial real estate deal. We will have guest speakers in the commercial real estate industry that will provide actual practical and transactional experience. The goal is for you to have a practical and working knowledge of (1) the framework of language necessary to understand and work in the due diligence process; and (2) what it takes to bring together and close a commercial real estate transaction.

title: Retail Real Estate Development
description: Retail real estate influences the built environment and daily living. This course will explore how well known projects in Metropolitan Washington serve the complementary objectives of developers creating wealth and retailers delivering goods and services to shoppers. Beginning with site visits to supermarket-anchored centers like City Vista (Safeway) and department-store anchored properties like Mazza Gallerie (Neiman Marcus), students will be exposed to principles of market penetration, operating statements, valuation, trade areas, site selection and design, acquisition, zoning, development, and financing. Students will have the opportunity to observe why some properties work for developers better than others, and to analyze and describe their observations. Teaching materials will include texts produced by major professional associations as well as materials developed by the instructor when he served as Vice President Real Estate for Giant Food. Students will use Excel to learn whether a project yields an operating profit and creates value for sale or financing. They will study how: Developers decide where to locate new projects Tenant mix is determined Retailers decide where to place a store Developer and retailer use the lease to allocate prospect of success and risk of failure Developers gain control of a site, zone it appropriately, design it to attract shoppers, build and lease it, and secure construction and long-term financing The course will conclude with a class on career options in commercial real estate

title: RE Entrepreneurship
description: Real Estate Entrepreneurship is a course designed to explain and explore entrepreneurship in the context of the business of real estate. Utilizing the dynamic Washington, DC market as a backdrop, the course will use case studies, share experiences from accomplished guest speakers, and draw from the instructors芒聙聶 first-hand understanding to immerse students in the when, where, how, and why of undertaking entrepreneurial endeavors. It discusses the theory and practice of entrepreneurship generally, the motives of entrepreneurs as compared with other businesspeople, and the specific application of entrepreneurship to the real estate sector. The coursework will focus on the mechanics of putting together deals, creating real estate businesses, the constant need to 芒聙聹sell芒聙聺 as an entrepreneur, and paths to becoming an entrepreneur, with emphasis on the constant balancing act required in a start-up operation. At the conclusion of the course, students will have enhanced insight and skills to undertake an entrepreneurial real estate venture either now or at some future point in their careers.

title: Distressed Properties and Real Estate Workouts
description: Real estate companies, banks and investment firms face strategic challenges during rapidly changing economic cycles. This course will focus on strategies in analyzing markets, repositioning troubled assets/debt, cost control techniques and managing internal change and conflict during uncertain times. The course will incorporate lectures, class discussion, case studies and guest lecturers. The guest lecturers will include real estate transaction workout/bankruptcy attorneys, bankers, finance executives, real estate developers, market analysts and strategic planners. Topics include: 芒聙垄 Evaluating financing sources and structures 芒聙垄 Monetary and non-monetary loan default resolution 芒聙垄 Developing an asset or portfolio plan 芒聙垄 Purchasing and selling real estate in a credit constrained market 芒聙垄 Bankruptcy and consensual workouts 芒聙垄 Developing and implementing workout plans and alternative strategies 芒聙垄 Evaluating your overall position within the market 芒聙垄 Strategic planning in a rapidly changing economic environment

This course will focus on strategies in analyzing markets, repositioning troubled assets/debt, cost control techniques and managing internal change and conflict during uncertain times. The course will incorporate lectures, class discussion, case studies and guest lecturers. The guest lecturers will include; real estate transaction workout/bankruptcy attorneys, bankers, finance executives, real estate developers, market analysts and strategic planners. title: Private Equity
description: The course芒聙聶s objective is to introduce the world of real estate private equity investment to the student by reviewing the role of private real estate in a portfolio, as well as reviewing various strategies for gaining exposure. We will look at the private equity investment platform from the perspective of 1) an investor, 2) the general partner of an institutional style fund, and 3) the developers who access this capital. At completion, the student should be conversant in different types of offerings, risks and rewards, and how they may fit together as part of an overall real estate investment fund strategy. From the perspective of an investor, we will examine the justification for real estate in the context of a portfolio of varied alternatives. Then, we will consider the role of real estate private equity in satisfying a real estate investment allocation requirement. We will also consider the varied strategies available for accessing real estate exposure. And, lastly, we will review the due diligence appropriate for selecting a real estate investment sponsor (or 芒聙聹GP芒聙聺). From the perspective of an institutional fund sponsor (or GP), we will consider the challenges, and opportunities, associated with capitalizing an investment platform with investors organized via a private equity style fund. We will also look at the unique regulatory and tax issues associated with such a fund. And, review acquisition and asset management issues critical to a successful result. From the perspective of a developer, seeking equity for a real estate project, we will consider the benefits and risks associated with raising equity from an institutional fund. The capstone of the course will be the organization, by students, in groups of three to four, of a real estate private equity investment proposal suitable for an institutional investor. Each group will their proposal to a committee comprised of the course instructor and other industry practioners. This proposal will be accompanied by a full business plan for the fund芒聙聶s general partner sponsor.

title: Real Estate Private Equity
description: The course芒聙聶s objective is to introduce the world of private real estate investment to the student by reviewing the role of private real estate in a portfolio as well as reviewing various strategies for gaining exposure. We will look at the alternatives from the perspective of both a general partner of a fund, but also from the perspective of an investor in a real estate private equity fund operator. At completion, the student should be conversant in different types of offerings, risks and rewards, and how they may fit together as part of an overall real estate investment fund strategy. First, we will examine the rationale for private versus public real estate investment in the context of an overall real estate portfolio strategy. Once the theoretical justification is made, we will examine various strategies for gaining private real estate exposure, including reviewing private funds, fund of funds, property and/or geographic specific funds as well as private REIT芒聙聶s. As part of this course, we will examine different fund structures, review fund governance, how the different vehicles are marketed as well as tax issues affecting each of the different investment structures. Furthermore, we will review best in practice due diligence procedures for various investments. Then we will review the implementation of the investment strategy including a review of the investment and asset management processes. Given the current state of the markets, we will examine the investment process in the context of evaluating distressed property opportunities. In addition, we will cover negotiating joint ventures, execution of venture business plans, and troubleshooting when plans go awry.

title: Investment Banking
description: The Real Estate Investment Banking course presents the opportunity to apply real estate finance principles to actual real life transactions through the use of case studies. The course builds on MPRE 621, Foundations of Real Estate Finance, and MPRE 735, Structured Finance, but MPRE 735 is not a prerequisite. Students will get a review of the real estate capital markets and investment underwriting and analysis with the remaining course classes taught through interactive discussion of a variety of real world case studies that cover topics including: Levered Investment Analysis, Debt Recapitalizations, Equity Investments, Note Purchases, and Lending Analysis from a lender芒聙聶s perspective.

title: Investment Banking
description: The Real Estate Investment Banking course presents the opportunity to apply real estate finance principles to actual real life transactions through the use of case studies. The course builds on MPRE 621, Foundations of Real Estate Finance, and MPRE 735, Structured Finance, but MPRE 735 is not a prerequisite. Students will get a review of the real estate capital markets and investment underwriting and analysis with the remaining course classes taught through interactive discussion of a variety of real world case studies that cover topics including: Levered Investment Analysis, Debt Recapitalizations, Equity Investments, Note Purchases, and Lending Analysis from a lender芒聙聶s perspective.

title: Corporate Real Estate
description: Real Estate enthusiasm and education often default to the supply side. When focus shifts to the demand side it is usually from a macro or aggregated sub-market perspective. This course concentrates on the demand side in granular detail, but with the dynamics of a dual viewpoint: A. The Corporate Real Estate Professional (CRE) and his/her objectives and the constraints. B. The Owner, Developer, Investor who negotiates with the CRE to develop or lease his project and must understand these issues for successful design, marketing and deal making. Corporate Real Estate is one of the top two or three cost centers in all major corporations, but it is also the source of stabilized net operating income for any successful commercial property, and ultimately, the basis of all capitalized value. Hence the dual viewpoints. But corporate real estate is not always well managed strategically; matching operational planning with market opportunities. This presents an opportunity to expand upon current practices within the corporation or, conversely, for developers to leverage these inefficiencies to their benefit. A broader understanding of the CRE function, essentially the in-house developer, should also open up a career avenue with an alternate risk/return profile. Coursework will include case study and group project work, with an emphasis on interactive participation. We will consider a broad range of property types and transaction mechanisms both in the US and abroad, with an underlying theme of design, location (urban design) and configuration. 芒聙垄 Site Selection and Competition at different scales and across different markets 芒聙垄 Headcount driven Government Incentives and the Public/Private Partnership 芒聙垄 Structured Finance and alternative delivery mechanisms 芒聙垄 Headquarters Campus development drivers & constraints 芒聙垄 Build-to-Suite, Sale/Leaseback and Development Partnerships 芒聙垄 Alternative Workplace Design and the impact on demand and configuration 芒聙垄 Corporate Environmental demands and the Green Lease 芒聙垄 Corporate Agility, product & business cycle impact of lease term and flexibility 芒聙垄 Portfolio Management and Decision Metrics, including M&A activity 芒聙垄 Disposition, Consolidation, Reorganization and Lease Restructuring

title: Real Estate Economics
description: This course applies economic analysis to the study of urban real estate markets. The goal of the course is for students to understand and apply economic principles to the analysis of real estate. The course provides an overview of real estate in the U.S. relative to other asset categories, and examines unique aspects of both residential and nonresidential property in urban markets. Topics covered include growth of the urban core, determination of rent and vacancy, loan-product choice, property price indexes, and economics of the real estate cycle. Previous coursework in economics is expected, preferably through intermediate economics, and familiarity with basic terms and concepts in economics. Calculus is not required, but a good understanding of algebra and comfort in interpreting graphs is important. Format is lecture with occasional guest speakers. Required texts for this course are: Urban Economics and Real Estate Markets by Denise DiPasquale and William C. Wheaton (Prentice-Hall); A Primer on U.S. Housing Markets and Housing Policy by Richard K. Green and Stephen Malpezzi (Urban Institute Press).

title: Urban Economics
description: This course examines macro economic, social, and political forces that shape the built environment and real estate markets. It provides the framework for understanding more detailed, project specific market analysis and feasibility analysis, which while discussed in class, are not its primary focus. The course explores: Economic rationales for cities and metropolitan areas. Economic and social processes that spawn land use patterns. Economic development concepts, growth management, and planning issues. The use of data collection methods and trend analysis. Present characteristics and future prospects for real estate development. The course is structured around active class participation, drawing upon student day-to-day experiences, preparation of two written papers, and a final exam. Standard textbook and periodic readings are required, supported by the instructor芒聙聶s 30 years of experience in real estate research and consulting practice.

title: Structured Finance
description: The Structured Finance Course examines how large commercial real estate financings are put together. The course will explore the evolution of the real estate finance transaction. Who the players were and who they are now. How have structures changed with the influence of Wall Street as a major player having entered the market in the early 90芒聙聶s and the issues they faced during the credit crisis of late 2007? We will examine the options available and the problems currently facing both Borrowers and Lenders. The course will give a practical overview of how we finance the entire capital structure from senior loans to mezzanine tranches to equity. Guest lecturers, who are major forces in the business, will tell you their stories and impart their wisdom in this challenging time for structured finance. Upon completion of this course, students should have a full grasp of how deals are financed. As a result, the student should be an asset to their employer in this field. The course will include some lecturing, discussion and a significant amount of team projects designed to teach the student the anatomy of 芒聙聹the deal.芒聙聺 Class participation (20%), significant team projects including the creation of a finance memorandum, as well as specific research assignments to help train the real estate mind (30%), how well the professor feels you grasp the knowledge (20%), and a final examination (30%) will serve as the learning and assessment tools.

title: Real Estate Statistics and Modeling
description: The goal of this course is to allow students to develop a firm foundation in statistical analysis techniques to include linear and multiple regression analysis. Students will be able to construct computer models to predict and understand relationships between variables with specific emphasis on real estate. Students will understand two methods for modeling and forecasting: stochastic and multivariate regression. Students will understand the strengths and weaknesses of statistics and learn to recognize pitfalls in existing statistical models. Most importantly, students will be able to discuss and understand statistics in greater depth than would result from an introductory course, and they will learn how to best use statistics in real estate forecasting. In addition, multiple types of pro forma models will be constructed. Students will construct a multifamily model and add functionality to the model throughout the semester. At the end of the class, students will have a scalable multi-family model with stochastic modeling incorporated and a waterfall distribution of equity returns on the back-end. Further, students will be exposed to portfolio management and understand the difference in project and portfolio variances as well as how to construct a simple 4-asset portfolio model. Prerequisites: None! General knowledge of basic mathematics, algebra, finance, and MS Excel. Objective: Gain a very solid understanding of the statistics and how they are used in real estate analysis. An understanding of distribution types and confidence intervals and how they are used and must be used in all fundamental analyses. Students will have a solid understanding of how to construct forecasting models including multivariate regression models and medium-complexity pro form as with stochastic modeling, i.e. Monte Carlo analysis, to analyze future risk expectations.

title: Asset Management for Commercial Real Estate
description: Course Objectives: 1. To introduce students to the many facets of asset and property management for commercial real estate. 2. To have an understanding of the many disciplines of commercial real estate management including asset and property management, leasing, accounting, lease administration, construction management, marketing and physical operations. 3. To work in a team environment to produce a 芒聙聹Management Plan芒聙聺 for a specific asset and present the completed product to a review committee of professionals in the industry. 4. To develop and enhance students thought process and presentation skills. 5. To provide a forum for candid discussion and development of ethical standards on which to base decisions.

title: Construction Management
description: The core business of real estate development is the design, construction and delivery of buildings and improvements to real property. This course presents the fundamentals of (1) project delivery methods, (2) preconstruction services and contractor procurement, (3) field and project management processes, (4) construction materials, equipment and systems, (5) design and construction contracts, (6) technology tools, sustainability and trends in construction, and (7) ethical considerations.

title: Green/Sustainable Construction
description: In today芒聙聶s real estate environment owners, tenants, and jurisdictions are all asking how sustainability can be incorporated into new and existing buildings. Sustainable Construction and the impact on building operation is a graduate level course which builds on the foundation of the Green Building and Construction Management courses, and allows students to understand the link between design, construction and ultimately building operations. The course will present how the design impacts the construction and operation of the building as they relate to sustainable practices. The course will cover, the impact of design decisions upon construction and operations, including cost benefit analysis of design decisions. We will examine sustainable construction practice and how it impacts on owners and tenants and leases. What state and local legislation is driving sustainable building construction and operations. Ethical considerations of sustainability practice in construction and operations.

title: Construction Estimating & Procurement
description: The goal of the course is to provide students with an in-depth understanding of how a general contractor pursues, estimates, bids and procures work. The course will cover the full range of activities from conceptual estimating, to scoping and bidding projects, to the buyout and award of subcontracts. Upon completion of the course, students should have a greater understanding of how a general contractor estimates and submits their pricing and proposals to both public and private clients, as well as how the general contractor evaluates subcontractors, compares subcontractors芒聙聶 pricing and scopes, and how the general contractor awards subcontracts for the various trades.

Upon completion of the course, students should have a greater understanding of how a general contractor estimates and submits their pricing and proposals to both public and private clients, as well as how the general contractor evaluates subcontractors, compares subcontractors芒聙聶 pricing and scopes, and how the general contractor awards subcontracts for the various trades.

title: Introduction to International Real Estate Investment
description: The primary purpose of this course is to learn how to participate in international real estate transactions. This translates into working on investments involving foreign investors in the United States, as well getting a clear understanding of the motivations and subsequently the cultural differences encountered of US investors investing overseas, taking a close look at development, real estate brokerage, architecture and corporate users. The course will bring in speakers with experiences of working with different nationalities to relate case studies and work in their sectors. The schedule of the speakers, especially those from out of town, will determine which class they speak at and this schedule will be constantly updated. Readings preparing for the next class will follow the sessions, topic and speakers, as revised. Class participants will be given $100 million to invest in the US, and on the last day of the class present orally for no more than 12 minutes and provide a written report on how, why and where they invested the monies, which will represent 70% of their grade. They will be asked to also present investing $100 million in their chosen country from the US, which will contribute 15% to their grade. They will also be asked to write 500 words on their favorite course speaker. Other contributors to their grade will be their class participation and attendance.

Class participants will be given $100 million to invest in the US, and on the last day of the class present orally for no more than 12 minutes as well as providing a written report on how, why and where they invested the monies, which will represent 70% of their grade. They will be asked to also present investing $100 million in their chosen country from the US which will contribute 15% to their grade. Participants will be asked to provide an outline by March 14th, 2011 of their presentations. They will also be asked to write 500 words on their favorite course speaker. Other contributors to their grade will be their class participation and attendance.

title: Selected Regions in International Real Estate: Central & Eastern Europe
description: The course will examine real estate markets in the European Union and trace the expansion of free markets into Central and Eastern Europe from the Fall of the Berlin Wall through the current global financial crisis. The course will begin with an analysis of the established EU core real estate markets in Western Europe. The UK will be looked at in depth along with Ireland. The UK lease has traditionally been regarded as the most Landlord friendly and we will contrast this with the French lease. We will look at London, Paris, Amsterdam, Frankfurt and Madrid as the favored investment capitals. We will look at the role of the Chartered Surveyor and the UK Lawyer and compare them to their US counterparts. We will look at why Europeans invest abroad and the roles of their Pension Funds. We will look at the make-up of the big owners of European Real Estate and look at where Americans have invested in Europe. We will analyse the goals and leadership of the European real estate advisors and their global reach. For more than 50 years, real estate in Central and Eastern Europe (CEE) remained cut off from private market forces. Following the fall of the Berlin Wall, the expansion of the European Union starting in the 1990s generated tremendous demand for modern office, retail, residential, industrial and hotel facilities. Anticipating cap rate compression, developers rushed to complete projects that could be sold once these countries either joined the European Union, or simply became less exotic and more acceptable to institutional lenders and investors. Today, with a 20-year track record of development, investment, leasing and sales 芒聙聯 through several cycles and more than one 芒聙聹crisis芒聙聺 芒聙聯 the region provides a wealth of development case studies that offer lessons for predicting real estate trends in other Emerging Markets across the globe. This section will start with a brief historical context and study the nuances of individual country markets and the evolution of office, retail, and hotel markets across the CEE region. We will look at specific deals and market trends to examine the differences and similarities between EU, Central European and Russian markets, large and small nations and dynamic verses incremental approaches finishing with a look at Turkey as an EU candidate along with Israel and Greece. Our goal will be to build a framework for the analysis of new markets and the measurement and management of real estate risks using development and investment criteria such as cap rates, loan rates and terms, and varying types of investors. Instructors Julian Josephs, FRICS, has been working in International Real Estate since 1973. He started out with Jones Lang Wootton in London, Brussels and Antwerp, before moving to Paris in 1974 with Hampton & Sons. With an INSEAD MBA he arrived in Chicago, via New York in 1977 and coordinated Romanek Golub芒聙聶s European Investors $300 million expansion into the US. He moved to Washington DC in 1980 and has spearheaded a multi-sector development program over the last 25 years. As a Past World President of FIABCI he has been a spokesperson for the Global Real Estate Industry and has been teaching International Real Estate in Graduate Real Estate Programs since 1993. Contact Information: julian.josephs@gmail.com (202) 320 0733 (cell) Glenn Williamson (SFS 1983) has worked in CEE as a real estate developer and advisor since 1994. He led the development team at Golub for the first Class A office building completed in St. Petersburg, Russia which won the FIABCI Prix d芒聙聶Excellence in 2000, and advised owners on more than $200 million of projects throughout the CEE region for PricewaterhouseCoopers in Warsaw, Poland. Since forming his own firm, Amber Real Estate LLC in 2003, (www.amberreal.com) Williamson has continued to travel regularly to the region to plan and execute projects in Poland, Hungary and Bulgaria. Contact Information: glenn.williamson@amberreal.com, (202) 256.3922 (cell)

title: Negotiations
description: Overview: This course will cover approaches for managing differences between individuals and groups as seen through the lens of negotiation. Students will investigate models of negotiation decision-making. The goal of the course is to provide students both a conceptual and practical grasp of negotiating concepts which are applicable in their professional and personal lives. Principal focus will be on interest-based negotiation with consideration given to the psychological underpinnings of difficult conversations. This approach is now taught in business schools, law schools and school of public policy and widely taught as better business practice to profit and non-profit groups. It has also been the basis for resolving violent conflicts. Students will explore, through role exercises, two and multi-party negotiations. Focus will be both on the importance of substance and procedural considerations in complex negotiation. The conceptual framework is applicable in real estate negotiations as well as in other public policy and personal settings. Please Note: This course is based upon experiential learning. Thus a substantial amount of personal learning is accomplished through participation in and critique of class negotiation exercises. The value of the exercise is often dependent on the participation of your fellow students. Therefore, students should make every effort to attend all classes.

title: Independent Study
description: The Independent Study course, MPRE 901, is designed to accommodate students who wish to pursue real estate-related academic endeavors which, in some manner, cannot readily covered by conventional MPS/RE courses. In appropriate circumstances, students doing internships may receive Independent Study credit for deliverables created in the course of their internship. Independent Study also provides flexibility for students who have been detailed to another city, are pursuing international real estate research, have a prolonged illness, and in similar situations. A student who wishes to take MPRE 901 should approach the MPS/RE Associate Dean as early as possible prior to the semester in which Independent Study is contemplated. The student must submit a brief proposal for the student芒聙聶s Independent Study project, which the MPS/RE Associate Dean must approve. In conjunction with other MPS/RE Faculty members as appropriate, the MPS/RE Dean will determine the appropriate deliverables from the student, and will assign the most appropriate MPS/RE Faculty member for the project that the student proposes. A student may only take MPRE 901 once.

title: Capstone
description: The required Capstone Course marks the culmination of a student芒聙聶s career in the Master of Professional Studies in Real Estate program, and the student芒聙聶s transition from journeyman to advanced real estate professional. The Capstone Course offers the student the opportunity to explore a major real estate topic in significant depth and make a substantial contribution to knowledge in a selected sector of the real estate profession. Based upon previous courses and professional experience, the student formulates a Capstone Project topic. Under the tutelage of a senior real estate professional from Metropolitan Washington or beyond, the student researches the chosen topic, participates in any appropriate activities related to the topic, and produces a substantial work which constitutes a significant contribution to real estate knowledge. Because MPS/RE encompasses institutional, financial, legal, marketing, accounting, environmental, planning, design, engineering, and other disciplines, it is impossible to specify a single genre of Capstone Project, and the Capstone Project is thus defined chiefly by quality. With the fullest support of the entire MPS/RE program, the student has maximum freedom to pursue a real estate topic that embodies the very essence of the student芒聙聶s professionalism, and should be one of the best pieces of work the student ever does. In addition to other requirements, students must earn a grade of "B" or higher in the Capstone course to qualify for graduation.

title: Applied Ethics in Sports
description: A course that allows students to explore their own values and challenges them to codify and commit to their own code of ethics in relation to professional codes of conduct and best practices. The Ethics course is required of all MPS students.

title: Sports Leadership and Management
description: An examination of management issues and organizational behavior in the industry, relating mostly to staffing, motivation, and communication. In application, it introduces theories on leadership and the tools and techniques of how to effectively lead a sports organization on a daily basis.

title: Diversity and Social Responsibliity in Sports
description: A thorough introduction to the history and socioeconomic underpinnings of sports. This course examines the issues of ethnicity, gender, and international scope in relation to the modern sports world. Through readings, discussion, and role-playing, this course explores the stereotypes, misconceptions, and social elements of a changing sports landscape.

title: CityLab - The Geneva Experience
description: City Lab is a multi-disciplinary course where students, faculty, and professional partners from various backgrounds work in tandem with a city芒聙聶s leaders to solve real-world problems at an urban and metropolitan scale. The course offers a Jesuit-inspired clinical experience consistent with a commitment to applied studies. Georgetown芒聙聶s traditional strengths in curriculum and faculty will fortify the clinical experience. Since most urban issues take time to identify, study, and analyze, City Lab provides an opportunity to develop a long-term relationship with a city to tackle multiple urban challenges and planning projects that may be intertwined and interrelated. City Lab will serve as a true urban laboratory whereby the city will benefit from the academic expertise of the University: human resources, technology, sports industry management, public relations, real estate, sociology, law, and history among others. Please note: (1) Students should secure advance permission from their department to count this class as an elective. (2) With permission from their department, students can enroll in the course in consecutive fall and spring semesters. (3) Students who enroll in City Lab will be expected to travel to Geneva, Ohio for brief visits during the semester. Dates of travel will be flexible for student convenience. There will be no additional costs to students associated with this travel. Explore Geneva, Ohio, the site for this year's City Lab experience: //www.genevaohio.com/ //www.city-data.com/city/Geneva-Ohio.html //www.facebook.com/pages/City-of-Geneva-Ohio/160590477317101

title: Sports Marketing Strategy
description: An examination of marketing issues specific to the sports industry, based on the application of basic marketing principles to a range of sports organizations. This course will analyze the field of sports and lifestyle marketing with the practices, applications, and strategy of mainstream marketing. Course content will include a historical overview of sports marketing, delving into areas such as the team-side versus agency-side of the industry, including sponsors and corporations, sporting goods, special events, and sports enterprises in general.

title: Sports Communications and Public Relations
description: An analysis of the role of public relations and mass media in sports communication. Hands-on training in the tools and technology of sports PR is combined with an examination of the public芒聙聶s relationship with athletes and sports. This course explores the progressive techniques and activities used to promote sporting events. Discussion topics include brand awareness, media management, message recall, and the examination of four news mediums: television, internet, print, and radio.

title: Sales Promotion, Licensing and Sponsorship
description: An exploration of techniques used to sell and generate revenue in the business cycle. Topics include sales proposal development, sponsor solicitation, licensing rights, new business development, endorsements and merchandising, commercialization of technology, and corporate partnerships.

title: Sports Digital Media and Consumer Engagement
description: A review of how to develop and apply essential data in marketing decision-making in the sports industry, including database design, website development and management, privacy issues, and social media. This course considers the application of marketing principles, research methodologies, design implementation, and the analysis and interpretation of data. Incorporating horizontal and vertical distribution systems that integrate with the digital marketplace, the role of technology is explored in relationship to providing management the necessary support and maintenance of customer profiles. Students will gain an understanding of distribution channels, segmentation, demographic characteristics, and e-marketing methods and their applications.

title: Sports Business and Finance
description: This course provides a broad overview of the sports business marketplace, including the financial and accounting acumen necessary for executives to succeed in sports organizations. The goal is to provide hands-on and practical experience to real-time business challenges. Topics as they relate to these sectors include fiscal and budgetary control, ownership, and the skills used in the day-to-day operations of professional and amateur sports organizations.

title: Sports Law, Contracts, and Negotiation
description: This course provides an in-depth overview and analysis of legal principles, ethical issues, and salary cap negotiations in professional sports. Issues that are identified, analyzed, and explored include athlete representation, labor law, antitrust exemption, arbitration, collective bargaining, salary cap administration, free agency and the application of ethics as a method in the decision making and problem solving process.

title: Sports Event Planning and Facility Management
description: This course provides students with an understanding of the complexity involved in major sports events and facilities in relation to their social, cultural, and global environment. Featured topics include the analysis of goals and resources, the development of the revenue streams, design and planning, scheduling and operations, staff management, and the implementation of action deadlines.

title: Economics, Brand Management and Under Armour
description: An economic analysis of a variety of issues affecting the sports industry, including ticket pricing, luxury taxes, competitive balance, apparel and retail strategies, revenue sharing, the impacts and rationale of government subsidies, and the global currency marketplace.

title: Internship I
description: Practical learning opportunities will also take place outside the classroom. The SIM program will contact organizations of interest with individual students to successfully undertake, implement, and complete a timely industry-specific internship under the supervision of an internship advisor. These organizations encompass the professional, amateur, corporate/agency, municipal, and non-profit sectors of the sports industry. Action Items: * The course will include an internship handbook and will follow a monthly meeting schedule with the internship advisor and program director. * Students will keep a weekly journal that will detail their objectives, activities, benchmarks and assessment tools. * At terms end, student will submit a project portfolio to the internship advisor and program director.

title: Capstone
description: A semester-long opportunity for students to apply what they have learned in the program to produce one substantial piece of work under the tutelage of an industry sponsor and the program faculty. The subject will be appropriate to the students chosen interest. Board members and industry guest lecturers will visit and students will have the opportunity to present their work for the purpose of review and advice. Each student will be assisted in devising a strategy to support the topic of interest, appropriate to the course goals, by semesters end. In addition to other requirements, students must earn a grade of "B" or higher in the Capstone course to qualify for graduation.

title: Ethics in Technology Management
description: Provides a firm foundation of essential concepts, skills, and strategies to allow for informed decision-making and effective leadership. The course emphasizes ethical responsibilities and core values endemic to the professional world, cutting across several disciplines but focusing particularly on the connections between applied ethics and areas such as technology, business management, and law. You will explore real-world dilemmas and the framework for reaching ethical decisions. Topics could include intellectual property rights, government regulations, privacy, and others. Professional guests from various fields芒聙聰such as business, law, and government芒聙聰will present case studies representative of their experiences. The Ethics course is required of all MPS students and should be taken in the first semester of study.

title: Management of Technology
description: The Management of Technology course provides theoretical and practical experience in using information technology to support organizational decision-making processes. You will examine the requirements of an organization to optimize its competitive strategy and core competencies. You will learn different tactical, strategic, and organizational factors relative to various information systems, as well as how to gauge the effectiveness of an organization芒聙聶s information system. The course addresses some of the unique issues in managing different types of IT professionals, from senior-level architects to junior-level infrastructure support personnel. You will explore tools and techniques related to hiring, developing, assessing, and retaining staff芒聙聰as well as theories/methods of managing remote workers and virtual teams.

title: Project Mgmt for IT Professionals
description: Project Management for IT Professionals will master both the practical and theoretical sides of project management for IT professionals. In keeping with the ANSI standard for project management, this comprehensive course covers such core concepts as triple-constant theory, critical path method, and tracking earned value. You芒聙聶ll follow the project lifecycle芒聙聰from planning to monitoring to control芒聙聰and learn how to avoid the most common challenges of successful project management.

title: Project Risk & Change Management
description: This course is designed to introduce Project Risk and Change Management related to the integration of new technology initiatives. In this course we will use a combination of presentation, discussion, and research to understand the risk of new technology project failure in the absence of a comprehensive change management strategy. The process is introduced from the systems view of organizational change and includes data collection methods (qualitative and quantitative), diagnosis, and the management of system-wide technology change integration. In addition to the practical component of the course of study, substantive and validated theory is integrated that spans 50+ years of change management literature.

title: Managing Diverse Organizations in a Flat World
description: This timely course focuses on defining project structure and reporting Relationships; assigning project roles and responsibilities; and staffing, motivating, and leading an organization that can compete effectively worldwide. Specific topics include conflict resolution, process control and automation, outsourcing, offshoring, real-time access to information, and other practices required for effective management in the changing demands of the flat world.

title: Technology Entrepreneurship
description: This course examines the startup process from both sides芒聙聰exploring the perspective of the entrepreneur as well as the corporation pursuing a model of open innovation. Based largely on case study discussions, the course focuses on intellectual property, high-tech product development, venture finance, high-tech market strategy, strategic alliances, and entrepreneurial leadership skills.

title: Marketing Technology Products & Services
description: Discover how to leverage the Internet and identify new marketing opportunities for technologybased products and services. In this up-to-theminute course, you芒聙聶ll learn to develop and execute marketing plans that integrate social networking tools and other emerging media options. Content also covers market research, strategy development, customer segmentation,and competitor analysis芒聙聰with a special focus on business intelligence and related technological methods of effective information and resource management.

title: Enterprise Modernization & Technology Assertion
description: Enterprise modernization changes every dimension of an organization芒聙聰how it views itself, responds to its needs, and conducts its business. In this course, you芒聙聶ll focus on the management challenges posed by emerging technologies and learn how to overcome these obstacles. You芒聙聶ll explore how these technologies affect (and are affected by) political, social, economic, and cultural factors. Specific topics include emerging standards, nanotechnologies, artificial intelligence, artificial life, and expansion of the digital world.

title: R & D Management
description: What challenges do managers face as they seek to make research and development (R&D) serve the needs of the corporation in an increasingly competitive global marketplace? This course explores managing R&D in a corporate environment from both strategic and tactical perspectives芒聙聰 providing a deep understanding of technology as a powerful tool for competitive advantage and growth. Topics include key R&D business processes, issues in global R&D management,and methods of measuring and optimizing the return on R&D.

title: Software Life Cycle Management
description: Gain a deeper understanding of the process of analyzing and designing enterprise-wide systems. This course focuses on the software engineering principles that can be leveraged for software application development. You芒聙聶ll focus on object-oriented analysis and design concepts using Unified Modeling Language methodology use cases, classes and object diagrams, inheritance,behavioral modeling, data abstraction, and information hiding. Additional topics include overloading, polymorphism, and programming languages such as .NET and Java.

title: Software Testing Techniques
description: Learn how to design and implement practical steps for structured software testing. This course will introduce you to different methods for testing software and provide a step-by-step methodology for testing applications. Specific topics include test cycles and objectives, common software errors, effective test case design, testing tools review, test group management, problem tracking systems, and effective test result documentation. You芒聙聶ll also explore how testing can impact project schedules and budgets.

title: Driving Organizational Performance Through BI,CI & CRM
description: Today data is everywhere. The most competitive commercial companies are harnessing it to formulate their go-to-market strategies, make course corrections and outthink the competition. Governments are using data to develop solutions to social issues, and prevent terrorist attacks. This course explores how government agencies, commercial companies and nonprofits are using Data Marts, Data Warehouses (DW) and Business Intelligence (BI) to achieve their strategic goals and objectives. During the course students are introduced to the concepts through real-world demonstrations of data warehousing and business intelligence using current platforms available in the marketplace from vendors like Microsoft, Microstrategy, Oracle, and others.

title: Managing Information Security
description: The Managing Information Security course is designed for managers charged with protecting IT systems, this course prepares you to anticipate the threat of Internet attacks芒聙聰and develop effective strategies to defend against them. Learn the core concepts of data security, signature algorithms, key distribution, hash functions, and identification schemes. You芒聙聶ll even explore public/private key cryptography, secret key schemes (DES and IDEA), public key schemes (RSA and ElGamal), and systems based on elliptic curves. The pertinent implementation aspects and current security estimations will be covered for all schemes.

title: Enterprise Architecture
description: This is an introductory course in Enterprise Architecture (EA). The course defines EA as a series of interrelated reference models designed to foster a holistic top-down approach to managing Information Technology (IT) assets. Effective use of IT is essential to serving customers and/or constituents and this course explores how to align an organization芒聙聶s business goals with the use of technology. The course explains the use of EA as a structured discipline for managing and governing IT, enabling everyone in the organization, from strategic leaders to tactical office workers, to see how IT affects productivity. The course explains the benefits of EA such as: 芒聙垄 Fosters a common consistent approach to managing IT assets and technology use; providing the necessary discipline to support business agility. 芒聙垄 Provides the framework to manage business processes as assets. With the relative cost of technology ever affordable, an organization芒聙聶s business processes (how it produces goods and services) is what differentiates a successful organization from its competitors. Technology by itself is no longer a differentiator. 芒聙垄 Provides a direct link between business needs and technology use by establishing the vertical alignment of organizational strategies, business processes, and technology solutions.

title: IT Security Compliance/Forensics
description: This course examines the various laws related to information security and protection of data focusing on private and government sectors and contemporary policy issues related to forensics. Also covered in the course are information security operations that may be at issue in legal proceedings such as the identification, preservation, collection and analysis of digital evidence for legal purposes. Legal and ethical issues will be covered. Best practices and tips for collecting forensic data will be discussed.

title: Information Assurance & Risk Assessment
description: This is an overview of enterprise security, privacy and information security assessment and management, and cyber security. This course intends to concentrate on tangible and intangible costs and examines concepts of information assurance and security risk assessment security. This course will help students understand how to assess information security risks and develop solutions. Topics covered include social engineering, risk assessment, recovery and response, enterprise security, and formal techniques and policies.

title: Cryptography & Network Security
description: This fundamental course covers the theory of encryption and standard protocols for data communications and network security. Students will learn the goals of cryptography such as data privacy, authenticity and integrity. Topics that may be covered include PKI, digital signatures, message authentication codes, hash functions, etc. An examination of network security defenses and countermeasures are also covered.

title: Threats/Vulnerabilities
description: Focused on threats, vulnerabilities, patch management, incident response, and security operations to identify and protect against internal and external threats. Various security threats will be covered including hacker attacks, e-mail borne viruses, backdoor problems, and internal sabotage. Cybercrime, cyber security, and global information security will be discussed. Students will learn how to prevent and respond to such types of attacks using various assessment tools, intrusion detection systems, etc.

title: Systems Requirements & Analysis
description: Gives you a strong understanding of the system development life cycle (SDLC). You will learn methods of gathering, analyzing, and prioritizing business requirements with a focus on the ease of use for IT staff and end-users alike. The course covers many of the specifics of gap analysis through correctness and completeness methods, the use of case models, process and data modeling, database design principles, and more. In addition, the course covers SDLC models, Unified Process and CASE tools, Rapid Application Development methodologies, Package Evaluation and Selection, and more. Finally, the course will be well-grounded in practice through the coverage of relevant requirements standards (IEEE Standard 830) and current government practices for requirements management (i.e., System Requirements Specifications (SRS).

title: Financial Analysis for Managers
description: Provides theories and practical techniques related to acquiring, accounting for, and allocating an organization芒聙聶s financial assets. It analyzes basic business problems that managers face as they make technology decisions for their organizations. Some of the topics include return on investment, theories of portfolio management, financial statement and discounted cash flow analysis, interest rate determination, capital budgeting methodologies, concepts of risk and return, asset pricing, and valuation models. You will apply the knowledge you gain to issues you face within your work environment and to real-world examples through business cases and case studies.

title: Capstone
description: During the semester in which students are enrolled in the Capstone Course, it is strongly recommended that they only take one additional course along with it. Students must earn a grade of B or better in the Capstone Course in order to graduate. The Capstone Course is the culmination of the student's academic and professional experience in the MPS program. Over the course of the semester, students will be asked to apply the knowledge gained during the program. With a focus on technology for social good, students will incorporate the skills necessary for analyzing key issues, thinking creatively, and making sound decisions in order to develop and execute plans. Projects will most likely address global and local challenges.

title: International Relations: Theory and Practice
description: The first part of the course offers a brief historical background to the theory of international relations. Part II examines in detail mainstream theoretical arguments (i.e., structural, unit-level, cognitive and world society) about the nature of international relations and seeks to demonstrate the utility of theory for practitioners. The third part of the course applies these theories to a variety of important issues such as nationalism, ethics, environmental change, security, North-South relations, and trade and development. The course concludes with a discussion of several scenarios of the future that emphasize different trends in international relations.The main objectives of the course are to provide students with a useful set of analytical tools, an appreciation of the complexity of the field and of the value of different approaches to it, and an understanding of some of the major issues facing contemporary policymakers. In addition, the course offers students an opportunity to refine presentation and discussion skills.

title: Comparative Political Systems
description: This is a graduate-level introduction to comparative politics, starting with an examination of the behaviors, organizations and institutions involved in national policy-making: voting and electoral systems, political parties and interest groups, legislatures, and executives. The focus will be on relating system types to policy outcomes 芒聙聯 are the kinds of policies enacted by parliamentary democracies different from those of presidential systems? What is the impact of unitary vs. federal government structure? Finally we will compare institutions and behaviors across political systems (such as the UK, Canada, India, Brazil, South Africa, for instance) and evaluate the role of political culture in policy formulation.

title: Development Orthodoxies
description: 芒聙聵International Development芒聙聶 as a field of academic inquiry and field operations is both relatively recent and constantly changing. Successive theories have been promoted vigorously, and then discredited as incomplete, ineffective or even pernicious. The 芒聙聵best practices芒聙聶 associated with each of these theories have been first widely publicized and then gradually abandoned as outdated and flawed. This course will examine the most significant theories (or 芒聙聵orthodoxies芒聙聶) of international development over the last 50 years.
