These include classical development theories (such as Rostowian 芒聙聵take-off芒聙聶 and modernization theories), critiques from the left (such as dependency theory), human capital and basic human needs approaches, neoclassical theories (such as the Washington Consensus) and more recent theories of social capital and institutional reform. For each of these orthodoxies, the course will explore how development theory has shaped development practice. It will systematically focus on four distinct aspects or questions: 芒聙垄 key dimensions or definitions of development (that is, 芒聙聵what is development?芒聙聶); 芒聙垄 theories of social change underlying these definitions (that is, 芒聙聵what drives development?芒聙聶); 芒聙垄 critical assumptions of these theories (that is, 芒聙聵what are the limitations of this perspective of development?芒聙聶); 芒聙垄 practical implications of these theories, including the bureaucratic structures, professional vocabularies, programmatic approaches, operational tools and instruments to manage international development (that is, 芒聙聵how can we shape and manage development?芒聙聶). Students will be expected to participate actively in class seminars, contributing one Issues Paper to guide seminar discussions during the term. Additional written assignments (including a country development assessment paper and take-home examine) will also be required.

title: Development Economics: Theory, Evidence, & Policy
description: The principal objective of this course is to familiarize students with the basic tools and theoretical concepts that development economists most frequently use both to analyze and understand the leading economic development issues and to give relevant policy advice to decision makers. Related empirical evidence is also examined. Students are expected to attain a level of proficiency in development economics whereby they can hold their own and feel comfortable discussing economic development issues, particularly policy-related issues, with development economists and economic policymakers. The course starts with the basics: the meaning and measurement of development, including how to access and use important databases, the relationship between economic growth and development, and the overall growth and development experience of countries and regions in the developing world. In addition, the course offers a general introduction to multivariate regression analysis through a workshop with hands-on computer lab experience, aimed at giving students access to the vast empirical literature that now uses these methods. The course then turns to the major subject areas, including: (i) theories of economic growth and their relevance for developing countries; (ii) growth accounting or growth empirics; (iii) the underlying long term determinants of economic growth芒聙聰initial conditions, economic policies, institutions; human capital, exogenous factors; (iv) theories of structural change, including the policies, institutions and conditions at both local and national levels that enable countries to progress from rural agrarian societies to developed industrial economies; (v) poverty and inequality and the linkages between them and economic growth and development; (vi) policymaking, the role and functions of the state, public goods and externalities and macroeconomic management including the role of central banks and exchange rate regimes; (vii) foreign trade, finance and investment in the developing world; and (viii) the role and effectiveness of foreign aid and debt relief. The course ends with an overview of the current state and frontiers of development economics. The format of the classes is a mix of lectures and seminar-style discussions. Students are encouraged and expected to actively participate.

title: Foundations of Corporate Finance
description: The course objective is to provide a foundation in the fundamentals of financial accounting, analysis and decision making and to gain practical experience in applying analytical tools to practical situations. Considerable preparation and classroom time will be devoted to exercises that will challenge the student芒聙聶s grasp of what is being learned. A two-session Excel workshop will integrate accounting and financial analysis precepts with basic instruction on spreadsheet modeling and analysis. By the end of the program, students should have developed competencies in the following area, with specific skill sets in the areas highlighted: 芒聙垄 Understand the principles of accrual accounting and how to critically evaluate the financial statements of an enterprise to make an informed assessment of the viability and strength of that enterprise. 芒聙垄 Understand the capital budgeting process, how to model the financial performance of an enterprise in Excel (or comparable) spreadsheet format, how to apply the analytical tools and decision criteria that will be introduced throughout the course to analysis of specific investment situations. 芒聙垄 Understand how different forms of risk impact enterprise and securities valuation, and corporate decision making. Understand basic decision theory and risk assessment and mitigation strategies. 芒聙垄 Understand the fundamentals of stock and bond valuations and principles of long term financing. Understand options for raising debt and equity on capital markets and the securities issuance process.

title: FDI Risk in Emerging Markets
description: The course will introduce students to the risks associated with the entry of a Multinational Company into an Emerging Market Environment. The course will consider both the risks faced by a Company that is considering such an investment as well as the risks confronted by the country for which the investment is being considered. While the primary focus of the course will be on investments by companies that are based in OECD or comparably developed countries, the course will also look at the burgeoning cross border investments that are being made today by 芒聙聹Emerging Giants,芒聙聺 i.e., large and successful companies headquartered in the Emerging Markets. The course will identify the issues that need to be addressed in assessing the Investment risk and then consider examples of how different companies have measured and managed such risks. While this is not a 芒聙聹Finance芒聙聺 course, we will look at some of the metrics employed by companies in making the final investment decision. Students without a robust background in Finance will, however, NOT be at a disadvantage in the course. The course is intended to make the students familiar with and conversant in the language and practice of Risk Management in general and of managing the risks associated with Emerging Markets Investments in particular. At the end of this course, the students should be able to carry on an informed and value-added discussion of the risks associated with such investments with a potential employer or with their new colleagues in a corporate or government workplace. The course will enable the successful student, who works hard, to present her/himself as a knowledgeable and potentially valuable resource to an employer in these regards. Specifically, the students should come away from the course with: 芒聙垄 Familiarity with the basic language and concepts of Risk and Risk Management. 芒聙垄 Familiarity with the basic language and concepts of Foreign Direct Investment in Emerging Markets. 芒聙垄 Familiarity with how the above language and concepts translate into 芒聙聹real life芒聙聺 decision- making around the risks of such investments for a company or government. 芒聙垄 Ability to analyze the issues facing a company or government in the context of a specific decision regarding Investment risk. 芒聙垄 Ability to use the basic tools of financial analysis in the assessment of the 芒聙聹Risk Reward芒聙聺 equation associated with a specific investment.

title: Private Sector Actors in Foreign Policy
description: For students who plan an international business career, this course will provide insight into the role that business has in making foreign policy. For those who want to work in the public sector (or NGOs), this course will explore the role of companies in foreign policy making. Students will learn how the private sector influences U.S. and foreign government policies, as well as the nuances and limitations of the private sector in the bilateral and multi-lateral process. US foreign policy is developed and implemented through a highly complex network of actors within the US government and outside, both domestically and abroad. The primary focus of this course will be on the ways in which businesses influence the foreign policy making process. The course will also touch on other non-governmental actors, which alongside businesses, influence the foreign policy-making process. These policy stakeholders, outside of the USG, are often as important as or more important than the 芒聙聹official芒聙聺 policymakers. It is for this reason that the nature of partisan politics in the relationship between the executive and legislature, the role of civil society, the business community, the media (traditional and social) and foreign governments and constituencies have made foreign policy so complex. Our 芒聙聹body politic芒聙聺 is not easy to understand. The more familiar MSFS students are with the roles businesses and other non-governmental actors play, the better informed they will be to step into business and NGO roles and to understand and interact with non-government actors. Students will learn the interplay of these various actors and their impact on US foreign policy, development strategies and civil society in the U.S. and abroad.

title: International Mediation: Strategy and Methods
description: This seminar explores the role of mediation as an instrument of conflict management and a foreign policy technique. Students will consult both theoretical and case study materials, and become adept at analyzing the suitability of diverse mediatory approaches and actors to concrete conflict situations at diverse stages of the conflict life cycle. Adopting the mediator芒聙聶s perspective, they will address the challenge of how to design and conduct mediation as a form of third party intervention in violent international conflict. The course will identify and explore strategies and techniques used to overcome obstacles to a mediated settlement in intractable conflicts. Simulation and mediation analysis exercises will be used to illustrate the perspectives of mediators and conflict parties, and to introduce elements of mediation strategy.

title: Analytical and Statistical Skills
description: This course is designed as an introduction to the use of quantitative methods for graduate students. The progression of this course includes the theory and rubric of scientific research designs and the practice in generating, interpreting, and ultimately employing quantitative methods to critically assess empirical works as well as informing one芒聙聶s own research questions. The goals of this course are to develop statistical literacy coupled with analytical and research abilities. We will cover topics such as descriptive statistics, including measures of central tendency, dispersion, and association; inferential statistics, including probability theory, Normal distributions, and statistical significance; and OLS regression, including the use of dummy variables, interactions, factor analysis, and extensions to regression for categorical and limited dependent variables. The course is designed to enable students to critically assess and contribute to a broad range of quantitative work in the policy setting (e.g. risk assessment, policy analysis), applied fields (e.g. development, political organization), and applied theoretical work in international affairs (e.g. academic and professional works such as Foreign Affairs). The course will include weekly exercises in the discussed analytical techniques (including independent lab work) and three policy papers that include presentations. It will include a final exam and a participation evaluation.

title: United Nations in Peace and War
description: At a time when the world is facing truly global challenges, the question of why the United Nations matters in the 21st century is more relevant than ever. The United Nations is still the only global organization with universal membership that can confer the international legitimacy needed to address a wide range of issues in an increasingly globalized world. The goal of the course is to give students an understanding of the UN芒聙聶s history, politics and global role; its possibilities and limits; and its strengths and weaknesses. The course will combine thematic issues and specific case studies. It will examine the UN芒聙聶s role in conflict prevention, peacekeeping, peacebuilding, human rights, development, and humanitarian assistance. It will assess the Organization芒聙聶s institutional structure and the functions of its major organs particularly the Security Council, General Assembly, and the Secretariat led by the Secretary-General. Professor Dr. Abiodun Williams is Vice President of the Center for Conflict Analysis and Prevention, United States Institute of Peace. From 2001 to 2007, he served as Director of Strategic Planning in the Executive Office of the UN Secretary-General. He also served in three peacekeeping missions in Bosnia, Haiti, and Macedonia. He taught in the MSFS Program from 1988 to 1994, and won the SFS Teaching Award in 1992.

title: Managing International Public Affairs
description: This course is designed for students who aspire to a leadership position in any organization 芒聙聯 business, government, or non-profit. The operating premise of the course is that understanding and appreciating the role of public affairs is as important to organizational leadership as having financial, technical, or operating skills. Public Affairs is a discipline that monitors, interprets and 芒聙聹manages芒聙聺 an organization芒聙聶s social and political environment to further the organization芒聙聶s strategic objectives while maintaining its legitimacy or 芒聙聹license to operate芒聙聺 among its stakeholders. Public affairs is global in its reach, but always local in its application. The course will be in two parts. Part I will cover the various sub-functions that make up public affairs: stakeholder relations; issue management; reputation management; crisis management; government relations; and community relations, including corporate social responsibility. The international dimension of public affairs will be emphasized throughout. Part II will apply the framework and concepts explored in Part I to the practice of public affairs in the United States, Australia, China, and Mexico. Cases will be used to illustrate the practice across geographies and sectors. Its focus is practical and current, taking into account the impact of technology, money, and organizational behavior.

title: The UN & Emerging Institutions
description: Multilateral politics have become more and more central to international affairs since 1945. This course focuses on the primary locus of multilateral politics 芒聙聯 the United Nations 芒聙聯 as a system any professional in international affairs should grasp. This course will address the role and reform of the UN Security Council and UN General Assembly; the unique politics of human rights, development, and reform in the UN; and the UN's major independent agencies, funds and programs, from the ILO to UNICEF. It will examine the implications of equal legitimacy for all governments, of blocs and caucuses in the UN, and of funding arrangements. In an era of globalization, other institutions of global governance have been needed in addition to the UN. Building critical thinking about the comparative advantages of various arrangements to solve particular problems, the course will treat other economic, functional, and regional organizations as alternatives or complements to the UN, as well as hybrid and public-private partnerships (such as the UN and African Union partnering on peacekeeping or the Global Fund to Fight AIDS, Tuberculosis and Malaria). These UN and multilateral actors are animated by a particular form of politics in which learning in the course how 芒聙聹the game芒聙聺 is played will benefit future professionals in international organizations themselves, governments, nonprofits, think tanks, universities, the media, and business. The course will intensively seek to hone oral presentation and writing skills useful in professional settings. It will require comprehension of a wide array of topics (treated in a take-home final examination), but allow some focus in areas of student interest in UN and multilateral affairs, especially through papers.

title: Trade Negotiations & Commercial Diplomacy
description: Trade Negotiations and Commercial Diplomacy (TNCD) builds practical analytical and negotiation skills for diplomacy that is aimed at influencing domestic and foreign government policy and regulatory decisions affecting global trade and investment. Commercial diplomacy is the art of building consensus among domestic and international stakeholders when developing national policies that affect trade and when negotiating and implementing international trade agreements. Trade negotiations are no longer just about tariffs and quotas. Today芒聙聶s interdependent world demands skills and knowledge over a wide range of governmental activity affecting international commerce 芒聙聯in areas such as health, safety, and environment standards; consumer protection; competition policy; bribery and corruption laws: agricultural support programs; industrial subsidies; and regulations covering services such as banking, telecommunications and accounting. TNCD focuses on the analysis, advocacy, and negotiating in a public policy arena. These skills are built in a three part progression. First focus is on the pure negotiating skills. Beginning with simple bilateral, single issue events, we build to complex multiparty, multi-issue negotiations. Classes are a combination of lecture/discussion, simulation exercises, and analytical debriefing. Second, we apply this new understanding to the complex trade negotiation through case studies and dialogue with practitioners. Finally, we focus on the broader scope of commercial diplomacy: getting trade (and other kinds of) agreements signed, legislated and implemented-- identifying and controlling strategic stakeholders, coalitions, and helpful or hurtful peripheral parties; effective lobbying; and writing and speaking for effective advocacy. The course will highlight a complex, day-long negotiation exercise. Students will prepare supporting strategic analysis for the negotiator and then serve as members of a national negotiation team. Students will develop, in the context of international agreements on investment and trade, in-depth understanding of the factors that bear on the policy process at home and abroad, political advocacy tools and techniques to assure support for desired outcomes, and the skills needed to structure and complete successful negotiations.

title: Practical Evaluation
description: For each of the elements listed below, we will introduce the main concepts, discuss tools, and then engage the students in applying the tools through a 芒聙聹live芒聙聺 project.芒聙聺 1. What is evaluation? -Vocabulary of evaluation -Challenges of evaluation 2. Evaluation design: quantitative and qualitative approaches 3. Evaluation tools and methods 4. Practical data collection procedures 5. Practical data analysis 6. Engaging stakeholders in the design and implementation 7. Planning and managing evaluation for maximum effectiveness 8. Budgeting the evaluation 9. Assessing the feasibility and usefulness of evaluation 10. Communication of findings By the end of this course students will be conversant in evaluation methodology and procedures, become discerning users of existing data, differentiate between types of methods and tools for practical evaluation, be able to discuss why evaluation techniques are appropriate to a country setting, apply the appropriate techniques to various projects, and be able to plan and carry out an evaluation of a project of their choice.

title: Small and Medium Enterprise Development
description: Pre-Requisite: For this course, microenterprise development coursework or relevant experience is desirable but not required. Statistics show that small and medium enterprises represent a high share of developing countries芒聙聶 private sectors. Recently, a new dynamic has started that integrates business management approaches related to organization, process, or service models into human development efforts. Many of these new ideas are tested and promoted by small enterprises. Thus, the course will look at private sector and SME development as an objective, the private sector as an actor for development, and business management as a toolbox for innovative development ideas. The course will begin with an introduction to the economics and dynamics of the sector as well as basic theory, and then it will focus on foundations of, and obstacles to growth, including the regulatory regime, finance, training/technical assistance, market linkages and business development services. Through case studies, exposure to practitioners芒聙聶 best practices, and an operational approach, the course will analyze what works and why in small and medium enterprise development. It particularly will evaluate alternative solutions regarding their scalability, sustainability, and their return on investment.

title: Social Entrepreneurship
description: Pre-Requisite: For this course, microenterprise development coursework or relevant experience is desirable but not required. Despite many apparent successes, social enterprises present a unique challenge for development practitioners: while there is a strong desire to promote them, it is difficult to quantify the precise ways in which they affect development. For this reason, there are a number of ways from which practitioners can approach social enterprise promotion. These vary from financially- to technically-focused, sector-specific to country-wide issues. Finally, the most appropriate actor to lead the intervention may be the national government, the subnational administration, business associations, NGOs, international donors and organizations, or the private sector. In exploring these issues, this seminar will bridge common themes and issues in the International Development and International Business and Commerce concentrations. Through case studies, exposure to practitioners芒聙聶 best practices, and an operational approach, the course will analyze the emerging field of social entrepreneurship. The course will be divided into two parts. Part I will begin with an introduction to social capital markets as they relate to social enterprise formation and growth. Part II will focus on social enterprises and entrepreneurs as units.

title: Economic Dimensions of International Security
description: This class will help international affairs professionals better understand and work within a traditional gap between international security and economic affairs, focusing on economic constraints and opportunities affecting security. While security and economics too often exist in separate worlds or are compartmentalized-in both academia and government-we will focus on their intersections, constitutive effects, and costs and benefits that economics has on international security. We will draw upon the alternative schools of thought to analyze recent historical and contemporary global cases, examining the motivations, ends, and means of international actors as well as the strategic environment shaping their choices. Designed primarily for those interested in economics but working in political or security fields, this course will also directly address tools and lessons for policymakers and practitioners.

title: The Internet and Authoritarian Countries
description: The Internet has become a very complex and contentious public space where a variety of actors 芒聙聯 governments, civil society organizations, individual protesters etc 芒聙聯 compete not only to get around censorship but also to gain visibility and mobilize influential audiences. Blogs, bulletin boards and social networks have deeply changed the relationship between authoritarian governments and their citizens, notably by enabling an increasing number of online protests; the same technologies used by those contesting state authority are also used by the authorities themselves for purposes such as surveillance, censorship and propaganda. This course is designed for students who seek a deeper understanding of the political stakes of the Internet in authoritarian countries. Each class session will explore a specific aspect of these intricate issues: Internet access and the digital divide, censorship and resistance, civil society, online deliberation, public opinion framing etc. Most important, students will be encouraged to look at these issues from a comparative perspective, through a variety of case studies and a close watch of current events in such countries as China, Iran, Russia or Egypt. As a corollary objective, this course will help students to make better decisions when they are confronted with strategic, legal and/or ethical questions related to social media, be they future corporate advertisers, diplomats or rights activists.

title: Petroleum in the Global Economy
description: This course will introduce students to the basics of the international petroleum industry and its role of the international economy. The first part of the course will establish the basics and background of the industry. Students will be introduced to the economics of petroleum exploration and production, refining, marketing and use, including natural gas and alternatives forms of energy, and to the basic business models of the international petroleum industry. Drawing on this knowledge base, the second part of the course will examine the history of the petroleum industry, the global petroleum resource base, the structure and dynamics of the international petroleum market, the impact of petroleum on the environment and an overview of energy policies.

title: Business & the State in African Development
description: This course investigates the role of and the prospects for business in African development. We will examine the changing role and perception of the private sector in Africa, and the interactions of businesspeople and business organizations with the African state. Historically, the private sector role in Africa has been both positive and negative; we will explore the various tradeoffs and the implications for development and democracy, drawing on country, sectoral and firm-level cases from around the continent.

title: International Corporate Risk
description: While the Global Economic Crisis certainly lay bare the inadequacy of public sector supervision of financial markets, these events also heavily underscored the weakness of private sector financial market participants, credit rating agencies, banks and corporates alike, in identifying, measuring and/or mitigating risk. Nonetheless, risk is essential to free economies as it is the act of taking risk from which corporate profits are derived. Therefore, this course will not be geared to either the absolute avoidance or elimination of risks but rather to developing an understanding of how the risk/reward equation can be optimized so as to create reliable income streams from international credit exposures. The course will focus on risk identification from a debt lender芒聙聶s perspective in a variety of industries and countries. While distinct factors create different risks in different industry/country settings, the goal of the class will be to impart a disciplined risk assessment framework that can be applied to a broad swath of careers in the private and public sector. By developing a comprehensive understanding of potential risks faced by international companies, students will be able to assess their potential for default and recommend strategies to optimize the risk/reward equation. The objective is to train highly skilled risks analysts with a strong theoretical background and practical knowledge about operating in international markets. Through textbook readings, case studies and in-class lectures and discussions, students will be given the analytical rubric to help identify, explain, measure, benchmark and mitigate risks in the context of companies operating in today芒聙聶s international economy. In order to take the study of risk from the purely theoretical to the practical, students will be asked to prepare a semester-long proposal for a specific international enterprise. Shaped in the form of an Information Memorandum, the proposal will incorporate the rubric of risks from our class to highlight and analyze the risk considerations 芒聙聯 both positive and negative 芒聙聯 of lending to a real company currently operating in the global economy.

title: The Political Economy of Global Climate Change
description: The Climate Transition is the process by which humanity must reduce emissions of greenhouse gases and adapt to already changing climates. The success of this Transition will hinge on new institutions, or rules of the game, that will apportion access to the atmospheric commons. These arrangements are inherently distributional in nature, affecting contemporary actors and current and future generations. Progress further depends on new and disruptive technologies and incentives that foster the innovation and financing needed to deliver carbon neutral tools and processes. This course begins with a focus on areas of consensus within climate science, and proceeds upon this foundation to explore the following four interconnected but distinct themes, or questions: (a) the basis and evolution of climate policy (or 芒聙聹how do we conceptually frame this threat?芒聙聺) (b) the role of climate change in development (or, 芒聙聹how do the 芒聙聵North芒聙聶 and 芒聙聵South芒聙聶 respond to this dilemma together?芒聙聺) (c) the emergence of economic opportunities and new climate technologies, including geoengineering schemes (or, 芒聙聹can the profit motive and technical 芒聙聵fixes芒聙聶 help us avoid calamity?芒聙聺), and finally, (d) the political economy of climate-induced conflict (or, 芒聙聹will climate stress on resources lead to instability in parts of the globe?芒聙聶). Special topics may also include the impact of climate change on public health, and religious and ethical perspectives on this challenge. Students are expected to be prepared for each class and to participate in discussions. Written assignments and a take home exam will be required.

title: Business Operations in Emerging Markets
description: The purpose of this course is to help the student transition from the consideration of high-level global business strategies to a focus on more 芒聙聹on-the-ground芒聙聺 implementation of these strategies in emerging markets, defined by the local conditions faced by the operating 芒聙聹subsidiary芒聙聺 of the global firm. It will commence with a presentation of concepts, in which we will look at the operational, marketing, strategic and investment challenges of subsidiaries of multinational corporations, as well as some large-scale local companies, confronting local market and non-market conditions. The next phase will use case studies of multinational corporations and some local corporations operating in the Middle East, China, India, Africa, Russia, Central Asia, the Caucasus, and Latin America, to assist in an understanding of the challenges of operating businesses in developing countries. The course芒聙聶s last phase will involve a simulation exercise in which students will develop an operational strategy for a business unit operating in a developing market. Students will build the business skill-sets necessary for conducting situational analyses, making business recommendations, and developing an operation plan for firms doing business in developing country environments.

title: Public Diplomacy
description: This course examines concepts, practices, institutions, and critical issues in public diplomacy. It is intended for students considering careers in government, international organizations, the private sector, and civil society. The course assumes the central importance of a public dimension in today's diplomacy and that diplomatic practice is changing in fundamental ways. There are more diplomatic actors dealing with a larger number of issues. More diplomatic engagement occurs in public spaces. Information technologies are accelerating the speed of diplomacy and transforming its forms and core functions. Diplomats need street smarts and media skills. Diplomacy increasingly is 芒聙聹whole of government芒聙聺 in concept and practice. Public diplomacy is an instrument used by states and non-state actors to understand cultures, attitudes, and behavior; build and manage relationships; and influence thoughts and actions to advance their interests and values. Drawing on the experiences of diplomats and a growing academic literature, we will explore what this means for the changing actors, issues, methods, and mediated environments of diplomacy in the 21st century. The course will focus on five themes: 1. Perspectives on public opinion, global public engagement, strategic communication, soft power, nation branding, and propaganda; 2. Diplomacy's context: networks, global governance, non-state actors, and social media; 3. Comparative public diplomacy of large and small states, and regional entities; 4. US multi-stakeholder diplomacy and the roles of the White House, the Departments of State and Defense, USAID, embassies, cultural diplomats, international broadcasters and civil society; and 5. Issues in academic study and future diplomatic practice. Students will be expected to do readings in advance and discuss them critically in class. Course requirements include written public diplomacy products (a briefing memo, speech, and strategy paper for a public diplomacy actor) and oral work (class participation, short presentations, role playing in a mock press conference, and a participatory case study).

title: The Political Economy of International Organizations
description: What are International Organizations (IOs) and what role do they play in world politics? In this course we will carefully examine various IOs, considering their historical origins, ostensible functions, the international and domestic political forces that impact their operations, and their effectiveness. We will begin the course by addressing some overarching theoretical and methodological issues so that we have a core set of analytical tools we can apply to our study of specific IOs. From a theoretical perspective, we will consider various paradigms, such as realist, liberal, bureaucratic, and constructivist. From a methodological point of view, we will be concerned with questions of endogeneity, that is, separating the circumstances under which IOs take action from the inherent effects of their actions. We will then delve into specific IOs. We begin with the International Financial Institutions (the IMF, World Bank, and regional development organizations). We then turn to international trade organizations (the GATT/WTO, NAFTA, CAFTA, MERCOSUR). Next we turn to the various arms of the United Nations (the General Assembly, the UN development agencies, the Security Council, and Peace Keeping Operations). We subsequently analyze security organizations, notably the expansion of NATO. Finally, we turn to the European Union. As we examine each institution, we will keep several questions in mind: Does this international organization represent anything more than the interests of its most powerful members? How are the foreign policy goals of its most powerful members pursued - /or not/? What role do domestic politics play when countries interact with the international organizations? How does the pursuit of the private incentives of individuals working in IOs influence IO effectiveness? What ideas and norms in international politics influenced the creation of the various IOs and what impact have the IOs in turn had on international ideas and norms? In short, we are concerned with the political economy, broadly conceived, of international organizations. As in other courses in political economy, essentially we will be grappling with centralized versus decentralized mechanisms of allocation, where the two mechanisms often interact. The various centralized and decentralized mechanisms allocation in specific countries have effects across borders -- for example, there are economic, security, and environmental externalities. IOs thus represent answers to failures of decentralized mechanisms of allocation, or, simply put, answers to international market failures. IOs are thus supranational centralized allocation mechanisms, which may or may not effectively achieve their ostensible goals. When evaluating whether these centralized mechanisms of allocation achieve their intended goals, it will be important to consider not just their inherent effects, but also an important counterfactual: what the world would be like in their absence? Note, the course makes no a priori judgements about the value of IOs, and students are encouraged to think critically about the constellation of IOs in the world. The course requires only that students consider both centralized and decentralized mechanisms of allocation as viable solutions to international problems - there is no ideological preference for one approach over the other. The challenges of trying to understand the interests, institutions, and information of actors in an international context are great, and much remains to be learned. The course is designed not just to familiarize students with IOs, but also to stimulate their curiosity about questions that really have yet to be answered satisfactorily. An important goal of the course is also the equip students with the analytical tools required to address such questions.

title: Financial Crises & Global Institutions
description: The course will put the international financial crises of the past few years into comparative and historic perspective and examine the role of the primary policy maker actors 芒聙聯 both national and international. Students will cover the different kinds of crises, different types of policy responses, and the influences that affect the key actors. Working through the global financial crises of the past 30 to 40 years, with particular emphasis on key crises including the ongoing crisis in Europe and U.S. crisis in 2008/09 -- students will become familiar with the structure and purposes of the key International Financial Institutions 芒聙聯 both formal (IMF and Financial Stability Board) and informal (G-7 and G20). They will consider some of the key policy challenges facing the international financial system and examine how these institutions and the key policy makers interact with, and react to, financial markets, domestic politics, and international pressures.

title: Democracy & Human Rights, Strategic Approaches to External Support
description: While support for democracy and human rights is in the end in the hands of those who reside with societies, external actors have played a critical role in the past in providing assistance and linkages to groups and individuals pushing for reform within their societies. This course will examine those efforts and explore analytic and programmatic lessons learned in broad arena of democracy and human rights. The course is taught by two former practitioners, and will involve a number of speakers from the major democracy and human rights organizations in the Washington DC area. The first part of the course will be focused on students attaining familiarity with the basic international treaties, and foundational literature on human rights and democratization, as well as outlining the spectrum of groups and organizations that are active in the external support of democratization. The second part will explore further the debates within the community: are international human rights universal, are their legitimate limits that should be placed on freedom of expression and association, is external support for democratization and human rights a valid incursion into state sovereignty. We will also explore the funding and structure of the organizations that are most active in providing external support, and how that funding affects perceptions and impact of their work. The third part of the course will be focused on understanding and applying a strategic assessment of the major domestic actors within a country and students will learn to identify the most critical areas of need 芒聙聯 and those areas in which external assistance could potentially might a difference. Class discussions will refer to two countries at different stages of their commitment to democracy and human rights: Serbia and Egypt. Students will be asked to select an additional country for their written assignments (preferably one they do not know well) and bring relevant insights from those countries into class discussions as well. Possible countries include: China and Indonesia, Russia and Ukraine, Uganda and Ghana, Nicaragua and Mexico Tunisia and Turkey, Pakistan and India. Students will be asked to write a concise 2-3 page assessment of the state of human rights (focused on freedom of association and expression) in their chosen country, a 4-5 page analysis of the major domestic actors within a society that are critical to progress in democracy and human rights, and an 8-10 page strategic approach for an external actors interested in assisting reform efforts within a particular country.

title: Identity and Conflict
description: This course will introduce students to the concept of identity and its various components 芒聙聯 racial, ethnic, religious, national 芒聙聯 and explore how and why issues of identity often are at the core of contemporary international conflicts. We also will investigate the unique challenges that face negotiators and mediators when trying to manage identity-based conflicts. We will define conflict very broadly, looking not only at the role of identity in violent or potentially violent conflicts (such as the Balkans, India-Pakistan, Cyprus, and Israeli-Palestinian situations) but also in other types of international interaction (such as the debate over Turkey芒聙聶s accession to the EU, immigration and migration, and even international commercial transactions). The goal will be for the student to come away with a better understanding of the role that issues of identity play, even if indirectly, in many contemporary international conflicts, and to be better equipped to recognize and assess identity issues when they are present. Although parts of the course will be theoretical, the thrust is practical, reflecting the fact that identity issues are real, volatile, and have a pronounced impact on the way in which international conflicts unfold. The role of identity in international interaction shows no signs of lessening.

title: Global Marketing Strategies
description: Marketing strategy is increasingly impacted by the international context at every level. As global corporations grow ever more dominant within the world economy their role in shaping the future, for good or ill, hardly can be exaggerated. At the same time, competition between these leviathans has never been fiercer. Yet on top of the traditional managerial challenges implicit in balancing the conflicting demands of globalization and localization a whole new set of trends is confronting marketers in their quest for competitive advantage. This course is designed to fully acquaint students with these issues, to impart the most current theory and knowledge of the subject, and equip them with the abilities necessary to make the most relevant and effective decisions in this most dynamic of business arenas. In short its purpose is to build the skills needed to analyze, develop and evaluate international marketing strategies. It will be applicable both to those who plan to use this expertise directly in their careers and those who are seeking an understanding from an outside perspective.

title: The EU: Regional Cooperation & Global Governance
description: We live in a world that challenges the traditional ways in which we have governed societies and citizens. Can the nation-state adequately cope with the effects of cross border trade and capital flows? Can it deal with environmental challenges that span the globe, with foreign policy questions not easily decided by one state on its own? Regional integration is one form of new global governance that might provide some answers to the political, social and economic issues we face. This course therefore examines the development, evolution and institutions of the European Union as a template for potential regional integration in other areas of the world. After considering theories of regional integration, we will look at the historical roots of the European Union and its examine its policy areas today. Student led team projects then turn to other regions of the world to consider the potential for further development in the regional governance structures in the Gulf, Latin American, Africa, and Asia. Lively class participation in each seminar will be required, and the teams will lead a class on their region, and do a joint written report on their findings.

title: International Trade & Investment
description: This course focuses on the practice of international trade and investment policy from the viewpoint of a practitioner (government, business, labor union, environmental organization, media, etc.) The course covers the basic elements of modern-day trade policy, including domestic policy mechanisms, regional or bilateral free trade agreements, and the World Trade Organization, as these address trade in goods, trade in services, private direct cross-border investment, the protection of intellectual property rights, and linkage between trade and social issues. The course is designed to help the student develop practical skills in solving problems presented in international trade and investment policy; gain specific experience in collaborative problem solving through classroom simulations and other exercises; and refine oral and written briefing skills critical to the success of a practitioner in this area. The first part of the semester will focus on the rules that emerge from international trade and investment and that increasingly govern global trade policy making. The second part of the semester will address specific problems in policy making that arise in interagency and international negotiations.

title: The Art of Advocacy Inside & Outside of Government
description: This course examines how advocacy influences political leaders and other stakeholders responsible for shaping international policy for managing global crisis and conflict. The course uses a case format to study examples of advocacy in five real world situations. The objective is to build a pragmatic understanding of the impact of advocacy on policy and the management of complex global problems, including famine, health pandemics war, and environmental disaster. The class will include interactive class discussions with policymakers and advocates who played principal roles in each case. The course will build practical skills, including: the ability to identify, evaluate, and formulate effective advocacy strategies and tactics; and the ability to develop common practitioner tools, such as concise briefing and messaging materials used in advocacy initiatives. Key requirements include a reading for each case, preparation of a discussion guide for one case study (to be completed in teams), a concise one page advocacy briefing memo for each case, and a short final paper.

title: International Financial Institutions
description: The class will cover global economic governance and assess how it has evolved in the post war period that has seen growing trade and financial linkages between advanced, emerging market, and low income countries. The main focus will be on international financial institutions and how they have conducted economic surveillance, crisis prevention, and crisis management to improve economic security. Students will become conversant in the role and structure of these organizations, their cooperation with bilateral and other multilateral standing groups like the G-7, Paris Club, the Financial Stability Forum (FSF), and the G20 that steer international financial and economic policy, and the relationships between the main actors shaping international financial and macroeconomic policy. They will gain an understanding of how global governance is changing in response to the weaknesses triggered by the recent financial crisis, and the key challenges going forward. Although the main focus of the course will be on emerging market and industrial economies, the course will also review development issues facing low income countries.

title: Strategy: Security/Development/Business
description: In an international environment characterized by scarce resources with which to confront complex problems, strategic decision-making is a necessity. Students planning to enter the field of international affairs, therefore, must be nimble in terms of strategic thought and action. This course will focus on building this strategic capacity. The course is interdisciplinary and is designed for students from all 3 MSFS concentrations. It will address the strategy-making process in the national security, development and business worlds, and will use cases from all 3 of these sectors. The course will start with the question of how to define strategy, what are the logics of strategy and why strategy is important. From there the course will deconstruct the strategy-making process into 4 parts: setting strategic goals, assessing strategic resources, mapping strategic environments, and choosing among strategic options. In order to expose students to how strategy is actually conducted, practitioners from each of the sectors will visit the class to review actual strategic successes and failures. The course will then transition from the 芒聙聹analyzing strategy芒聙聺 phase to the 芒聙聹making strategy芒聙聺 phase. In this section students will confront current security, development and business scenarios, and will be required to establish strategic goals, present their environmental analyses, and then come up with strategy proposals. Strategic scenarios will include the U.S. strategy towards Iran, the surge strategy for Iraq, poverty reduction strategies, and Apple Computer芒聙聶s strategy. The course will culminate in a strategy simulation, involving governments, development organizations and businesses.

title: Ethics in International Relations
description: Limited to and required for International Relations and Security Concentrators in the MSFS Program, this course addresses ethical dilemmas arising in international affairs careers in the public, private, and nonprofit sectors. It will offer frameworks as decision-making tools, including those drawn from natural, national, and international law, while emphasizing discussion of specific issues and cases. It will hone decision-making judgment on key problems as mass atrocities, dealing with illiberal governments, forms of slavery, gender justice and opportunity, poverty and hunger, corporate responsibility, and public health. It will explore choice of means, such as military force, counterterrorism measures, economic sanctions, use of contractors rather than public institutions, compromise, and nonpartisanship, as well as dissent and resignation in professional settings. The course will build skills of concise and effective written and oral argumentation based on ethical reasoning.

title: China's Challenge to US
description: In the United States today, people from all walks of life share suspicions about China. Some believe China is a military threat; others are convinced that China has been stealing jobs from American workers and, especially in recent months, many think the Chinese purposely ship defective goods and poisoned food to the US. China has posed a challenge to the United States since the 19^th century, undermining assumptions about US-China relations, convictions about the nation芒聙聶s international behavior and beliefs about China芒聙聶s people and politics. This class will focus attention on the critical contingent moments, from the late19th to the early 21st century, when Americans and Chinese struggled against each other and when they cooperated. It will examine the course of Chinese-American relations looking at myths and reality, weighing cultural clashes and exploring conflicting political and strategic goals. The class will, further, seek to use the past to help evaluate and understand the contemporary scene as well as the prognosis for future Sino-American relations. Issues to be covered include security, politics and economic exchange, the environment and human rights.

title: Development and Demography in Asia
description: In this class you will learn skills that are critical for specialists in a data-driven world: Be able to interpret regression results, and other statistical tables. Compute standard demographic measures of mortality, fertility, and migration. Prepare statistical and demographic graphs and tables, such as a population pyramid and time series data. 芒聙垄 Have a basic understanding of geographic information systems. 芒聙垄 Be able to locate and determine the reliability of demographic data. 芒聙垄 Develop research methods skills relating to questionnaire design and sampling. This course is a seminar and students should participate actively in class. That can be best accomplished by reading carefully each assignment prior to class, posting to the discussion site, and being respectful of your classmates芒聙聶 views and opinions. Students are expected to attend every class meeting and must read all of the required materials prior to class. Each student must post on a weekly basis to the on-line class site. Required readings are available on-line. Complete the five two-page policy briefs on the dates they are due. Each class will be in three parts: 1) a case study; 2) discussion of the assigned readings; 3) a lab relating on demographic data, statistical methods, and/or research methods. We will discuss demographic issues across Asia to give you breadth in the topics, and use country examples to give you depth in specific subareas/regions. Topics will include: Mortality and Fertility Transitions in Asia; China芒聙聶s Policy to Limit Births; Implications of Imbalanced Sex Ratios; Family Systems; Marriage and Marriage Markets; Ageing; Diet, Nutrition, and Famine; Smoking, public health and mortality; Immigration; Urbanization

title: Comparative Regional Economic Development (CRED)
description: The main objective of this course is to help develop and hone the skills needed to diagnose a country芒聙聶s major development constraints or challenges and provide realistic policy advice. The course seeks to achieve this goal by providing students with an international comparative perspective on the key development issues facing countries throughout the developing world. The course is organized around five regional segments or modules corresponding to the world芒聙聶s major developing areas or regions: (i) East Asia; (ii) South Asia; (iii) Sub-Saharan Africa; (iv) Latin America; and (v) the Middle East and North Africa. Under each module, a set of two or three leading development issues are analyzed. These issues are selected with a view to analyzing a particular issue in the context of a region in which that issue is most prominent or challenging. The intention, therefore, is to present each major development issue in stark relief and encourage comparisons and contrasts across regions and countries where appropriate. For example, equity and distributional issues and macroeconomic stabilization are examined against the backdrop of Latin America where these issues have been particularly acute, while economic stagnation, how to initiate and sustain economic growth and aid effectiveness are analyzed in the context of Sub-Saharan Africa. Similarly, how to make growth more pro-poor is studied in South Asia, and the challenges of rebalancing growth, raising productivity and moving up the global value chain are examined in East Asia. Finally, resource management and governance issues related to changing the role and functions of the state are investigated in the oil-rich countries of the Middle East and North Africa.

title: Operational/Advocacy NGOs
description: This course will examine the operations of nongovernmental organizations (NGO芒聙聶s) addressing significant global problems. It is designed to build professional skills for working in NGO芒聙聶s or with NGO芒聙聶s, including local and international organizations. We will examine the various roles played by NGO芒聙聶s, including research, service delivery, monitoring, organizing, and political advocacy. The course will look at specific examples of NGO contributions to global solutions, including in the areas of arms control, conflict resolution, development, global health, the environment, women芒聙聶s rights, and trafficking in persons. Students will prepare short papers and design an advocacy plan for an NGO (real or notional) to confront a problem faced by vulnerable populations in a developing country and receive feedback from NGO experts.

title: Consulting Methods
description: This course provides an overview of the core methods and skills employed by leading consulting firms serving federal and commercial markets. Students will be introduced to common consulting frameworks, tools and methodologies and develop an understanding of how to apply these tools to address key challenges facing government and commercial clients. Students will develop these skills through hands-on learning and team projects, and will also have the opportunity to learn from and network with eminent guest speakers. The course is designed to meet the needs of students from all three MSFS concentrations. Students that are interested in learning consulting techniques and those interested pursuing a consulting industry career will particularly benefit. The instructors are principals in Deloitte Consulting, LLP.

title: International Project Finance and Investment
description: The course will focus on design, development, structuring and execution of international project finance transactions. The semester will commence with the building blocks of structured finance including discussions of the differences between corporate and project financing and reviewing the interests of the various participants. We will then move quickly into the discussion of risk identification, allocation and mitigation, which is the foundation of creative financial solutions. The second half of the course will commence with a detailed review of the concepts and use of financial analysis and financial modeling followed by a discussion of the role of contracts in mitigating risk. The second half will also include a series of case studies as well as guest speakers that will discuss topics such as financial documentation, syndication, re-structuring troubled projects and the increasing emphasis on environmental and social issues in successful project implementation.

title: Finance & Global Climate Change
description: The course will equip students with the basic tools to engage in finance and climate change debates and strategies at the international level. Global climate change will be a core challenge of international security, business and development in the 21st century. As the impacts of climate change increase so will risks to global security caused by issues such as mass migration and increasingly scarce natural resources. Businesses will need to identify the risks and capture the opportunities related to climate change to ensure growth and security in their supply chains. For developing countries and emerging economies, mainstreaming climate change into broader institutional and governance decisions is an increasing necessity to ensure sustainable development and will be a pre-requisite for attracting climate change finance. Understanding the policy direction and implementation of climate finance strategies is therefore an important skill to enhance meaningful engagement in international relations. Tackling the climate change challenge is much more complex challenge than simply setting policy goals to reduce greenhouse gas emissions: it requires strategic global investment at a tremendous scale.

title: International Negotiation
description: The objectives of this course are to explore and discuss the various theories of and approaches to negotiation analysis; develop an understanding of the many factors that influence the international negotiation process (including power, domestic politics, culture); and integrate theory and reality by analyzing case studies. Students also will participate in a negotiation simulation over two class periods. The ultimate goal of the course is to enhance our ability to understand the factors that affect the process and outcome of international negotiations, and thereby enhance our ability to identify conflicts that are ripe for negotiation as well as assess the dynamics of on-going negotiations. A better understanding of the negotiation process and the factors that determine may even make us more effective negotiators.

title: Congressional Decision-Making and U.S. Foreign Policy
description: This seminar will examine how Congress participates 芒聙聯 or does not participate 芒聙聯 in U.S. foreign policy, and look at the ways in which others attempt to work with and influence Congressional decision-making. It will examine the basic premise that 芒聙聯 in any democracy 芒聙聯 politics is closely connected to the making and conduct of foreign policy and that in the United States a great deal of the political process plays out in Congress. The first part of the course looks at Congress芒聙聶 role in foreign policy decision-making historically and in the present, the second examines how Congress is organized to make foreign policy decisions and assert its influence, and the third addresses the interplay between Congress and other political actors in foreign policy decision-making. The instructor was a Foreign Service Officer who also worked on Capitol Hill and had extensive dealing with Congress on foreign policy.

title: The BRICs & the World Economy
description: Although Brazil, Russia, India and China are often lumped together since a report in 2001 by Goldman Sachs famously grouped them as the BRICs and projected that they would be larger economically than the developed countries by 2050, they are very different economically, politically and structurally. The growth and development of these emerging economies raises many challenges and opportunities, not just to their own development strategy, but also for the rest of the world. In the aftermath of the current global financial and economic crisis they are on track to become larger than the developed countries faster than earlier projected. Their rise also raises issues of competition for markets, and natural resources; the role of population, education, and technology in global competition and relative wages between developed and developing economies; the most effective development models in terms of the mix of the state vs. the market, and authoritarian vs. democratic governance systems; the appropriate structure of global governance institutions and mechanism and their representation within them; security and the stability of the global system; and not least, global warming and sustainability in light of their large contributions to CO2 emissions and different approaches to what they consider appropriate burden sharing between developed and developing countries in dealing with this challenge. This course will begin by reviewing the history and development of these countries and the challenges to their development. It will then explore the issues highlighted above. Students will be asked to write position papers on some of the above issues from the perspective of these countries, as well as from the US, the EC, and the rest of the developing world. By the end of the course students will have a good grasp of how the BRICs are impacting and being impacted by the world economy.

title: Conflict Management and International Security
description: The seminar's central focus is the challenge of creating security and building peace in the 21st Century. It will introduce students to the "intellectual map" of the peace-maker by exploring a wide range of literatures and cases, exploring the roots and sources of conflict, and illustrating the varieties of third party intervention for conflict management. Drawing upon readings and discussion, students will examine changing patterns of conflict and explore a range of institutions, techniques and strategies for responding to the challenge of disorder.

title: Energy in a Low-Carbon World
description: This course is designed to present students with an introduction to the key issues and challenges confronting government policy-makers and business leaders in global energy. The course focuses on the impacts and intersection of emerging but potentially transformational events: 1. Forecasts of rising global energy demand, especially for fossil fuels 2. Growing concern about the dangers posed to US and global security by global climate change (GCC), especially the role and impact of CO2 emissions from fossil fuel use in the energy sector 3. Supply-side challenges in moving to low carbon sources for power generation and transportation 4. Demand-side challenges, especially in achieving greater energy efficiency 5. The changing dynamics of international oil and gas markets 6. The potential convergence of the transportation sector with electricity 7. Challenges posed by, and potential impacts of, critical geopolitical regional 芒聙聹hotspots芒聙聺 With a growing consensus on the science, causes and impacts of global climate change (GCC), especially since the issuance of the Fourth Assessment Report of the UN Inter-Governmental Panel on Climate Change (IPCC) in 2007, a lively policy debate has emerged on how to meet the world芒聙聶s energy and economic needs, while at the same time protecting the environment. A cornerstone of this debate centers on how to de-carbonize the power and transportation sectors. There has been renewed interest in low carbon technologies such as wind and solar, nuclear power, and clean coal technologies such as carbon capture and storage, as well as greater attention being paid to energy efficiency and natural gas. Nevertheless, despite a growing global consensus on the need to reduce carbon emissions, use of coal proceeds apace with India and China developing new coal-fired power generation at an alarming rate. Simultaneously, recent oil price volatility has fueled debate over the future direction of the oil industry, and its impact on economic performance and competitiveness of alternative fuels. In addition, a variety of regional energy issues are emerging to play a more critical role in the policy picture, for example: the importance of China and India and their energy policy to handle booming economic development; the role of Russia as an energy superpower, its impact on European energy security, and inter-relationship with Central Asian oil and gas infrastructure, and; the emergence of several other key countries and regions in the global energy industry.

title: Political and Social Challenges to Security
description: This course will examine how global security and stability are impacted by political and social challenges, which have traditionally fallen outside of security analysis and policy making. We will investigate the impact of these issues (refugees, human rights, and democracy promotion) on existing and emerging conflict areas, the arguments for and against factoring these issues into security analysis, and the resulting practical realities on foreign policy development and implementation. We will examine the challenges of policy making when these issues are at odds with one another. Students will explore how policy makers and practitioners manage various elements of the foreign policy making process in light of the connections between these issues, and how equipped governments and international organizations are to handle such linkages. We will look at both historical and current case studies.

title: Media & World Affairs
description: This course examines the relationship between the media and the conduct of international relations, beginning with Julius Caesar's self-serving account of the Gallic Wars and continuing through the media coverage of the Bush administration's case for pre-emptive war on Iraq, the global financial crisis and environmental issues. It also traces the evolution of the American news media from two developments in the 15th and 16th Centuries: the invention of moveable type and the translation of the Bible into English and German. The course includes a number of weekly writing assignments, and it places a heavy emphasis on the distillation and organization of large amounts of material into clear, concise prose, the kind that's valued not only in journalism, but also in industry, government and NGOs. In addition, there is a final paper of 2,000-2,500 words on a topic to be chosen by the student and approved by the professor.

title: Change, Power, and Media in the Arab World
description: Whether you plan to pursue a career in journalism, diplomacy, banking or some other field, this course will give you insight into the interplay of power and the media in a crucial part of the globe likely to retain the key to world peace and war. Through a close look at that interplay, the course also aims to challenge assumptions held by the Arabs about the outside world and held about them by it. Among the many other questions the course aims to raise will be: Who is Arab? How has that affiliation changed over the centuries? How is it likely to change in light of the Arabs芒聙聶 current quest for more freedom to decide who and what they are? And what implications will that quest have for their relations with each other, with their immediate neighborhood and well beyond? We then look at the issue of whether the Arab media have been vectors or causes of change. What lessons are to be drawn from either conclusion? What political and economic prospects, if any, could either conclusion entail? Students will have an opportunity to visit TV and newspaper newsrooms and work on assignments based on such visits. One of the objectives is direct exposure to different journalistic practices for comparison and conclusions about what the media scene in a given culture tells us about that culture and its power structure. Another objective is to make the students think about the limits and opportunities of cultural and political bridge-building.

title: Global Business and Public Policy Practicum
description: Global companies play an increasingly important role in shaping public policy on international trade and related issues. Students aspiring to leadership in the public and private sectors need to understand "real-world" government relations as practiced by today's major companies. This course focuses on the practice of business-government relations, and in particular, the role of the global company on international economic issues. The Fall 2010 course introduced students to the government relations programs of such companies as Proctor & Gamble, Caterpillar, Xerox, Bayer, McDonald芒聙聶s, Google, Starbucks, Boeing, Novartis and IBM. For 2011 the course will introduce students to the important role of government relations messaging and communications, including the shaping of public opinion芒聙聰now a vitalcomponent of successful public policy programs. Students examine government relations crisis management, with particular reference to Google芒聙聶s decision to shut down its search engine in China rather than comply with Chinese law requiring self-censorship of Internet searches. We芒聙聶ll contrast the business case for complying with Chinese law and Google芒聙聶s commitment to the principle of Internet freedom. To create a personal experience of the practice of government relations each student is teamed with a Washington-based corporate government relations executive involved in international business policy. A highlight of the course each year, the executive mentor assigns an issue project important to their company and the student has the opportunity to study and make a presentation on the issue for the company芒聙聶s Washington office. Emphasis is on internal and external communication skills for effective Washington representation, as well as advocacy, networking, decision-making processes and the respective roles of Capitol Hill and Executive Branch agencies. With reference to key issues, students will learn how major corporations identify priorities and develop and advocate policy positions.

title: International Law
description: This course will provide a detailed examination of the fundamentals of public international law. Taking a case-method approach, the course will discuss the sources of international law, the relationship of international law to domestic law, international personality, jurisdiction under international law-- including general principles of jurisdiction, extradition, the law of the sea, and air and space law, immunity from jurisdiction, state responsibility for injury to aliens, international human rights law, the law relating to the recourse to force, and the law concerning the conduct of hostilities. The primary text of the course will be: *International Law: Cases and Materials, 5th ed. 2009*, by Lori Damrosch, et al. Students will be evaluated based on class participation and *four* short legal memoranda.

title: Good Governance and Capable
description: Over the last decade, the effectiveness of the state and how states are governed have emerged as central topics among donors and the aid policy community. There is now increased consensus that well functioning and accountable public sector institutions that make sound policies, regulate markets, mobilize public resources, and deliver services are the foundation for sustainable and broad-based socioeconomic development. Whether it is responding to natural disasters, curtailing humanitarian crises, mitigating conflict, or effectively implementing development policy 芒聙聯 a capable state and its institutions of governance must lead the way. Increased interest in state capacity building and good governance has also been fueled by the on-going debate on aid effectiveness. After decades of post-war development assistance, there is growing concern that a lack of attention to state capacity and good governance has led to an unsustainable addiction to foreign assistance. The preferred prescription has been increased 芒聙聹country ownership芒聙聺 and use of host country systems. This presupposes capable state institutions that can effectively plan, design, execute and evaluate development programs in an accountable and transparent manner. Yet, daily headlines more often highlight governance failings than they do success, suggesting a continued need for reflection and improvements in how the foreign aid community promotes good governance and builds capable states. This class explores the relationship between good governance, capable states, and sustainable development. The emphasis is not on why the problem of governance exists, although we will establish conceptual foundations about the nature and scope of the state. Rather, our focus is on how the development community tackles the challenge, and what aspiring development managers can learn from collective experience in this field. Working both individually and in teams, students will gain a practical understanding of approaches, methods and solutions that are used today to tackle the problem of governance and state building. Students are encouraged to challenge assumptions, and openly examine the successes, failures and track-record of a unique type of development assistance. Class discussion will center on practical experience of development managers and real-world reading material 芒聙聯 case studies, toolkits, operational handbooks, and program evaluations. Students will gain practical skills to analyze arguments, assess governance challenges, and make recommendations on program design, focus and priority.

title: Introduction to Microfinance
description: Microfinance is usually viewed as a development success in that it has demonstrated the capacity to improve standards of living among the world芒聙聶s poorest in a sustainable way. Microfinance development programs target their financial services to low-income micro entrepreneurs with fewer than 10 employees. The purpose of this class is to provide an introduction to best practices in the design, management, and evaluation of microfinance programs. At the end of the course, students will be able to describe the role of microfinance in the financial services sector; the profile of the average microfinance client; the range of providers of microfinance services; the role of government in developing microfinance; and the principles of microfinance performance management.

title: Foreign Policy Making in Developing Nations
description: This course is aimed at four objectives: (1) to give students a broad understanding of the foreign policy process in developing nations in the major developing areas, dealing specifically with each area both as a region, and with a closer examination of the foreign policy 芒聙聯 outcomes and process 芒聙聯 of several of the major states; (2) to present for criticism and discussion some of the major theoretical approaches to the analysis and comparison of foreign policy and the processes by which it is made; (3) to allow students to deepen their expertise in the region of their interest through the research and presentation of a foreign policy study of a state in this region; and (4) to sharpen students' analytic skills through creation of a common research design so that the students' research will fit together into a collaborative framework. This will facilitate both a specific understanding of the foreign policy in individual nations as well as an ability to make comparisons between states and generalizations about the topic as a whole.

title: Fin Reg & Reform in Dev Countries
description: Financial markets have grown in size and sophistication over the last decade in the developing, newly industrializing, and post-Communist world. Financial liberalization in these countries has often involved contrasting polices. On the one hand, governments in capital-scarce economies have retreated from direct involvement in the allocation of financial resources. On the other hand, government authorities have also intervened in financial markets to protect consumers and investors, to promote fairness and transparency in financial transactions, and to maintain stability. Financial crises in emerging markets throughout the 1990s, however, raise several questions regarding the effectiveness of the financial reforms undertaken in those countries. Meanwhile, more recent challenges to the global financial system have required governments and international financial institutions to rethink regulatory standards. This seminar explores how and why governments intervene in financial markets.
