title: Ethics: Just Wars
description: When is it right for a nation to go to war? Once at war, what sort of behavior becomes permissible for soldiers and commanders? And what obligations do soldiers, sovereigns and states have in the aftermath of wars? Throughout the semester, we will tackle these questions (and many tangential and subsidiary ones) through the lens of contemporary just wary theory. We'll begin with a brief unit situating the just war tradition among other historically prominent perspectives on war芒聙聰realism, pacifism, militarism and crusadism. We will subsequently turn our attention to issues of jus ad bellum, or moral issues surrounding the initiation of war. Subtopics will include aggression, preemptions, preventive wars, interventions, secessions, reprisals. We shall then turn our focus to conduct within war (jus in bello), and take a close look at the Doctrine of Double Effect, noncombatant immunity, the moral responsibility of soldiers fighting unjust wars, and conditions of "supreme emergency". Finally, we芒聙聶ll discuss a recently popular movement in just war theory that focus on justice in the conclusion of war (jus post bellum). While our readings will come primarily from contemporary western just war theory, we will also examine the historical roots of the western just war tradition, as well as the ethics of war in other cultures. In our final unit, we will briefly explore the Islamic, Jewish and Chinese traditions of just war.

title: Ethics: Privacy
description: This course will examine the philosophical dimension of privacy. It will start by getting our philosophical bearings in the various distinctions between the public and the private through readings of John Locke, John Stuart Mill and Hannah Arendt. We will then move on to the different policy perspectives. Warren Brandeis and Warren Samuels in their famous law review article defined privacy as the right to be left alone. Further commentary raised the question of the whether privacy was a single value or a combination of other values. Alan Westin defined privacy as the right to control information about oneself. Since research showed that people differed in their preferences about information collection and use, these ideas naturally led to a focus on informed consent 芒聙聯 information collection and use could be made legitimate through a process of appropriate notice and choice. The fair information practices approach to privacy built on that idea and added a number of other protections such as restrictions on further use of information collected for a specified purpose. This approach to protecting the privacy interests of data subjects treats privacy as a fundamental aspect of human dignity and can be traced back to the human rights tradition in political philosophy. An alternative policy approach focuses less on the rights of data subjects and more on the harms that people might suffer as a result of the misuse of information. This outcomes-based framework can also include the idea of granting to data subjects a property right in information that concerns them. It approaches privacy through the lens of economic cost-benefit analysis and can be traced back to the utilitarian perspective in political philosophy. For both approaches the question of how to balance privacy against other values has to be addressed. The final section applies the insights gained from this philosophical assessment of privacy to two contemporary policy issues: government access to personal information for national security purposes and the collection and use of personal information by businesses for purposes such as online behavioral advertising

title: Ethics: Moral Skepticism
description: Moral issues pervade our lives: As friends, as daughters and sons, as students, as consumers, as citizens (for example), we are faced with question about how to treat others properly and how to live a meaningful and fulfilling life. But fundamental doubts about morality are often just as pervasive: How can there be objective moral truths if people disagree so deeply and pervasively about moral issues? Are there objective moral requirements, or is morality a matter of mere preference? Do moral norms apply universally, or are they relative to cultures? How do moral values fit in our scientific picture of the world? How could we ever come to know universal and objective norms? Can we live a good life if we are constrained by moral norms? This course will introduce you to the philosophical study of morality by way of discussing and addressing major skeptical challenges to morality. The course aims to enable you to engage in moral debate in a serious, critical, and self-reflective way. In the first part of the course, we will explore and analyze skeptical challenges to morality carefully, and consider general responses to skeptical arguments. In this context, we will discuss what would be required for the justification of moral claims and the rational solution of moral conflict. In the second part, we are going to explore important moral theories, analyzing how they try to meet skeptical challenges and how they help us navigate moral issues in our lives. In the last part of the course we are going discuss what a civil and peaceful engagement with moral disagreement and moral conflict in modern societies should look like.

This course will introduce you to the philosophical study of morality by way of discussing and addressing major skeptical challenges to morality. The course aims to enable you to engage in moral debate in a serious, critical, and self-reflective way. In the first part of the course, we will explore and analyze skeptical challenges to morality carefully, and consider general responses to skeptical arguments. In this context, we will discuss what is required for the justification of moral claims and the rational solution of moral conflict. In the second part, we are going to explore important moral theories, analyzing how they try to meet skeptical challenges and how they help us navigate moral issues in our lives. In the last part of the course, we are going discuss what a civil and peaceful engagement with moral disagreement and moral conflict in modern societies should look like.

title: Ethics: International Law
description: In this course on the philosophy of international law we will consider different approaches to and the different ethical, legal, and political values relevant to developing and evaluating systems of laws and institutions on a global scale. The course will begin with a careful consideration of John Rawls芒聙聶s The Law of Peoples followed by an assessment of his view through the arguments raised by his critics. We will then turn to Samantha Besson and John Tasioulas芒聙聶s new anthology The Philosophy of International Law. Our class will cover selections on international legitimacy, international democracy, sovereignty, just war, humanitarian intervention, and responsibility, along with relevant areas of law including international criminal law, human rights law, and environmental law. This course will give students a background in legal theory as applied to the international context and provide students an opportunity to think and write on some of the most pressing issues facing the world today.

title: Ethics: Ethics of Authenticity
description: This course will examine the normative ideal of authenticity and related concepts such as self-discovery and self-realization. In Ancient Greek thought, ethics was not limited to the study of our moral duty, but was rather more broadly construed as an attempt to give an account of what constituted a good life for a human being. Modern moral theory has, in contrast, focused almost exclusively on questions about what we owe to one another as a matter of moral duty. Beginning with an overview of contemporary moral theory, we will then raise questions about whether this duty centered approach to ethics is adequate if it fails to tell us how to lead good lives. We will examine in detail a distinctively modern approach to answering the good life question: the claim that the good life is the life which fulfills one芒聙聶s true self. We will read selected texts by authors who attempt to defend this ideal of authenticity as well as several by thinkers who critique the very idea of authentic selfhood as being based on a mistaken essentialism about the self. Students will be invited to reflect on films, poetry, and other cultural works in order understand how the ideal of authenticity may animate their own thinking about life-choices and personal development.

title: Ethics: Global Justice
description: Do national boundaries matter, morally? What responsibilities do people in the developed world have to help people in developing countries? How should the burdens of coping with global warming be divided in the international system? When and to what degree is clinical research in the developing world morally permissible? These are ethical questions on a global scale, and they are being answered, reflectively or accidentally, by individuals, governments, and non-governmental organizations every day. They must not be taken lightly. This course provides an introduction to the problem of international justice, with attention to both its theoretical and practical dimensions. Major philosophical themes will include human rights theory, the moral significance of national and cultural boundaries, and the currency of distributive justice; we will extend these frameworks to practice-oriented discussions of global inequality and poverty, environmental devastation, the ethics of vaccination and global research, and violence against women and children. Readings for the course come predominantly from contemporary authors in ethics and political philosophy, with some time devoted to relevant scholarship in international relations, contemporary bioethics, as well as selected texts from the history of political and moral thought. Though interdisciplinary in its subject matter, this course has a firm methodological home in moral philosophy. Students will study core ethical frameworks and will complete the course with some understanding of how historically important philosophical ideas continue to shape contemporary discussions of global justice.

title: Ethics: Justice
description: 芒聙聹Justice is the first virtue of social institutions芒聙聺 the political philosopher John Rawls writes in his Theory of Justice. We primarily evaluate social institutions and the communities that we live on how well they are doing on distributing goods and bads, benefits, opportunities and burdens, in the appropriate manner. We criticize societies that allow, foster or perpetrate injustice. We take justice to be a guiding principle for policy and a constraint for political decision-making. Understanding what justice requires of us and our social and political institutions is therefore important for all active members of a political community. By exploring the concept of justice, this class will introduce you to basic ethical and philosophical questions about social and political institutions. In the first part of the semester, we will explore prominent contemporary theories of justice. The goal of a theory of justice is to identify and argue for a particular conception of justice, spelling out what justice requires and establishing principles that we can use to assess real-life institutions, We are also going to look at the relationship between justice and the realization of other ideals such as equality, liberty, rights, legitimacy and recognition. The first part of the course will be primarily lecture-based. There will be a take-home midterm exam at the end of this section. For the second part of the semester, we will together look at some focal areas of injustice in our society. A timely and adequate political philosophy needs to look not just at what justice ideally requires, but has to help us understand particular instances of injustices in our society, the kinds of suffering they cause, how they are reproduced, and what responses are possible. In particular, we are probably going to look at issues of class, race, gender & sexuality, and disability. For this part of the class, students will work in small research groups, which will together be responsible for doing some basic do research on these issues and for presenting their results in class. Students will write a final research paper on a topic within the area of their research groupm

title: Ethics: Reasons & Persons
description: What do we have most reason to do? What is it rational to do? Are the answers to these questions affected by our view of what it means to be a person? And do the answers to all of these questions lead us to surprising conclusions about how we should be addressing major moral issues today? These difficult and important questions are the ones asked by Derek Parfit in his modern classic, Reasons and Persons (Oxford: Oxford University Press, 1984). At the most general level, the book tackles questions concerning three main topics: morality, rationality and personal identity. As Parfit sees it, questions concerning what one morally ought to do are intimately bound up with questions concerning what it is rational for one to do and what it means to be and to stay a particular person. In this course, we will adopt Parfit芒聙聶s perspective, investigating questions of moral theory alongside questions of rationality and personal identity. The goal of this course, then, is to survey a wide variety of views on each of these three issues, taking Parfit芒聙聶s book as our starting point. The ultimate question will be for us, as it was for Parfit: what should I do? However, attempting to provide an answer to this question will require delving into each of the topics raised in Reasons and Persons. Thus, in addition to investigating theories of morality such as The Self-Interest Theory, Consequentialism, and The Common-Sense View of Morality, we will also spend some time asking how different views on the nature of rationality and of personal identity affect our reactions to each moral theory. The only text that you will be required to purchase for this course is Parfit芒聙聶s Reasons and Persons, but additional sources will be utilized (and supplied via blackboard) as well. Additional authors may include (but are not limited to): R.M. Hare, Thomas Nagel, Robert Nozick, T.M. Scanlon, Henry Sidgwick, and Bernard Williams

title: Ethics: Nature & Philosophy
description: What is the connection between what I am and how I should live? Is there really any connection at all? Could a scientific understanding of human nature, informed by evolutionary psychology tell us anything about how we should live? Many students come to an ethics class thinking that there is some kind of a connection between human nature and what is right and wrong or good or bad for humans. Many philosophers have also seen some sort of a connection between human nature and ethics. However, there are a great many different ways of understanding what nature is and how human nature might relate to morality. In this class, we芒聙聶ll start with Aristotle芒聙聶s very ancient understanding of ethics and nature and the relationship between the two, and Thomas Aquinas芒聙聶s Christianized Aristotelianism. Then we will work our way through more modern views in Hume and perhaps Hobbs, and we will consider how human nature, scientifically understood, might relate to morality. In particular, we will ask what human nature, as a product of biological evolution, might have to do with ethics. We will also consider the views of some philosophers like Immanuel Kant who don't seem to think that human nature has much at all to do with what is right or wrong or good or bad. We will close by looking at how some contemporary philosophers in the Aristotelian and Thomstic tradition try to rehabilitate a classical understanding of human nature and its relation to morality.

title: Ethics: Rights
description: This course will be divided into three sections corresponding to the following important questions concerning individual rights. First, do we have any rights? We will begin with Aquinas芒聙聶s discussion of rights as the object of justice and consider whether Aquinas芒聙聶s theory is consistent with the notion of subjective rights. Second, do we have any natural or human rights, and, if so, why and what are they? In this section, we will consider whether there are any natural rights, as opposed to only positive rights guaranteed by law, and evaluate a few different responses to this question. Assuming we do have natural rights, we will consider the further question of what justifies such rights and evaluate both deontological and utilitarian justifications for natural rights. We also will consider, drawing primarily from work in the context of global justice, the Universal Declaration of Human Rights and a few theoretical approaches to international human rights. Third, what rights should be guaranteed by law? In this final section, we will look specifically at the Bill of Rights and consider a few contemporary issues and cases involving individual rights. The objective of this course is not to answer these questions definitively, but to invite careful consideration of the different views and thoughtful and well-reasoned argument when presenting one芒聙聶s own view. Objectives Through the reading assignments, lectures, and class discussions, students will learn or gain familiarity with the following: 1. Important historical views on rights; 2. Important contemporary views on rights; 3. Three influential moral theories: natural law, Kantianism, and utilitarianism (and, specifically, how each theory offers a different justification for natural rights); 4. Key concepts and terms concerning rights such as the Hohfeldian incidents and rights inflation; 5. Certain traditional debates in legal philosophy concerning rights; 6. Important legal documents on human rights such as the Bill of Rights and the Universal Declaration of Human Rights; 7. Current issues involving international human rights; and 8. Historical and current issues involving women芒聙聶s rights, religious rights, minority rights, gay rights, and environmental rights. Requirements In this course students will be required to attend classes, complete reading assignments, take a midterm and final exam, and write a two-part short paper on one of the contemporary issues discussed in the textbook. Grades will be determined as follows: 1. 10% Participation 2. 20% Short paper (part I) 3. 30% Short paper (part II) 4. 25% Midterm exam 5. 25% Final exam Required Text: Patrick Hayden, The Philosophy of Human Rights (Paragon House, 2001) Students will read selections from the Hayden text and other items and articles that will be posted on Blackboard. Some of the authors of the assigned materials include: Thomas Aquinas, Hugo Grotius, Edmund Burke, Immanuel Kant, John Stuart Mill, Thomas Paine, Brian Tierney, John Finnis, Joel Feinberg, H.L.A Hart, Thomas Pogge, Martha Nussbaum, Ronald Dworkin, Will Kymlicka, Catherine MacKinnon, and Edith Brown Weiss. The instructor reserves the right to make some minor changes, but the above information otherwise reflects what will be covered in the course.

title: Ethics: Human Rights, Global Justice
description: In this course we examine three interrelated sets of issues of justice: (i) the evolution of human rights theory to include 芒聙聹second generation芒聙聺 rights, i.e., universal rights pertaining to social and economic well-being; (ii) the disagreement between 芒聙聹cosmopolitan芒聙聺 theories that hold that duties of distributive justice are global in scope and 芒聙聹political conceptions芒聙聺 that claim that strong duties of distributive justice (if there are any) arise only among fellow citizens within a nation-state; and (iii) theories that claim that some globally applicable duties of justice, even if weaker than duties to fellow citizens, limit the kinds of disadvantages that may be imposed upon the global poor. Course requirements (and percentage of final grade) include 3 papers of approximately 8 pages (20% each), active class participation (20%) and 2-page summaries (20%) of 4 of the daily reading assignments (your choice from throughout the term). There are two required texts, plus additional readings to be posted on Blackboard: (i) Global Ethics: Seminal Essays (Paragon Issues in Philosophy) [Paperback] Thomas Pogge (Author), Keith Horton (Author) ISBN-10: 1557788707 ISBN-13: 978-1557788702 (ii) Global Justice: Seminal Essays (Paragon Issues in Philosophy) [Paperback] Thomas Pogge (Author), Darrel Moellendorf ISBN-10: 1557788693 ISBN-13: 978-1557788696

title: Ethics: Cooperation & Conflict
description: To many of us, cooperation seems like the ideal for human relationships and interactions. Our everyday life seems filled with encouragements to work together and strive for a harmony of effort and interests. The rhetoric of democratic politics often strikes this note, and so does commonplace talk of what makes for a healthy and rewarding intimate relationship. We often make related judgments about conflict. We often see conflict not only as something undesirable in itself, but as something to be overcome through a unity of vision and purpose common among different people. The American national motto, emblazoned on the Seal of the United States and its currency, reflects this optimism about the possibility of cooperative mutual identification: E pluribus unum (芒聙聹Out of many, one芒聙聺). But however often we are encouraged to work together and told that interpersonal harmony is possible and desirable, conflict is everywhere and it shows no clear or consistent signs of general decline. The experience of divisions of every kind, even the internal conflict of one芒聙聶s being 芒聙聹divided against oneself芒聙聺 or 芒聙聹of two minds芒聙聺 on a subject, seems as common as the hope that such divisions can be overcome. It is tempting to say that conflict芒聙聰between divergent religious perspectives, between social and economic classes, between one芒聙聶s own impulses or desires芒聙聰is as close to an uncontroversial 芒聙聹fact of life芒聙聺 as can be found. In light of these facts, it is natural to wonder what we should make of this apparent conflict between the commonplace ideal of harmony within human life and the enduring fact of conflict itself. A range of philosophical questions about how we ought to understand and deal with conflict arise: If cooperation is so important, why is this? Does the value of being cooperative depend upon something apart from cooperative activity itself? That is, should we agree to cooperate with others only when there is something additionally in it for us? Or is cooperation somehow worthwhile for its own sake? Is the value of cooperation really best understood in terms of the removal of the causes of conflict? Should we try to get rid of conflict entirely? What would that be like? Why would it be desirable? What would we have to be like for this to be possible? Are we really like that? If not, can we expect to be? And what芒聙聶s so bad about conflict, after all? When is it bad, and why? Must we always try to work together, or can conflict be productive? These ethical and political questions are some of those which will concern us in this course. They concern what sort of individual and collective life is best for us, and they go to the heart of who we are and what it means for us to be free and for our world to be just. Our aim will be to deepen and sharpen our ability to think through the problems and possibilities of cooperation in human life by exploring the question of the sources, meaning and value of conflict. We will benefit from the guidance provided by a diverse selection of thoughtful voices within philosophical ethics and politics, including those of Thomas Hobbes, Malcolm X, Immanuel Kant and Friedrich Nietzsche. The course芒聙聶s graded content will include one short and two medium-length written assignments as well as class participation, of which contributions to in-class discussions of the assigned material will form a part.

title: Ethics: Public Policy
description: This course is an introduction to the study of ethics, from a theoretical and practical perspective, as it relates to public policy. The course will begin with a close reading of classic texts including works of Aristotle, Kant, and Mill, which try to make sense of what makes human lives and actions good, happy, and meaningful. We will subject these views to contemporary reflections on what it means to be a moral actor, considering pointed critiques of traditional western thinking about ethics. Throughout, we will be asking ourselves about the constraints that ethical considerations might ask us to impose on our own freedom, and whether there are elements of the ethical life that are not within our control. Finally, we will apply these ethical frameworks to public policy asking how ethical considerations inform (or fail to inform) our public policy through close study of specific issues of contemporary national, political and global significance such as international migration, poverty, and corporate globalization. Throughout the course we will explore the question of how and when morally evaluative judgments are possible concerning the practices of individuals, communities, corporations, and nations. Requirements of the course will include two papers, a mid-term and final examination, and class participation.

title: Ethics: Life Choices
description: 芒聙聵Life Choices芒聙聶 is a course in practical ethics designed around the theme of genuine practicality: everything studied here is designed to make you, the student, better at reasoning concerning issues that do, or will, matter to you. The general idea here is that the late teens-early twenties is an era marked for most people by major decisions, many of which have a moral component (even if one doesn芒聙聶t realize it at the time). In college, many students are exposed, for the first time, to drugs, opportunities for sex, new ideas concerning gender and sexuality, and will perhaps make choices that structure much of the rest of their lives. If it makes sense to ask, regarding these issues, 芒聙聵What should I do,芒聙聺 or 芒聙聹Is it wrong to do this,芒聙聺 then a space for thinking carefully about these issues in a rigorous way could be valuable. My goal for this class is to provide exactly this space, along with the tools for such rigorous thinking. While each of you is certainly capable of thinking about and talking through these issues on your own, it is my job to give you a methodological and theoretical foundation for careful, systematic reasoning. Although the course is designed to be practical for you, we will not restrict ourselves to issues that a college student is necessarily facing at this moment. Think of the theme this way: If an issue is relevant for someone in college and, say, the decade following, then it is fair game for this course. With that in mind, then, I have tentatively divided the course into the following sections: (1) Forbidden Fruit: Drugs and Sex; (2) Family Ethics: Abortion, Adoption and the Nuclear Family; (3) Global Citizenship: Animals, the Environment and Being Rich. The goal for each student, then, is to think carefully about central moral issues concerning: mind-altering drugs (both legal and not), the value of sex and all of its related issues (whether to have sex, whether some kinds of sex are bad or wrong, birth control, gender roles, etc); the value of various 芒聙聵models芒聙聶 of family and parenting, including whether there are obligations concerning bringing new life into the world (or keeping new life out of the world); and how one will comport herself thoughtfully in society 芒聙聯 whether, for instance, morality requires vegetarianism, ecological conservation, or a life dedicated to famine relief. In this class perhaps more than others, it is worth noting: my goal IS NOT to convince you of any one position on any issue. Rather, my goal is for you to emerge from this class a more thoughtful member of society, capable of justifying the difficult choices you are and will be making in the coming years. We will do this not only through sustained engagement with the ideas and one another, but through developing better reasoning skills, as well as a more theoretical understanding of ethics. We will discuss, for instance, the various ways in which the rightness and wrongness of acts seem determined by their consequences, the nature of the act itself, the character of the performing agent, or the fact that an agreement has been entered into. More particularly, we will focus on difficult questions such as: when do we get to decide to privilege ourselves and loved ones over strangers? Ever? These broader, theoretical considerations will be brought to bear as tools for thinking carefully about each particular issue.

title: Introduction to Logic
description: The primary aims of the course are: (1) to assist students in the development of their abilities to reason logically and think critically; and, (2) to introduce students to formal methods for representing and evaluating -- in terms of soundness and validity -- arguments. We will spend roughly half the semester covering topics in deductive logic, focusing our attentions on syllogistic logic and propositional logic. The rest of the semester we will survey common fallacies and look at selected topics in inductive logic. Time permitting, we will study the methods of elementary quantification. This course satisfies the logic requirement for undergraduate Philosophy majors.

title: Introduction to Logic
description: We gain much of the knowledge we possess through reasoning, and logic is the study of how we ought to reason. Mastery of logic is useful not only to philosophers, but to all those who go on to engage in any kind of persuasive practice (i.e., all of you). The goal of this course will be to familiarize students with the standards and common mistakes of reasoning, and to prepare philosophy majors for further study in philosophical logic or other branches of the discipline. After a brief overview of arguments in general, the first half of the semester will be devoted to deductive logic芒聙聰syllogistic and propositional logic. These two units will give students a working knowledge of validity and soundness. For the last third of the semester, we will turn our attention to the more widely applicable inductive logic, through which we will broaden our understanding of the standards of reason. This course will satisfy the logic requirement for undergraduate Philosophy majors.

We will spend roughly half the semester covering topics in deductive logic, focusing our attentions on syllogistic logic and propositional logic. The rest of the semester we will survey common fallacies and look at selected topics in inductive logic. Time permitting, we will study the methods of elementary quanti?cation. This course satis?es the logic requirement for undergraduate Philosophy majors.

Mastery of logic is useful not only to philosophers, but to all those who go on to engage in any kind of persuasive practice (i.e., all of you). The goal of this course will be to familiarize students with the standards and common mistakes of reasoning, and to prepare philosophy majors for further study in philosophical logic or other branches of the discipline. After a brief overview of arguments in general, the first half of the semester will be devoted to deductive logic芒聙聰syllogistic and propositional logic. For the last third of the semester, we will turn our attention to the more widely applicable inductive logic, through which we will broaden our understanding of the standards of reason. This course will satisfy the logic requirement for undergraduate Philosophy majors.

title: Christian Creeds
description: In the past thirty years or so there has been a lot of work within philosophy on various Christian doctrines, including the rationality of Christian belief, the divine attributes, creation, the Trinity, the Incarnation, the Atonement, resurrection of the body, and heaven and hell. We are going to read and discuss together some of this work. Our aim is to use the tools of philosophy to try to make sense of various ideas central to the Christian creeds. We are not trying to use philosophy to prove that the Christian creeds are true; we are asking, rather, if the propositions of the Christian creeds are true, what light philosophical investigation can shed on them. I should note up front that I am a Christian and will not pretend to be neutral on the question of the literal truth of the creeds. But anybody with an interest in these questions, whether or not he or she shares these commitments, is welcome to take part in these investigations.

title: Metaphysics
description: In contemporary philosophy metaphysics means philosophical inquiry into what seem to be the most fundamental features of reality and of ourselves and into questions of ultimate explanation. This course is an introduction to a number of classic issues in metaphysics as they have been treated in Anglo-American philosophy (with some incursions from the Antipodes) in the twentieth and twenty-first centuries. We will focus on the following issues: (1) Past, present and future. Time seems to flow: it seems that what is now present was once future and will be past. But does it really flow or is the appearance of flow really a kind of illusion to which we are subject? (2) Parts and wholes. The everyday things around are complexes, having parts, and it seems that they continue to exist even as they change in their parts, as my body seems to as its atoms are continually replaced. But is this appearance of continued existence correct, or does a change of parts literally result in a new thing? (3) Mind and Body. What sort of thing are we? physical organisms perhaps, or maybe non-physical minds or souls? And if we are physical entities of some kind, how are we to account for consciousness in physical terms? (3) Free will. What is it? Could we have it if everything that happens is the ineluctable consequence of what went before it? Could we have it if the past leaves some 'slop' as to what will be, or would our actions just be a matter of chance? Although these four topics may seem quite different, they in fact are closely related in many respects, as we shall see.

title: Existentialism
description: Many of the philosophers we'll discuss under the label 'existentialism' either predated the term or rejected it outright. Nonetheless, we can think of existentialism as a trajectory of modern thought driven by a set of roughly overlapping concerns (e.g. human freedom and the basic threats to it that are presented by the "leveling down" of human life by diverse forces of homogenization, such as universal morality and rationality, mass society, and modern science; commitment; anxiety; the absurd; authenticity) expressing itself variously in philosophy, literature, and the arts. We will start the semester by exploring these concerns, centered mostly the around basic threats to human freedom, felt by the existentialists in order to get a feel for what they are responding to. We will then explore the philosophical responses to these threats offered by Jean-Paul Sartre, S脙赂ren Kierkegaard, Friedrich Nietzsche, Albert Camus, and Martin Heidegger (among others).

title: Political Problems
description: Should abortion be legal? What about capital punishment, or 芒聙聹enhanced interrogation?芒聙聺 Should the law restrict immigration? We will approach these questions in two ways. The first approach involves understanding each of these issues on their own terms芒聙聯芒聙聯getting clear, insofar as we can, about whether or not abortion, capital punishment, and the like are immoral. That murder and rape are abundantly wrong is part of the reason why they should be illegal; the moral status of the issues above is similarly relevant to their legal status. Readings in this part of the course will include selections from Judith Jarvis Thompson, Maggie Little, David Luban, Russ Shafer-Landau, Christopher Wellman, and others. But while morality is relevant to law, the fact that something is immoral isn芒聙聶t a sufficient reason to make it illegal. It would, in normal circumstances, be immoral for me to lie to my friends; yet a blanket law against lying is a bad idea. To get clearer about what the law芒聙聶s position on these moral issues should be, we芒聙聶ll study legal philosophy. We芒聙聶ll consider a variety of positions concerning what the law is and should be芒聙聯芒聙聯ranging from Natural Law theory to Legal Positivism. And we芒聙聶ll consider some different political ideologies, such as Liberalism and Conservatism. Readings will include selections from Mark Murphy, H.L.A. Hart, Ronald Dworkin, Joshua Cohen, and others. To know what the law芒聙聶s position on a moral issue should be, we need to understand two things: the relevant moral issue and the law. My hope is that in this course, we will develop a more nuanced understanding of both.

title: What Is an I?
description: The main topics of this course will be notions of the self and personal identity. What is the self - this thing we refer to when we say "I"? Is it a mind? Is it a body? Is it a soul? Is it a 'thing' at all? And what makes you the same person today as you were yesterday or will be tomorrow? Is it some psychological feature? Some bodily feature? Something beyond these? Some philosophers 芒聙聯 for example, John Locke & Derek Parfit 芒聙聯 have suggested that personal identity depends on psychological continuity. In Locke芒聙聶s formulation of it, the idea is that I am the same person to the extent that I am conscious of my past and future thoughts and actions in the same way as I am conscious of my present thoughts and actions. Others 芒聙聯 for example, Bernard Williams 芒聙聯 have argued that bodily identity is always necessary for personal identity. Both these views seem to be right in some sense, but also appear to be problematic. People can lose their memories or certain mental capacities, for example, yet we often treat them as largely the same person, only with altered mental capacities. Bodies can change, as they do in adolescence, through the process of aging, or in cases of amputation and transsexual transformations. Consider how these changes do or do not affect our conception of a person's sameness. Or consider a third position: Thomas Reid defends mind-body dualism (the belief that there are two kinds of substances 芒聙聯 mental and physical). For Reid, 芒聙聹I am not thought, or feeling; I am something that thinks, and acts, and suffers. My thoughts, and actions, and feelings, change every moment; 芒聙娄 but the self, or I, to which they belong, is permanent."[1] Thus, for Reid, that self that is permanent is some immaterial substance distinct from one芒聙聶s body. As the quote from Reid suggests, one芒聙聶s views about personal identity will be greatly informed by one芒聙聶s views on the nature of the person, or self; and vice versa. We will explore accounts of the self put forth by philosophers like Descartes (who argued that I am a thinking thing 芒聙聯 a mental substance), David Hume (who maintained that we have no evidence for thinking that the mind is a substance at all 芒聙聯 rather what I am is nothing more or less than a bundle of perceptions), and materialists like Ryle and the Churchlands (who think that what we are is a physical body whose parts are connected up in such a way that we get the sorts of phenomena we often ascribe to a mind). We will also explore the work of philosophers like Christine Korsgaard, Daniel Dennett, Richard Moran, and Charles Taylor, who suggest that what makes you the person you are are facts about what you value, the different groups you belong to, the roles you play in these different groups, and the attitudes that other people take towards you. We will consider what views such as these, which suggest that we have a role in constituting ourselves, imply about our responsibilities to ourselves and others. Course requirements include regular participation in class (which includes coming to class equipped with at least one question about the reading for that week), two papers, and two exams. [1] Hoy, Ronald C. and Oaklander, Nathan L., ed. Metaphysics : Classic and Contemporary Readings. Belmont: Wadsworth, 1991, 125.

title: Civic Engagement & Education (CBL)
description: This course combines reflection and action. In addition to the twice-weekly meetings on campus, students will be expected to invest significant additional time working with high school students at a low-performing public school located in Southeast D.C. The school serves a student population that is 100% African American and predominately from low-income families. GU students will travel to the high school two times a week to lead a group of students as they all work together to identify and address a community-based problem. Georgetown and the high school students will plan and carry out a public event at which they will share the work they have done during the semester. Students should expect to commit roughly 6-8 hours per week traveling to and from the school and working with the high school students. The GU and high school students will also plan and carry out a public event at which they will share their work with the community. In addition to the work at the high school and the conference, the course will include several other out-of-the classroom responsibilities such as hosting a visit by the high school students to the GU campus and planning with GU classmates for the meetings with the high school students. These community-based experiences will be supplemented by readings, class discussions, and blog writing assignments. We will study the role of schooling in preparing and enabling (or failing to prepare and enable) youth to have a voice in their schools and communities. We will focus specifically on the relationships between democracy and schooling; the kinds of knowledge, skills, and character traits needed to be effective in working toward social change through community action; the conditions that must be provided if students are to become active participants in democratic life; and the role of the hidden curriculum in denying students from particular demographic backgrounds access to the kind of learning experiences that enable effective civic and academic engagement. The focus will be primarily on schools and students in Washington, D.C. public schools. All GU students enrolled in the course are expected to take a very active role in the conduct of the course itself and in the work within the high school. However, this course does not require any previous experience working with youth or in schools. While the course is very demanding in several senses, students who enroll report that it is also an incredibly valuable learning experience unlike anything they have experienced through their other, campus-based coursework.

title: Philosophy and Star Trek
description: Star Trek is very philosophical. What better way, then, to do philosophy, but to watch Star Trek, read philosophy and hash it all out in class (and on Blackboard)? That芒聙聶s the plan. This course will center on topics in metaphysics that come up again and again in Star Trek. In conjunction with watching Star Trek, we will read excerpts from the writings of great philosophers, extract key concepts and arguments and then analyze those arguments. Questions we will wrestle with include: I. Is time travel possible? Could you go back and kill your grandmother? What is time? II. What is the relation between your mind and your brain--are they separate items or identical? Can persons survive death? Could a machine someday think? Is Data a person? III. What is a person? Must you have the same body to be you? Same memories? When do we have one person, and when do we have two (think of the episodes where people "split" or "fuse")? IV. Do you have free will, or are you determined by the laws of nature to do exactly what you wind up doing (while believing you have free will)? Or both? What is freewill? Text: Metaphysics: Classic and Contemporary Readings, 2nd ed., Ed. by Hoy & Oaklander (Wadsworth, 2005). Four short papers.

title: Phenomenology
description: Phenomenology is a scary word for an unusually influential philosophical method. This course surveys the major attempts to do phenomenology, and thereby provides an introduction several of the 20th century芒聙聶s most important philosophical movements. More specifically, we芒聙聶ll study the major applications of phenomenology, and consider whether they deliver the intellectual goods. We芒聙聶ll read selections from Brentano, Husserl, Heidegger, Gadamer, Arendt, Stein, Sartre, de Beauvoir, Merleau-Ponty, Derrida, and Derrida. Assessment in this course will include: participation, a presentation, and two (1,000 word) papers. Books required: Moran, Dermot. Introduction to Phenomenology. New York: Routledge, 1999. Moran, Dermot, and Timothy Mooney, eds. The Phenomenology Reader. London: Routledge, 2002.

title: Philosophy & The Wire
description: The idea of responsibility pervades social life. Being responsible, holding responsible, taking responsibility 芒聙聯 in addition to being parental buzzwords, these notions are a central part of the ordinary fabric of living as an agent among others. But the ordinary is just as often puzzling as not, and philosophy has a rich history of scratching beneath a surface where things make sense to find that there are questions whose answers we have merely been taking for granted. The focus of this class will be some of the many faces of responsibility staring up at us from beneath the surface. Our inquiries will be organized by two different perspectives on the concept of responsibility. First, we will investigate what is required for the possibility of responsibility. Challenges here arise in the form of the metaphysical problem of free will, the nature of agency and intentional action, coercive or corrosive influences over choosing and acting, and the problem of moral luck. Discussion of the possibility of responsibility will touch on key issues in metaphysics, philosophy of action, moral psychology, and experimental philosophy. Second, we芒聙聶ll look at different aspects of responsibility in practice, including punishment and forgiveness as ways of holding an agent responsible, loyalty as a way of being responsible to others, and collective responsibility as a way of being responsible for more than what one accomplishes exclusively under her own power. 芒聙聵The Wire芒聙聶 is a critically acclaimed HBO television series that ran from 2002-2008. It chronicles urban plight in contemporary Baltimore through the experience of a large and diverse cast of remarkable characters: drug dealers, drug addicts, police, teachers, politicians, longshoremen, journalists and lawyers, among others. Because it encompasses such a large range of intricately developed characters interacting in complex ways, the series is a realistic and compelling setting in which to launch philosophical inquiries into the idea of responsibility. This is an important distinction about the focus of the class and the way in which it will use the series. The class is not about 芒聙聵The Wire芒聙聶; instead, the class will use 芒聙聵The Wire芒聙聶 as an environment for philosophical work, a dramatic and shared vehicle for scratching beneath the surface of the everyday concept of responsibility to find questions we hadn芒聙聶t thought to ask and to begin to answer them. Interested students should be cautioned: the series is emphatically TV-MA; it is violent (often graphically) and vulgar (often intensely and explicitly). Between them, the cast of characters will combine to perform all manner of violent acts and say offensive things about just about every type of person, and the series itself may be found offensive for how it portrays certain groups or individuals. Critical reaction will always be welcomed, but since viewings will be a mandatory part of the class, strong disinclination to experience this sort of unpleasantness should rule out enrollment.

title: Philosophy of Mind
description: Even if we are brains in vats芒聙聯that is, even if we芒聙聶re in the Matrix--we all think we have minds. But what are minds? Are they physical or non-physical? If non-physical, how do they interact with the physical world? If minds are physical objects, what color are they and how much do they weigh? Or perhaps minds aren芒聙聶t things at all, and the real question is: what is it to be minded? Is it to have a soul? To behave in a certain way? To have a certain sort of brain? To run a certain sort of software? Where do our beliefs, hopes, and desires fit into all of this? Will they show up our in our best cognitive science? Philosophy of mind is in the business of asking and answering these sorts of questions; and in this course, we will survey some of the discipline芒聙聶s leading schools of thought (no pun intended). Along the way we芒聙聶ll consider approaches to the mind-body problem, intentionality, cognition, and artificial intelligence. Course Requirements: 脗路 Two papers: typed, double-spaced, about 5 pages. Topics will be suggested in class. The second paper may be rewritten once (within 2 weeks of its being graded). 脗路 A major oral presentation. 脗路 Participation: A significant portion of your grade will be determined by (a) attendance and (b) your engagement with the material. Textbook David Chalmers (ed): Philosophy of Mind: Classical and Contemporary Readings (2002, Oxford UP) Braddon-Mitchell and Jackson: Philosophy of Mind and Cognition: An Introduction (2007, Blackwell Publishing)

title: Causation & Causal Inference
description: Does smoking cause lung cancer? Instinctively, after decades of scientific research and public health campaigns, we are likely to answer in the affirmative. Yet not all smokers develop lung cancer and not all individuals with lung cancer smoke. So what do we mean when we say smoking 芒聙聹causes芒聙聺 lung cancer? What constitutes a causal relationship? What methods do we have for identifying such relationships? The answers to these questions will have important implications for our abilities to explain, predict, intervene on, and assign responsibility for events. In this course, we will use readings, class discussions, and online modules to explore and critically examine classical and contemporary accounts of causation and causal inference, with an emphasis on philosophy of science. We will then use these conceptual tools to analyze 芒聙聹applied" problems of causal inference, including scientific claims and policy interventions.

title: Hallucinating
description: We all make mistakes. We all sometimes remember things that didn't really happen and sometimes forget things that really did. We all get confused by optical illusions, and phantom pains and itches. We all sometimes believe false things other people tell us. We all sometimes confuse our dreams for reality. We all sometimes believe things because we really want them to be, even though we have good reasons for thinking they aren't. And sometimes we're just plain hallucinating. How can we be sure that we're not mistaken about everything? What kinds of thing can we know for sure? What is knowledge anyway? We'll look at some old, not so old, and even less old answers to and ways of thinking about these questions. Some of these will come from philosophers. But some will come as well from science, science fiction, literature, film, art, etc. The views we come across will be very different, and we'll spend a lot of time trying to sort out our own reactions to these questions. We'll all probably end up with different answers ourselves---but at least they'll be our own answers, and we'll have good reasons for them. (That's the philosophy part.) Here are just a few of the things we'll be looking at: 芒聙垄 The films The Matrix and Existenz * Kafka's ``The Metamorphosis"; Thompson's Fear and Loathing in Los Vegas; Burroughs' Naked Lunch; Ellison's The Invisible Man; etc. * Hume's A Treatise of Human Nature (parts) * Recent research in cognitive science and psychology (of individuals and groups) * Some selections from Stanislaw Lem (the good stuff) * The Star Trek pilot episode, ``The Cage" * Some more philosophy stuff * Parts of Don Quixote * Religion and other mass delusions.

title: Substance Abuse: Monism/Dualsm
description: Is the mind a different kind of thing than the body? When you mold clay into a statue, does something new come into existence? Is a living organism anything more than the atoms and quarks that make it up? Taking a stand on these issues is taking a stand on how many kinds of things there are. For example, if you think that there is only one sort of thing, then you might think that there are only thoughts and no bodies. This is just one of many ways of being a monist. If you think that there are two sorts of things芒聙聯say, that the animal is different from the atoms and molecules that make it up芒聙聯then you are a type of dualist. In this course, we will critically assess our presuppositions about what there is by looking at different philosophers芒聙聶 answers to the question of what 芒聙聵substance芒聙聶 is and how many there are. We will read Spinoza (a monist), Descartes (a dualist) and Aristotle, who thought that there were at least as many kinds of thing as there are kinds of animal! We will also consider challenges to these views from Parmenides, Leibniz and Locke. We will think about whether the reasons that each gives for their views are good reasons. Students will write two short papers, each examining the view of a different author, and a final, longer paper. Students will be equipped to offer a position on what there is and arguments in favor of and against that position.

title: Ethical Theory
description: This course will be a systematic, intensive exploration of philosophical ethics. It aims to provide philosophy majors and minors with a broad overview of ethical theory, but in a way that enables a deep level of engagement with its central concerns and considerations. Students will gain or improve their grasp of the most influential versions of normative ethical theory, including Aristotelian and Humean virtue ethics, Stoic ethics, Kantianism, utilitarianism, and social contract theory. We will consider the accounts of moral psychology presupposed by those theories and how well they cohere with contemporary research. We will also consider important questions in metaethics such as the existence of moral properties, the status of moral claims, and the nature of reasons. Finally, we will examine the resources of ethical theory for resolving practical ethical problems. The class will be conducted in a seminar fashion. Students will be responsible for presenting the material and leading discussions about it. Students will also be required to write two short (2-3 page) papers and one longer (10-12 page) paper with a mandatory first draft and revision. There will be a take-home, essay-based final exam. The primary textbook for the course will be: Cahn and Markie, Ethics: History, Theory, and Contemporary Issues 4th ed. (Oxford, 2009) Students will also be asked to purchase a copy of Kant芒聙聶s Doctrine of Virtue, if they do not already own it.

title: Dante and the Christian Imagination
description: This course will examine the question of the meaning of human freedom on the basis of careful reading of major portions of Dante Alighieri's La Divina Commedia. The study will be an integrated approach that draws together elements from the disciplines of literature, psychology (especially the theories of Jung), philosophy, and theology. Based on an analysis of the text and discussion of selected critical literature, students will be asked to reflect on Dante's interpretation of the nature of freedom, how it functions in the formation of personal identity, and the role of imagination in the formation of culture and worldviews. In this context we will discuss the specifically Christian and medieval character of Dante's imagination as well as the problem of finding appropriate metaphors to situate these issues in the transformed historical context of contemporary life. The basic assumption of the course is that Commedia, while framed in terms of the fourteenth-century medieval culture, can speak vividly and directly to modern readers in terms of human experiences which are universal and fundamental, regardless of differences in time and place. Fall.

title: Tutorial:  Philosophy
description: Permission Required.

title: Honors Thesis Res Tutorial
description: Permission required.

title: Text Seminar: Plato芒聙聶s Phaedrus
description: Plato芒聙聶s Phaedrus is a literary and philosophical masterpiece. Ostensibly a set of speeches about love, the dialogue both explores and performs the question of how talk can make a difference in a life. Topics include rhetoric, philosophy, myth, writing, the structure of the soul, self-knowledge, madness and inspiration. Because the Phaedrus shows more than says its claims, the question of how to read the text appropriately will be constantly raised and reformulated as we read. Accordingly, we shall have to read the text more than once. We begin by getting a sense for Plato芒聙聶s philosophy and the character of Socrates, reading both the Apology and Phaedo. After reading the Phaedrus through quickly and familiarizing ourselves with its main ideas and argumentative and rhetorical trajectory, we will read it again. We will work through the text slowly and carefully, and pay particular attention to how the dialogue performs its argument. In the final part of the course, we will discuss relevant secondary literature while preparing final research papers. Course requirements will likely include: a presentation, an annotated bibliography, a final paper proposal, and a final research paper. Texts: Plato, Phaedrus, tr. Alexander Nehamas and Paul Woodruff, Hackett. 0-87220-220-8 Plato, Phaedrus, ed. C.J. Rowe, Aris and Philips, 1986. 9780856683145 Plato, Five Dialogues, tr. G.M.A. Grube, revised John M. Cooper. Second Edition. Hackett. 0-87220-6

title: Text Seminar
description: TBA

title: Aquinas, Scotus, Ockham
description: St. Thomas Aquinas, John Duns Scotus and William of Ockham are generally acknowledged as the three greatest philosophers of the high middle ages (roughly ca. 1250-1350), a time of great intellectual activity that saw, among other things, the development of European universities and the influx of Greek and Islamic thought. But they were not just great medieval philosophers; they were great philosophers period, and they exercised a lasting impact on Western thought. They shared a common Christian outlook and were concerned with its elaboration and investigation, but, contrary to false stereotypes about medieval thinkers, differed sharply and in profound ways on most philosophical issues they tackled. The aim of this course is to provide an introduction to the thought of these thinkers. We shall be focusing on the following four areas: (1) The existence and nature of God, and how we can talk meaningfully about God; (2) Contributions to metaphysics, with a focus on the notion of being and views about the fundamental constituents that comprise reality; (3) What the mind is and how it works; (4) What the will is, how it is free, and what is involved in acting morally or immorally. This course will not presuppose a prior knowledge of ancient or medieval philosophy, though you should have taken at least a couple of philosophy courses.

title: Knowledge and the Common Good
description: Technoscientific knowledge thoroughly penetrates today芒聙聶s society, informing our understanding of the world and structuring our behavior in both individual and public domains. Not surprisingly, some sciences have been involved in public controversy (e.g., biotechnology, environmental sciences). Consequently, educated citizenship increasingly presupposes an ability to reflect on the sciences and their relationships with society. This course will focus on questions raised by the idea of scientific knowledge as a social good: What is scientific knowledge? What makes it good? When does it count as a common good? These and similar questions refer to both epistemology and social-political theory. The course will move from general analyses of knowledge, goodness, and scientific practice to concrete case studies that illustrate course themes. The course should be of interest not only to philosophy majors, but also to science majors and anyone interested in the role of science in today芒聙聶s world. Course material does not presuppose any technical scientific background. Course methods will mix lecture, discussion, and perhaps some group work.

title: Symbolic Logic
description: A study of a formal system representing rules of good reasoning. The concepts of consequence, validity, derivability, and truth under an interpretation will be examined. This course is also part of the interdisciplinary curriculum in Cognitive Science. This course satisfies the major's requirement in logic. Fall.

title: History of Ancient/Medieval Philosophy
description: The course will be an in-depth survey of ancient and medieval philosophy, beginning with the pre-Socratic philosophers and ending with figures of the fourteenth century. The first part of the course deals with ancient Greek philosophy (taught by Asst. Prof. Kate Withy). We will trace the ways in which the metaphysical questions of the pre-Socratic philosophers become entangled with ethical questions in post-Socratic philosophy (Plato, Aristotle). Topics include: reality, God, causation, the soul, knowledge, the good life, and philosophical practice and method. The second half of the course will be taught by Professor Mark Henninger. In this medieval part of the course we will trace how metaphysical questions influenced the medievals芒聙聶 thinking on ethical questions. After beginning by examining the relation of philosophy and theology, we will look at topics including: how language 芒聙聵hooks up with芒聙聶 the world (the problem of universals), the metaphysical basis of ethical theory in a world of stable Aristotelian natures, the relation of ontological and moral goodness, and finally we will look at the medieval formulation of issues dealing with free will. Texts: Aristotle, The Basic Works of Aristotle, ed. Richard McKeon. The Modern Library, 2001. ISBN: 0375757996. List price: $21.95 Plato, Five Dialogues, Second Edition, tr. G.M.A. Grube, rev. John M. Cooper. Hackett, 2002. ISBN: 0872206335. List price: $7.95 Medieval Philosophy: Essential Readings with Commentary, ed. by Gyula Klima with F. Allhof and A. Jayprakash Vaidya. Blackwell Publishing, 2007. ISBN-13: 978-1-4051-3565-8, paperback. List price: $43.95

title: History of Modern Philosophy
description: This course is a survey of modern philosophy from the 17th through the end of the 18th centuries. We will focus on the development of modern theory of knowledge and normative philosophy. In the course of this survey we will read texts from some of the most influential philosophers of the period, including Descartes, Hobbes, Leibniz, Locke, Hume, Rousseau, and Kant. We shall approach the development of modern philosophy with an emphasis on the theme of enlightenment, as Kant described it: "Enlightenment is man's release from his self-incurred tutelage. Tutelage is man's inability to make use of his understanding without direction from another. Self-incurred is this tutelage when its cause lies not in lack of reason but in lack of resolution and courage to use it without direction from another. Sapere aude! "Have courage to use your own reason!" 芒聙聰 that is the motto of enlightenment. (Kant, What is Enlightenment?, 1784)" It is characteristic of modern philosophy to understand the subject, agent, or deliberator as the ultimate source of authority for knowledge claims, moral principles, and political institutions. We will pay close attention to the arguments for and implications of this idea.

title: Social and Political Philosophy
description: TBA.

title: Hegel/Psychoanalsyst on Subjectivity
description: In this seminar we will question the notion of rational ego by introducing the idea of desire as part of the human person. We will introduce the distinction between ego and subject and then further argue that individuals develop and/or take up particular subjective positions. Finally, we will argue that subjective positions allow the individual to develop certain capabilities but not some others. Such a view of the human person poses several philosophical challenges. Instead of accepting one ideal for human beings, i.e., that of the rational individual, we must accept that there are many forms of organizing one芒聙聶s life. In order to develop these ideas we will rely on Hegel in the philosophical tradition and upon Klein, Winnicott, Ogden, Bion, Lacan and Freud in the psychoanalytic tradition. We will stress the fact that both traditions present the notion that there is a hierarchical relation between the different subjective positions. We will also stress that such an assumed hierarchical relation is implicitly unacceptable for most subjective positions. Hence, we will show that the new capabilities of other subjective positions cannot be seen, that when they are seen they will be denied, and that when they cannot anymore be denied that the individual needs to change its position. It therefore follows that psychotherapy will face great challenges. Indeed, the therapist cannot assume that the mentally ill person can see where they have to go. Furthermore, the therapist must accept that the mentally ill person cannot accept the advice given to them. The art of therapy is to let the persons themselves talk and present the world of new abilities in a way that the patient can accept, understand and do something with it. First, in Hegel we will analyze the radical difference between the four positions of self-consciousness (in the Phenomenology): the master/slave; the stoic; the sceptic; and the unhappy consciousness. We will also analyze the radical difference between the four moral positions described in his Philosophy of Right and the two moral positions analyzed in the Phenomenology. Finally, we will analyze the logical/historical development of one subjective position (The law of the Heart) and see how Hegel argues that the Romantic reformer misunderstands the function of the law and the otherness of other people and because of it becomes tyrannical and paranoid. This analysis will thus demonstrate that there is dynamic logic to a subjective position. Second, we will make use of the psychoanalytic tradition. The phenomenon of denial makes it clear that there is a distinction between conscious and unconscious and between the ego and the subject (Freud and Lacan). Melanie Klein introduced the idea that children develop two quite different ways to relate to themselves, other people and perceived dangers: the schizoid-paranoid position and the depressive position. Winnicott and Ogden elaborated the ideas of Klein. Thus Ogden points out clearly that concern and care for the other is impossible in the schizoid-paranoid position but is of paramount importance in the depressive position. The reason is that the schizoid-paranoid position includes the idea of omnipotence. Thus one is absolved of caring and of being concerned for the other. Winnicott, but also Ogden, makes it clear that the (m)other can hinder or facilitate the transition of one position to the other. Ogden even adds a third position: the autistic-contiguous position. Finally, we will introduce the student to several psychoanalytic positions well known in psychoanalysis. First, we will discuss the difference between neurotic, pervert and psychotic structure. Next, we will study the difference between obsessive-compulsive, paranoid, hysterical and impulsive style. Where Hegel teaches us the great gaps between different 芒聙聹subjective positions,芒聙聺 there the psychoanalytic tradition teaches us the profound dependence upon others in order to move from an inferior to a better subjective position. It is clear that there are important epistemological, ethical, anthropological (psychological), and metaphysical implications of the notion of 芒聙聹subjective position.芒聙聺 We will consider some of them. Bibliography Hegel. Phenomenology of Spirit. Oxford UP Lacan. Ecrits. Ogden. The Matrix of the Mind. Aronson. Shapiro. Neurotic stylesFurthermore, articles will be made available through the library and the WEB The class will be conducted as a seminar. There will be three take home exams and a research paper.

title: Philosophy of Language
description: Course Goals & Description A study of the nature of meaning and reference from a philosophical perspective, focusing on the relationships among meanings, referents, properties, idea, conventions, intentions, the uses of language and speech acts. Topics in reference, or how words refer (what Helen Keller finally realized) include names and descriptions, the distinction between sense and reference, the puzzle of non referring terms, causal theories of reference. Topics in meaning include: the identification of meaning with truth conditions, the nature of propositions, theories of linguistic understanding, and the roles of mind and world in determining meaning, the distinction between meaning and implication, the roles of context and convention in language use, speaker meaning versus linguistic meaning and speech act theory. Texts & Readings Text: A.P. Martinich: The Philosophy of Language, 4th ed. Assignments & Expectations of Students Requirements: one oral presentation on an article, and two 7 page papers.

title: Perspectives on Common Goods
description: Course Description: Conceptions of the common good and its cousins, such as the public interest and general will, boast a long pedigree in Western thought. Although analyses of the common good typically are confined to political theory, recent work in social philosophy has opened new avenues of reflection that promise to deepen the understanding of that concept and broaden its applicability. In this course we examine this work with a view to its relevance for understanding the structure of social-political practices and the challenges they face. This task requires us to address both theoretical and practical questions, specifically: 芒聙垄 the metaphysical question, what is a common good? 芒聙垄 the social-theoretic question, what sorts of interdependence and need ground effective common goods? 芒聙垄 the epistemological question, how can we identify genuine common goods? 芒聙垄 the justice question: how do we divide responsibility for the common good? 芒聙垄 the practical question: how does the idea of a common good help us address contemporary challenges (e.g., environmental, economic, political, global)? Course Texts Jacques Maritain, The Person and the Common Good Alasdair MacIntyre, Dependent Rational Animals Barry Bozeman, Public Values and Public Interest Charles Taylor, 芒聙聹Irreducibly Social Goods芒聙聺 Selected articles by Jane Mansbridge, J脙录rgen Habermas, Margaret Gilbert, Claus Offe, possibly Philip Pettit Course Format: This course will involve a mix of lecture and discussion, with the aim of helping students (a) to develop a better grasp of the issues, both conceptual and practical, connected with ideas of the common good, and (b) to formulate their own views on the basis of cogent argument. The course should be of interest especially to philosophy majors and minors, as well as graduate students. The assignments will include short papers (to help students engage readings), possibly a presentation (to prepare for the final), and a final paper (on a specific topic or case of interest).

title: Philosophy of Medicine
description: This course will examine medicine and medical practice as a subject for philosophical analysis. It begins with a summary of the historical relationships of the two disciplines from the ancient to the modern eras. It then moves to a critical analysis of medicine as a human activity and its ends, purposes, and ethical consequences. Fundamental concepts to be examined include: health, disease, normality, healing, clinical decision-making, patient-physician relationship. We will also examine sociocultural determinants of health as well as potential policy implications of our analysis. Course texts: Edmund D. Pellegrino, The Philosophy of Medicine Reborn: A Pellegrino Reader (ND Studies in Medical Ethics) (Notre Dame University Press, 2008). Selections from James A. Marcum, An Introductory Philosophy of Medicine: Humanizing Modern Medicine (Springer, 2008) to be placed on reserve or posted on Blackboard. Contemporary journal articles to be determined and placed on reserve or posted on Blackboard.

title: Bioethics and Public Policy
description: This seminar will focus on the intersection of bioethics, politics, and public policy. The course will be divided into four or five sections: (1) an investigation of the problems of technology that give rise to bioethical concerns; (2) an discussion of the actual and ideal relationships between ethics and politics; (3) an examination of the possibility of ethical expertise and what such expertise might consist in; (4) an examination of the political economy in the developed world and the ways in which it affects our conceptions of bioethics; and, if time and the class芒聙聶s interest permit, (5) an examination and comparison of national and international bioethics commissions, their purported missions, and a sample of some of their findings. There is some flexibility built into the course, and we will determine as a group whether we are able to address the first four sections adequately in the time allotted and whether to continue the theoretical discussion or to examine commissions and their reports.

title: Philosophy of Mind/Cog Sci
description: One of the reasons cognitive science is such a land of plenty for philosophers is that so many of its questions芒聙聰not just the grand bird芒聙聶s-eye view questions but quite proximal, in-the-lab-now questions芒聙聰are still ill thought out, prematurely precipitated into forms that deserve critical reevaluation. If philosophy is, as my bumper sticker slogan has it, what you芒聙聶re doing until you figure out just what questions to ask, then there is a lot of philosophy to be done by cognitive scientists these days." In this course we will attempt to examine some of the core issues in the philosophy of cognitive science that deserve this sort of critical revaluation. The main focus of the course will be on the issue of memory. What is it, and how do we study it. Over the course of the semester we will look at a number of broad issues about the methodology that ought to be adopted in studying the mind. We will ask whether the mind is best understood in computational terms; we will ask whether the mind is a system of dedicated modules; and we will ask whether the mind extends beyond the boundaries of skin-and-skull. Over the course of the semester you will write two short papers, and you will be expected to in class discussion. No previous knowledge of cognitive science is required; however, some previous background in the psychology, philosophy of science, philosophy of language, or metaphysics is likely to be helpful.

title: Ethics of Death and Dying
description: The ethical problems of the care of the terminally ill have been among the most controversial issues in medical ethics over the past decade: the definition of death, intentional killing or mercy, forgoing life-support, living wills, surrogate decision-making, ethics committees, neonatal death, and demands for futile care. This course will explore these issues combining lecture, discussion, and film. The course will not emphasize the sociological and psychological aspects of death and dying, concentrating instead on philosophical, ethical, and public policy issues. There will be a mid-term paper and a final exam. The final exam will be a take-home final distributed the last day of class and due on the scheduled examination date.

title: Aristotle's Metaphysics
description: The aim of this seminar is critically to examine Aristotle芒聙聶s views on ontology and theology. We shall engage in a close reading of selected passages from his work in the following order: (1) The Aristotelian meta-theory of metaphysics. Readings: Selections from Metaphysics I, III, IV and VI. Special emphasis: I. 1 - 2; IV. 1; V. 7; IV. 2; VI. 1. (2) Aristotle芒聙聶s general ontology. Readings: Categories 5; Met. VII, VIII, IX. Special emphasis: VII. 1, 2, 3, 7, 8, 13, 17; VIII. 2; IX. 8. (3) The ontology of living beings, especially of human beings. The aim here will be to understand Aristotle芒聙聶s conception of the soul, the outlines of his theory of perception, and his views on thought and desire. Readings: On the Soul selections from II and III On the Movements of Animals 6 - 11. Special emphasis: On the Soul II. 1; III. 4 - 11. (4) Aristotle芒聙聶s theology. We skip the astronomy and move on to the key passages on God as thought of thought and an object of love. Readings: Met. XII. Special emphasis: XII. 7 and 9. (5) A renewed, compact version of Aristotelian metaphysics. Readings: Aquinas, On Being and Essence (De Ente et Essentia) Bibliography: J. L. Ackrill, A New Aristotle Reader, Princeton. Jonathan Barnes, The Complete Works of Aristotle, 2 vols. Princeton. Aquinas, Selected Philosophical Writings, Oxford. Ross, Aristotle. Can be used as a handy running commentary. For literature on Aristotle see the Topic References in Ackrill and the extensive bibliography in Barnes, The Cambridge Companion to Aristotle , 295 - 384.

title: Gender & Philosophy
description: In this course, we will analyze conceptions of gender, sex, and identity by focusing on the paradoxical efforts societies make in creating and enforcing conditions that are supposed by those societies to be 芒聙聹natural.芒聙聺 We will frame the issue by looking at Michel Foucault芒聙聶s landmark Discipline and Punish, which highlights the extent to which we are the product of disciplinary institutions as well the way those institutions attempt to pass themselves off as natural and inevitable rather than as the historical accidents that they are. We will then make a first go of analyzing the gendering project by examining the ways in which both men and women are the product of robustly normative systems. Subsequently, we芒聙聶ll examine the specific role that conceptions of sexuality play in the gendering process, highlighting the privilege that heterosexual identity brings with it. We will further consider how the lives of trans and intersex persons complicate theorizing about gender and sexuality. In the penultimate section of the class, we will examine suggestions regarding what constitutes the proper moral and political responses to deeply ingrained practices based upon sexism and compulsory heterosexuality; we will first take up suggestions at countering these problems in general, and then we will develop strategies for addressing them in the profession (i.e., academic philosophy). The final session of the course will address teaching issues of gender and sexuality at the undergraduate level; we will look at incorporating these issues/readings into non-specialized courses (e.g., intro to phil or intro to ethics), approaching these issues in specialized courses, and developing assignments relevant to these topics. For more information, please see: //bit.ly/gEEbqi
