Designing development policy today requires a careful understanding of socio-political and economic contexts. The aim of this course is to discuss a few contexts in some detail that will inform a term project describing the economic histories of a country of the student's choice.

title: INTERNATIONAL ECONOMICS
description: This course is divided into two parts. The first deals with the essential issues of globalization: Why nations trade with each other, the importance of trade, the factors that determine trade and production, the gains that accrue from trade, the effect of trade on the distribution of national income, the formation of regional trade blocs, and the nature and costs of government policies that impede trade. The second part deals with issues of international finance: the balance of payments, the determination of exchange rates, alternative exchange rate regimes and their implications for domestic monetary and fiscal policies, the macroeconomic effects of international capital movements, and dollarization.

title: HOMELAND SECURITY
description: This course is intended to provide a broad and deep understanding of homeland security policy. The September 11th terrorist attacks put homeland security at the very top of the national public policy agenda. Securing the homeland is an extremely complex mission that requires a coordinated and integrated effort from all levels of society: the federal government, state and local governments, the private and nonprofit sectors, and the American people. The concept of homeland security focuses on protecting the nation from terrorist attacks but it also encompasses planning and preparing for natural disasters. The goal is to provide students a thorough understanding of the policy, strategy, legal and organizational issues and challenges associated with the defense of the U.S. homeland, the efforts underway to meet those challenges, and the range of means and methods that are or could be brought to bear on this subject.

title: GENDER & DEVELOPMENT
description: Gender discrimination remains pervasive worldwide despite considerable advances in gender equality in recent decades. In no region of the developing world are women equal to men in legal, social, and economic rights. Gender gaps remain widespread in access to and control of resources, in economic opportunities, and in power and political voice. Women and girls bear the largest and most direct costs of these inequalities -- but the costs cut more broadly across society, ultimately harming everyone. This course examines the links between gender inequality, development, and public policy. It provides an overview on key gender issues in development policy and explores in detail several cutting-edge debates in the field, including on: the gender dimensions of poverty, the gender impacts of globalization, labor market policy, and the role and impacts of women in politics and policymaking

title: POST-CONFLICT RECONSTRUCTION
description: The purpose of this course is to provide students with an introduction to the components and challenges of post-conflict reconstruction. Students will gain familiarity with planning, the Essential Tasks Matrix, the interagency and multinational process, civil-military relations in PCR, and project monitoring and evaluation. Topics include the benefits of sequenced vs simultaneous approaches; phases of reconstruction; and operating in a complex, unstable, unsustainable post-conflict environment. Case studies will serve to highlight 芒聙聵lessons learned芒聙聶 from previous and on-going reconstruction efforts.

title: CYBER CONFLICT & NATIONAL SECURITY POLICY
description: This class examines the concepts and reality of cyber statecraft, international conflict, competition and cooperation in cyberspace and discusses the resulting challenges faced by the national security community. We will focus not on the technology of cyberspace but the national security threats, challenges, and policy responses including lessons from history and other kinds of conflict. The class is primarily concerned with national security policy, but will look at domestic issues such as balancing security and privacy, regulation and innovation.

title: CLIENTS IN THE POLICY ANALYSIS PROCESS
description: This module course is designed for MPP students in their last semester and students who receive the consent of the instructor. It focuses on the role of clients in the policy analysis process. One core aspect of policy analysis involves the relationship between the analyst and the individual he or she is advising 芒聙聯 the client. This module includes examination of the literature on clients in policy analysis as well as exposure to the range of clients and their relationship to analysts. Students will be asked to examine their own analytic work and to think about the clients who would be involved in that topic.

title: U.S. NATIONAL SECURITY: POLICY AND ISSUES
description: U.S. national security includes both international security and homeland security, and the boundaries between these are necessarily imprecise. Homeland security includes the Departments of Homeland Security, Defense, Health and Human Services, other federal agencies, as well as thousands of state and local entities across the country. International security includes the roles and missions of the Departments of Defense, State, the Agency for International Development, and parts of a dozen other agencies. Some organizations, like the Intelligence Community and the National Guard, serve both communities. Some issues, like cybersecurity and counter-intelligence, cut across both arenas. This course focuses on the processes for integrating policies and resources across the federal government (the Interagency Process) and how to coordinate across federal, state, and local government levels (Intergovernmental Relations). It also includes the roles of Congress and the interactions between the Executive and Legislative branches.

title: THESIS WORKSHOP
description: The thesis is a two-semester project required of all public policy students for graduation. For the thesis, students conduct original research on a government or public program or issue of their choosing, and summarize their findings in a thesis to be completed during the second semester. GPPI faculty have developed The Thesis Guide on the GPPI website as a reference and aid for students who are completing their thesis projects (//gppi.georgetown.edu/audience/current/thesis/40182.html).

title: THESIS WORKSHOP II
description: The thesis is a two-semester project required of all public policy students for graduation. For the thesis, students conduct original research on a government or public program or issue of their choosing, and summarize their findings in a thesis to be completed during the second semester. GPPI faculty have developed The Thesis Guide on the Web as a reference and aid for students who are completing their thesis projects.

title: AFRICAN ECONOMIC DEVELOPMENT
description: PPOL 702: AFRICAN ECONOMIC DEVELOPMENT. The main objectives of this module are to familiarize students with the most important economic development issues confronting and challenging African countries today and to demonstrate how economic analysis may be used to better understand and address these development issues. The module begins with an analysis of Africa芒聙聶s post-independence development experience, often characterized as a growth tragedy, and the factors, both exogenous and policy-induced, that help to explain that performance. The module then turns to current African development issues, including: the transition to more market-friendly policies and the effectiveness of structural adjustment programs; Africa芒聙聶s unfinished reform agenda, particularly downsizing and changing the role and functions of the state in the economy, including the establishment of new regulatory systems and the divestiture and privatization of state-owned enterprises; macroeconomic stabilization, including the role of Africa芒聙聶s central banks and exchange rate regimes; the persistence of poverty and income inequality in African countries; the capacity and credibility of public sector institutions and the difficulties of governance and economic policy making in ethnically divided and war-torn African states; Africa芒聙聶s marginalization in world trade and limited access to private capital markets; Africa芒聙聶s investment in human capital and the fight against HIV/Aids; the constraints imposed by Africa芒聙聶s limited physical infrastructure; how to deepen Africa芒聙聶s financial sector; the agricultural sector芒聙聶s role in supporting broad-based economic growth; the effectiveness of official development aid (ODA); the burden of Africa芒聙聶s external debt and the highly indebted poor country (HIPC) initiative; and finally the scope for regional economic cooperation among African countries. (1.5 credits, 1st module).

title: POLITICAL ECONOMY OF FOREIGN AID
description: This module will examine how the foreign aid business works. We will look at mechanisms typically used by USAID and other bilateral aid agencies to deliver aid. We will also look at new forms of assistance such as the Gates Foundation. The economic impact of aid will be considered in depth, including food aid and relief aid. The potential impact of the proposed Millennium Challenge Account will also be analyzed. Finally, we will look at whether the aid business might be reformed to work more effectively.

title: THE POLICIES & POLITICS OF THE EUROPEAN UNION
description: PPOL 705: THE POLICIES & POLITICS OF THE EUROPEAN UNION. This course introduces students to the evolution, decision making structures and key policies of the European Union. Themes include, but are not limited to the EU芒聙聶s democratic deficit, the new institutional framework under the Lisbon Treaty, tensions between the small and big member states, the single market, the EU as a global actor and its relations with the United States. It will explore topical questions, including: Who leads in the EU? Should the EU enlarge further? Why does the EU芒聙聶s economic potential not translate into greater political weight? Are we seeing a revitalization or decline of the transatlantic relationship? The classes consist of a mixture of lectures, debates and panel discussions. (1.5 credits, 2nd module).

title: LATIN AMERICAN DEVELOPMENT
description: The main objectives of this course are to familiarize students with the most important economic development issues confronting and challenging Latin American countries today and to demonstrate how economic analysis may be used to better understand and address these development issues. The module is essentially divided into two parts. The first part deals with cross-cutting issues that have challenged many if not most of the countries in the region. These issues include the sustainability of recent macroeconomic management and stabilization improvements, including the capacity of related fiscal and monetary institutions; progress with respect to structural reforms aimed at making markets at all levels work more freely and efficiently; the persistence of extreme poverty and high income inequality, their underlying causes, how this constrains growth and development and what countries are or should be doing about it; and finally, whether the countries of the region can muster what it takes to escape from the so-called 芒聙聹middle income trap.芒聙聺 The second part of the course accounts for the region芒聙聶s considerable diversity. It does this by taking a closer sub-regional look at some of the unique development challenges facing (i) the poorest countries in South America (Bolivia and Paraguay), (ii) the most affluent countries in the region (Chile and Argentina), (iii) the emerging resource-abundant powerhouse Brazil, and (iv) the highly export-dependent countries of Central America.

title: IMPLEMENTATION OF SOCIAL POLICY
description: This course focuses on the implementation of social policy. Thus, the course will examine how social policies are delivered to citizens through a sometimes complex array of public, nonprofit and for-profit institutions. Further, it will discuss the increasingly diverse set of policy tools used by policymakers to address urgent social problems including: tax credits and deductions, vouchers, and contracting with nonprofit and for-profit organizations. As such, the course will provide students will a deeper understanding of the key institutions in social policy implementation and specific skills and knowledge of successful strategies to manage the implementation process to achieve important policy and management goals. Also, social policy implementation increasingly involves networking and collaborative governance. Consequently, students will learn important frameworks and tools to develop and sustain key networks in support of specific policy objectives. The course will also address the role of front-line workers and clients in the implementation process. The class will employ multiple learning formats including case discussion, selected reading from the policy implementation literature, and guest speakers from the local social policy community. (1.5 credits, 1st module).

title: PERFORMANCE MANAGEMENT
description: PPOL 714: PERFORMANCE MANAGEMENT. This module introduces students to the concepts, practices, and challenges of public sector organizational performance management. Students will learn about the evolution and current state-of-the-art of performance management as means of measuring and managing policy/program performance. The module includes material on strategic planning, the development and use of performance measures, reporting on performance, and efforts to link performance and budgets. Students will examine and analyze organizational performance management in at least one federal department or major program. By the end of the module, students should be able to assess an organization芒聙聶s performance management system and develop a plan for improving its policy/program performance. (1.5 credits, 1st module).

title: GLOBALIZATION & WORKER RIGHTS
description: PPOL 718: GLOBAL WORKER RIGHTS. This module is designed to provide students with an understanding of international labor standards, specifically child labor, forced labor, employment discrimination and freedom of association and collective bargaining. For example, wanting ILS in trade agreements is a political issue, while wanting developing countries to implement them is a development issue. With increased international economic interdependence over the past half century, conditions in the workplace and treatment of workers have become more global. Globalization has become more contentious and raised a number of economic issues to the forefront for policymakers to consider. For example, anti-globalization and other demonstrations at high-level international meetings are becoming more commonplace. Expanded open trade has increased the size of the world economic pie; however, how this pie is being sliced up remains an issue. We will examine the role of the international labor standards in this controversy. (1.5 credits; 1st module)

title: SOCIAL ENTERPRISE
description: PPOL 719: SOCIAL ENTERPRISE. (1.5 credits, 2nd module). Course Description TBD.

title: US IMMIGRATION POLICY
description: While the architecture of US immigration policy has much to be admired, there are long-standing problems and new challenges. This module covers the basics of immigrant history, demographics and integration; policies on admission and enforcement; and today's debates over immigration reform. The goal is to understand how policy has changed and how it is structured today. At the same time, attention is given to the demographic composition and socioeconomic integration of the foreign born which, in large part, results from past policies. Students will be expected to participate in class discussion, write critical memos, and write a short final, subject paper. (1.5 credits; 2nd module)

title: GANGS AND GANG VIOLENCE
description: This module will focus on the unique set of policy challenges presented by street gangs and youth gangs. The course will address definitions and the meaning of gang involvement from a policy perspective and from the perspective of individual gang members. Students will review research, statistics, and accounts of the consequences of gang involvement for individuals and the impact of gang violence on society. We will examine group dynamics within the gang to better understand what lies behind the violence. A variety of societal and public policy issues will be addressed as we review programs and policies to reduce gang activity and violence including multiple documented failures and a modest number of promising efforts. 芒聙聹Smart on crime芒聙聺 approaches to reducing gang activity and violence at the community level increasingly involve multi-disciplinary approaches that go beyond law enforcement and the justice system to involve public health, education, and others. We will explore the merits of engaging community members as partners in reducing violence. Students will be expected to participate in dynamic class discussion, prepare a number of brief critical review papers, deliver an oral briefing and a related policy recommendation memo.

title: EAST ASIAN ECONOMIC DEVELOPMENT
description: The main objective of this module is to provide students with an understanding of the leading economic development issues that emerge from a comparative analysis of the East Asian economies. The emphasis is on those issues related to the diverse economic growth and development experiences of the countries of the region. The East Asian region offers a virtual smorgasbord of different growth and development experiences. The course compares and contrasts these records with a view to explaining the differences and drawing some general lessons. Some of the key topics covered include: the miracle vs. myth controversy surrounding the economic performance of the East Asian tigers; East Asia芒聙聶s financial crisis, recovery, efforts to restructure and reduce vulnerability to external shocks and quest to raise and sustain growth in the future; the Philippines芒聙聰East Asia芒聙聶s next tiger or stray cat?; the transition from central planning to market芒聙聰the transition economies of East Asia (TEAs); Japan芒聙聶s stagnation, jagged economic recovery and future challenges; regional economic cooperation, particularly in the monetary, trade and investment spheres; and, more generally, the region芒聙聶s competitiveness and future in the global economy. In addition, the course covers the performance of global capital markets in East Asia, and the issues associated with the activities of the world芒聙聶s multilateral trade, finance, and development institutions within this region. As an entire other module is devoted to China, in order to avoid any duplication, this module covers China only in the broader regional context of achieving long term sustainable growth throughout East Asia

title: ECONOMICS OF GLOBAL CLIMATE CHANGE
description: PPOL 727: GLOBAL CLIMATE CHANGE. This course will explore the economic dimensions of responding to the risk of global climate change from human-induced changes to the earth芒聙聶s atmosphere. Framing the problem economically, this course will assess both U.S. domestic and international climate policies, paying close attention to actions within Congress and the Obama administration and diplomatic processes under the United Nations Framework Convention on Climate Change. Students will learn about the economic tools that inform debate of climate policy design and how different policy approaches strike different tradeoffs across economic efficiency and distributional outcomes. The course will emphasize the process and substance of climate policymaking within and across governments. (1.5 credits, 1st module).

title: ECONOMIC ANALYSIS FOR PUBLIC POLICY
description: Economic analysis is widely used in various public policy fields such as environmental policy, industrial organization, social welfare, and labor market. Economic principles of consumer and producer theory, social welfare economics, public goods and externality, market failure, market structure, imperfect information, business cycle, monetary and fiscal policy, and economic growth offer many insights into understanding of economics in deriving solutions to the issues of public policy. These economic principles and some quantitative methods will be studied in detail to understand the application of economic theories and quantitative models to public policy analysis. The course will provide students with the concepts and tools of economics to analyze the role of government intervention, its justification, and implications for efficiency and fairness, using specific policy areas as examples. FOR MPM STUDENTS ONLY.

title: INTELLIGENCE & PUBLIC POLICY
description: This module examines major intelligence activities and issues, exploring what intelligence is and why policy-makers need it. The module covers the organization of the U.S. Intelligence Community 芒聙聯 both before and after 9/11 芒聙聯 and examines the roles and responsibilities of the various intelligence entities that comprise the U.S. Intelligence Community. Counterintelligence, covert action, and the ethical dilemmas inherent in all intelligence operations will be addressed as well as the role of intelligence in a democratic society such as the United States and the relationship between the media, the public, and the Intelligence Community. Students will examine intelligence issues in the context of past, current, and future world events, and gain an understanding of the complexity of intelligence and the kinds of issues that intelligence practitioners much consider.

title: FINANCIAL MANAGEMENT
description: This module will consider the role federal financial management leaders play in shaping and implementing federal policies and programs. Over the past 20 years, federal financial leaders have transitioned from 芒聙聹back room芒聙聺 to 芒聙聹board room,芒聙聺 playing an increasingly important role in planning and decision-making at the executive level of today芒聙聶s agencies. The more recent economic downturn and the large federal deficit have only served to underscore the importance of having timely, reliable and relevant financial information to guide major policy decisions for the future. We will explore the responsibilities, skills and tools that a financial leader needs to create the well managed, high-performing finance organization. Some of the topics covered will include executive leadership and organizational culture; budgets and appropriations; managing people, processes and technology to reduce risks; and financial reporting, transparency and accountability. Guest speakers will present specific problems and challenges for consideration. No background in accounting or financial management is required.

title: SOCIAL INNOVATION
description: Social innovation is increasingly of interest in the policy arena. This course explores the topic, beginning with an introduction to innovation in the business sector and moving to an understanding of innovation in the social sector outside of government. We will then analyze ways that government policy inhibits, advances, and incorporates social innovation, including both historical and contemporary examples. Specific topics will include social entrepreneurship, disruptive innovation, social program design, the roles that volunteer and national service play in social innovation, and cross-sector collaboration. The class will employ multiple learning formats including case discussion, selected reading, and guest speakers involved in social innovation policy.

title: DECISION MAKING FOR PUBLIC POLICY
description: This course will explore the political and practical aspects of managing major policy change through the public policy process. The emphasis will be on the crucial executive skills of commissioning, interpreting, and communicating policy analysis. FOR MPM STUDENTS ONLY.

title: DEFENSE MANAGEMENT
description: The foremost responsibility of the United States government is to provide for the common defense. This seminar style course examines the context for national security, the making of defense policy, and the implementation of that policy. Part I surveys the context of American defense and national security. It will emphasize the post 9/11 security environment. Part II studies the defense policymaking process, with emphasis on who participates and how. Part III examines the implementation of defense policy within the Department of Defense. It will stress current activities and processes. Part IV looks at contemporary issues in national security and defense policy.

title: GLOBAL HEALTH: FINANCE & THE PRIVATE SECTOR
description: This masters-level global health course will explore principal building blocks of health systems, including health financing, the role of the private sector, and capacity building. It will be taught by a collaborative team of lecturers with a trove of real world experience in strengthening health systems in developing countries. Class sessions will not only cover technical theories and approaches, but also how in practice one generates the information and analyses--so that students learn about the "why", "what", and "what does it mean" that is the usual content of such classes, and, in addition, get some of the "how.芒聙聺

title: SEXUALITY & PUBLIC POLICY
description: PPOL 771: SEXUALITY & PUBLIC POLICY. Sexual orientation and gender identity have come to forefront of American public policy in the past decade. Evolving ideas about civil rights has created a patch work of policies in the states and at the national level. This course will examine classic civil rights laws such as Title IX of the Education Amendments of 1972 and recent initiatives such as the Employment Non-Discrimination Act (ENDA). Topics will include military policy, adoption, free speech, hate crimes, sexual harassment, and marriage/ relationship recognition. (1.5 credits, 1st module).

title: LEADING CHANGE IN EDUCATION: THEORY & PRACTICE
description: PPOL 806: LEADING CHANGE IN EDUCATION: THEORY AND PRACTICE. This module will focus on the characteristics of successful change efforts and developing a better understanding of the organizational, leadership and management challenges of implementing new policy direction in education. In some ways, education institutions are not much different from other organizations in the public, private or non-profit sectors. The reality remains that it is extremely hard to make lasting change and build new organizational capacity, particularly in complex institutional settings like schools and school systems. No matter how strong our policy proposals, no matter how compelling and well researched our advocacy for increasing rigor or supporting students, we will have difficulty achieving desired results without attention to leading organizational change and supporting high performance. We芒聙聶ll draw from the academic literature as well as examples from both K-12 and higher education. (1.5 credits; 2nd module).

title: MENTAL HEALTH POLICY
description: The module is designed for students interested in exploring issues in mental health policy in the United States. Topics to be covered include: historical, political, and economic influences on the development of U.S. mental health policy; current trends in mental health policy; financing mental health treatment; recent and proposed reforms of the mental health system; legal issues in the provision of mental health care; and special issues in mental health policy such as child/adolescent mental health and co-occurring mental health and substance use disorders.

title: RISK ASSESSMENT
description: PPOL 810: RISK ASSESSMENT: METHODS & USES FROM ACROSS THE POLICY SPECTRUM. Resources are finite, and policy-making commands trade-offs. For example, an agency or department might focus on the prevention of nuclear terrorism at the expense of other priorities, such as violent crime policing or pandemic planning/response. But such a decision might not represent the best use of scarce resources, depending on the risk associated with each priority. This module examines risk assessment as a decision-making tool in the public policy context. It introduces students to the concepts and methods of risk, risk assessment, and risk management; and it presents applications of these concepts and methods in policy settings including homeland security, financial regulation, public health, and the environment. (1.5 credits, 1st module).

title: EMERGENCY MANAGEMENT
description: . Emergency Management today is an integral component of the homeland security enterprise. This introductory course will explore the history of emergency management and its evolution in the post-9/11 and Hurricane Katrina era. The course will review the principles and practices of emergency management at the local, state, and national levels and will explore the concepts of prevention, protection, preparedness, mitigation, response and recovery.

title: STRATEGIC PLANNING & PUBLIC POLICY
description: PPOL 813: STRATEGIC PLANNING & PUBLIC POLICY. (A more formal course description is coming). Topics for this course may range from grand strategy to strategy/strategic planning in the policy arena; differences between strategy, strategic planning, operational planning, and budgeting; the use of strategy/strategic planning in business (as a counterpoint to policy); examples of good and bad strategies across government; criticisms of strategic planning; etc. (1.5 credits, 2nd module).

title: THE LAW OF POLITICAL PARTICIPATION
description: PPOL 816: THE LAW OF POLITICAL PARTICIPATION. Modern campaigns and other forms of political advocacy have grown sharply in terms of sophistication and cost. This course provides an overview of the legal framework developed in response and challenges students to weigh the competing values and policy choices it reflects. Students will gain a valuable understanding of the rules governing political activity and how they influence the conduct of political campaigns. We will also examine the impact that new media and other forms of communication have on a regulatory structure struggling to keep up with the pace of technological change. Topics covered include campaign finance and the First Amendment, ballot propositions, lobbying and government ethics. No background in law or experience in campaigns is required for this course. (1.5 credits, 1st module).

title: WASHINGTON & WALL STREET: HOW PUBLIC POLICY & FINANCIAL MARKETS INTERACT
description: PPOL 817: WASHINGTON & WALL STREET: HOW PUBLIC POLICY & FINANCIAL MARKETS INTERACT. This course will explore the two worlds of Washington and Wall Street, how they relate to each other and the difference this makes for American politics and for the global economy. One instructor is a former senior White House official; the other was voted by portfolio managers the top Washington analyst for the last several years. The course will begin with a comparison of Washington and Wall Street, the different institutional structures, imperatives and outlooks of each 芒聙聯 and the challenges this creates as each world relates to the other. Early classes will also focus on how to forecast: 1) likely Washington policy outcomes; and 2) how those outcomes affect financial markets. In this regard, the focus will not be on what policy 芒聙聹should芒聙聺 be, but rather on what policy outcomes and their market impact 芒聙聺will芒聙聺 be: how Wall Street and other observers can correctly predict Washington events and policy 芒聙聯 and how Washington officials can better assess the effect of their policies on the private markets. The course will then address these issues in the context of various macroeconomic policy issues (e.g., fiscal, monetary, trade and currency policies). Special attention will be given to more intriguing subjects that reveal the complex relationship between Washington and Wall Street. For example, how can interest rates be so low with large current and projected deficits? How do markets react to the fear of protectionism? What is the unique relationship between Washington and Wall Street in times of financial crises? (1.5 credits, 2nd module).

title: EMPIRICAL METHODS FOR PUBLIC POLICY
description: This module explores empirical methods in public policy analysis, in the context of public-sector, labor and development economics. We will discuss several research topics, focusing on their motivation, and examine the associated empirical work and econometric techniques. Emphasis will be placed on rigorously introducing various techniques, on applying them to concrete examples and on interpreting the results of such applications correctly.

title: GLOBALIZATION, POVERTY, AND INCOME INEQUALITY
description: . This module examines the nature and impact of globalization on the production of goods and services in the U.S. and abroad; how the mismeasurement of globalization has led to biases in real GDP and labor productivity growth rates; challenges in the measurement of the output of service industries and how the share of output devoted to services has shifted across developed and developing nations; and the impact of globalization on poverty and income inequality throughout the world. The course begins with a review of alternative frameworks for viewing globalization including an examination of global supply chains, a look at how iPods and semi-conductors are produced, and critical measurement issues related to factoryless manufacturing as production is offshored. The second section of the course examines the emerging consensus that the mismeasurement of offshoring is overstating real GDP and real productivity growth rates in developed nations. In addition, we examine how globalization has dramatically changed the balance of economic activity between goods-producing and service-providing industries across developed and developing economies. We also look at the issue of how to define the output of the service sector, a critical measurement challenge to accurately estimating real economic growth and productivity. We conclude this section by examining two cutting-edge proposed solutions to address the separate problems of measuring the impact of globalization on real GDP and productivity and measuring the output and productivity of the service sector. The third section of the course examines the potential (and to date, realized) impact of globalization on poverty and income inequality. We begin by examining how poverty and income inequality is measured in the U.S. and abroad, including the proposed new Supplemental Poverty Measure by the Obama Administration. We will look at theories underlying the relationship between global production trends, poverty, and income inequality as well as evaluating the progress of the U.N. Millennium Development Project in meeting goals for poverty reduction worldwide.

title: EFFECTIVE PEOPLE MANAGEMENT IN GOVERNMENT
description: For government to be effective it must have a well-qualified, motivated, and productive workforce. This course will examine the elements of effective human capital (people) management in government. It will review civil service laws, policies, and practices and the actions of public policy officials, managers, and human resources professionals that help and sometimes hinder the ability of government organizations to build and maintain the workforce it needs. The course will draw in part (but not exclusively) on lessons learned from the successes and failures of past and current efforts to improve the federal civil service encompassing over 1.9 million federal civilian employees. Active class discussions will examine topics such as: compensation policies including whether 芒聙聹pay for performance芒聙聺 is a viable option in government; effective employee recruitment, assessment, and selection practices; incorporating veterans preference and diversity goals into a merit-based civil service system; the role of public employee unions; dealing effectively with poor performers and recognizing and encouraging good performance; the business case for investing in training and development; and managing a multi-sector workforce that relies on contractors and other non-government organizations to help get the job done. Course participants will hone their understanding of the opportunities to be personally involved in the development of effective workforce management programs, strategies, and practices regardless of one芒聙聶s role in a government organization.

title: LOBBYING & GOVERNMENT RELATIONS
description: PPOL 824: LOBBYING & GOVERNMENTAL RELATIONS. The First Amendment to the Constitution guarantees Americans the right 芒聙聹to petition the Government for a redress of grievances.芒聙聺 In fact, public policy advocacy, or lobbying, is a key component of the functioning of American government and is as old as our republic. This module will focus on advocacy in the public policy process: the rules that govern lobbying activity, tactics of successful lobbying, how decision-makers deal with advocates, and how advocacy plays out and impacts public policy in the Legislative and Executive branches of the federal government. (1.5 credits, 2nd module).

title: INFRASTRUCTURE ECONOMICS AND FINANCE IN DEVELOPING COUNTRIES
description: This module introduces students to the fundamental of infrastructure in non-industrialized, lower income countries. It begins with analysis of the role of infrastructure in promoting economic growth and poverty alleviation. It will then explore how characteristics of specific infrastructure services (eg. water supply, telecommunications, energy, and transport) together with country economic and social conditions can be combined to inform the design of investment programs, service delivery mechanisms, and financing instruments towards those ends. Lastly, it will familiarize students with the substantive issues surrounding central public policy debates regarding private sector management and financing, subsidies, regulation and social/environmental impacts. (1.5 credits, 1st module).

title: SOUTH ASIAN ECONOMIC DEVELOPMENT
description: PPOL 830: SOUTH ASIAN ECONOMIC DEVELOPMENT. The main objective of this module is to enable students to understand and explain the economic development performance of the countries of South Asia with a view to assessing the region芒聙聶s future development prospects. The countries covered include the region芒聙聶s five largest: Bangladesh, India, Nepal, Pakistan and Sri Lanka. These countries, which account for over 1/5 of the world芒聙聶s population, are among the poorest in the world, and, therefore, the course necessarily focuses on poverty-related issues, including, in particular, the relationship between poverty, inequality and economic growth in South Asia. The first part of the module covers the nature and causes of South Asia芒聙聶s development performance. In this context, the major determinants of growth are analyzed, including: sector policies; macroeconomic management; human capital development; governance capacity; saving and investment rates; financial intermediation and technological progress. Both theoretical frameworks and empirical evidence are examined. The second part of the module is devoted to other special and related contemporary development issues in South Asia, including: the sustainability of India芒聙聶s recent spectacular growth performance; the urgent need to improve human capital; the lessons from East Asia; regional cooperation and integration in South Asia; and the region芒聙聶s future development prospects. (1.5 credits, 2nd module).

title: CHINESE ECONOMICS AND POLICY
description: This course will review important dimensions of China's successful economic development, from 1978 to today, including its successful response to the global financial crisis. The course will provide both an overview of China's 30+ years of rapid growth and expanding international commerce as well as analysis of the institutional restructuring and strategic policy methods employed. It will look at all major dimensions of the economic record, including macroeconomic fluctuations, rural developments, industrial and commercial enterprise reforms, population and labor force trends, poverty and inequality measures, social services and safety nets, fiscal and taxation systems, financial system evolution, energy and environmental challenges, international trade and capital movements, including exchange rate controversies, and the links between rapid economic development and outbreaks of social unrest. China芒聙聶s controversial potential global role in the post crisis world will also receive significant attention. The course will stress analytical thinking and student development of independent points of view on the various contentious issues raised by China芒聙聶s record.

title: Managing the Implementation of Development Projects
description: PPOL 841: MANAGING THE IMPLEMENTATION OF DEVELOPMENT PROJECTS. This module will present the tools and procedures for planning and implementing projects in developing countries including methods and practices for managing the entire project life cycle. The topics covered will include the project preparation process; the role of the project manager; risk analysis and common problems; the use of the logical framework to define measureable objectives; scheduling with the work breakdown structure and the critical path method; designing and operating control and reporting systems for cost, time, resources and performance; and a case study to put it all together. The course will be built on the experience of the successes and failures of hundreds of development projects and will present an overall framework for developing an effective project management system. (1.5 credits, 1st module). Professor Robert Youker.

title: PROCUREMENT & PROJECT DEVELOPMENT ANALYSIS
description: PPOL 842: PROCUREMENT & PROJECT DEVELOPMENT ANALYSIS. The course examines the rationale and instruments deployed in the design, procurement of goods and services and financial and socio-economic analysis of development projects. Topics addressed will include technical aspects of project design and appraisal, overall introduction to procurement of goods, procuring consultants and other services, analysis of the project cycle from preparation to implementation, testing the viability of projects, and view of the project in the macro environment. (1.5 credits, 2nd module).

title: AGRICULTURE, FOOD POLICY, & RURAL DEVELOPMENT
description: . Global poverty is a predominantly rural phenomenon. It is estimated that three-quarters of those living on less than a dollar a day reside in rural areas. This is often provided as a rationale for focusing on growth in the agricultural sector to drive economic development. This course considers the merits and limitations of this rationale and assesses how best to achieve rural development. The course examines the role of agriculture in development and the effectiveness of different prominent agricultural policies in encouraging growth and poverty reduction. The course will look in depth at examples of specific development initiatives, assessing their effectiveness and identifying possible explanations for their success or failure. Students will have the opportunity to take a position on key current debates within the development community giving a rationale for a particular position.

title: THE ECONOMICS OF CONFLICT & VIOLENCE
description: In its multiple forms, conflict is one of the main global obstacles for development in the world today. This course explores the economic and social underpinnings explaining the occurrence and duration of conflicts. It focuses on civil conflict, terrorism and violent crime. The course reviews economic theories of conflict and crime, systematizing their implications in the socioeconomic (growth, poverty, social capital, and national culture) and political (democracy) spheres. The course examines both theoretical and empirical advances and draws from recent works in the field. Also, a session assesses the merits of available conflict datasets for future empirical work. (1.5 credits, 1st module).

title: TAX POLITICS & POLICY
description: . Federal tax law affects virtually every aspect of Americans芒聙聶 lives. In addition to claiming roughly a fifth of the national product, the tax system with its complex incentives and penalties is a major component of government policy toward health care, housing, poverty alleviation, retirement security, education, and host of other areas. What are the main features of our tax system and how did they get to where they are today? What is meant by fairness, efficiency, and simplicity in taxation and how do we choose among these goals? What drives the making of tax policy and how does the presentation of tax data and analyses influence the process? How does the tax law treat rich people, poor people, and different size families? Is major tax reform possible and, if so, what direction should a reform take? The objectives of the course are: 1) to understand the basic principles of tax policy, 2) to understand how analysis and the presentation of data mix with ideology and special interests to affect tax policy outcomes, and 3) to gain a deeper understanding of some of the major tax policy issues and choices facing the United States today.

title: Public Speaking
description: A performance course designed to introduce basic principles of communication and the classical roots from which they were derived. Students will prepare and present speeches in both formal platform settings and informal group discussions. While attention will be given to extemporaneous delivery, the emphasis of the course is on work behind-the-scenes: organizing ideas, structuring messages, and adapting messages for specific audiences. Attention will also be given to methods for evaluating oral discourse. Students who experience anxiety in public speaking situations are encouraged to enroll. Fall and Spring.

title: Public Speaking

Students will prepare and present speeches in both formal platform settings and informal group discussions. While attention will be given to extemporaneous delivery, the emphasis of the course is on work behind-the-scenes: organizing ideas, structuring messages, and adapting messages for specific audiences. Attention will also be given to methods for evaluating oral discourse. Students who experience anxiety in public speaking situations are encouraged to enroll. Fall and Spring.

title: Public Speaking: Persuasion
description: Persuasive Communication is a performance course designed to help you become a more effective, confident, and competent communicator. Specifically this course is organized to help you: 1) Develop the ability to organize a persuasive message effectively by structuring and adapting material to your audience and the rhetorical situation. 2) Develop the ability to convey persuasive messages orally with clarity, credibility, and conviction. 3) Develop critical thinking and analytic skills by examining how persuasive communication impacts our culture and exerts social influence.

title: Intensive First-Level Russian I
description: The course meets six hours per week with two professors. Emphasis on standard Moscow pronunciation and intonation through extensive work with on-line audio materials and in-class videos; comprehension and practice of everyday spoken Russian as used in different social situations. Fundamentals of Russian grammar and acquisition of basic vocabulary.

title: Intensive Second-Level Russian I
description: The course meets six hours per week with two professors. It completes the introduction to the fundamentals of Russian grammar and develops students' control of everyday spoken and written Russian. The course includes work with audio and video cassettes.

title: Russia A-Z
description: This course will offer students an introduction to major topics in Russian culture, beginning with Christianization in 988 and extending to the present. Its primary goals are: to provide students with the necessary cultural background for study abroad in the Russian Federation; and to acquaint them with fields and topics of research in Slavic. It will be team taught by members of the Department of Slavic Languages and will be primarily in lecture format (all lectures in English).

Its primary goals are: to provide students with the necessary cultural background for study abroad in the Russian Federation; and to acquaint them with fields and topics of research in Slavic. It will be team taught by members of the Department of Slavic Languages and will be primarily in lecture format (all lectures in English).

title: Third-Level Russian I
description: This course focuses on major topics of grammar that are reviewed and analyzed in greater depth. Special attention is paid to writing compositions and analyzing grammatical structures as they function in text. The course also includes the study of syntax, word order, word formation, and less frequently encountered structures.

title: Third-Level Russian
description: This course focuses on major topics of grammar that are reviewed and analyzed in greater depth. Special attention is paid to writing compositions and analyzing grammatical structures as they function in text. The course also includes the study of syntax, word order, word formation, and less frequently encountered structures.

title: Issues in Russian Society & Culture I
description: This course focuses on oral proficiency. A variety of materials--textbook, video and audio cassettes, Russian literature selections and readings in the Russian press--will be used to develop the students' ability to converse on daily topics and handle cultural, political and social issues in Russian life today.

title: Russia(n) in Context

title: Forth-Level Russian
description: The goal of the course is to enlarge students' vocabulary and to develop their ability to conduct conversation and discussion on a variety of issues and topics that pertain to the use of the language in their future professions. Class discussions focus on contemporary issues and are based on articles from the Russian press, the Internet, and television programs.

title: Forth-Level Russian
description: The goal of the course is to enlarge students' vocabulary and to develop their ability to conduct conversation and discussion on a variety of issues and topics that pertain to the use of the language in their future professions. Class discussions focus on contemporary issues and are based on articles from the Russian press, the Internet, and television programs.

title: Russian Phonology
description: An examination of the sound system of contemporary Russian, including suprasegmentals (stress, intonation, juncture). Readings on important issues in Russian phonology by both Russian and Western specialists. Particular emphasis on socio-cultural themes: 1) the significance of phonological differences between standard literary Russian and colloquial speech; 2) nonstandard pronunciations, both regional varieties and urban "prostorechie," and assessments of them by different populations; 3) phonological and intonational developments in emigr? Russian and the implications thereof for speakers in the motherland; 4) more broadly, mainstream attitudes toward identifiably non-mainstream speech forms. (This course satisfies one semester of the College's social science general education requirement.) Prerequisites: RUSS-211 and -212 or RUSS-213 and 214, or equivalent. Taught in Russian.

title: Professional Russian I
description: Designed for graduate students in CERES and MSFS who will be using Russian in their professional lives, this course aims to enhance the participants' command of the language in both the oral and written forms. The course will focus on the preparation, delivery and discussion of oral presentations and written essays on topics related to the students' professional interests. Topics will range from international relations, politics and economics to Russian literature and popular culture.

title: Professional Russian II
description: Designed for graduate students in CERES and MSFS who will be using Russian in their professional lives, this course aims to enhance the participants' command of the language in both the oral and written forms. The course will focus on the preparation, delivery and discussion of oral presentations and written essays on topics related to the students' professional interests. Topics will range from international relations, politics and economics to Russian literature and popular culture.

title: Tutorial: Senior Honors Thesis
description: Tutorial: Senior Honors Thesis

title: Pushkin: Eugine Onegin
description: A close reading of the text and analysis of the themes and structure of Pushkin's novel in verse, accompanied by the study of its historical, cultural, and literary contexts. Primary text will be read in Russian (with parallel English text available), and class discussions will be conducted primarily in Russian. Students will write several brief assignments in Russian and a longer research paper in English.

title: Between Opposites: Literature and Revolution (20th Century Russian Literature I)
description: In this course we will explore tension points of Soviet literature: masses and individuals, gender revised, search for new language, how to achieve immortality? Our focus is early Soviet culture as struggle of opposite social concepts and aesthetic systems. We will observe bizarre forms that traditional genres can take when they are filled with incompatible meanings. Every ideological concept in early Soviet times has its antagonist. What artistic prisms does it choose to represent its own truth? Can it appear in disguise of its rival? We will read plays (Gorky and Mayakovsky), poems (Akhmatova and Blok), novels (Gladkov and Platonov), and verify our interpretations on material of visual arts (paintings, films). Conducted in English, mainly in form of discussions.

title: Narrative & Memory: Dostoevsky's Murders
description: How does Dostoevsky's narrative manipulate the reader's memory and sense of responsibility? Are we ourselves Dostoevskian murderers who have forgotten about the murders we committed? The four great murder novels--Crime and Punishment, The Idiot, Demons, (a.k.a., in English, The Possessed), and The Brothers Karamazov--seem to offer an answer. Memory and forgetting implicate the reader--specifically, the ongoing dialogue between people's memories and the lapses thereof. The author uses this dialogue to encode/"program" the reader's memory and to "infect" him/her with various faults of the characters' memory. (Not offered 2004-05)

title: History of the Russian Language
description: The course is divided into three major units: 1) Historical phonology, beginning with the major Proto-Slavic sound changes, and an examination of Old Russian texts; 2) Historical morphology and syntax, with an explicit comparison of early East Slavic to Contemporary Standard Russian (CSR), and an examination of Old Russian texts; 3) The history of the literary language, especially its codification in the eighteenth and early nineteenth centuries and the standardization efforts of the Soviet period. Sociocultural topics incude the function of Church Slavonic versus the vernacular East Slavic in Kievan Rus芒聙聶 and medieval Russia; the role of Church Slavonicisms in the various developmental stages of the language; the arguments over the codification of the language between 芒聙聹Westernizers芒聙聺 and 芒聙聹Slavophiles芒聙聺 during the eighteenth and early nineteenth centuries; the role and function of Russian as the lingua franca of the multiethnic Russian empire and Soviet state; gender and the status of women as reflected in linguistic developments during the Soviet period; the standardization of the language during the Soviet period and linguistic prejudices against non-standard speech varieties. (This course satisfies one semester of the College's social-science requirement as a linguistics offering.)

title: Sixth-Level Russian
description: The goal of this course is to develop participants芒聙聶 professional command of Russian speech in oral and written forms. Special attention will be paid to the grammatical and lexical precision of the ideas and opinions on sophisticated professional topics. The focus is of this course is on the preparation, delivery, and discussion of oral presentations and written essays on topics related to participants芒聙聶 professional interests. Topics will range from the fields of international relations, economics and politics, to Russian and Soviet culture and literature.

title: Law and Disorder From Socialism to Post-Socialism
description: Western media frequently portray the former Communist states of Europe and Eurasia as being mired in a peculiar mix of corruption, crime and chaos. The objective of this course is to familiarize students with the legal cultures, institutions, and practices fostered under Soviet style socialism and then analyze with them current efforts to establish new legal systems. We will address the role of law (and criminality) in both the emerging democracies and new illiberal regimes among the states of the former Soviet Union and Eastern Europe. We will give special attention to corruption, the influence of Western ideas and institutions, and popular attitudes toward law as an element of consolidating democracy.

title: History and Politics of Central Asia
description: This course offers a survey of the history and politics of Central Asia from Russian conquest to the present. While focusing on the five countries of contemporary Central Asia (Kazakhstan, Kyrgyzstan, Tajikistan, Turkmenistan, and Uzbekistan), the course will frame the region in its broader Eurasian context, looking at transnational social and religious movements, diaspora populations, and shared cultural practices that unite the Central Asian states and link them to their neighbors. Combining an historical survey of the region with a study of contemporary Central Asian politics and society, the course will examine both imperial expansion and contemporary geopolitics, everyday life and international security, the Soviet legacy and the future of Islam.

title: Romania in the Long 20th Century
description: Course Description: On 1 January 2007 Romania joined the European Union and the extended family of European democracies. That the country should have satisfied the criteria for EU membership barely seventeen years after overthrowing one of the most odious dictatorships in Europe of the twentieth century is nothing short of remarkable. Romania芒聙聶s membership formally and finally reversed its political consignment at the end of World War II to the Soviet sphere of influence 芒聙聯 with the attendant social and cultural consequences - and, at the same time, will harness and mesh the country into the economic and political development of the Union. The course will discuss these features of the country芒聙聶s experience and will focus of on the period from 1914 to the present day. It will be divided into four parts. The first will chart Romania芒聙聶s course during World War I, the second will address the challenges faced by the enlarged state after the war, the drift into alliance with Nazi Germany and Romania芒聙聶s role in the geography of the Holocaust, the third will examine the period of Communist rule, and the final part the degree to which Romania, like the other former Communist states of East Central Europe, has, with the prize of European membership in its sights, completed two decades of revolutionary change. Political organization and - to a large degree - social structure have been transformed, property control distanced from the state, and Marxism-Leninism replaced as the dominant ideology by liberalism and nationalism. This is a reading-oriented seminar; the emphasis will be on close reading and discussion of the assigned material. Each seminar will begin with a brief student presentation, followed by a short presentation, occasionally by power-point, by me. We will spend the remaining class time discussing the assigned readings and their relationship to the themes of the course. Course Requirements Students are expected to attend all classes and to complete assigned readings in the agreed time. Students will be graded on both class participation and written work. Graduate students will occasionally have different reading assignments and different assessment tasks.

title: Police State & Transitional Justice
description: The focus of this course is on the coercive mechanisms of selected totalitarian and authoritarian regimes. It addresses the question of to what extent and how democratic governments in the above regions deal with the crimes and abuses of their authoritarian predecessors, and to what degree the countries of these region regard a reckoning with the past as an essential component of the sustainability of democracy. The course will be divided into three parts. The first will take a historical approach and look at forms of the police state in Nazi Germany, the Soviet Union, and certain countries of East Central Europe after World War II, the second will consider the theory and practice of transitional justice, and the third will examine the approaches to transitional justice taken in selected states, providing case studies that include, for a wider comparative dimension, the experience of South Africa under apartheid, Chile and Argentina. This is a reading-oriented seminar; the emphasis will be on close reading and discussion of the assigned material. Each seminar will begin with a presentation by Dr Deletant, followed by a brief student presentation. The remaining class time will be spent discussing the assigned readings and their relationship to the themes of the course.

title: Introduction to Area Studies
description: REES-500 is a seminar course designed to enhance CERES students芒聙聶 ability to write and conduct research at the graduate level and introduce a variety of disciplinary lenses used to study the region. We will begin with a discussion of the development of 芒聙聵area studies芒聙聶, turn to the disciplinary approaches that economics, history, political science, literary/cultural studies, and anthropology/sociology use and then focus on individual research projects. Students should end the course with a better understanding of the study of Central and Eastern Europe (CEE), the former Soviet Union (FSU) and Eurasia and enhanced skills for rigorous thinking and writing according to the traditions of the disciplines listed above.

title: Russia & China: Past, Present & Future
description: Russia and China are two of the most important players in an evolving multi-polar global order. Together, they dominate the Eurasian landmass. These two neighbors might enjoy the closest of economic and political relations, but instead they have a legacy of strategic rivalry, punctuated by fleeting periods of cooperation. The government of the United States has not always proven adept at understanding the complex relationship between Russia and China. Looking ahead, are Russia and China destined to be friends, enemies, or 芒聙聹frenemies芒聙聺? Will these two nuclear-armed great powers, one the world芒聙聶s most populous nation, the other the world芒聙聶s biggest country with enormous untapped natural resources, forge a constructive relationship based on common interests? Or are they more likely be drawn into a potentially disastrous competition for economic, political, and military clout, not only in their overlapping spheres of interest in the 芒聙聹near abroad,芒聙聺 but also on the global stage? Our course will explore the many dimensions of this odd couple 芒聙聯 their geographic, historical, political, economic, anthropological, sociological, and ideological interactions 芒聙聯 to gain a better understanding of what motivates both countries and drives their dynamic in the 21st Century. What kind of relationship will these two great powers forge in the future, and what are the implications for U.S. policymakers? How can the United States better achieve its foreign policy objectives with each member of the odd couple by correctly judging their relationship with each other? Classes will be a blend of lecture and group discussion and analysis.

title: Migration and Diaspora in Eurasia
description: Eurasia is characterized not only by remarkable ethnic, religious, and linguistic diversity, but also by an astounding mobility of populations. Nomads, diaspora communities, exiles, refugees, cross-border populations, and labor migrants have long been central to the history and politics of the region. Defining Eurasia broadly to include not only the former Soviet Union but also adjacent areas of Eastern Europe, the Middle East, and Afghanistan, this research seminar will bring the story of these mobile communities to the fore. After being introduced to a selection of relevant literature on the subject, students will be expected to produce an original, article-length research paper. While this is an interdisciplinary seminar and students may choose to write on any historical or contemporary migration or diaspora community of interest to them, they are expected to base their paper on substantial primary source research. Possible topics include (but are no means limited to): Germans in the Russian Empire, pastoral nomads in the Soviet Union, refugee populations fleeing war and conflict in the region, cross-border communities in Afghanistan and Iran, or the experience of migrants from the Caucasus and Central Asia in contemporary Russia.

title: Afghanistan: Politics, Geopolitics and Security
description: Since 2001, Afghanistan has been in the news as a security challenge to the US and NATO. However, the geopolitical significance of this Central Asian state reaches much farther back in history. The purpose of this course is to focus attention on the country of Afghanistan in the context on regional security and broader geopolitical considerations. In this, the goal is to understand what is at stake for the citizens of Afghanistan and the external powers that are currently engaged in the country. The course is roughly divided into three parts. First, although this is not a history course, attention will be spent on the basic trends and events in Afghanistan芒聙聶s past. Outside interventions and movements (ethnic groups, religious movements, and empires) have shaped what is seen in the country today. Second, the course will assess the key issues confronting the modern, post-Taliban state of Afghanistan today. How does the Afghan government (and people) view the challenges of governance, economic development, insurgency, terrorism, and narcotics trafficking? Third, the course will conclude with the roles and interests of outside powers, such as China, Pakistan, Iran, the United States and other regional players. No prior knowledge of, or expertise in Afghanistan and Afghan affairs is required for this course.

title: Russian Law and Legal Reform in Comparativeve Perspective
description: This seminar is devoted to an examination of post-Soviet Russian legal reform. Among the questions we will examine from different perspectives: What were the starting points for reform of the Soviet legal system? What reforms have been accomplished in the past 18 years? How do we measure the distances between these points? The first half of the seminar will consider the theoretical, political, and historical aspects of these questions in assessing Russia's legal development since the collapse of the USSR. The second half of the seminar is devoted to a more substantive examination of selected legal topics. This seminar will take an interdisciplinary and comparative approach. The legal systems of nineteenth century Russia, the post-socialist states of Central and Eastern Europe, the United States, and Western Europe will all be points of comparison. No previous study of law or legal training (in any legal system) is required, or even expected. Our discussions will center on texts drawn from political science, history, and law.

title: Building Capitalism: Post-Communist Transition
description: The purpose of this course is to provide graduate students with an empirical understanding of what postcommunist economic transformation was about, its aims, issues, and outcomes. It offers a broad empirical and thematic overview of post-communist economic transformation in Eastern, Central Europe and Central Asia. It will discuss how communism collapsed, alternative reform programs, the development of output, liberalization, financial stabilization, privatization, corruption and governance, social developments and policy, and the role of the outside world. It is designed for graduate students in various social sciences with a regional focus on Eurasia. An understanding of economic reasoning is required. After this course, a student should feel that he or she understands what postcommunist economic transformation was about, what the main issues and disputes were, and what the main differences between the transition countries were.

title: Introduction to Environmental Science
description: This is an introductory course on the science of environmental systems. We will survey the breadth of this interdisciplinary field with lectures, discussions, and laboratory and field exercises designed to introduce the basics of what I think every citizen should know. Whatever one's ideological perspective, environmental science is about the home of humanity, our basic life-support systems. We introduce the earth's spheres: atmosphere, hydrosphere, lithosphere, and biosphere. These make life, including human life, possible, and understanding them is analogous to a scuba diver understanding scuba tanks. Humans influence each of these spheres, sometimes in ways that affect the production of ecosystem services for humans and the whole ecosystem. Thus, we study the scientific aspects of how these systems work and how humans are changing and, in some cases, degrading them. For example, we study the systems of the atmosphere that support and influence life, and we study air pollution and how the atmosphere may both accentuate and ameliorate pollution. Environmental science has always been a science that integrates natural with social science because it studies natural systems not only to understand nature but also to understand human relationships with nature. Global Warming, for example, is a central concern of environmental science, but to understand it fully requires we must understand the history and science of natural climate change, the history of human-induced greenhouse gas emissions, and the models that predict climate change. Moreover, environmental science has always been an applied science; thus we must also introduce the practical approaches for solving problems. The course will emphasize the positive gains as well as the abject failures of human-environment interactions. We will compare different management approaches (such as market-based, grass-roots, and command-and-control) and the government (such as the EPA) and non government entities (such as the World Bank) that influence them.

title: STIA Tutorial
description: Permission of STIA Field Chair (Dr. Elizabeth Stephen) Required

title: STIA Tutorial
description: Permission of STIA Field Chair Required

title: Science & Technology in the Global Arena
description: This course is an introduction to how science and technology affect foreign affairs, and how international affairs influence science and technology. It is the gateway course required of SFS juniors majoring in science, technology and international affairs (STIA), and College science majors taking the STIA certificate. It may also be taken by undergraduates and graduate students from anywhere in the university without special permission. It has no prerequisites, and is suitable both for the policy generalist and for students with backgrounds in science. The course uses examples drawn from environment, security, nuclear policy, information, communications, energy, homeland defense, health, and manufacturing technology, and explores issues of sustainability, scientific risk and uncertainty, the links of science and technology with economics and geopolitics, scientific advice to governments, and government support to research and innovation. It explores the role of technological innovation in increasing productivity and competitiveness, and in solving critical social problems.

title: Environmental Geomorphology
description: This is a basic upper division course in geomorphology, a core subject of geography and geology and integral to many other sciences. Geomorphology is a science that studies the landforms of planetary surfaces and the processes that create these landforms. The field has a long history that stretches back to the earliest speculation about the earth; humans are innately interested in the landscape about them. Since many universities have many related courses where we have but one, I focus on three central themes: (1) a survey of geomorphic forms and processes, (2) some basic analytical skills including some field experience, and (3) some practical applications, especially to ecology and geological hazards. The analytical skills we develop in this course include: map and areal photography interpretation, landscape analysis, some field methods, natural hazards assessment, and the applications of some basic mathematical models. We will concentrate on the external agents of geomorphic change (water, ice, wind, and chemical change), but we must also study the tectonics and structure of the earth in order to understand its surface.

title: Energy and Int'l Sec.
description: This course will provide an introduction to the fundamentals of energy and how it affects international relations. The course will provide students with an introduction to the basics of oil, gas, electricity and non-fossil fuel energy. It will also focus on the challenges faced by oil and gas exporting states (especially in governance and economic stability), as well as the challenges faced by importing states as they seek both to secure sufficient energy for their nations芒聙聶 futures, and to respond to the emerging threat of climate change.

title: Global Health Systems and Politics
description: This course examines health systems and health policies from the global perspective and at the level of national comparisons, with a focus on but not a limitation to developing countries. Regional and national comparisons and analysis will be made with respect to 1) population health芒聙聰including but not limited to Millennium Development Goals; 2) health policies and systems and their relationship to broad public sector policies and systems; 3) roles of and resources for health providers; 4) cultural, socioeconomic and other influences on health policies, systems and outcomes; 5) the impact of conflict and natural disasters; 6) the role and function of community-based health services; and 7) resource allocation with respect to addressing critical and emerging health problems, including balancing communicable and non-communicable diseases. The course will also address the role of international donor, regulatory and other bodies in health systems and policies and in particular their impact on health systems and policies in developing countries. Understanding of how health care is delivered in diverse contexts and particularly in low-resource countries will enable those who are engaged in international health at a variety of levels 芒聙聯 from providing direct clinical service to providing technical assistance to strengthen health systems and policies to have an appreciation of the variable contexts in which they will be engaged.

title: Global Mental Health
description: This survey course is intended to provide students with an introduction to, and overview of select topics in global mental health. Following an examination of the epidemiology of mental illness domestically and internationally, the course will move into a series of case studies that document the importance of focusing on mental health and illness in the global context. The course further seeks to communicate to students the complexity of addressing this area by providing examples of individual, interpersonal and societal determinants and moderators of mental health around the world, and describing the reciprocal relationship between mental and physical health. The course will conclude by presenting intervention models aimed at overcoming the treatment gap and reducing disparities in mental health. Course assignments are designed to impart fundamental applied research skills that will serve students in their ensuing educational and/or professional pursuits.

title: Gender & Sustainability
description: This course will focus on the gendered perspectives of environmental sustainability and eco-efficient agriculture in order to reach food security and equitable access to resources. The course will present an overview of gender analysis to understand patterns of land use and conservation strategies, both individually and collectively and how male and female farmers contribute differently to conservation and agricultural production efforts. Students will be presented with real case studies from Africa, Asia and Latin America, documenting gender gaps in the access to a large range of agricultural resources, including land and farm labour, forest, livestock, extension services, and other assets. Current international trends and research streams on gender and agriculture will be discussed and analyzed with the aim to influence policy to reach lasting change.

title: Global Health and Ethics
description: This course is designed to introduce students to ethical issues in health care policy, planning and service delivery in the global context. Through the application to practical decision-making at all levels of the healthcare system of a broad range of ethical principles and moral theories the students will gain an understanding of 1) the definitions of healthcare ethics and bioethics, including comparative interpretations; 2) the complexities of healthcare ethics and bioethics in the global context; 3) the protocol and systems in place to ensure adherence to ethical principles; and 4) their specific roles and responsibilities to ensure such adherence. Consideration of healthcare ethics and bioethics is of increasing concern in the international health sphere in light of a) the existing and emerging pandemics b) the development, testing and dissemination of new medical technology, including that related to genetic research; c) the expanding global reach of health provider organizations; and d) increasing implementation of research studies in developing countries by researchers in high-income countries. The need for those engaged in international health at a wide range of levels to have an understanding of global healthcare ethics and bioethics and the means available to address them is paramount.

title: Systems Thinking and Modeling Methods
description: This course is an introduction to systems thinking and modeling complex and interconnected problems using system dynamics. What is system dynamics? In short, it is a 芒聙聹computer-aided approach to policy analysis and design.
